title: Enhancing Malware Detection with Generative AI: Using Variational Autoencoders to Boost Machine Learning Classifiers' Performance
summary:
The advancement of malware poses obstacles for cybersecurity, necessitating the development of advanced detection techniques. This paper proposes an approach to enhance malware detection through the use of a generative artificial intelligence model. Specifically, variational autoencoders (VAEs) are used with the random forest, XGBoost and sequential model machine learning classifiers. Generated synthetic malware samples are used to address the critical issue of data scarcity for new or less common malware types. This approach can be used to augment datasets to improve classifier robustness.
  The proposed methodology uses VAEs to create high-quality diverse synthetic datasets that closely mimic real-world malware data. The effectiveness of these augmented datasets is evaluated by comparing the performance of the machine learning classifiers when they are trained with the original data and when they are trained with the synthetic data-augmented datasets. The results demonstrate a notable improvement in the accuracy, precision, recall and F1-scores of the classifiers, when they are trained using the augmented datasets. The enhanced performance for detecting various malware classes shows the potential of this approach to facilitate adaptation to evolving malware threats effectively. This work demonstrates the utility of generative AI for cybersecurity. It also provides a foundation for future research aimed at developing more resilient and adaptive malware detection systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) to generate synthetic malware samples for data augmentation, addressing the problem of data scarcity for rare or new malware types. The augmented datasets are used to train Random Forest, XGBoost, and Sequential Model classifiers, yielding notable improvements in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score compared to training on original data alone.
AI.motivation: The core motivation is the data scarcity problem for new or less common malware types, which hampers the robustness and adaptability of machine learning-based malware detection systems. Without sufficient training samples, classifiers struggle to generalize to emerging threats.
AI.method: The methodology employs Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) to generate high-quality, diverse synthetic malware samples that closely mimic real-world data. These synthetic samples are used to augment the original training datasets. Three classifiers — Random Forest, XGBoost, and a Sequential (neural network) model — are trained on both the original and the augmented datasets for comparative evaluation.
AI.result: Training classifiers on VAE-augmented datasets yields notable improvements across all evaluated metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The enhanced performance is observed across multiple malware classes, demonstrating the approach's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI, specifically VAEs, demonstrates significant utility for cybersecurity applications by mitigating data scarcity in malware detection. This work lays a foundation for future research into more resilient and adaptive malware detection systems that can better cope with evolving threats.

title: Subtle Injection for Ground-truth Inference of LLM Training Data
summary:
As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly trained on scraped web corpora without authorisation, content owners require forensic methods to prove that their documents were included in a model's training set. We propose \textbf{SIGIL} (\textbf{S}ubtle \textbf{I}njection for \textbf{G}round-truth \textbf{I}nference of \textbf{L}LM training data), a framework that embeds imperceptible \emph{canary sequences} into protected text and code such that any LLM trained on those documents exhibits statistically detectable behavioural signatures when probed with targeted queries.
  SIGIL defines five canary strategies -- lexical-rare, lexical-phrase, syntactic, semantic, and code-pattern -- and a \emph{Membership Inference Score} (MIS) grounded in the Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing framework with formal false-positive rate (FPR) control. Simulator parameters are calibrated against the empirical membership inference literature, yielding realistic heterogeneous results across $36{,}000$ trials: overall AUC $= 0.892$, rising from $0.831$ at $0.1\%$ injection to $0.947$ at $10\%$. Detection rates range from $33\%$ to $78\%$ across model-size and injection-rate conditions. Code Pattern canaries achieve the highest AUC ($0.903$, Cohen's $d = 1.84$); Syntactic Structure the lowest ($0.875$, $d = 1.63$). All four experimental factors -- injection rate, model size, canary strategy, and mixture ratio -- have significant independent effects on MIS ($p < 0.001$). SIGIL maintains AUC $> 0.86$ even under $100\%$ paraphrasing ($\text{AUC} = 0.864$), confirming robustness through semantic leakage that survives surface-level rewriting.

AI.tldr: SIGIL embeds imperceptible canary sequences into text/code; LLMs trained on these documents later exhibit statistically detectable behaviors when probed, enabling forensic proof of training data inclusion with formal false-positive rate control.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly trained on unauthorized scraped web corpora, content owners need forensic methods to prove their documents were included in a model's training set. No existing method provides imperceptible, statistically rigorous, and robust membership inference across diverse content types.
AI.method: SIGIL proposes five canary strategies (lexical-rare, lexical-phrase, syntactic, semantic, code-pattern) embedded imperceptibly into protected content. A Membership Inference Score (MIS) is grounded in the Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing framework with formal false-positive rate (FPR) control. Simulator parameters are calibrated against empirical membership inference literature, with evaluation across 36,000 trials manipulating injection rate, model size, canary strategy, and mixture ratio.
AI.result: Overall AUC = 0.892 (range: 0.831 at 0.1% injection to 0.947 at 10%). Detection rates span 33%–78% across conditions. Code Pattern canaries achieve highest AUC (0.903, Cohen's d=1.84); Syntactic Structure lowest (0.875, d=1.63). All four experimental factors have significant independent effects (p<0.001). Robustness confirmed under 100% paraphrasing (AUC=0.864).
AI.conclusion: SIGIL provides a practical, statistically rigorous forensic framework for proving unauthorized training data inclusion in LLMs. Its imperceptible canaries survive paraphrasing through semantic leakage, and the Neyman-Pearson framework ensures reliable FPR control. Code-pattern canaries are most effective, and detection power scales with injection rate.

title: MalTree: Tracing Malware Evolution from Embeddings at Scale
summary: Malware detection remains largely reactive: machine learning models trained on known samples degrade as threats evolve. Understanding evolutionary relationships among malware families can inform proactive defense, but traditional reverse engineering can take months to years to uncover such lineage relationships. We propose MalTree, a framework that applies bioinformatics inspired phylogenetic techniques (UPGMA and Neighbor-Joining) at scale to model malware evolution automatically using structural, behavioral, and image-based features. We introduce temporal validation using VirusTotal timestamps to assess whether inferred trees reflect actual evolutionary order. MalTree achieves 87% temporal consistency, indicating that inferred evolutionary relationships closely align with real-world emergence timelines. Our analysis shows that some families mutate over 10 times faster than others, suggesting that detection strategies should be tailored to family-specific evolutionary tempos. Case studies, including the Mirai botnet, confirm that inferred relationships from our phylogenetic tree align with documented threat intelligence. Our framework provides a foundation for shifting malware analysis from sample-by-sample classification toward lineage-aware evolutionary modeling.

AI.tldr: MalTree uses bioinformatics-inspired phylogenetic techniques (UPGMA, Neighbor-Joining) with structural, behavioral, and image features to automatically model malware evolution at scale. It achieves 87% temporal consistency validated against VirusTotal timestamps, reveals family-specific mutation rates differing by over 10×, and demonstrates that inferred lineages align with documented threat intelligence (e.g., Mirai).
AI.motivation: Current malware detection is reactive and degrades as threats evolve. Understanding evolutionary relationships among malware families could enable proactive defense, but traditional reverse engineering is prohibitively slow (months to years). A scalable, automated method for inferring malware lineage is needed.
AI.method: MalTree extracts structural, behavioral, and image-based features from malware samples, then applies phylogenetic tree-building algorithms (UPGMA and Neighbor-Joining) to infer evolutionary relationships. Temporal validation uses VirusTotal first-seen timestamps to assess whether branching order in inferred trees matches real-world emergence sequences.
AI.result: The framework achieves 87% temporal consistency, confirming that inferred phylogenetic trees largely reflect actual emergence order. Analysis reveals some malware families mutate over 10 times faster than others. Case studies (e.g., Mirai botnet) show inferred relationships match documented threat intelligence.
AI.conclusion: MalTree provides a foundation for shifting malware analysis from reactive sample-by-sample classification toward proactive, lineage-aware evolutionary modeling. Family-specific evolutionary tempos suggest detection strategies should be tailored accordingly.

title: AgileOS: A GPU Operating System Layer for Protected CUDA Services
summary:
Modern GPU applications increasingly interact with storage systems, network devices, vendor libraries, and GPU-resident services rather than executing only isolated compute kernels. This shift creates a need for operating-system-like protection around GPU services, where service metadata, device queues, memory-mapped I/O regions, and library-internal state should not be directly exposed to untrusted application kernels. However, today's CUDA programming model, by default, still gives each application direct ownership of its CUDA context, device pointers, runtime handles, module loading path, and kernel launches, leaving protected GPU services to build their own ad hoc interfaces and isolation mechanisms.
  This paper presents the initial design and prototype scope of AgileOS, a GPU operating-system layer for protected CUDA services. AgileOS virtualizes CUDA at the library boundary: applications link against client-side CUDA Runtime, Driver, and selected library shims, while a trusted runtime worker owns the real CUDA context and mediates supported operations. To protect service state and module interfaces, AgileOS also defines a GPU memory-management model that separates user allocations from protected module/MMIO ranges, using pointer validation and memory access guards via PTX injection. AgileOS is modularized and flexible, supporting a range of protected services and existing libraries such as cuFFT and PyTorch. The prototype includes client-side interceptors, worker-side CUDA handlers, virtualized CUDA object tables, protected AgileOS modules, a GPU memory manager that separates user allocations from protected module/MMIO ranges, selected trusted library adapters, and the PTX-level kernel memory guard.

AI.tldr: AgileOS is a GPU operating-system layer that virtualizes CUDA at the library boundary to provide OS-like protection for GPU services. It mediates application access to CUDA contexts through a trusted runtime worker, separates user memory from protected module/MMIO regions, and enforces access control via PTX-level memory guards, enabling safe coexistence of untrusted kernels with protected services.
AI.motivation: Modern GPU applications increasingly depend on storage, networking, vendor libraries, and GPU-resident services, not just isolated compute kernels. The default CUDA model grants each application direct ownership over its context, pointers, handles, and kernel launches — exposing sensitive service metadata, device queues, MMIO regions, and library-internal state to untrusted application kernels. This necessitates OS-like protection mechanisms around GPU services.
AI.method: AgileOS virtualizes CUDA at the library boundary: applications link against client-side CUDA Runtime/Driver shims while a trusted runtime worker holds the real CUDA context and mediates all operations. Key mechanisms include: (1) virtualized CUDA object tables for handles and resources; (2) a GPU memory manager that separates user allocations from protected module/MMIO ranges; (3) pointer validation and memory access guards enforced via PTX-level kernel injection; (4) modular trusted library adapters for existing libraries like cuFFT and PyTorch.
AI.result: The initial prototype demonstrates a working implementation with client-side interceptors, worker-side CUDA handlers, virtualized object tables, protected AgileOS modules, the GPU memory manager with region separation, trusted library adapters, and PTX-level kernel memory guards. AgileOS supports a range of protected services and existing libraries, showing feasibility of the modular, flexible approach.
AI.conclusion: AgileOS provides an initial design and prototype for a GPU operating-system layer that retrofits OS-style protection onto CUDA without requiring hardware changes. By virtualizing at the library boundary and using software-enforced memory isolation, it enables protected GPU services to coexist safely with untrusted application kernels, addressing a growing need as GPU workloads become more diverse and interconnected.

title: Mind the Gap: Bridging Behavioral Silos with LLMs in Multi-Vertical Recommendations
summary: In multi-vertical e-commerce platforms like DoorDash, relatively newer product verticals such as grocery and retail present a significant opportunity for personalization innovation. A key challenge lies in solving the "cold start" problem for users. This paper introduces a novel framework for enhancing recommendation quality by transferring knowledge from data-rich verticals (e.g., restaurants at DoorDash) to data-sparse ones. We leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform generative inference, synthesizing sparse, high-dimensional features that encapsulate latent user affinities. Specifically, we employ a hierarchical Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline to derive multi-level taxonomic features from user restaurant order histories and search queries. These generated features, encoding both long-term cross-vertical preferences and short-term intent, are integrated into a production Multi-Task Learning (MTL) ranking model. We demonstrate through extensive offline and online evaluation that this approach significantly improves personalization and engagement in emerging business verticals, effectively bridging the behavioral data gap.

AI.tldr: DoorDash uses LLMs with a hierarchical RAG pipeline to transfer user preference knowledge from mature verticals (restaurants) to cold-start verticals (grocery/retail), generating sparse cross-vertical features that improve a production MTL ranking model.
AI.motivation: Newer product verticals (grocery, retail) on multi-vertical e-commerce platforms suffer from user cold-start problems due to sparse behavioral data, limiting personalization quality compared to mature verticals like restaurants.
AI.method: A hierarchical Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline uses LLMs to synthesize sparse, high-dimensional taxonomic features from users' restaurant order histories and search queries. These features capture both long-term cross-vertical preferences and short-term intent, and are fed into a production Multi-Task Learning (MTL) ranking model.
AI.result: Extensive offline and online evaluations demonstrate significant improvements in personalization and user engagement in emerging business verticals, effectively bridging the behavioral data gap between mature and new verticals.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven generative inference with hierarchical RAG is an effective approach for cross-vertical knowledge transfer, enabling strong personalization in data-sparse verticals by leveraging abundant data from established ones.

title: Architecturally Significant MLOps Guidelines for ML Model Integration and Deployment: a Gray Literature Review
summary: Context. Despite the growing adoption of Machine Learning Operations (MLOps), teams often approach MLOps projects in an ad hoc manner due to the lack of consolidated architectural guidance. The community would benefit from a reference that synthesizes knowledge to inform the architectural design of MLOps systems, especially regarding the integration and deployment of ML models. Objective. In response, our goal is to provide a comprehensive overview of architecturally significant guidelines for the integration and deployment of ML models in MLOps systems. Method. We conduct a gray literature review of 103 web sources to analyze state-of-practice knowledge on MLOps model integration and deployment. We then apply thematic analysis to synthesize these practices into recommended guidelines. Results. We contribute a collection of 25 architecturally significant MLOps guidelines for model integration and deployment, organized into five categories, and describe their impact on the overall system architecture. Conclusion. Our results serve as an overview of state-of-practice MLOps guidelines to support researchers and practitioners with the integration and deployment of ML models in their MLOps systems.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically reviews 103 gray literature sources to synthesize 25 architecturally significant guidelines for integrating and deploying ML models in MLOps systems, organized into five categories, addressing the lack of consolidated architectural guidance in the field.
AI.motivation: MLOps adoption is growing, but teams lack consolidated architectural guidance, leading to ad hoc project approaches. There is a need for a synthesized reference that informs the architectural design of MLOps systems, particularly for model integration and deployment.
AI.method: A gray literature review of 103 web sources was conducted to capture state-of-practice knowledge on MLOps model integration and deployment, followed by thematic analysis to synthesize practices into recommended guidelines.
AI.result: The study produced a collection of 25 architecturally significant MLOps guidelines for model integration and deployment, categorized into five groups, with descriptions of their impact on overall system architecture.
AI.conclusion: The synthesized guidelines provide researchers and practitioners with a comprehensive, state-of-practice reference to support the architectural design of MLOps systems, specifically for integrating and deploying ML models.

title: LinkNav: Surfacing Interconnected Information in Scientific Articles
summary: We present LinkNav, an enhanced experience for reading academic papers which makes explicit connections between related but non-adjacent passages. To create the experience, we instruct a language model to generate questions that may arise while reading a passage and then search for answer passages elsewhere in the document, forming intra-document connections when answers are found. We confirm that these building blocks work well to power the experience, with an answer detection pipeline that works with high precision, resulting in a reasonable number of connections being made for a document. On a dataset of academic papers, we find that connected passages are on average ten segments away from each other, making explicit connections that a reader may have otherwise missed.

AI.tldr: LinkNav enhances academic paper reading by automatically generating questions from passages and linking them to answer passages elsewhere in the document, surfacing non-adjacent connections readers might otherwise miss.
AI.motivation: Academic papers often contain related but non-adjacent passages whose connections are implicit and easily overlooked by readers. Making these cross-document relationships explicit can improve comprehension and navigation.
AI.method: The system uses a language model to generate questions from a given passage, then searches for passages elsewhere in the document that answer those questions. An answer detection pipeline (validated for high precision) identifies when answers are found, forming intra-document links between the source and answer passages.
AI.result: The answer detection pipeline operates with high precision. On an academic paper dataset, connected passages are found to be on average ten segments apart, confirming that the system surfaces non-obvious, long-distance connections within documents.
AI.conclusion: The question-generation and answer-detection building blocks successfully power an enhanced reading experience, producing a reasonable number of meaningful intra-document connections that make implicit cross-passage relationships explicit.

title: MADRAG: Multi-Agent Debate with Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Training-Free Analytic Essay Scoring
summary: We present MADRAG, a training-free framework for analytic essay scoring that combines multi-agent reasoning with retrieval-augmented grounding. Unlike standard LLM-as-judge approaches, which are prone to bias and unstable scoring, MADRAG decomposes evaluation into an interactive process: an Advocate identifies strengths, a Skeptic critiques weaknesses, and a Judge aggregates their arguments into a final score. Crucially, the Judge is augmented with rubric-aligned exemplar retrieval, enabling calibration through comparison with scored examples. Our results show that MADRAG significantly outperforms prompt-based baselines while approaching the performance of supervised systems without requiring task-specific training. Ablation studies demonstrate that retrieval drives calibration gains, while debate improves reasoning on higher-level traits. Our findings highlight the complementary roles of structured interaction and external memory in reliable LLM-based evaluation.

AI.tldr: MADRAG is a training-free framework for essay scoring that uses multi-agent debate (Advocate, Skeptic, Judge) combined with retrieval-augmented exemplars to achieve reliable, calibrated evaluation approaching supervised system performance without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM-as-judge approaches for essay scoring suffer from bias and instability. The authors aim to address these limitations by introducing structured multi-agent deliberation and rubric-aligned exemplar retrieval to improve both calibration and reasoning quality.
AI.method: MADRAG employs three agents: an Advocate that identifies essay strengths, a Skeptic that critiques weaknesses, and a Judge that synthesizes arguments into a final score. The Judge is augmented with retrieval of rubric-aligned scored exemplars for calibration. The framework is entirely training-free, requiring no task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.result: MADRAG significantly outperforms prompt-based baselines and approaches the performance of supervised systems. Ablation studies reveal that retrieval drives calibration gains, while the debate mechanism improves reasoning on higher-level scoring traits.
AI.conclusion: Structured multi-agent interaction and external memory (retrieval) play complementary roles in enabling reliable LLM-based evaluation, with each component contributing distinct benefits to scoring accuracy and robustness.

title: Improving Cross-Lingual Factual Recall via Consistency-Driven Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained predominantly on English data encode substantial world knowledge, yet often fail to express it reliably in other languages, a phenomenon known as cross-lingual factual inconsistency. To study and address this, we introduce PolyFact, a large-scale parallel multilingual factual QA dataset containing 100K Wikidata-grounded facts across 12 typologically diverse languages. Using PolyFact, we compare light continual pretraining (CPT), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for improving cross-lingual factual recall in Qwen-2.5-7B and OLMo-2-1124-7B. We find that GRPO consistently outperforms SFT, improving both cross-lingual consistency and generalization to unseen languages, while CPT on parallel data yields limited additional gains. Mechanistic analyses further show that GRPO reorganizes multilingual routing by reducing language specialization in MLP layers and attention heads, thereby promoting more shared cross-lingual representations. We release our code, models, and dataset.

AI.tldr: PolyFact is a 100K multilingual factual QA dataset across 12 languages. GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) outperforms SFT and CPT for improving cross-lingual factual consistency in LLMs by reorganizing multilingual routing toward more shared representations.
AI.motivation: LLMs trained predominantly on English acquire substantial world knowledge but often fail to express it reliably in other languages — a phenomenon termed cross-lingual factual inconsistency. There is a need for both a benchmark to study this problem and effective methods to mitigate it.
AI.method: The authors introduce PolyFact, a parallel multilingual factual QA dataset with 100K Wikidata-grounded facts spanning 12 typologically diverse languages. Using this dataset, they compare three training strategies on Qwen-2.5-7B and OLMo-2-1124-7B: (1) light continual pretraining (CPT) on parallel data, (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and (3) reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). They also conduct mechanistic analyses probing how these methods affect multilingual routing in MLP layers and attention heads.
AI.result: GRPO consistently outperforms SFT on both cross-lingual consistency and generalization to unseen languages. CPT on parallel data yields only limited additional gains. Mechanistic analyses reveal that GRPO reduces language specialization in MLP layers and attention heads, promoting more shared cross-lingual representations.
AI.conclusion: GRPO is an effective method for mitigating cross-lingual factual inconsistency by fostering shared cross-lingual representations. The PolyFact dataset and trained models are publicly released to facilitate further research.

title: Elmes*: Automated Construction of Fine-Grained Evaluation Rubrics for Large Language Models in Long-Tail Educational Scenarios
summary: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) for education requires measuring how models teach, not only what they know. Existing benchmarks emphasize domain-general correctness or depend on manually designed rubrics that scale poorly to long-tail pedagogical scenarios. We introduce Elmes*, an end-to-end framework for constructing, refining, and applying fine-grained scenario-specific rubrics. Elmes* combines a declarative multi-agent engine for teacher--student--judge interactions with SceneGen, a self-evolving module that co-optimizes evaluation criteria and test data from expert-defined pedagogical dimensions. Using Elmes*, we build Edu-330, covering 330 scenarios across 11 subjects, 3 grade bands, and 10 task types, with over 1{,}000 second-level indicators. Experiments on Edu-330 and four expert-authored gold-standard scenarios show that educational capability is multidimensional: top-tier LLMs differ mainly in creativity and values integration, knowledge-strong models may fail at Socratic scaffolding, and the education-specialized InnoSpark achieves the best human-evaluated average score. LLM judges preserve human-comparable rankings with much lower scoring variance, but exhibit judge-specific biases such as self-preference. Ablations show that expert-scored few-shot anchoring improves human--LLM alignment, while reasoning enforcement and greedy decoding are model-dependent. Elmes* thus provides scalable diagnostic infrastructure for pedagogically grounded LLM evaluation.

AI.tldr: Elmes* is a framework for building fine-grained, scenario-specific rubrics to evaluate how well LLMs teach, not just what they know. It introduces Edu-330 (330 educational scenarios with 1,000+ indicators) and shows that educational capability is multidimensional, with rankings preserved by LLM judges but subject to biases like self-preference.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks for education focus on domain-general correctness or use manually designed rubrics that don't scale to diverse, long-tail pedagogical scenarios. There is a need for scalable, fine-grained evaluation infrastructure that captures how models teach across multiple pedagogical dimensions.
AI.method: Elmes* combines (1) a declarative multi-agent engine for teacher–student–judge interactions and (2) SceneGen, a self-evolving module that co-optimizes evaluation criteria and test data from expert-defined pedagogical dimensions. Using this framework, the authors construct Edu-330, spanning 330 scenarios across 11 subjects, 3 grade bands, and 10 task types with over 1,000 second-level indicators. They evaluate LLMs on Edu-330 and four expert-authored gold-standard scenarios, with ablations on few-shot anchoring, reasoning enforcement, and decoding strategies.
AI.result: Educational capability is multidimensional: top-tier LLMs differ mainly in creativity and values integration; knowledge-strong models can fail at Socratic scaffolding; the education-specialized InnoSpark achieves the best human-evaluated average score. LLM judges preserve human-comparable rankings with lower scoring variance but exhibit judge-specific biases such as self-preference. Expert-scored few-shot anchoring improves human–LLM alignment, while reasoning enforcement and greedy decoding effects are model-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Elmes* provides scalable diagnostic infrastructure for pedagogically grounded LLM evaluation, enabling fine-grained assessment of teaching capability beyond mere knowledge correctness. The framework addresses the scaling limitations of manual rubric design and reveals meaningful differences among LLMs along pedagogical dimensions.

title: Detecting and Mitigating Bias by Treating Fairness as a Symmetry Operation
summary: Machine learning systems deployed in high stakes socioeconomic settings routinely display bias. We formalize bias as a symmetry breaking operation: a classifier is fair if its outputs remain invariant under the counterfactual operation of switching a sensitive attribute, with merit features held fixed. We implement loss based regularization as a symmetry restoring mechanism and evaluate the framework on four synthetic datasets with varying levels of noise, correlation, and bias. The framework achieves upwards of 90\% violation reduction, with accuracy costs around 5\%. This framework does not require causal graph knowledge, is computationally lightweight, and generalizes to any sensitive attribute definable as a bit-flip, making it suitable for contexts where local sources of discrimination remain absent from mainstream benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes fairness as counterfactual invariance under sensitive attribute flipping, proposes loss-based regularization as a symmetry-restoring mechanism, and demonstrates 90%+ bias violation reduction with ~5% accuracy cost across synthetic datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing machine learning systems exhibit bias in high-stakes socioeconomic applications. The authors aim to address discrimination without requiring causal graph knowledge, targeting contexts where local discrimination sources are absent from mainstream fairness benchmarks.
AI.method: Bias is formalized as symmetry breaking: a classifier is fair if its outputs are invariant under counterfactual flipping of a sensitive attribute while holding merit features fixed. A loss-based regularization term is introduced as a symmetry-restoring mechanism. The framework treats sensitive attributes as bit-flips, avoiding causal graph dependencies.
AI.result: Evaluated on four synthetic datasets with varying noise, correlation, and bias levels, the framework achieves over 90% violation reduction with approximately 5% accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is computationally lightweight, generalizes to any sensitive attribute expressible as a bit-flip, and is suitable for deployment in contexts lacking mainstream benchmark coverage for local discrimination sources.

title: The Custody Envelope Threshold: Authority-Scaled Admission of External Artifacts in Institutional Infrastructure
comment: 32 pages. Preregistered framework and protocol paper; empirical pilot is a separate planned study. OSF preregistration and replication package: https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/E57FJ
summary: Modern infrastructure depends on externally maintained artifacts such as package-registry dependencies, CI/CD actions, container images, Terraform providers and modules, developer extensions, model artifacts, and AI tool servers. These artifacts are easy to fetch but difficult for institutions to admit, govern, and revoke. This paper proposes the Custody Envelope Threshold, an authority-scaled model of artifact admission. It argues that direct institutional admission is defensible only when object identity, ingress path, and revocation capacity are sufficiently closed relative to the execution authority delegated to the artifact. When this threshold is not met, institutions tend to proxy, policy-mediate, vendor-mediate, internalize, quarantine, or reject the artifact. The framework is operationalized as a four-condition ordinal instrument and connected to reference-monitor reasoning, least privilege, and transaction cost economics. It is applied to package dependencies, GitHub Actions, container images, Terraform providers and modules, developer extensions, and open model artifacts, with Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers treated as held-out evidence. The paper also specifies a validation design, deterministic prediction function, and OSF replication package for testing whether high-scrutiny institutions converge toward stronger custody closure for high-authority artifacts.

AI.tldr: Proposes the Custody Envelope Threshold — a model for artifact admission based on whether object identity, ingress path, and revocation capacity are sufficiently closed relative to delegated execution authority. Artifacts not meeting the threshold are proxied, policy-mediated, vendor-mediated, internalized, quarantined, or rejected. The framework is operationalized as a four-condition ordinal instrument and applied across multiple artifact types, with MCP servers as held-out evidence.
AI.motivation: Modern infrastructure relies on externally maintained artifacts (dependencies, CI/CD actions, container images, Terraform components, model artifacts, etc.) that are easy to fetch but difficult for institutions to admit, govern, and revoke — creating a governance gap between ease of acquisition and institutional control.
AI.method: Proposes the Custody Envelope Threshold framework — an authority-scaled model where direct institutional admission is defensible only when identity, ingress path, and revocation capacity are sufficiently closed relative to execution authority. Operationalizes this as a four-condition ordinal instrument and connects it to reference-monitor reasoning, least privilege, and transaction cost economics. Validates through application to six artifact classes, with MCP servers serving as held-out evidence, and specifies a deterministic prediction function with an OSF replication package.
AI.result: When the custody envelope threshold is not met, institutions converge toward proxy, policy-mediate, vendor-mediate, internalize, quarantine, or reject strategies. The framework predicts that high-scrutiny institutions should converge toward stronger custody closure for high-authority artifacts, with MCP servers treated as a held-out test case.
AI.conclusion: The Custody Envelope Threshold provides a systematic framework for determining when direct artifact admission is institutionally defensible versus when alternative governance strategies are required, offering a predictive model that can be empirically validated across diverse artifact ecosystems.

title: Towards Retrieving Interaction Spaces for Agentic Search
summary: Retrieval for search agents is still inherited from non-agentic information retrieval: a retriever ranks the corpus and the agent reads a small set of returned documents. Recent direct corpus interaction (DCI) work shows that agents can instead interact with the raw corpus through shell tools such as grep and file reads. But unbounded interaction does not scale: every broad shell command is a scan over the whole corpus, and latency degrades sharply as the corpus grows. We argue that the role of retrieval for agentic search is not just to select documents that fit in the LLM context window, but to construct an interaction space: a bounded subset of the corpus the agent can explore with associated tools. Two design consequences follow. The space needs a boundary supplied by retrieval, and the objects within it should be processed for interaction. As a proof of concept, we propose RISE (Retrieving Interaction SpacE): we use BM25 to construct the interaction space; meanwhile, its documents are processed during indexing for shell-style navigation. On BrowseComp-Plus, RISE matches the pure-shell DCI baseline at 78% accuracy with gpt-5.4-mini at roughly one quarter of the per-query cost. At 1M documents, RISE-BM25 reaches 81% on gpt-5.4-mini, whereas DCI on gpt-5.4-nano degrades to 60% with 33 of 100 wall-clock failures.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RISE, which reframes retrieval for search agents as constructing a bounded interaction space rather than merely ranking documents. By using BM25 to define a subset of the corpus and preprocessing documents for shell-style navigation, RISE matches pure direct corpus interaction (DCI) accuracy at significantly lower cost and latency, and scales to 1M documents where DCI degrades sharply.
AI.motivation: Current retrieval for search agents inherits from non-agentic IR paradigms: a retriever ranks documents and the agent reads only top results. Recent DCI work allows agents to interact with the raw corpus via shell tools (grep, file reads), but unbounded interaction scales poorly — every broad command scans the entire corpus, causing latency to degrade as corpus size grows. The motivation is to bridge this gap: retrieval should not just select documents for an LLM context window, but construct a bounded, tool-accessible interaction space.
AI.method: The authors propose RISE (Retrieving Interaction SpacE). It uses BM25 to construct a bounded interaction space — a subset of the corpus the agent can explore with associated tools. Documents within this space are processed during indexing to enable shell-style navigation (e.g., keyword search within the subset). The approach enforces two design principles: (1) the space must have a boundary supplied by retrieval, and (2) objects within it should be preprocessed for interaction efficiency.
AI.result: On BrowseComp-Plus, RISE with gpt-5.4-mini matches the pure-shell DCI baseline at 78% accuracy while incurring roughly one-quarter of the per-query cost. At a 1M-document scale, RISE-BM25 achieves 81% accuracy on gpt-5.4-mini, whereas DCI on gpt-5.4-nano degrades to 60% accuracy with 33 out of 100 wall-clock failures.
AI.conclusion: The role of retrieval in agentic search should be redefined: rather than simply ranking documents for an LLM to read, retrieval should construct a bounded, preprocessed interaction space. RISE demonstrates that this paradigm preserves accuracy while dramatically reducing cost and latency, and crucially enables scaling to large corpora where unbounded DCI fails.

title: Queen-Bee Agents: A BeeSpec-Centered Architecture for Governed Enterprise MCP Orchestration
comment: Technical report. Prototype-level systems evidence; 59 enterprise-style tasks
summary: Enterprise agent systems increasingly need to connect large language models to private tools, internal knowledge, and Model Context Protocol (MCP) interfaces. In this setting, raw task capability is insufficient: organizations also require policy enforcement, tenant-scoped isolation, and execution that remains within explicit operational boundaries. We present Queen-Bee, a governed multi-agent architecture in which a Queen control plane retrieves capabilities, plans task-scoped execution, and compiles a structured BeeSpec that is executed by specialized Bee agents under constrained tool access. We implement a working prototype with tenant-scoped MCP connectors, audit-backed execution-time governance, retrieval-driven weak incubation, and multiple provisioning backends. We evaluate the system on 59 enterprise-style tasks spanning governance-sensitive requests, retrieval-driven provisioning, scoped local execution, and chemistry workflow integration. The retrieval-driven Queen-Bee variant achieves a task success rate of 0.964, zero governance failures, and substantially better scoped execution quality than both a static Queen-Bee baseline and a permissive single-agent baseline. We further show a multi-Bee chemistry workflow with explicit approval gating and a concrete top-3 shortlist grounded in real upstream evidence and screening artifacts. Additional comparisons with hybrid retrieval and LLM-guided provisioning show that richer provisioning backends are viable but do not outperform the lightweight structured retriever on the current small, highly structured capability registry. The results provide prototype-level systems evidence rather than a production deployment study, and suggest that enterprise agent platforms should be evaluated not only by capability, but also by governed provisioning, isolation behavior, scoped execution quality, and artifact-aware workflow coordination.

AI.tldr: Queen-Bee introduces a governed multi-agent architecture where a Queen control plane plans tasks and compiles BeeSpecs executed by constrained Bee agents, achieving 96.4% task success with zero governance failures on enterprise tasks through retrieval-driven provisioning and audit-backed execution-time governance.
AI.motivation: Enterprise LLM-agent systems require not just task capability but also policy enforcement, tenant-scoped isolation, and bounded operational execution. Existing approaches lack integrated governance mechanisms for tool access control and execution-time policy compliance in multi-agent settings.
AI.method: The Queen-Bee architecture separates concerns: a Queen control plane retrieves capabilities, plans task-scoped execution, and compiles structured BeeSpecs. Specialized Bee agents execute under constrained tool access with MCP connectors, audit-backed governance, retrieval-driven weak incubation, and multiple provisioning backends. Evaluation spans 59 enterprise tasks across governance-sensitive requests, retrieval-driven provisioning, scoped local execution, and chemistry workflows with explicit approval gating.
AI.result: The retrieval-driven Queen-Bee variant achieves 0.964 task success rate with zero governance failures, outperforming static Queen-Bee and permissive single-agent baselines on scoped execution quality. A multi-Bee chemistry workflow demonstrates approval gating with grounded top-3 shortlisting. Richer provisioning backends proved viable but did not beat the lightweight structured retriever on the current small capability registry.
AI.conclusion: Enterprise agent platforms should be evaluated on governed provisioning, isolation behavior, scoped execution quality, and artifact-aware workflow coordination — not just raw capability. Results are prototype-level evidence, not production deployment validation.

title: Adversarial Co-Thinking: Calibration and Triangulation Across Multiple GenAI Tools in HCI Writing
summary: This paper examines what happens when GenAI tools are fully embedded in the drafting of an academic paper rather than confined to late-stage polishing. To investigate how an intensive multi-tool GenAI workflow differs from conventional academic writing, I drafted this paper from the first sentence in parallel with three GenAI tools - Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini - comparing their outputs against my own intended contribution. Across this process, a recurring pattern took shape that I call adversarial co-thinking: using past peer reviews to calibrate the tools, then setting their outputs against one another to be tested rather than deferred to. I argue that surfacing genuine critique from tools that default to praise is a central practical challenge of working with these tools, and that the skill at stake is evaluative rather than generative. Adversarial co-thinking is a high-skill epistemic practice: it can amplify expertise where it exists, but it can also mask its absence. I further argue that current disclosure frameworks are poorly equipped to capture this shift. The paper offers four propositions for workshop discussion concerning autonomy, supervision, equity of access, and disclosure.

AI.tldr: The author investigates how embedding multiple GenAI tools (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini) throughout the entire writing process—termed "adversarial co-thinking"—transforms academic drafting. The core finding is that the critical skill shifts from generation to evaluation: pitting tools against each other using past peer reviews to extract genuine critique. The paper argues this amplifies expertise but can mask its absence, and that current disclosure norms are inadequate, proposing four discussion points on autonomy, supervision, equity, and disclosure.
AI.motivation: To understand how GenAI tools change academic writing when used from inception rather than just for final polishing, and to identify the emergent epistemic practices and challenges that arise from intensive, multi-tool workflows.
AI.method: The author conducted a first-person, reflexive experiment: drafting an academic paper from the first sentence in parallel with Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini, comparing their outputs against the author's own intended contribution. Past peer reviews were used to calibrate the tools. The methodology is itself the object of study—a performative inquiry into the workflow it describes.
AI.result: A recurring pattern emerged, coined "adversarial co-thinking": using peer reviews to calibrate tools, then setting outputs against each other to be tested rather than deferred to. The key challenge identified is surfacing genuine critique from tools that default to praise. The skill at stake is evaluative (judging outputs) rather than generative (producing text).
AI.conclusion: Adversarial co-thinking is a high-skill epistemic practice that amplifies existing expertise but can also mask its absence. Current disclosure frameworks fail to capture this nuanced human-AI collaboration. The paper proposes four discussion points for workshops: autonomy, supervision, equity of access, and disclosure norms.

title: Modeling U.S. Attitudes Toward China via an Event-Steered Multi-Agent Simulator
summary: Understanding the dynamic evolution of opinions, such as U.S. public attitudes toward China, is essential for assessing geopolitical risks. However, existing LLM-based multiagent simulators predominantly rely on static rules and fixed datasets, limiting their ability to capture the dynamic, event-driven nature of macro-level opinion shifts in real-world settings. To address this limitation, we propose an Event-Steered Multi-Agent Simulator (ES-MAS), in which significant events and daily news continuously drive opinion evolution through dynamic interactions among agents. We first construct the China-U.S. Relation Evolution (CURE) dataset, covering 20 quarters from 2021 to 2025, including 258 major events and over 14,000 daily news articles, and providing a comprehensive temporal foundation for modeling opinion dynamics. Building upon the CURE dataset, we propose a Dual-Stream Data Integration Engine (DSDIE) that aligns simulations with historical timelines via macro-level events while enabling personalized information exposure based on individual agent profiles and contextual signals. Furthermore, we design a News-Driven Dynamic Interaction (NDDI) module, which adaptively groups agents with shared news interests into localized interaction contexts, facilitating bottom-up consensus formation while mitigating the risk of isolated information cocoons. Experimental results on the CURE dataset demonstrate that ES-MAS substantially outperforms existing simulators in reproducing real-world historical trends, offering a scalable and effective framework for modeling dynamic opinion evolution.

AI.tldr: ES-MAS is an event-driven multi-agent simulator that models dynamic opinion evolution (e.g., U.S. attitudes toward China) by integrating a large-scale temporal dataset (CURE) with a dual-stream data engine and a news-driven interaction module, outperforming static rule-based simulators in reproducing real-world historical trends.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent simulators rely on static rules and fixed datasets, failing to capture the dynamic, event-driven nature of macro-level opinion shifts in real-world settings. There is a need for a simulator that continuously integrates significant events and daily news to drive realistic opinion evolution.
AI.method: The authors propose ES-MAS with three core components: (1) the CURE dataset spanning 2021–2025 with 258 major events and 14,000+ daily news articles across 20 quarters; (2) a Dual-Stream Data Integration Engine (DSDIE) that aligns simulations with historical timelines via macro-level events and enables personalized information exposure based on agent profiles and contextual signals; (3) a News-Driven Dynamic Interaction (NDDI) module that adaptively groups agents with shared news interests into localized interaction contexts to foster bottom-up consensus while preventing information cocoons.
AI.result: Experimental results on the CURE dataset demonstrate that ES-MAS substantially outperforms existing simulators in reproducing real-world historical opinion trends.
AI.conclusion: ES-MAS provides a scalable and effective framework for modeling dynamic opinion evolution in geopolitically sensitive contexts, bridging the gap between static simulation approaches and the event-driven nature of real-world opinion dynamics.

title: Re-Centering Humans in LLM Personalization
summary: Despite growing interest, most evaluations of large language models' (LLMs') personalization abilities have relied on synthetic data. It remains unclear how well current personalization systems work for real users. In this paper, we study the gap in LLM personalization performance when using synthetic versus human data. We collect human conversations (550 conversations) and judgments across three stages of personalization: extracting user attributes from conversations (5,949 judgments), pairing relevant attributes with new prompts (11,919), and incorporating relevant attributes into a personalized response (1,101). Incorporating human data reveals system limitations at each stage. Models struggle to extract attributes from human conversations, disagree with human judgments on relevant attributes, and generate personalized responses that humans judge no better than generic responses (though that LLM judges widely rate as better). We introduce two lightweight training-based interventions that shift automated personalization evaluation closer to human data in our first two stages. However, in our third stage we find that learned reward models achieve only modest correlation with human ratings, suggesting that human-aligned personalization quality judgments are difficult to model directly. Our collected data provides a foundation for studying how models should extract, select, and incorporate user information in ways that humans find useful.

AI.tldr: Human vs. synthetic data reveals a significant performance gap in LLM personalization across all three stages (extraction, selection, generation). Lightweight interventions help align automated evaluation with human judgments for extraction and selection, but modeling human-aligned quality judgments for personalized generation remains challenging, with learned reward models achieving only modest correlation with human ratings.
AI.motivation: Most LLM personalization evaluations rely on synthetic data, leaving unclear how well these systems perform for real users. The authors aim to quantify the gap between synthetic and human-data-driven personalization and identify where current systems fall short when deployed with real user data.
AI.method: The authors collect a dataset of 550 human conversations with annotations across three stages: (1) attribute extraction — 5,949 judgments on extracting user attributes from conversations; (2) attribute relevance pairing — 11,919 judgments on matching relevant attributes to new prompts; (3) personalized response generation — 1,101 judgments on quality of personalized responses. They benchmark LLM performance at each stage using both synthetic and human data, then introduce two lightweight training-based interventions to bridge the gap in automated evaluation, and test learned reward models for personalization quality.
AI.result: Across all three stages, models underperform on human data compared to synthetic data: they struggle with attribute extraction from real conversations, disagree with humans on attribute relevance, and generate personalized responses rated no better than generic ones by humans (though LLM judges rate them as superior). Lightweight interventions improve alignment in stages one and two, but learned reward models achieve only modest correlation with human ratings in stage three.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM personalization systems exhibit a substantial human-synthetic performance gap. While training-based fixes help for extraction and selection stages, directly modeling human-aligned personalization quality judgments remains an open challenge. The collected dataset serves as a benchmark for future work on human-centric personalization.

title: FAIR-Calib: Frontier-Aware Instability-Reweighted Calibration for Post-Training Quantization of Diffusion Large Language Models
comment: Accepted as a poster at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) refine tokens iteratively but commit them irreversibly, leading to a "stability lag" where early decisions remain fragile even after being written. We reveal that Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) error easily flips these borderline decisions at the write frontier, which are then permanently locked in and amplified. To address this, we propose Frontier-Aware Instability-Reweighted Calibration (FAIR-Calib), a two-stage PTQ framework for dLLMs. Stage I probes a full-precision teacher to estimate a position prior that combines frontier hits and masked-stage reliability. Stage II performs off-policy, layer-wise calibration by minimizing a reweighted hidden-state MSE, effectively prioritizing the protection of fragile frontier states without requiring expensive end-to-end diffusion rollouts. We further theoretically justify our weighted objective as a surrogate for output KL divergence. Empirically, FAIR-Calib consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on LLaDA and Dream (W4A4), significantly reducing frontier decision flips and suppressing post-commit mismatches across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Diffusion LLMs suffer from "stability lag" where irreversibly committed early tokens are vulnerable to PTQ-induced flips. FAIR-Calib addresses this via a two-stage calibration that protects fragile frontier states using a reweighted hidden-state MSE objective, theoretically justified as a surrogate for output KL divergence, and outperforms baselines on LLaDA and Dream.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) generate text through iterative refinement and irrevocably commit tokens, but early-decided tokens at the "write frontier" remain fragile. Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) introduces errors that easily flip these borderline decisions, and because commitments are permanent, the errors are locked in and amplified downstream. Existing PTQ methods fail to account for this unique vulnerability.
AI.method: FAIR-Calib is a two-stage PTQ framework. Stage I probes a full-precision teacher model to estimate a position prior that combines two signals: (1) frontier hits — identifying which token positions are at the decision boundary, and (2) masked-stage reliability — capturing how stable each position is during the iterative unmasking process. Stage II performs off-policy, layer-wise calibration by minimizing a reweighted hidden-state MSE loss, where weights are derived from the Stage I prior. This prioritizes protecting fragile frontier states without requiring costly end-to-end diffusion rollouts. The weighted MSE objective is theoretically justified as a surrogate for minimizing output KL divergence between the quantized and full-precision models.
AI.result: FAIR-Calib consistently outperforms state-of-the-art PTQ baselines on two dLLM architectures (LLaDA and Dream) under W4A4 quantization. It significantly reduces frontier decision flips and suppresses post-commit mismatches across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The stability lag in dLLMs — where fragile early decisions are irreversibly committed and later amplified — represents a critical vulnerability under quantization. FAIR-Calib's frontier-aware, reweighted calibration effectively mitigates this issue, providing a practical and theoretically grounded PTQ solution tailored to the iterative, commit-based generation paradigm of diffusion language models.

title: DiBS: Diffusion-Informed Branch Selection
summary: Sudoku is a representative constraint satisfaction problem that requires global structural reasoning under strict discrete constraints. The existing works of solving Sudoku mainly focus on two dominant approaches, i.e., traditional heuristic and deep learning solver. However, they suffer from two complementary limitations: learning-based solvers lack hard correctness guarantees, while complete symbolic solvers are still prone to long-tail search. To address these shortcomings, we propose a novel diffusion model-guided approach, termed as DiBS, for the branch selection search process. Specifically, DiBS keeps the symbolic solver complete and uses the diffusion model as a branch-ordering guide. The core method is ranking candidate values under the current partial assignment and lightweight consistency signal. Furthermore, we provide an in-depth theoretical proof to reveal how it works and why it works. Experiments on the challenging Royle 17-clue Sudoku benchmark show that our DiBS substantially reduces search cost relative to strong heuristic baselines, especially in nodes, backtracks, and long-tail percentiles. Besides, these results confirm that learned global guidance is effective on hard instances where branch-order mistakes are most expensive. All codes are available at https://github.com/shanxierdan/DiBS.

AI.tldr: DiBS combines a complete symbolic Sudoku solver with a diffusion model that guides branch ordering. The diffusion model ranks candidate values under partial assignments and lightweight consistency signals. On the hard Royle 17-clue benchmark, DiBS significantly reduces search cost (nodes, backtracks, long-tail percentiles) compared to strong heuristics, demonstrating that learned global guidance is most effective on hard instances where branching mistakes are costly.
AI.motivation: Existing Sudoku solvers suffer from two complementary limitations: (1) deep learning-based solvers lack hard correctness guarantees, and (2) complete symbolic solvers are prone to long-tail search (rare but extremely costly instances). The authors aim to retain the completeness guarantee of symbolic solvers while using learned global guidance to improve search efficiency, particularly on hard problem instances where poor branch ordering causes exponential blowup.
AI.method: DiBS is a diffusion model-guided branch selection search process. The symbolic solver remains complete and is augmented by a diffusion model that serves as a branch-ordering guide. The core mechanism involves ranking candidate values under the current partial assignment, leveraging lightweight consistency signals. The authors also provide an in-depth theoretical proof explaining how and why the diffusion guidance works.
AI.result: On the challenging Royle 17-clue Sudoku benchmark, DiBS substantially reduces search cost compared to strong heuristic baselines. Improvements are observed in key metrics: number of search nodes, number of backtracks, and long-tail percentiles (worst-case performance). The results confirm that learned global guidance is particularly effective on hard instances where branch-ordering mistakes are most expensive.
AI.conclusion: DiBS successfully bridges the gap between symbolic completeness and learned efficiency. By using a diffusion model solely for branch ordering rather than direct solution prediction, the approach maintains correctness guarantees while dramatically improving solver performance on the hardest Sudoku instances. Code is publicly available.

title: What Your Posts Reveal: A Benchmark and Agentic Framework for User-Level Privacy Leakage on Social Media
summary:
Public social media posts can reveal private information through weak cues scattered across text, images, or metadata. Such leakage is often cumulative and cross-post: cues that appear harmless in isolation may jointly expose a user's home, workplace, or routine. However, current research lacks a unified benchmark for user-level multimodal privacy leakage and an evaluation metric that captures exposure severity beyond binary accuracy.
  To address these gaps, we propose SopriBench, a synthetic benchmark guided by leakage patterns abstracted from a private reference corpus of Rednote and Instagram accounts, covering 50 user profiles and 1,569 images with attributes, contextual sensitivity, granularity, leakage type, inference difficulty, and supporting evidence. We further introduce the Privacy Exposure Score (PES), which weights value granularity by contextual sensitivity. Inspired by abductive reasoning, we introduce Argus, a training-free agentic framework for cumulative leakage inference. Argus forms hypotheses from accumulated evidence, verifies supporting evidence, and aggregates cross-post cues into privacy profiles, achieving 0.55 PES, a 25% improvement over the strongest baseline, with the largest gain on cross-post leakage.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SopriBench, a synthetic benchmark for multimodal privacy leakage evaluation, a new metric called Privacy Exposure Score (PES), and Argus, a training-free agentic framework that uses abductive reasoning to infer cumulative privacy leakage across social media posts, achieving a 25% improvement over baselines.
AI.motivation: Current research lacks a unified benchmark for user-level multimodal privacy leakage and an evaluation metric that captures exposure severity beyond binary accuracy. Existing approaches fail to capture the cumulative, cross-post nature of privacy leakage where individually innocuous cues combine to reveal sensitive information.
AI.method: The authors introduce (1) SopriBench: a synthetic benchmark with 50 user profiles and 1,569 images, annotated with attributes, contextual sensitivity, granularity, leakage type, inference difficulty, and supporting evidence, derived from a private reference corpus of Rednote and Instagram accounts; (2) Privacy Exposure Score (PES): a metric weighting value granularity by contextual sensitivity; and (3) Argus: a training-free agentic framework inspired by abductive reasoning that forms hypotheses from accumulated evidence, verifies supporting evidence, and aggregates cross-post cues into privacy profiles.
AI.result: Argus achieves 0.55 PES, a 25% improvement over the strongest baseline, with the largest performance gain observed on cross-post leakage scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed benchmark, metric, and agentic framework collectively advance the evaluation and inference of cumulative, cross-post multimodal privacy leakage, demonstrating that abductive reasoning over scattered cues significantly improves privacy exposure detection.

title: DREAM: Dynamic Refinement of Early Assignment Mappings
summary: Generative recommendation advances item retrieval by reformulating it as autoregressive generation of Semantic IDs (SIDs), compact token sequences that encode item semantics. While SIDs offer a strong semantic prior, current SID-based methods assign each item a single static identifier through offline tokenization before sufficient user feedback is observed. For cold-start items, this one-shot commitment produces poorly discriminative codes, generating misaligned paths that remain unrefined because the associated tokens are rarely sampled during training. We identify this early static commitment, not model capacity, as the fundamental cold-start bottleneck in SID-based generative recommendation. To overcome this bottleneck and bridge the disjoint objectives of tokenization and generation, we propose DREAM (Dynamic Refinement of Early Assignment Mappings), a three-stage framework that resolves this flaw through progressive refinement. First, an intent-aware tokenizer rebuilds the SID space through counterfactual contrastive learning, generating a diverse pool of behavior-aligned candidates per cold-start item. Second, the frozen recommendation backbone serves as an evaluator, selecting the most reliable candidate based on multi-context user support without retraining. Third, a dynamic beam mechanism maintains multiple weighted SID hypotheses throughout training and inference, preventing premature collapse to a single assignment. Extensive experiments on three Amazon benchmarks show that DREAM substantially outperforms state-of-the-art generative and sequential baselines on cold-start metrics.

AI.tldr: DREAM addresses the cold-start bottleneck in SID-based generative recommendation by replacing static one-shot Semantic ID assignment with a three-stage progressive refinement framework: intent-aware tokenization with counterfactual contrastive learning, frozen backbone evaluation for candidate selection, and dynamic beam search for multi-hypothesis maintenance during training and inference.
AI.motivation: In SID-based generative recommendation, items receive static Semantic IDs via offline tokenization before sufficient user feedback is observed. For cold-start items, this early commitment yields poorly discriminative codes and misaligned token paths that are rarely sampled during training, making them unrefinable. The authors identify this early static commitment — not model capacity — as the fundamental cold-start bottleneck, and that tokenization and generation objectives remain disjoint.
AI.method: DREAM is a three-stage framework: (1) Intent-aware tokenizer: rebuilds the SID space using counterfactual contrastive learning to generate a diverse pool of behavior-aligned candidate SIDs per cold-start item. (2) Frozen backbone evaluation: the pre-trained recommendation model acts as an evaluator, selecting the most reliable candidate based on multi-context user support without retraining. (3) Dynamic beam mechanism: maintains multiple weighted SID hypotheses throughout training and inference, preventing premature collapse to a single assignment.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three Amazon benchmarks demonstrate that DREAM substantially outperforms state-of-the-art generative and sequential baselines on cold-start metrics.
AI.conclusion: Early static SID commitment is the core cold-start bottleneck in generative recommendation. DREAM's progressive refinement — combining intent-aware tokenization, frozen evaluator selection, and dynamic beam search — effectively resolves this flaw by bridging the tokenization-generation gap, yielding significant gains on cold-start performance.

title: AI-Driven Test Case Generation from Natural Language Requirements: A Survey of Techniques and Research Gaps
summary: Software testing is critical for verifying that systems meet specified requirements, yet remains among the most time-consuming and expensive activities in development. Requirements-based test generation allows test cases to be derived early from requirements artifacts, but generating them directly from natural language is challenging due to inherent ambiguity and imprecision. Recent advances in AI, natural language processing (NLP), and large language models (LLMs) have made automating this pipeline increasingly feasible, while introducing new risks including hallucination, reduced traceability, and inconsistent evaluation. This survey addresses four research questions: what AI and NLP techniques have been proposed for generating test cases from natural language requirements; what tools and frameworks support these approaches; how generated test cases are evaluated; and what research gaps remain. Following Kitchenham and Charters' systematic review guidelines, we searched major scholarly databases spanning 2000-2025 and, after applying strict inclusion criteria, identified 21 primary studies. The literature is organized into three evolutionary eras, revealing that no existing approach simultaneously satisfies six key quality dimensions: automation, ambiguity handling, domain applicability, traceability, evaluation thoroughness, and hallucination control. The survey makes three main contributions: a three-era evolutionary synthesis of AI-based test generation; a six-criteria gap analysis showing no current approach fully addresses all quality dimensions; and four actionable research guidelines targeting hallucination, traceability, complexity sensitivity, and compliance.

AI.tldr: A systematic survey of 21 studies (2000–2025) on AI/NLP/LLM-based test case generation from natural language requirements. The literature is organized into three evolutionary eras, revealing that no existing approach simultaneously satisfies six key quality dimensions: automation, ambiguity handling, domain applicability, traceability, evaluation thoroughness, and hallucination control. The paper contributes an era-based synthesis, a gap analysis, and four actionable research guidelines.
AI.motivation: Requirements-based test generation is valuable for early verification but is hindered by the ambiguity and imprecision of natural language requirements. While AI, NLP, and LLMs have made automation increasingly feasible, they introduce risks of hallucination, reduced traceability, and inconsistent evaluation. The survey systematically investigates the state of the art to identify what has been achieved and what gaps remain.
AI.method: The authors conducted a systematic literature review following Kitchenham and Charters' guidelines. They searched major scholarly databases for studies published between 2000 and 2025, applied strict inclusion criteria, and selected 21 primary studies. These were organized into three evolutionary eras and assessed against six quality dimensions: automation, ambiguity handling, domain applicability, traceability, evaluation thoroughness, and hallucination control.
AI.result: No existing approach simultaneously satisfies all six quality dimensions. The literature reveals a progressive evolution across three eras of AI-based test generation, but persistent gaps remain in hallucination mitigation, traceability maintenance, handling of requirement complexity, and compliance with standards.
AI.conclusion: The survey contributes: (1) a three-era evolutionary synthesis of AI-driven test generation; (2) a six-criteria gap analysis showing no approach fully addresses all quality dimensions; and (3) four actionable research guidelines targeting hallucination control, traceability, complexity sensitivity, and compliance. These findings chart a roadmap for future research in the field.

title: Exploring Reinforcement Learning for Fluid Transitions Between Clinical Mental Healthcare and Everyday Wellness Support
summary: Mental health struggles wax and wane, yet clinical and wellness interventions typically operate separately, causing frequent breakdowns at care transitions. We explore reinforcement learning (RL) as a means to build digital health systems that deliver clinical and wellness interventions proactively, as part of a coherent care journey. We ask: what complexities does designing such a system involve? We built a contextual bandit that dynamically selects journaling prompts from clinical and wellness repertoires to optimize for an overarching health goal (sustained journaling) and deployed it in a four-week exploratory study (N=38). We found that, first, many benefits of RL-optimized intervention sequences appeared only after interventions ended, raising the question: Should systems that offer coherent clinical-wellness care journeys include stepping-back periods? If so, when and how? Second, participants most engaged with RL-generated interventions deepened their engagement over time, while those most engaged with a constant intervention tended to burn out and drop out later. It raises the question: When should a system blending clinical and wellness interventions reduce intensity to prevent burnout in versus sustain it to maximize treatment gains?

AI.tldr: A contextual bandit RL system was built to dynamically select clinical and wellness journaling prompts for sustained engagement. Deployed in a 4-week study (N=38), findings revealed that RL benefits emerged post-intervention and that engagement patterns differed: RL users deepened engagement over time while constant-intervention users burned out, raising key design questions about stepping-back periods and intensity modulation.
AI.motivation: Mental health care suffers from fragmentation between clinical interventions and wellness support, leading to breakdowns at care transitions. The authors aim to explore whether RL can unify these into a coherent, proactive digital health journey, and to surface the design complexities involved in building such systems.
AI.method: The authors built a contextual bandit that dynamically selects journaling prompts from both clinical (e.g., CBT-based) and wellness (e.g., gratitude) repertoires, optimizing for sustained journaling as a proxy health goal. The system was deployed in a four-week exploratory field study with 38 participants.
AI.result: Two key findings: (1) Benefits of RL-optimized sequences manifested primarily after interventions ended, suggesting a need for "stepping-back" periods. (2) Participants most engaged with RL-generated interventions showed deepening engagement over time, whereas those highly engaged with constant interventions experienced burnout and dropout.
AI.conclusion: Designing coherent clinical-wellness care journeys with RL raises critical open questions: whether and when to incorporate stepping-back periods, and how to balance intensity reduction (to prevent burnout) against sustained intensity (to maximize treatment gains) when blending clinical and wellness interventions.

title: Learning Multi-Agent Communication Protocol: Study on Information Entropy Efficiency in MARL
summary: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) have emerged as a fundamental paradigm for distributed problem-solving, where autonomous agents collaborate to achieve complex objectives. Within this framework, Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) with communication has demonstrated remarkable success in cooperative tasks. However, existing approaches predominantly pursue performance gains through increasingly complex architectures and expanding communication overhead, lacking principled metrics to evaluate the efficiency of information exchange. In this paper, we focus on enabling agents to learn efficient multi-agent communication protocols that balance performance and information compactness. We propose the Information Entropy Efficiency Index (IEI), a novel metric that quantifies the ratio between message entropy and task performance in learned communication protocols. A lower IEI indicates more compact and efficient message representations. By incorporating IEI into training loss functions, we encourage agents to develop communication protocols that achieve high performance with improved communication efficiency. Extensive experiments across diverse MARL algorithms demonstrate that our approach achieves equivalent or superior task performance compared to baseline methods while improving communication efficiency. These findings challenge the prevailing assumption that performance improvements require complex architectures or increased communication overhead and highlight the potential of improving both task success and communication efficiency to enable scalable MAS.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Information Entropy Efficiency Index (IEI), a metric that measures the ratio of message entropy to task performance in multi-agent communication. By incorporating IEI into training, agents learn compact yet effective communication protocols that match or surpass baseline performance while reducing communication overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing MARL communication methods focus on maximizing task performance through complex architectures and high communication overhead, without principled metrics to evaluate communication efficiency. There is a need for agents that balance performance with information compactness to enable scalable multi-agent systems.
AI.method: The authors propose the Information Entropy Efficiency Index (IEI), defined as the ratio between message entropy and task performance. A lower IEI signifies more efficient communication. The IEI is incorporated into training loss functions, incentivizing agents to learn communication protocols that achieve high performance while minimizing redundant or inefficient information exchange.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse MARL algorithms show that the IEI-guided approach achieves equivalent or superior task performance compared to baselines while improving communication efficiency, challenging the assumption that higher performance requires greater communication complexity.
AI.conclusion: Improving both task success and communication efficiency simultaneously is feasible and desirable for scalable MAS. The IEI provides a principled metric for this purpose, demonstrating that compact, efficient communication protocols can rival or exceed the performance of more complex, high-overhead alternatives.

title: UnpredictaBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating Distributional Randomness in LLMs
summary: We introduce UnpredictaBench, an evaluation that tests the ability of large language models (LLMs) to capture true underlying distributions.
