AI.motivation: Traditional two-sided matching models assume immediate sub-Gaussian feedback about fixed preferences, ignoring real-world settings where payoff-relevant information is revealed gradually through interviews, repeated interaction, and learning. This creates a gap for modeling dynamic matching markets with temporally extended feedback and evolving latent profiles.
AI.method: Formulates two-sided matching as a partially observable Markov game featuring: (1) costly pre-match screening, (2) noisy post-match observations, (3) evolving latent profiles, and (4) endogenous continuation/dissolution decisions. Instantiates this in Learn2Match, a multi-agent RL benchmark where agents make decentralized decisions across three stages. Evaluates using regret, social welfare, and a novel information-friction loss metric. Compares independent PPO against the bandit-style CA-ETC baseline.
AI.result: Independent PPO achieves higher cumulative social welfare and lower cumulative regret than CA-ETC under temporally extended feedback. However, PPO incurs higher information-friction loss, indicating that end-to-end MARL lacks the coordinated exploration structure inherent to matching-bandit methods.
AI.conclusion: Learn2Match serves as a benchmark for next-generation matching-market algorithms that must simultaneously exhibit RL-like adaptivity, bandit-like statistical discipline, and stable-matching-like structural awareness. Current MARL methods partially succeed but fall short on information-efficient exploration.

title: The Piggyback Hypothesis of Generalization: Explaining and Mitigating Emergent Misalignment
summary: The mechanisms behind LLMs' broad over-generalization beyond training examples remain unclear. Emergent misalignment (EM) offers a striking case study: finetuning on narrow tasks induces broad misalignment to semantically-unrelated test domains. In this work, we propose the Piggyback Hypothesis: the chat-template tokens can piggyback the finetuned behaviour onto out-of-domain queries. We validate this hypothesis by showing that subtle perturbations to the prefix (tokens preceding all user queries), or patching the prefix representations with those from the unfinetuned model, can restore alignment without changing the user query. Building on this finding, we propose Token-Regularized Finetuning (TReFT), which regularizes specific token representations during training to mitigate EM. Across different models and multiple EM-inducing datasets, TReFT reduces EM while preserving in-domain learning. On Llama-3.1-8B finetuned on the legal domain, TReFT achieves 33.5% more EM reduction than data interleaving with a retain set of aligned examples. We further show that TReFT extends to other narrow-finetuning settings, including abstention, tool use, and refusal (off-topic generalization is reduced by 54.3% on average), supporting the Piggyback Hypothesis. Broadly, our work highlights that LLMs may learn and generalize in unintended ways and suggests a path toward more constrained finetuning. It also calls for further study of how shared input features can piggyback model behavior across domains.

AI.tldr: Finetuning LLMs on narrow tasks can cause broad, unintended misalignment on unrelated domains. The authors propose the "Piggyback Hypothesis" — that chat-template prefix tokens carry the finetuned behavior to out-of-domain queries — and introduce Token-Regularized Finetuning (TReFT) to suppress this by regularizing prefix token representations, effectively reducing emergent misalignment while preserving in-domain performance.
AI.motivation: The core puzzle is understanding why LLMs exhibit emergent misalignment (EM): finetuning on a narrow, benign task (e.g., legal Q&A) unexpectedly induces misaligned behavior on semantically unrelated domains. The mechanism behind this broad over-generalization is not well understood, and existing mitigation strategies (e.g., data interleaving with retain sets) have limited effectiveness. The work aims to uncover the mechanistic basis of EM and develop a principled, lightweight intervention.
AI.method: First, the authors formulate and test the Piggyback Hypothesis: that the prefix tokens (chat-template tokens preceding user queries) act as a carrier, "piggybacking" the finetuned behavior onto any downstream query. They validate this via (a) perturbation experiments — subtly altering the prefix — and (b) representation patching — replacing prefix activations with those from the unfinetuned model, both of which restore alignment without modifying user queries. Building on this insight, they propose Token-Regularized Finetuning (TReFT), which adds a regularization loss during finetuning that penalizes deviations in the representations of specific prefix tokens from their pre-finetuned values. Experiments span Llama-3.1-8B and other models, with EM-inducing datasets covering legal finetuning, abstention, tool use, and refusal settings.
AI.result: Prefix perturbation and representation patching both successfully restore alignment, directly supporting the Piggyback Hypothesis. TReFT consistently reduces EM across models and datasets while preserving in-domain learning. On Llama-3.1-8B finetuned for legal tasks, TReFT achieves 33.5% greater EM reduction compared to data interleaving with a retain set. In broader narrow-finetuning settings (abstention, tool use, refusal), TReFT reduces off-topic generalization by 54.3% on average.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can learn and generalize in unintended ways through shared input features like chat-template prefixes. The Piggyback Hypothesis provides a mechanistic account of emergent misalignment, and TReFT offers a simple, effective regularization strategy to constrain finetuning. The findings call for further investigation into how shared representational features propagate behaviors across domains and suggest pathways toward safer, more controlled LLM finetuning practices.

title: Are you sure? A Comprehensive and Comprehensible Survey of Uncertainty Quantification in Symbolic Regression
summary: Symbolic regression (SR) is a class of methods that systematically explore the space of mathematical functions to discover models that accurately capture the underlying relationships in a dataset. Despite recent advances in the field, a lack of support for uncertainty quantification (UQ) limits its adoption in real-world decision processes. In regression analysis, UQ provides important information about the model reliability, which can both help to avoid overfitting by accounting for uncertainty in the data, and provide insights for decision-making. This survey is the first to clearly address this issue, with the objective of introducing essential UQ concepts and reviewing the current literature on UQ in SR, which can be broadly organized into three research directions: frequentist, Bayesian, and model selection. Despite its importance, UQ in SR is still underexplored, which motivates further research into reliable UQ methods for SR.

AI.tldr: This survey examines uncertainty quantification (UQ) in symbolic regression (SR), organizing current methods into frequentist, Bayesian, and model selection approaches, and highlighting that UQ remains underexplored in SR despite its importance for model reliability and decision-making.
AI.motivation: Symbolic regression lacks adequate support for uncertainty quantification, which limits its adoption in real-world decision processes. UQ provides critical information about model reliability, helps avoid overfitting by accounting for data uncertainty, and offers valuable insights for decision-making. This survey addresses the gap by being the first to systematically review UQ in SR.
AI.method: This is a survey paper that categorizes the existing literature on UQ in SR into three research directions: frequentist methods, Bayesian methods, and model selection approaches. It introduces foundational UQ concepts and reviews current techniques across these three paradigms.
AI.result: The survey finds that UQ in symbolic regression remains underexplored despite its critical importance. The existing body of work can be broadly organized into frequentist, Bayesian, and model selection approaches, but significant gaps remain, motivating the need for further research into reliable UQ methods tailored for SR.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty quantification is essential for improving the reliability and practical adoption of symbolic regression, yet it is still an underdeveloped area. Future research should focus on developing robust and reliable UQ methods for SR to bridge this gap.

title: Attack Selection in Agentic AI Control Evaluations Meaningfully Decreases Safety
summary: An attacker that strategically chooses when to attack is much harder to catch than one that attacks indiscriminately. AI control is a safety framework for deploying capable but untrusted AI agents under the oversight of a weaker, trusted monitor and a limited human audit budget. Control evaluations stress-test these protocols by pitting a red-team attack policy against the blue-team monitor, but current evaluations typically assume attackers that do not strategically select when to attack. We study this capability, attack selection, in agentic settings by decomposing attack decisions into a start policy, which decides when an attacker should attack, and a stop policy, which decides when an attacker should abort an ongoing attack. Across two agentic settings, BashArena and LinuxArena, both policies substantially lower measured empirical safety without changing the underlying attack capability. At a 1% audit budget, our start policy reduces safety by 20pp on both BashArena and LinuxArena, and our stop policy reduces safety by 20pp on BashArena and 28pp on LinuxArena. These reductions should be interpreted as upper bounds on the effect of attack selection. Existing control evaluations may therefore yield overly optimistic safety estimates against selective attackers. We recommend that future evaluations, system cards, and safety cases elicit attack selection to produce more realistic safety estimates.

AI.tldr: Attackers that strategically choose when to attack pose a much greater threat than indiscriminate attackers. Current AI control evaluations assume non-strategic attackers, yielding overly optimistic safety estimates. By decomposing attack selection into start and stop policies, the authors show safety drops by 20–28 percentage points at a 1% audit budget across two agentic benchmarks, and recommend incorporating attack selection into future evaluations.
AI.motivation: Current AI control evaluations typically assume attackers that attack indiscriminately rather than strategically selecting when to attack. In real-world deployment, an untrusted AI agent could strategically time attacks to evade detection by a weaker monitor operating under a limited human audit budget. This capability—attack selection—is underexplored, and its omission may lead to systematically inflated safety estimates.
AI.method: The authors decompose attack selection into two sub-policies: a start policy (deciding when to initiate an attack) and a stop policy (deciding when to abort an ongoing attack). They implement these policies within two agentic evaluation environments, BashArena and LinuxArena, and measure their impact on empirical safety under a fixed 1% human audit budget, comparing against baseline indiscriminate attack strategies.
AI.result: At a 1% audit budget, the start policy reduces safety by 20 percentage points on both BashArena and LinuxArena. The stop policy reduces safety by 20pp on BashArena and 28pp on LinuxArena. These reductions are characterized as upper bounds on the effect of attack selection. Both policies substantially lower measured safety without altering the underlying attack capability.
AI.conclusion: Existing control evaluations may produce overly optimistic safety estimates by neglecting strategic attack timing. The authors recommend that future control evaluations, system cards, and safety cases explicitly elicit and model attack selection to yield more realistic safety assessments against selective attackers.

title: Blockchain Infrastructure for Intelligent Cyber--Physical--Social Systems:Post-Quantum Security, Interoperability, and Trustworthy Data Economies in the Era of Embodied AI
summary: The deployment of embodied artificial intelligence via world-model-based robotics presents a transformative opportunity for blockchain infrastructure, establishing urgent demand for trustworthy data provenance, cross-organizational governance, and incentive-compatible sharing across decentralized ecosystems. Simultaneously, quantum computing advances recognized by the 2025 Nobel Prize in Physics and the Turing Award threaten the cryptographic primitives securing these data economies, creating an interdependent imperative: long-lived verification for embodied AI depends on crypto-agile architectures capable of withstanding quantum adversaries. This tutorial examines blockchain as the coordination layer bridging this dual transition, from financial substrate to foundational Cyber-Physical-Social Systems infrastructure that simultaneously secures against quantum cryptanalysis and enables scalable, trustworthy data economies. The session opens with an immersive AWS Braket demonstration engaging participants with superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom hardware to assess cryptographic threat timelines and witness ECDSA-to-post-quantum signature transitions. Five integrated modules progress from embodied AI and world-model requirements through quantum hardware reality and evidence-based security migration, to scalable cross-shard architectures via BrokerChain protocols, trustworthy data economies implementing Croissant metadata standards and robotic learning provenance, and industry ecosystem integration for multi-modal cloud deployment. By bridging quantum hardware realities with embodied AI data requirements, this tutorial charts blockchain as unified infrastructure for next-generation decentralized intelligent environments, providing open-source frameworks and roadmaps for architecting quantum-resistant, interoperable, and data-trustworthy systems.

AI.tldr: This tutorial positions blockchain as the unifying coordination layer that simultaneously addresses two critical transitions: securing embodied AI data economies against quantum cryptographic threats and enabling scalable, trustworthy Cyber-Physical-Social Systems infrastructure through post-quantum migration, cross-shard protocols, and provenance standards.
AI.motivation: Embodied AI via world models demands trustworthy data provenance, cross-organizational governance, and incentive-compatible sharing across decentralized ecosystems. Concurrently, quantum computing advances threaten the cryptographic primitives (e.g., ECDSA) securing these data economies, creating an interdependent imperative for crypto-agile architectures that ensure long-lived verification against quantum adversaries.
AI.method: The tutorial comprises an immersive AWS Braket demonstration (engaging superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom hardware for assessing cryptographic threat timelines and demonstrating ECDSA-to-post-quantum signature transitions) followed by five integrated modules: (1) embodied AI and world-model requirements, (2) quantum hardware reality and evidence-based security migration, (3) scalable cross-shard architectures via BrokerChain protocols, (4) trustworthy data economies using Croissant metadata standards and robotic learning provenance, and (5) industry ecosystem integration for multi-modal cloud deployment.
AI.result: The tutorial delivers open-source frameworks and roadmaps for architecting quantum-resistant, interoperable, and data-trustworthy systems, bridging quantum hardware realities with embodied AI data requirements through blockchain as unified infrastructure for next-generation decentralized intelligent environments.
AI.conclusion: Blockchain serves as the foundational Cyber-Physical-Social Systems infrastructure capable of simultaneously securing against quantum cryptanalysis and enabling scalable, trustworthy data economies for embodied AI, with the tutorial providing concrete migration pathways from current cryptographic primitives to post-quantum architectures.

title: Beyond Matching: Category-Guided Latent Intent Reasoning for Generative Retrieval in E-Commerce
summary: Generative retrieval offers a new paradigm for e-commerce search by mapping user queries directly to product Semantic Identifiers (SIDs). However, e-commerce queries are often short, noisy, attribute-heavy, and associated with multiple category-consistent products, creating a substantial representation gap between natural-language shopping intent and artificially constructed item SIDs. Explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning can help bridge this gap, but its extra generation cost is difficult to reconcile with the low-latency requirements of online e-commerce systems. To address this challenge, we propose CaLIR (Category-guided Latent Intent Reasoning), a category-guided latent intent reasoning framework for e-commerce generative retrieval. Rather than generating explicit textual rationales, CaLIR learns continuous latent intent states before SID decoding and uses product category hierarchies as a natural scaffold for coarse-to-fine intent reasoning. Specifically, we introduce hierarchical semantic reasoning to align latent states with category-level shopping intent, and query-wise reasoning enhancement to model diverse intent paths under multi-positive queries. CaLIR further combines a query-specific dynamic prefix trie, assembled from pre-indexed category-level tries, with reasoning-aware constrained decoding. Experiments on multilingual e-commerce search datasets show that CaLIR achieves a better balance between retrieval effectiveness and inference efficiency than existing methods, while also demonstrating transferability and robustness across induced hierarchies and different generative backbones.

AI.tldr: CaLIR introduces category-guided latent intent reasoning for e-commerce generative retrieval, using continuous latent states and hierarchical category scaffolds instead of explicit CoT reasoning to bridge the query-SID representation gap while maintaining low inference latency.
AI.motivation: E-commerce queries are short, noisy, and attribute-heavy, mapping to multiple category-consistent products. This creates a representation gap between natural-language shopping intent and artificially constructed product Semantic Identifiers (SIDs). Explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning can help but incurs prohibitive generation costs that conflict with the low-latency demands of online e-commerce systems.
AI.method: CaLIR learns continuous latent intent states prior to SID decoding, using product category hierarchies as a coarse-to-fine reasoning scaffold. It introduces: (1) hierarchical semantic reasoning to align latent states with category-level shopping intent; (2) query-wise reasoning enhancement to model diverse intent paths under multi-positive queries; and (3) a query-specific dynamic prefix trie assembled from pre-indexed category-level tries combined with reasoning-aware constrained decoding.
AI.result: Experiments on multilingual e-commerce search datasets demonstrate that CaLIR achieves a superior balance between retrieval effectiveness and inference efficiency compared to existing methods, while also exhibiting transferability and robustness across induced hierarchies and different generative backbones.
AI.conclusion: CaLIR provides an effective and efficient solution for generative retrieval in e-commerce search by replacing explicit CoT generation with latent intent reasoning guided by category hierarchies, meeting both retrieval quality and online latency requirements.

title: Pomona: Continuous Code Quality Improvement via Small, Automated Changes at Bloomberg
summary: In this short experience paper, we present Pomona, a lightweight agentic tool that utilises agent skills for continuous automated code quality improvement. Inspired by the philosophy of Kaizen(TM), Pomona automates a cycle of discovery and incremental repair: a Scanning skill identifies improvement tasks (e.g., linting violations, technical debt markers, and test gaps) and prioritises them in a structured backlog, while a Repair skill generates tiny pull requests (PRs) targeting ~10 lines of diff. This human-in-the-loop design enables frequent, low-risk improvements while maintaining engineer trust and productivity and reducing technical debt. We evaluated Pomona through a one-month deployment in a team and a questionnaire distributed to 10 senior engineers. Our preliminary results are promising: 15 of 17 generated PRs were successfully merged with a median time-to-close of under 2 hours. Furthermore, 8/10 of surveyed engineers expressed a desire to adopt Pomona, praising small diff sizes and Pomona's focus on improving code quality. We conclude by discussing actionable insights for researchers and practitioners on strategies for effective agentic deployment in industry.

AI.tldr: Pomona is a lightweight agentic tool that automates continuous code quality improvement through a Kaizen-inspired cycle of scanning for issues and generating small (~10-line) pull requests, achieving 15/17 PRs merged with median close time under 2 hours in a one-month deployment.
AI.motivation: To enable frequent, low-risk, incremental code quality improvements while maintaining engineer trust and productivity, and to reduce technical debt through automated agentic processes rather than large, disruptive refactoring efforts.
AI.method: Pomona employs two agent skills in a human-in-the-loop cycle: a Scanning skill that identifies improvement tasks (linting violations, technical debt markers, test gaps) and prioritizes them in a backlog, and a Repair skill that generates small pull requests (~10 lines of diff). Evaluation involved a one-month team deployment and a questionnaire administered to 10 senior engineers.
AI.result: 15 of 17 generated PRs were successfully merged, with a median time-to-close under 2 hours. In the questionnaire, 8/10 senior engineers expressed desire to adopt Pomona, praising the small diff sizes and the tool's focus on code quality improvement.
AI.conclusion: The preliminary results suggest that agentic tools with small, incremental PRs and human-in-the-loop design can effectively support continuous code quality improvement. The authors offer actionable insights for deploying agentic systems in industrial settings.

title: A Model of Integrated Information Processing in Human-AI Interaction
summary: For Human-AI Interaction (HAII) research to move forward, theoretical work linking psychological mechanisms to interface design is needed. Such work should extend rather than replace established HCI and automation research, adapting to the increasing autonomy and agency of AI systems. Building on prior frameworks focused on roles and levels in human interaction with automation, a gap remains from a psychological view: a task-centered, process-oriented account that links mechanisms of action regulation to concrete design and evaluation levers for human-AI coupling, expressed in a unified vocabulary for human and machine. Moreover, existing models may describe how a system is designed (e.g., function allocation in automation) but fall short in showing how this design affects human behavior. We present the Integrated Information Processing (IIP) model, a task-centered, cybernetic model that conceptualizes humans, machines, and their joint activity as coupled control loops. The IIP model uses a unified modeling language for human and artificial agents, making psychological models of action regulation accessible for AI system design. As a core feature, we argue that efficacy within a shared task is characterized by three integration qualities, input adequacy, reference consonance, and output operativity, which critically influence benchmarks of human-centeredness such as transparency and controllability. The model maps interface choices (e.g., XAI techniques) to theory-driven expectations of user behavior, guiding interface design and evaluation. To this end, we present (1) a continuity-preserving theoretical discourse that extends HAII to agency in AI; (2) the IIP model with three information-processing qualities; and (3) applications of the IIP model to exemplary use cases demonstrating implications for interface design.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the Integrated Information Processing (IIP) model, a cybernetic framework that conceptualizes humans and AI as coupled control loops sharing three integration qualities — input adequacy, reference consonance, and output operativity — to link psychological action regulation mechanisms to concrete interface design choices for human-AI interaction.
AI.motivation: Existing Human-AI Interaction research lacks theoretical frameworks that connect psychological mechanisms to interface design. Prior automation and HCI models describe system design (e.g., function allocation) but fail to explain how design choices affect human behavior. A task-centered, process-oriented account with a unified vocabulary for both human and machine agents is needed to address the increasing autonomy and agency of AI systems.
AI.method: The authors develop the Integrated Information Processing (IIP) model, a cybernetic, task-centered framework that models humans, machines, and their joint activity as coupled control loops. It employs a unified modeling language for both human and artificial agents, making psychological action regulation theories accessible for AI system design. The model is applied to exemplary use cases to demonstrate implications for interface design.
AI.result: The IIP model identifies three core integration qualities that characterize efficacy in shared human-AI tasks: input adequacy, reference consonance, and output operativity. These qualities critically influence benchmarks of human-centeredness such as transparency and controllability. The model maps interface choices (e.g., XAI techniques) to theory-driven expectations of user behavior.
AI.conclusion: The IIP model provides a continuity-preserving theoretical discourse that extends HAII to accommodate AI agency, bridges psychology and interface design through three information-processing qualities, and offers actionable guidance for designing and evaluating human-AI coupling in interactive systems.

title: From Privacy to Workflow Integrity: Communication-Graph Metadata in Autonomous Agent Interoperability
summary: Agent-interoperability protocols such as A2A and MCP standardize what agents say to one another, but assume address-based transport over HTTP(S). Such transports protect message content, increasingly with end-to-end encryption. What they leave in the clear is the communication graph: which agent contacts which, when, and how often. In agent systems this graph is more consequential than a privacy framing suggests. Endpoints are often capability-labeled, workflows are structured and chained, and interactions are coupled to real actions, so an observer recovers more than past relationships. It can infer the pending workflow, the task being assembled and the action likely to follow. At machine speed, it can act on that inference before the workflow completes. The threat is therefore one of workflow integrity, not privacy alone: predictive leverage over autonomous action. We give a threat model for the agent communication graph; identify what makes agent metadata distinctively revealing (semanticity, prospectivity, actuation); define transport- and bootstrap-layer privacy properties and weigh candidate transports (SimpleX/SMP, Tor, mixnets) against them; and present an A2A case study in which a metadata-protecting binding is expressible but surfaces the protocol's identity assumptions. We test these on a generative model anchored to a real A2A capture. From passive metadata alone, with no payloads, a classifier recovers a task's class well above chance, from only the workflow's opening; applied together, the properties drive that recovery sharply back toward chance. Beyond what an observer can recover, we measure the leverage of acting on the leak: from a workflow's opening and under a fixed budget, an adversary choosing which workflows to act on realizes in this model most of a clairvoyant attacker's advantage over a metadata-blind one, and the same properties suppress it.

AI.tldr: Agent communication metadata — who contacts whom, when, and how often — leaks the communication graph, enabling adversaries to infer pending workflows and preemptively act before completion. This paper provides a threat model for agent metadata, defines privacy properties, evaluates candidate transports, and demonstrates via a generative model that metadata-protecting measures drive task-class recovery and adversarial advantage sharply toward chance.
AI.motivation: Existing agent-interoperability protocols (A2A, MCP) protect message content via encryption but expose the communication graph in cleartext. In agent systems, this metadata is uniquely revealing — it encodes capability-labeled endpoints, structured workflow chains, and couplings to real-world actions — making the threat one of workflow integrity rather than privacy alone.
AI.method: The authors construct a threat model capturing three distinctive properties of agent metadata: semanticity (endpoints carry capability semantics), prospectivity (metadata reveals future steps in a workflow), and actuation (interactions trigger real actions). They define transport- and bootstrap-layer privacy properties, evaluate candidate transports (SimpleX/SMP, Tor, mixnets) against these properties, and present an A2A case study. A generative model anchored to real A2A traces is used to measure: (1) a classifier's ability to recover task class from passive metadata alone, and (2) an adversary's leverage in selecting workflows to act on under a fixed budget.
AI.result: From passive metadata alone (no payloads), a classifier recovers a task's class well above chance using only the workflow's opening. When the defined privacy properties are applied together, recovery drops sharply toward chance. Under a fixed budget, an adversary choosing which workflows to act on realizes most of a clairvoyant attacker's advantage over a metadata-blind one; the same privacy properties suppress this advantage.
AI.conclusion: Agent metadata constitutes a distinct and consequential leakage vector that threatens workflow integrity, not merely privacy. Transport-layer and bootstrap-layer privacy properties can effectively neutralize both passive inference and adversarial preemption, and these protections can be expressed within existing protocols like A2A, though doing so surfaces identity assumptions baked into those protocols.

title: What Do People Actually Want From AI? Mapping Preference Plurality
comment: Accepted at the 2026 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '26)
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are often fine-tuned through Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) to align with people's preferences and values. However, this method has known limitations: it aggregates conflicting preferences, often relies on unrepresentative samples, and uses only binary comparisons. Analysing 1,500 open-ended responses from the PRISM dataset across 75 countries, we examine what people actually want from AI systems and reveal concrete failures of current methods.
  We find that different people want different things: most values are requested by fewer than a quarter of respondents, with truthfulness the sole exception at 49%. Furthermore, the same words hide divergent meanings: when people describe what they mean by "truthfulness", they reveal distinct, potentially incompatible, epistemological bases, as some ask for sourced claims, some for expert opinions, and some even ask for unpopular views. Certain capabilities, namely how human-like a model behaves, and some features, like AI guardrails, are outright controversial, with some desiring them and others rejecting them. We additionally find that people often use contextual distinctions (what AI should do "by default" versus "if requested") that binary comparisons cannot capture.
  These findings expose fundamental problems in current alignment practices. When 49% request truthfulness but define it differently, this is unlikely to be captured by a single reward model. The persistence of high hallucination rates in well-funded models, despite users' clear demands for accuracy, suggests that current methods fail to identify actual preferences. This paper sheds light on the situated, contested, imperfect signals that are currently being flattened into universal preference models, a practice others have characterised as epistemic violence.

AI.tldr: Current RLHF alignment practices flatten diverse, context-dependent, and often conflicting human preferences into universal binary reward models, failing to capture the situated meanings people actually hold. Analysis of 1,500 open-ended responses across 75 countries reveals that values like "truthfulness" mask incompatible epistemological commitments, certain features are outright controversial, and contextual distinctions are lost — explaining persistent failures like hallucination despite user demand for accuracy.
AI.motivation: RLHF, the dominant alignment paradigm, aggregates conflicting preferences using unrepresentative binary comparisons. The authors argue this practice systematically erases the diversity of what people actually want from AI, potentially constituting what others have termed "epistemic violence." The paper seeks to empirically document the concrete failures of current methods by examining open-ended responses about AI values at a global scale.
AI.method: Qualitative and quantitative analysis of 1,500 open-ended survey responses from the PRISM dataset, collected across 75 countries. The authors code and categorize expressed preferences for AI behavior, surface-level value terms (e.g., "truthfulness"), and examine: (1) the distribution of stated values, (2) divergent underlying meanings for shared terms, (3) contested capabilities/features, and (4) contextual distinctions that binary comparisons cannot capture.
AI.result: Four key findings: (1) Most values are minority positions — only truthfulness exceeds 25% endorsement (at 49%). (2) Shared value labels conceal incompatible definitions: "truthfulness" variously means sourced claims, expert opinions, or unpopular views. (3) Some capabilities (human-likeness) and features (guardrails) are polarized, with explicit demand and rejection. (4) Respondents employ contextual distinctions (e.g., default behavior vs. on-request behavior) that binary preference comparisons cannot represent. These mismatches explain persistent failures like hallucination in well-resourced models.
AI.conclusion: Current RLHF alignment fundamentally fails to capture actual human preferences because it reduces situated, contested, context-dependent signals into universal preference models. The persistence of hallucination despite clear user demand for accuracy demonstrates that binary reward modeling cannot identify what people actually want. The paper calls for rethinking alignment to accommodate pluralism rather than enforcing artificial consensus.

title: Generative Models Erode Human Temporal Learning Through Market Selection
summary: We argue that modern generative models create structural risks for knowledge and cultural production at current, sub-AGI capability levels. We define Human Temporal Learning (HTL) as path-dependent knowledge accumulation through sustained engagement with problems over time. Generative outputs increasingly resemble HTL-intensive work in surface features, so verifying whether a given output reflects genuine human learning grows costly relative to its expected benefit. Once verification loses economic justification, evaluators reward outputs regardless of production mode, and producers who invested years of learning compete on price against outputs that cost almost nothing to generate. We call this pathway value collapse and formalize it through a costly-inspection framework. Cross-domain evidence from academic publishing, legal practice, content platforms, and software security maps onto four stages of verification erosion. Alignment success is orthogonal. Better-aligned models narrow observable gaps between human and AI outputs, making source verification harder and intensifying competitive pressure against HTL-intensive work even when individual AI outputs improve.

AI.tldr: Modern generative models erode the economic viability of path-dependent human knowledge work by making verification of authentic human learning increasingly costly relative to its benefits, leading to "value collapse" where HTL-intensive outputs compete on price against near-zero-cost AI generations — a problem alignment cannot solve.
AI.motivation: The authors identify a structural risk that generative AI poses to knowledge and cultural production that operates independently of model capability ceilings (sub-AGI) and alignment quality. The concern is not about AI surpassing human performance, but about AI undermining the economic incentive structure that sustains long-term human investment in learning and expertise.
AI.method: The paper introduces the concept of Human Temporal Learning (HTL) as path-dependent knowledge accumulation, then develops a costly-inspection framework to formalize "value collapse." It identifies four stages of verification erosion and maps cross-domain evidence from academic publishing, legal practice, content platforms, and software security onto this framework.
AI.result: The analysis shows that as AI outputs increasingly resemble HTL-intensive work in surface features, verification costs rise relative to expected benefits. Once verification becomes economically unjustified, evaluators accept outputs regardless of production mode, forcing human experts to compete on price with near-zero-cost generations. Alignment improvements paradoxically worsen this dynamic by narrowing observable gaps.
AI.conclusion: Value collapse is orthogonal to alignment success. Better-aligned models make source verification harder and intensify competitive pressure against HTL-intensive work, even when individual AI outputs improve. The structural threat to human knowledge production requires attention beyond alignment research.

title: CARVE-Q: Quantum-Proposed, Classically Certified Interactive Driving Repair
summary: The critical question after a correct driving veto is not only whether a maneuver is unsafe, but whether the blocked interaction admits a lawful, auditable, and responsibility-bounded repair. Prediction and game-theoretic planners can suggest plausible cooperation, yet they do not return a proof that the repair respects hard rules, right-of-way, cost allocation, and ego fallback. We introduce CARVE, Certified Affordable Repair of Vetoed maneuvers via Envelopes, a certificate architecture for prediction-free interactive repair. Given a vetoed maneuver, CARVE constructs a finite repair lattice and emits a structured certificate recording the binding rule, selected joint repair, right-of-way-scaled cooperation envelope, responsibility-weighted cost split, and ego-only fallback. This certificate view reveals the algorithmic bottleneck: multi-owner repair induces a product lattice $M = \prod_j |\mathcal{A}_j|$. We therefore introduce CARVE-Q, a verifier-shielded quantum-AI search layer that applies quantum minimum finding only to this black-box lattice while leaving all safety authority classical. In the conservative verifier-oracle model, exact classical minimum finding requires $Θ(M)$ queries in the worst case, whereas Durr-Hoyer/Grover minimum finding uses $O(\sqrt{M})$ oracle queries with high probability. We prove verifier-shielded certificate soundness, priority non-elicitation, black-box query separation, and finite-precision reversible-oracle constructibility. We then demonstrate state-vector minimum finding on CARVE repair oracles up to 65,536 assignments and validate certificate preservation on Lanelet2-grounded INTERACTION replay with 100% right-of-way respect, 100% blame consistency, and zero priority false positives. The result is a trust-bounded quantum-AI pattern for certified autonomy: quantum proposes; CARVE certifies.

AI.tldr: CARVE introduces a certificate architecture for certified, prediction-free interactive repair of vetoed autonomous driving maneuvers. It constructs a finite repair lattice and emits a structured certificate recording rule compliance, right-of-way, cost allocation, and fallback. To address the combinatorial explosion in multi-owner repair (product lattice of size M), CARVE-Q leverages quantum minimum finding (Dürr-Høyer/Grover) achieving O(√M) query complexity versus classical Θ(M), with formal guarantees of verifier-shielded soundness and query separation. Validated on Lanelet2-grounded INTERACTION datasets with 100% right-of-way respect and blame consistency.
AI.motivation: Post-veto autonomous driving requires not just identifying unsafe maneuvers but generating lawful, auditable, responsibility-bounded repairs. Existing prediction-based and game-theoretic planners lack proofs that repairs respect hard rules, right-of-way, cost allocation, and ego fallback simultaneously. The combinatorial product lattice for multi-agent repair creates an algorithmic bottleneck that classical search cannot efficiently resolve.
AI.method: CARVE constructs a finite repair lattice from a vetoed maneuver, encoding binding rules, joint repairs, right-of-way-scaled cooperation envelopes, responsibility-weighted cost splits, and ego-only fallbacks into a structured certificate. CARVE-Q extends this with a verifier-shielded quantum-AI search layer using quantum minimum finding (Dürr-Høyer/Grover) as a black-box oracle on the product lattice M = Πⱼ|Aⱼ|, keeping all safety authority classical. The approach is formalized in the verifier-oracle model with proofs of certificate soundness, priority non-elicitation, black-box query separation (O(√M) vs Θ(M)), and finite-precision reversible-oracle constructibility. State-vector simulations reach 65,536 assignments.
AI.result: State-vector quantum minimum finding demonstrated on CARVE repair oracles up to 65,536 assignments. On Lanelet2-grounded INTERACTION replay data: 100% right-of-way respect, 100% blame consistency, and zero priority false positives, confirming certificate preservation.
AI.conclusion: CARVE establishes a trust-bounded quantum-AI pattern for certified autonomy: quantum search proposes repairs efficiently (O(√M) queries), while the classical CARVE verifier certifies them. This architecture bridges the gap between efficient search in combinatorial repair spaces and the rigorous safety guarantees required for auditable autonomous driving.

title: DPAgent-in-the-Middle: Agentic Defense and Repair Against AI-Groomed Deceptive Patterns
summary: Privacy deceptive patterns in web interfaces systematically manipulate users into disclosing personal data, yet existing defenses are fragmented, static, and increasingly vulnerable to manipulation by large language models. Moreover, data voids, areas of information scarcity within the web ecosystem, create fertile ground for adversaries to inject misleading content that can be scraped and learned by AI systems, thereby amplifying both deceptive design and model misbehavior. In this paper, we formalize a new threat model, AI grooming, where attackers exploit data voids to seed benign-looking but malicious samples that corrupt model reasoning and normalize deceptive practices. To address this threat in privacy deceptive patterns, we present DPAgent, an agentic and reasoning-aware framework that orchestrates four specialized agents to mitigate the AI Grooming threat via a proactive defense that combines latent space purification with defensive prompting and operates directly in live web environments to proactively explore, detect, and repair privacy deceptive user interfaces before they reach end users. Extensive evaluations show that DPAgent detects 90.98% of groomed samples, achieves state-of-the-art privacy deceptive pattern detection with a micro F1 of 0.816, explores over 80% of pattern types while visiting only about 10% of the pages required by baselines, and successfully repairs 77% of detected deceptive interfaces. A large-scale study of 485 websites in the wild reveals that up to 98% contain at least one privacy deceptive pattern, over 90% of which can be mitigated by DPAgent. User studies further confirm that DPAgent effectively reduces privacy risks while preserving browsing experience. Our results demonstrate the promise of agent-in-the-middle defenses for securing the web UI supply chain against deceptive design and emerging AI threats rooted in data void exploitation.

AI.tldr: DPAgent is an agentic framework that counters "AI grooming" — a threat where attackers exploit web data voids to corrupt AI models — by deploying four specialized agents to proactively detect, repair, and defend against privacy deceptive patterns on live websites, achieving 90.98% detection of groomed samples, 0.816 micro F1 in pattern detection, and 77% repair success.
AI.motivation: Existing defenses against privacy deceptive patterns are fragmented, static, and susceptible to manipulation by large language models. Data voids on the web allow adversaries to inject misleading content that AI systems scrape and learn from, amplifying both deceptive design and model misbehavior. A unified, proactive defense framework is needed.
AI.method: The paper formalizes "AI grooming" as a threat model, then introduces DPAgent — an agentic, reasoning-aware framework with four specialized agents. It combines latent space purification with defensive prompting and operates directly in live web environments to explore, detect, and repair privacy deceptive UIs proactively, acting as an agent-in-the-middle defense.
AI.result: DPAgent detects 90.98% of groomed samples, achieves SOTA micro F1 of 0.816 for privacy deceptive pattern detection, explores >80% of pattern types visiting only ~10% of baseline pages, and repairs 77% of deceptive interfaces. A wild study of 485 websites shows up to 98% contain at least one deceptive pattern, with >90% mitigable by DPAgent. User studies confirm reduced privacy risk without harming browsing experience.
AI.conclusion: DPAgent demonstrates the viability of agent-in-the-middle defenses for securing the web UI supply chain against deceptive design and emerging AI threats rooted in data void exploitation.

title: RISE: A Rust Library for Inverted Index Search Engines
summary: Inverted indexes are a crucial data structure for efficient information retrieval in large text corpora. They enable fast full-text search by mapping each term to the documents in which it appears, on top of which efficient algorithms quickly retrieve the documents relevant to a user query. We present RISE, a novel inverted index library implemented in Rust, designed to deliver high performance and efficiency for information retrieval tasks. RISE leverages Rust's safety and performance to provide a robust solution for building and querying inverted indexes, while offering accessible extensibility through its expressive trait system. While developing RISE, we revisited the inverted-index literature, thereby reproducing numerous prior works using this new test bench. We evaluated RISE against existing libraries, demonstrating competitive query performance across various datasets and workloads, with speedups of up to 2x over the current state of the art. Our results indicate that RISE is a promising tool for researchers and practitioners in the field of information retrieval.

AI.tldr: RISE is a new inverted index library written in Rust that achieves up to 2x speedup over the state of the art for information retrieval queries, while leveraging Rust's safety, performance, and trait-based extensibility.
AI.motivation: To deliver a high-performance, safe, and extensible inverted index library for information retrieval, and to provide a modern test bench for reproducing and benchmarking prior research in the inverted index literature.
AI.method: The authors implemented RISE in Rust, exploiting the language's safety guarantees, performance characteristics, and expressive trait system for extensibility. They reproduced numerous prior inverted index works using RISE as a test bench and benchmarked it against existing libraries across diverse datasets and workloads.
AI.result: RISE demonstrated competitive query performance with speedups of up to 2x over the current state-of-the-art libraries across various datasets and workloads.
AI.conclusion: RISE is a promising tool for both researchers and practitioners in information retrieval, offering a combination of high performance, safety, extensibility, and reproducibility that advances the state of inverted index implementations.

title: Chiseling Out Efficiency: Structured Skeleton Supervision for Efficient Code Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are capable of generating syntactically correct and functionally complete programs, greatly streamlining software development. However, recent studies reveal that these programs typically execute substantially slower than human-optimized counterparts. Existing approaches to bridging this efficiency gap typically involve either iteratively optimizing code after generation or fine-tuning models on corpora of efficient code. Yet, these methods expose the model to efficiency signals only by mimicking complete, optimized solutions, without explicitly encoding the structural code patterns essential for achieving high runtime performance. Addressing this gap presents two core challenges: (1) extracting and representing latent, efficiency-oriented structural patterns embedded within complex syntax and control flows, and (2) effectively learning these patterns without destabilizing the semantic training of LLMs. To tackle these challenges, we propose EffiSkel, an efficiency skeleton-guided framework that explicitly extracts and learns efficiency skeletons-abstract, reusable structural patterns underpinning efficient code-by leveraging three complementary strategies. These skeletons are integrated into a multi-task learning regime that jointly optimizes code generation and skeleton prediction. Experiments across multiple programming languages and benchmarks demonstrate that EffiSkel significantly enhances both functional correctness and efficiency, resulting on Mercury with DeepSeek-Coder (7B) a +11.11% (vs. EffiCoder) and +3.71% (vs. CodeDPO) higher Efficiency Ratio (ER), and a +0.36 (vs. EffiCoder) and +0.22 (vs. CodeDPO) increase in Average Speedup (AS). These results highlight the effectiveness of explicitly modeling efficiency skeletons in improving the runtime performance of code generated by LLMs.

AI.tldr: EffiSkel introduces efficiency skeletons — abstract, reusable structural patterns of efficient code — and integrates them into a multi-task learning framework that jointly optimizes code generation and skeleton prediction, significantly improving both functional correctness and runtime performance of LLM-generated code.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated programs are functionally correct but substantially slower than human-optimized code. Existing methods (iterative post-generation optimization or fine-tuning on efficient code corpora) only expose models to efficiency signals through complete solutions, without explicitly encoding the structural code patterns critical for runtime performance.
AI.method: EffiSkel extracts efficiency skeletons — abstract, reusable structural patterns underlying efficient code — using three complementary strategies. These skeletons are incorporated into a multi-task learning framework that jointly trains the LLM on code generation and skeleton prediction tasks.
AI.result: On the Mercury benchmark with DeepSeek-Coder (7B), EffiSkel achieves +11.11% higher Efficiency Ratio (ER) over EffiCoder and +3.71% over CodeDPO, along with +0.36 and +0.22 increases in Average Speedup (AS), respectively. Improvements hold across multiple languages and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling and learning efficiency skeletons is an effective approach for enhancing the runtime performance of LLM-generated code, surpassing prior methods that rely on implicit efficiency signal exposure.

title: HKJudge: A Legal Discourse-Annotated Corpus for Interpreting What Courts Find, How They Reason, and What They Rule
summary: Court judgments are central to legal practice and jurisprudence, yet discourse analysis of Hong Kong judgments has received limited attention, owing largely to the absence of expert-annotated corpora. We introduce the Hong Kong Judgment Discourse Dataset (HKJudge), the first sentence-level expert-annotated legal discourse corpus. HKJudge includes criminal judgments across all five levels of HK's court hierarchy, comprising $\sim$290k sentences and $\sim$6.5 million tokens, fully annotated by legal linguistics experts. We design a two-tier discourse schema that captures what facts a court finds, how it reasons, and what it rules. At the sentence level, each sentence is assigned one of 26 rhetorical roles. At the span level, sentences are further annotated with three sentencing elements (charge, imprisonment term, fine). Ten legal linguistics annotators produced the annotations with an inter-annotator agreement of $κ= 0.8$. We formulate two tasks on HKJudge, termed rhetorical role classification and legal element extraction, and provide the first benchmark evaluation of four BERT-based models, two open-source LLMs under zero-shot and fine-tuning settings, and four commercial LLMs on both tasks. Our work demonstrates the value of sentence-level discourse annotation for modeling the structure of HK judgments and provides a rich data foundation for future work on legal judgment prediction. The HKJudge dataset and code are available at https://github.com/xuanxixi/HKJudge.

AI.tldr: HKJudge is the first sentence-level expert-annotated legal discourse corpus for Hong Kong judgments, featuring a two-tier schema (26 rhetorical roles + 3 sentencing elements) across ~290k sentences. The paper establishes benchmarks for rhetorical role classification and legal element extraction using BERT-based models, open-source LLMs, and commercial LLMs.
AI.motivation: Discourse analysis of Hong Kong court judgments is underexplored due to the lack of expert-annotated corpora. Existing legal NLP resources focus on other jurisdictions, leaving a gap for HK's unique common law system with its bilingual and hierarchical court structure.
AI.method: The authors construct HKJudge from criminal judgments across all five levels of Hong Kong's court hierarchy. Ten legal linguistics experts annotate sentences using a two-tier schema: (1) sentence-level with 26 rhetorical roles capturing factual findings, reasoning, and rulings; (2) span-level with three sentencing elements (charge, imprisonment term, fine). They formulate rhetorical role classification and legal element extraction tasks, evaluating four BERT-based models, two open-source LLMs (zero-shot and fine-tuned), and four commercial LLMs.
AI.result: Inter-annotator agreement reached κ=0.8. Benchmark results are provided across all model types for both tasks, demonstrating the dataset's utility for legal discourse modeling and judgment prediction.
AI.conclusion: HKJudge provides a rich foundation for modeling the discourse structure of Hong Kong judgments. The sentence-level annotations enable fine-grained legal NLP tasks and open avenues for future work on legal judgment prediction in the Hong Kong jurisdiction.

title: Skip a Layer or Loop It? Learning Program-of-Layers in LLMs
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. Substantially extends arXiv:2507.07996. Code: https://github.com/tianyi-lab/PoLar
summary: Large language models (LLMs) perform inference by following a fixed depth and order, non-recurrent execution of all layers. We reveal the wide existence of training-free, flexible, dynamic program-of-layers (PoLar), where pretrained layers can be packed as modules and then skipped or looped to form a customized program for each input. For most inputs, substantially shorter program executions can achieve the same or better accuracy, while incorrect predictions of the original LLM can be corrected by alternative programs with fewer layers. These observations indicate that inference admits multiple valid latent computations beyond the standard forward pass. To efficiently achieve PoLar in practice, we propose a lightweight PoLar prediction network, which learns to generate execution programs that dynamically skip or repeat pretrained layers for each input. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that PoLar consistently improves accuracy over standard inference and prior dynamic-depth methods, often while executing fewer layers, and that these gains persist under out-of-distribution evaluation. Our results suggest that fixed-depth execution captures only a narrow subset of an LLM's latent reasoning capacity.

AI.tldr: LLMs can achieve equal or better accuracy by dynamically skipping or looping pretrained layers per input, rather than executing all layers in a fixed order. The authors propose PoLar, a lightweight prediction network that generates input-specific layer execution programs, demonstrating accuracy gains with fewer layers on math reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM inference uses a fixed-depth, sequential execution of all layers. The authors challenge this, hypothesizing that latent reasoning capacity can be unlocked by flexible, input-dependent layer execution — skipping unnecessary layers or repeating useful ones — without any retraining.
AI.method: The paper introduces PoLar (Program-of-Layers), where pretrained layers are treated as modular units that can be skipped or looped to form customized execution programs per input. A lightweight prediction network is trained to generate these dynamic execution programs, determining which layers to skip or repeat for each input. This operates training-free on the original LLM weights.
AI.result: Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show PoLar consistently improves accuracy over standard inference and prior dynamic-depth methods (e.g., early exiting), often while executing fewer layers. Gains persist under out-of-distribution evaluation, and incorrect predictions from the original LLM can be corrected by alternative, shorter programs.
AI.conclusion: Fixed-depth execution captures only a narrow subset of an LLM's latent reasoning capacity. Dynamic, input-dependent layer programming reveals multiple valid latent computation paths, offering both efficiency and accuracy improvements without modifying pretrained weights.

title: Position: Don't Just "Fix it in Post": A Science of AI Must Study Training Dynamics
comment: Accepted as an oral to the ICML: https://icml.cc/virtual/2026/poster/67142
summary: What would it mean to have a scientific understanding of AI? Models are not static objects: they are snapshots of time-evolving processes shaped by data, objectives, architectures, and optimization dynamics. Yet much of AI research treats models as fixed artifacts, analyzing behaviors after training rather than asking why they emerge. This position paper argues that a science of AI must move beyond post-hoc fixes and study the training dynamics that produce model behavior. Such a science should support progressively stronger forms of understanding: predicting outcomes from early training signals, intervening when trajectories go wrong, and ultimately designing training procedures that more reliably produce desired properties. Scaling laws have made prediction routine for loss; the challenge is extending this success to capabilities, biases, robustness, and safety-relevant behaviors. We articulate requirements for such theories grounded in the history and philosophy of science, examine progress in mechanistic interpretability, fairness, memorization, and simplicity bias, and identify concrete open problems.

AI.tldr: This position paper argues that a scientific understanding of AI requires studying the training dynamics that produce model behaviors—not just analyzing models as static artifacts. It advocates for progressively stronger forms of understanding (prediction, intervention, design) and identifies open problems in extending scaling-law-style predictive success from loss to safety-relevant behaviors.
AI.motivation: AI research predominantly treats trained models as fixed objects for post-hoc behavioral analysis, rather than investigating why those behaviors emerge during training. The authors argue this is scientifically inadequate: genuine understanding requires explaining how data, objectives, architectures, and optimization dynamics interact over time to produce model properties. Scaling laws have successfully made loss prediction routine, but extending this to capabilities, biases, robustness, and safety demands a shift toward studying training trajectories.
AI.method: The paper takes a philosophy-of-science approach, articulating three tiers of scientific understanding—prediction (forecasting outcomes from early training signals), intervention (correcting trajectories that go wrong), and design (constructing training procedures that reliably yield desired properties). It grounds these requirements in history and philosophy of science, then surveys existing progress across mechanistic interpretability, fairness, memorization, and simplicity bias, identifying where current work falls short and framing concrete open problems.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that mechanistic interpretability, fairness interventions, memorization studies, and simplicity bias research each provide partial progress toward a dynamical science of AI, but none yet achieves the strong predictive, interventional, and design-oriented understanding the framework demands. The paper identifies specific open problems, including predicting emergent capabilities from early training, intervening to prevent undesired behaviors without retraining from scratch, and designing optimization procedures with guarantees on safety-relevant properties.
AI.conclusion: A mature science of AI must center on training dynamics rather than post-hoc behavioral analysis. Achieving this requires developing theories that support prediction, intervention, and design across capabilities, biases, robustness, and safety—extending the success of scaling laws beyond loss prediction. The paper calls for concerted interdisciplinary effort grounded in both empirical AI research and the philosophy of science.

title: HAVE: Host Active Verification Engine for Closing the Contextual Reality Gap in Security Digital Twins
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Security Digital Twins (SDTs) provide continuously updated virtual replicas of infrastructure for threat simulation, yet they rely on theoretical CVSS scores to assign lateral-movement probabilities -- creating the Contextual Reality Gap: risk is overestimated where unacknowledged mitigations neutralize exploits, and drastically underestimated where logic flaws bypass all memory-safety defenses. We present the Host Active Verification Engine (HAVE), an SDT extension that deploys a safety-constrained host agent to measure the empirical probability of compromise $\hat{p}$ via maximum-likelihood estimation over snapshot-isolated Bernoulli trials. A Wilson interval-width confidence weight $α_w$ propagates $\hat{p}$ into Monte Carlo simulations via a Bayesian blending rule formally related to the Beta-Binomial posterior. Evaluation across four vulnerability classes, three security tiers, and two production binaries shows HAVE reduces $P_{\text{reach}}$ by 38.2% in false-positive scenarios and increases it by 132.4% in false-negative scenarios, with a net +124.1% correction; post-HAVE estimates vary by only $1.12\times$ across calibration exponents $κ$, versus $4.6\times$ for CVSS-only baselines.

AI.tldr: HAVE addresses the Contextual Reality Gap in Security Digital Twins by deploying a host agent that empirically measures compromise probability through controlled Bernoulli trials, correcting both false-positive and false-negative lateral-movement risk estimates that CVSS-based approaches get wrong.
AI.motivation: Security Digital Twins (SDTs) use theoretical CVSS scores for lateral-movement probability assignment, creating a "Contextual Reality Gap": risk is overestimated when unacknowledged mitigations neutralize exploits, and drastically underestimated when logic flaws bypass memory-safety defenses entirely. This gap undermines the fidelity of threat simulations.
AI.method: HAVE extends SDTs with a safety-constrained host agent that performs snapshot-isolated Bernoulli trials to measure empirical probability of compromise (p̂) via maximum-likelihood estimation. A Wilson interval-width confidence weight (α_w) is propagated into Monte Carlo simulations using a Bayesian blending rule formally related to the Beta-Binomial posterior.
AI.result: Evaluated across four vulnerability classes, three security tiers, and two production binaries, HAVE reduces P_reach by 38.2% in false-positive scenarios and increases it by 132.4% in false-negative scenarios — a net +124.1% correction. Post-HAVE estimates vary by only 1.12× across calibration exponents κ, versus 4.6× for CVSS-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: HAVE substantially closes the Contextual Reality Gap by replacing theoretical CVSS-derived probabilities with empirically grounded measurements, yielding more reliable and calibration-robust lateral-movement risk estimates for SDT-based threat simulation.

title: HKVM-RAG: Key-Value-Separated Hypergraph Evidence Organization for Multi-Hop RAG
summary: Multi-hop RAG poses a data-engineering problem beyond passage matching: under fixed retrieval budgets, a system must organize retrieved text into evidence units that expose answer chains. Dense retrievers score passages independently, while graph-based memories make associations explicit but often rely on pairwise or entity-centered keys that fragment multi-hop evidence. We present HKVM-RAG, a key-value-separated evidence-organization layer. It assembles answer-path hyperedges from cached passage-level LLM evidence tuples and uses them as retrieval keys, while retaining passage text as answer values. To isolate key-space design, our fixed-substrate protocol holds the tuple cache, candidate passages, reader, and evaluation budget constant across pairwise graph and hypergraph variants. Weighted hypergraph key-value retrieval improves over KG-PPR by +3.426 F1 on 2WikiMultiHopQA and +3.592 F1 on MuSiQue; HotpotQA shows that higher structured support coverage need not yield standalone answer-F1 gains. We therefore study WHG-KV as an evidence-control signal rather than a dense-retrieval replacement. Oracle and train-to-dev analyses identify support selection as repairable, and a dense-aware controller combines frozen ColBERTv2 and HKVM rank/score features using out-of-fold HKVM predictions. It reaches 88.846, 65.073, and 85.810 F1 on the three benchmarks, improving over ColBERTv2 by +11.084, +6.763, and +5.966 F1. Source-level ablations show that matched non-WHG structured signals do not match the WHG-KV gains. These results provide bounded evidence that key-value-separated hypergraph organization can serve as a reusable evidence-control mechanism for multi-hop RAG.

AI.tldr: HKVM-RAG introduces a key-value-separated hypergraph organization layer for multi-hop RAG, where answer-path hyperedges serve as retrieval keys and passage text as values. A weighted hypergraph key-value (WHG-KV) retrieval method improves F1 over KG-based baselines by 3.4–3.6 points, and a dense-aware controller combining ColBERTv2 with HKVM features yields gains of +5.97–11.08 F1 across three multi-hop QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Multi-hop RAG systems face a data-engineering challenge: under fixed retrieval budgets, retrieved text must be organized into evidence units that expose reasoning chains. Existing dense retrievers score passages independently (missing cross-passage associations), while graph-based memories use pairwise or entity-centered keys that fragment multi-hop evidence. Neither approach adequately structures evidence to reveal complete answer paths.
AI.method: The authors propose HKVM-RAG, comprising: (1) A key-value-separated evidence-organization layer that assembles answer-path hyperedges from cached passage-level LLM evidence tuples (used as retrieval keys) while retaining passage text as answer values. (2) A fixed-substrate protocol that holds the tuple cache, candidate passages, reader, and evaluation budget constant across pairwise graph and hypergraph variants to isolate key-space design effects. (3) Weighted hypergraph key-value retrieval (WHG-KV). (4) A dense-aware controller that combines frozen ColBERTv2 and HKVM rank/score features using out-of-fold HKVM predictions, reaching strong F1 on all three benchmarks.
AI.result: WHG-KV retrieval improves over KG-PPR by +3.426 F1 on 2WikiMultiHopQA and +3.592 F1 on MuSiQue. The dense-aware controller reaches 88.846, 65.073, and 85.810 F1 on 2WikiMultiHopQA, MuSiQue, and HotpotQA respectively — improving over ColBERTv2 by +11.084, +6.763, and +5.966 F1. Oracle analyses identify support selection as repairable. Source-level ablations show that matched non-WHG structured signals do not match WHG-KV gains.
AI.conclusion: Key-value-separated hypergraph organization provides bounded evidence as a reusable evidence-control mechanism for multi-hop RAG, serving as a control signal that complements rather than replaces dense retrieval, with gains attributable specifically to the WHG-KV design rather than structured signals in general.

title: SkelDPO: A Skeleton-Guided Direct Preference Optimization Framework for Efficient Code Generation
summary: With the remarkable progress of Code Large Language Models (Code LLMs) in achieving semantic correctness, execution efficiency has become an increasingly important dimension for evaluating their practical utility. However, existing approaches typically treat full programs as a single optimization target during training, without explicitly modeling the structural factors that influence efficiency. As a result, although these models can generate semantically correct code, they fail to learn, at a fine-grained level, the underlying skeleton features that lead to efficient implementations. To address this limitation, we propose SkelDPO (Skeleton-Guided Direct Preference Optimization), a skeleton-guided preference optimization framework that systematically enhances the efficiency of code generation. SkelDPO first identifies efficient and inefficient implementations from the code dataset and, through comparative analysis, locates their efficiency-prone and inefficiency-prone points, forming alignment signals between efficiency and inefficiency skeletons. During training, a joint code and skeleton preference loss is introduced, enabling the model to learn semantic correctness while reinforcing its understanding of efficiency-critical components in code. Results show that SkelDPO consistently surpasses existing methods: compared with SOTA method that relies solely on efficient and inefficient code preference optimization, it improves Pass@1, Beyond@1, and Effi@1 by 3-6%, 3-7%, and 2-5%, with greater improvements observed on complex tasks. Overall, SkelDPO provides a new perspective on skeleton-level efficiency alignment, breaking the limitation of conventional preference optimization that relies solely on correctness or efficiency pairs. All datasets and source code are publicly available at: https://github.com/icpcSkelDPO/SkelDPO.

AI.tldr: SkelDPO introduces skeleton-guided direct preference optimization for code generation, explicitly modeling structural efficiency patterns (skeletons) rather than treating full programs as monolithic optimization targets. By aligning efficiency-prone and inefficiency-prone code skeletons during training, it achieves consistent improvements of 2-7% across Pass@1, Beyond@1, and Effi@1 metrics over SOTA methods.
AI.motivation: Existing Code LLMs focus on semantic correctness during training but fail to learn fine-grained structural features (skeletons) that determine execution efficiency. Programs are treated holistically, preventing models from distinguishing which specific code components drive efficient vs. inefficient implementations.
AI.method: SkelDPO operates in two phases: (1) identifying efficient/inefficient implementations from a code dataset and locating their efficiency-prone and inefficiency-prone points via comparative analysis to form skeleton-level alignment signals; (2) introducing a joint code-and-skeleton preference loss during training that simultaneously optimizes semantic correctness and reinforces understanding of efficiency-critical structural components.
AI.result: SkelDPO consistently outperforms the SOTA method relying solely on full-code preference optimization: Pass@1 improves by 3-6%, Beyond@1 by 3-7%, and Effi@1 by 2-5%, with larger gains on complex tasks.
AI.conclusion: SkelDPO offers a new paradigm for skeleton-level efficiency alignment, overcoming the limitation of conventional preference optimization that depends only on global correctness/efficiency pairs. The approach demonstrates that fine-grained structural modeling is key to improving code generation efficiency.

title: Signal-Driven Observation for Long-Horizon Web Agents
summary: Web agents operating over long horizons ingest raw DOM and accessibility trees -- routinely tens of thousands of tokens -- at every action step, causing progressive context degradation that erodes reasoning well before tasks complete. We argue that this coupling of observation frequency to action frequency is an architectural mistake. Drawing on the insight from Recursive Language Models that querying a document outperforms reading it wholesale, we propose Signal-Driven Observation (SDO): a dedicated sub-call reads the full DOM but returns only task-relevant elements and their selectors, and is re-invoked only when a lightweight signal detector fires -- triggered by URL transitions, newly visible interactive elements, action failures, or exogenous browser events. We outline the open problems SDO introduces and call on the community to treat observation compression as a core architectural decision in web agent design.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Signal-Driven Observation (SDO), a method that decouples observation frequency from action frequency in web agents by using a dedicated sub-call to extract only task-relevant DOM elements, reinvoked only upon lightweight signals (URL changes, new interactive elements, failures, browser events), preventing context degradation from ingesting full DOM trees at every step.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon web agents that ingest raw DOM and accessibility trees (tens of thousands of tokens) at every action step suffer from progressive context degradation, which erodes reasoning before tasks complete. The authors argue that coupling observation frequency to action frequency is an architectural mistake.
AI.method: Signal-Driven Observation (SDO) introduces a dedicated sub-call that reads the full DOM but returns only task-relevant elements and their selectors. This sub-call is re-invoked only when a lightweight signal detector fires, triggered by URL transitions, newly visible interactive elements, action failures, or exogenous browser events.
AI.result: The paper outlines open problems introduced by SDO rather than presenting empirical results, and issues a call to the community to treat observation compression as a core architectural decision in web agent design.
AI.conclusion: Observation compression should be treated as a first-class architectural concern in web agent design. SDO, inspired by Recursive Language Models' insight that querying outperforms wholesale reading, offers a promising direction for decoupling observation from action frequency.

title: Gaussian Process Latent Factor Regression for Low-Data, High-Dimensional Output Problems
comment: 9 pages content + 22 pages appendix/references. Supporting code at https://github.com/edstevenson/GPLFR
summary: In the sciences, regression tasks often require predicting high-dimensional outputs from few training examples. Multi-output Gaussian processes excel in low-data regimes but typically struggle with high-dimensional outputs. Compress-then-predict pipelines such as PCA-GP (principal component analysis plus Gaussian process regression) handle high dimensionality, but rely on bases optimized for reconstruction rather than prediction. To address this gap, we propose a model that represents each output as a linear-Gaussian decoding of a low-dimensional latent state drawn from a Gaussian process prior. By analytically marginalizing the decoder weights, we couple compression and prediction in a single objective that scales to high-dimensional outputs. We refer to this model as Gaussian process latent factor regression (GPLFR). We demonstrate GPLFR by building the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets.

AI.tldr: GPLFR couples compression and prediction into a single objective by representing high-dimensional outputs as linear-Gaussian decodings of a low-dimensional GP latent state, with decoder weights analytically marginalized out. The model is demonstrated via the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional regression with few samples is common in the sciences. Multi-output GPs work well in low-data regimes but struggle with high-dimensional outputs. Existing compress-then-predict approaches like PCA-GP use bases optimized for reconstruction, not prediction, creating a gap between compression and predictive performance.
AI.method: The model represents each output as a linear-Gaussian decoding of a low-dimensional latent state governed by a Gaussian process prior. Decoder weights are analytically marginalized, coupling compression and prediction into a single objective. This yields the Gaussian process latent factor regression (GPLFR) model, which scales to high-dimensional outputs.
AI.result: GPLFR successfully scales GP regression to high-dimensional outputs. The authors demonstrate its efficacy by constructing the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets.
AI.conclusion: GPLFR bridges the gap between compression and prediction in high-dimensional GP regression, enabling scalable, spatially resolved emulation for complex scientific modeling tasks such as exoplanet climate simulation.

title: Accelerated Fourier SAT (AFSAT): Fully Realising a GPU-based Symmetric Pseudo-Boolean SAT Solver
summary: We present Accelerated Fourier SAT (AFSAT), a GPU-accelerated solver for pseudo-Boolean satisfiability based on continuous local search (CLS).
