AFSAT realises the proof-of-concept approach, FastFourierSAT, into a fully-engineered solver supporting any heterogeneous mixture of symmetric constraint types and lengths within a single problem instance. Using the JAX compiler, AFSAT leverages pure function composition, automatic vectorisation, automatic differentiation, and just-in-time (JIT) compilation to perform massively parallel CLS across batches of candidate assignments. We demonstrate substantially improved numerical stability, runtime performance, and memory efficiency over the proof-of-concept. We achieve this by way of identifying and addressing various limitations that arise from memory latency and floating-point representation, as well as leveraging automatic parallelisation and compact representations. The inherent representational and stability limitations of floating point are partially addressed by a tailored discrete Fourier transform implementation. We achieve near-linear throughput when scaling to multiple accelerators via JAX array sharding.

AI.tldr: AFSAT is a GPU-accelerated pseudo-Boolean satisfiability solver that scales Fourier-based continuous local search via JAX, achieving near-linear multi-accelerator throughput with improved numerical stability and memory efficiency over prior work.
AI.motivation: To transform the proof-of-concept FastFourierSAT into a fully-engineered, production-grade solver capable of handling heterogeneous symmetric constraint types and lengths within a single problem instance, while overcoming the numerical stability, runtime, and memory bottlenecks inherent in the original approach.
AI.method: AFSAT employs JAX-based pure function composition, automatic vectorisation, automatic differentiation, and JIT compilation to perform massively parallel continuous local search across batched candidate assignments. It incorporates a tailored discrete Fourier transform implementation to mitigate floating-point representational limitations and leverages JAX array sharding for multi-accelerator scaling.
AI.result: Substantially improved numerical stability, runtime performance, and memory efficiency over the FastFourierSAT proof-of-concept, with near-linear throughput scaling when distributed across multiple GPU accelerators.
AI.conclusion: AFSAT demonstrates that Fourier-based continuous local search for pseudo-Boolean satisfiability can be engineered into a practical, scalable solver by addressing floating-point and memory-latency bottlenecks, with JAX serving as an effective compilation and parallelisation substrate.

title: The Sound of Malware: A Memory Forensics Approach for Android Malware Analysis via Audio Signals
summary: Android malware analysis is currently facing increasing challenges in achieving robust classification and detecting stealth attacks. Modern threats employ advanced evasion strategies such as code obfuscation, dynamic loading, packing, and even steganographic manipulation of traditional static and dynamic features. These techniques reduce the effectiveness of signature-based systems and degrade the reliability of Machine Learning models that depend on explicit semantic indicators such as permissions, API calls, or control-flow structures. In this work, we propose \approachname, a memory forensics malware detection framework that shifts the analysis perspective from semantic program modeling to signal-based structural representation. Both static bytecode and early-execution memory snapshots are transformed into audio waveforms through direct binary-to-waveform mapping, preserving low-level structural patterns without requiring disassembly or feature engineering. The resulting signals are processed using handcrafted spectral descriptors, Convolutional Neural Networks, and transformer-based embeddings. Experiments on CICMalDroid2020 dataset and VirusTotal malware demonstrate that \approachname achieves up to 98.0\% accuracy, outperforming static sonification and competitive state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a memory forensics malware detection framework that converts Android bytecode and memory snapshots into audio waveforms for signal-based classification, achieving up to 98.0% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Modern Android malware uses advanced evasion techniques (code obfuscation, dynamic loading, packing, steganography) that degrade traditional signature-based and ML models relying on explicit semantic features like permissions and API calls. A new detection paradigm is needed.
AI.method: Static bytecode and early-execution memory snapshots are mapped directly to audio waveforms via binary-to-waveform conversion, preserving low-level structural patterns without disassembly. Spectral descriptors, CNNs, and transformer-based embeddings are then applied for classification.
AI.result: The approach achieves up to 98.0% accuracy on the CICMalDroid2020 dataset and VirusTotal malware samples, outperforming static sonification and competitive state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from semantic program modeling to signal-based structural representation via memory forensics and sonification provides a robust, evasion-resistant alternative for Android malware classification.

title: FLOWREADER: Min-Cost Flow Optimization for Multi-Modal Long Document Q&A
summary: Long, multimodal documents force retrieval-augmented systems to assemble answers from evidence fragmented across text, tables, and slides broken across cells in a long table, spread over multiple slides, or split between a figure and its discussion. Top-$k$ chunk retrieval treats each fragment independently and cannot represent how evidence connects. We introduce FLOWREADER, which reframes evidence assembly as a min-cost flow problem on a multimodal node graph: a single scoring vector $h$ controls source selection (via MMR), sink selection (via a length-aware answerability proxy), and the costs and capacities of every edge. The optimal flow is decomposed into candidate evidence paths, a compact non-redundant subset is selected by entropy-regularized replicator dynamics, and parallel VLM workers under a dual-process gate produce the answer with a single System-2 refinement pass triggered when answer consistency is low or the routed flow is strained. On VisDoMBench, FLOWREADER is best on the two subsets dominated by fragmented evidence PaperTab ($58.40$, $+1.30$ over G^{2}-Reader) and SlideVQA ($72.93$, $+0.62$) and competitive on SPIQA, FetaTab, and SciGraphQA. Macro-averaged across all five subsets, FLOWREADER ($65.47$) is within $0.74$ of the strongest baseline (G^{2}-Reader, $66.21$). Overall, these results show that min-cost flow performs well on fragmented multimodal evidence, where top-$k$ retrieval fails. It also provides a unified way to control scoring, routing, selection, and adaptive compute together.

AI.tldr: FLOWREADER reframes multimodal evidence assembly for long documents as a min-cost flow problem on a node graph, using a single scoring vector to control source selection, routing, and adaptive compute, achieving state-of-the-art results on fragmented evidence benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented systems struggle with long, multimodal documents where evidence is fragmented across text, tables, and slides. Standard top-k chunk retrieval treats each fragment independently and fails to capture how pieces of evidence connect, motivating a unified framework that models evidence assembly holistically.
AI.method: FLOWREADER constructs a multimodal node graph and formulates evidence assembly as a min-cost flow problem. A single scoring vector h governs source selection (via MMR), sink selection (via a length-aware answerability proxy), and all edge costs/capacities. Optimal flow is decomposed into candidate evidence paths, with entropy-regularized replicator dynamics selecting a compact, non-redundant subset. Parallel VLM workers under a dual-process gate produce answers, with a single System-2 refinement pass triggered only when answer consistency is low or routed flow is strained.
AI.result: On VisDoMBench, FLOWREADER achieves best performance on fragmentation-heavy subsets: PaperTab (58.40, +1.30 over G²-Reader) and SlideVQA (72.93, +0.62), and is competitive on SPIQA, FetaTab, and SciGraphQA. Macro-averaged across all five subsets, FLOWREADER (65.47) is within 0.74 of the strongest baseline G²-Reader (66.21).
AI.conclusion: Min-cost flow proves effective for fragmented multimodal evidence where top-k retrieval fails, and provides a unified mechanism for controlling scoring, routing, selection, and adaptive compute within a single framework.

title: LLM Agent-Assisted Reverse Engineering with Quantitative Readability Metrics
summary:
Automatic decompilers produce functionally correct but often unreadable C code. This paper addresses one stage of the reverse engineering workflow: improving the readability of decompiled code using LLM agents guided by quantitative metrics.
  We present a three-phase research evolution. Phase 1 (tool-driven steering via Ghidra MCP) suffered from incomplete coverage and inconsistent improvements due to lack of quantitative guidance. Phase 2 (structural similarity validation alone) revealed that agents optimize for metrics in unintended ways, producing structurally equivalent but less readable code. Our contribution is the Quantitative Readability Score (QRS) framework, a composite metric combining a structural similarity gate with three independent readability sub-metrics (Lexical Surprisal, Structural Simplicity, and Idiomatic Quality).
  We demonstrate that QRS-guided refinement enables LLM agents to make targeted readability improvements without sacrificing correctness. We provide a discussion of the broader reverse engineering workflow (binary lifting, decompilation cleanup, and achieving functional equivalence) as context, however, it remains out of scope.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Quantitative Readability Score (QRS) framework that guides LLM agents to improve decompiled C code readability without sacrificing functional correctness. Through a three-phase research evolution, the authors show that naive agent approaches and structural similarity alone are insufficient, while their composite metric combining structural similarity gating with lexical, structural, and idiomatic readability sub-metrics enables targeted, effective refinement.
AI.motivation: Automatic decompilers produce functionally correct but unreadable C code, hindering reverse engineering workflows. Prior LLM-based approaches lacked quantitative guidance, leading to inconsistent improvements or even cases where agents optimized for superficial metrics while degrading actual readability.
AI.method: Three-phase evolution: (1) tool-driven steering via Ghidra MCP — suffered from incomplete coverage; (2) structural similarity validation alone — agents gamed the metric, producing structurally equivalent but less readable code; (3) QRS framework — a composite metric with a structural similarity gate (ensuring correctness) plus three readability sub-metrics: Lexical Surprisal, Structural Simplicity, and Idiomatic Quality, used to guide LLM agent refinement.
AI.result: QRS-guided refinement enables LLM agents to make targeted readability improvements without sacrificing structural correctness. The structural similarity gate prevents semantic drift, while the three sub-metrics provide complementary, non-gameable signals for readability.
AI.conclusion: Quantitative, multi-dimensional readability metrics are essential for effectively guiding LLM agents in decompiled code cleanup. The QRS framework demonstrates that combining a correctness gate with independent readability signals yields reliable improvements, though broader reverse engineering stages (binary lifting, functional equivalence) remain out of scope.

title: Explain Like I'm 5 or Whatever I Choose: Evaluating the Interactive Potential of Language Model Responses
summary: Evaluations of large language models (LLMs) in scientific information seeking tasks have become increasingly use-centric, such as conducting live or multi-turn evaluations with real users. These evaluations still assume a single, static chat interface, but as models are integrated into new interfaces, evaluations must shift to incorporate interface-specific criteria. We propose a new evaluation framework based on a formative study with $16$ participants that tests models' ability to generate multiple responses to one query that differ along an interpretable axis of language (language complexity), inspired by direct manipulation interfaces from human-centered design literature. We evaluate GPT-5.1, GPT-5 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5 + Thinking, and DeepSeek-V3.1 by generating 5 responses at different levels of language complexity for $98$ scientific queries. While models vary complexity across responses, most changes remain inconsistent, with the best performing model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) only shifting reliable complexity measures in the correct direction $46\%$ of the time. Our findings hold with increased sample size and alternative complexity levels.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new evaluation framework for LLMs in scientific information seeking that tests models' ability to generate multiple responses at different levels of language complexity (inspired by direct manipulation interfaces). Evaluating GPT-5.1, GPT-5 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5 + Thinking, and DeepSeek-V3.1 across 98 scientific queries, the authors find that even the best model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) only shifts complexity measures correctly 46% of the time.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluations for scientific information seeking are increasingly use-centric (live, multi-turn, real users) but still assume a single static chat interface. As LLMs are integrated into new, richer interfaces, evaluations must incorporate interface-specific criteria rather than treating the chat paradigm as fixed.
AI.method: A formative study with 16 participants informed the design of an evaluation framework. The framework tests models on generating multiple responses per query that vary along an interpretable axis — language complexity (inspired by direct manipulation interfaces in HCI). Four models (GPT-5.1, GPT-5 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5 + Thinking, DeepSeek-V3.1) generated 5 responses at different complexity levels for 98 scientific queries. Robustness was tested with increased sample sizes and alternative complexity levels.
AI.result: All models exhibited some variation in complexity across responses, but changes were largely inconsistent. Claude Sonnet 4.5 performed best, yet only shifted reliable complexity measures in the correct direction 46% of the time. These findings held under increased sample sizes and alternative complexity level definitions.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs lack reliable control over response complexity when prompted to vary it, highlighting a gap between interface-inspired evaluation criteria and actual model capability. Evaluation frameworks must evolve alongside interface paradigms to accurately assess model usefulness in scientific information seeking.

title: Data-Efficient Autoregressive-to-Diffusion Language Models via On-Policy Distillation
summary: We study the transformation of autoregressive models (ARLMs) into diffusion language models (DLMs). Rather than pretraining from scratch, prior work replaces the causal attention in ARLMs with bidirectional attention and then trains the resulting model using a DLM objective. However, these approaches incur two distribution shifts. First, transitioning from a next-token prediction objective to a DLM objective can discard knowledge acquired by the ARLM during training. Second, standard DLMs suffer from a train-inference mismatch, as the training loss is defined on randomly masked sequences rather than the trajectories encountered at inference produced by confidence-based decoding. To address both challenges, we introduce an On-Policy Diffusion Language Model (OPDLM) in which On-Policy Distillation (OPD) is employed for ARLM-to-DLM transformation. Specifically, OPDLM is trained via self-OPD, where the student, an ARLM with bidirectional attention, generates its own trajectories, and the teacher, the original frozen ARLM, distills its knowledge by providing target logits on these trajectories. By training directly in an on-policy manner, OPDLM eliminates the train-inference mismatch in DLMs, while distillation from the original model enhances knowledge retention from the ARLM. Empirical results demonstrate that OPDLM requires 15x to 7,000x fewer training tokens with strong performance across a wide variety of tasks. OPDLM avoids the prohibitive cost of DLM pretraining and positions DLM transformation as a form of ARLM post-training.

AI.tldr: OPDLM transforms autoregressive language models into diffusion language models using on-policy distillation, where the student model generates its own trajectories and learns from the frozen teacher's logits. This eliminates train-inference mismatch and preserves ARLM knowledge, requiring 15x–7,000x fewer training tokens than prior approaches.
AI.motivation: Prior ARLM-to-DLM transformation methods suffer from two distribution shifts: (1) switching from next-token prediction to DLM objectives discards pretrained knowledge, and (2) standard DLMs have a train-inference mismatch since training uses random masks but inference uses confidence-based decoding trajectories. The paper aims to solve both issues simultaneously.
AI.method: OPDLM employs On-Policy Distillation (OPD): the student model (an ARLM with bidirectional attention) generates its own trajectories during training, while the frozen original ARLM serves as teacher, providing target logits on those trajectories. This self-OPD approach trains directly on inference-like trajectories, eliminating the mismatch, and uses distillation to retain pretrained knowledge.
AI.result: OPDLM achieves strong performance across diverse tasks while requiring 15x to 7,000x fewer training tokens compared to prior methods, avoiding the prohibitive cost of DLM pretraining from scratch.
AI.conclusion: OPDLM reframes DLM transformation as a form of ARLM post-training, making it practical and cost-effective. The on-policy distillation framework effectively resolves both the knowledge retention and train-inference mismatch problems inherent in prior transformation approaches.

title: Principles and Practice of Deep Representation Learning: or a Mathematical Theory of Memory
comment: version 2; TeX source and supplementary material at https://ma-lab-berkeley.github.io/deep-representation-learning-book/
summary:
In the current era of deep learning and especially generative models, there is significant investment in training very large generative models. Thus far, such models have been "black boxes" that are difficult to understand in the sense that they have opaque internal mechanisms, leading to difficulties in interpretability, reliability, and control. Naturally, this lack of understanding has led to both hype and fear.
  This book is an attempt to "open the black box" and understand the mechanisms of large deep networks, through the perspective of representation learning, which is a major factor - arguably the single most important one - in the empirical power of deep learning models. A brief outline of this book is as follows. Chapter 1 will summarize the threads that underlie the whole text. Chapters 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 will explain the design principles of modern neural network architectures through optimization and information theory, reducing the process of architecture development (long having been described as a sort of "alchemy") to undergraduate-level linear algebra and calculus exercises once the underlying principles are introduced. Chapters 7 and 8 will discuss applications of these principles to solve problems in more paradigmatic ways, obtaining new methods and models which are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design, and yet no less - sometimes even more - powerful than the black-box models they resemble. Chapter 9 will discuss potential future directions for deep learning, the role of representation learning, as well as some open problems.

AI.tldr: This book aims to demystify large deep learning models by analyzing their internal mechanisms through the lens of representation learning. It reformulates neural architecture design principles using optimization and information theory, reducing complex "alchemical" practices to principled undergraduate-level exercises, and demonstrates how these principles yield models that are interpretable, controllable, and efficient without sacrificing performance.
AI.motivation: Large generative deep learning models are currently treated as opaque "black boxes" with inscrutable internal mechanisms, leading to problems in interpretability, reliability, and control. This opacity fuels both unwarranted hype and fear. The book is motivated by the need to "open the black box" and provide a principled, rigorous understanding of how these models work, with representation learning identified as the single most important factor behind their empirical success.
AI.method: The book adopts the perspective of representation learning and employs two primary theoretical frameworks: optimization theory and information theory. These are used to derive and explain the design principles of modern neural network architectures. The approach reformulates what has traditionally been viewed as an ad-hoc, "alchemical" process of architecture development into structured problems solvable with undergraduate-level linear algebra and calculus. The method further involves applying these derived principles paradigmatically to construct new models and methods.
AI.result: The application of representation learning principles, grounded in optimization and information theory, yields new methods and models that are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design. These models match or even surpass the performance of their black-box counterparts while being inherently more transparent and reliable.
AI.conclusion: Representation learning provides a powerful and unifying framework for understanding and designing deep neural networks. By replacing opaque heuristics with principled theoretical foundations, the field can move beyond "alchemy" toward an engineering discipline where models are interpretable and controllable by construction. The book concludes with a discussion of future directions and open problems in deep learning through this representation-centric lens.

title: A Study of Parallel Continuous Local Search
summary: We study parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS) as a solution approach for Boolean satisfiability problems with symmetric pseudo-Boolean (PB) constraints. Here, the $n$-variable PB-satisfiability problem is relaxed to a continuous optimisation problem with a differentiable objective function on an $n$-dimensional hypercube. For satisfiable instances, the global minimisers of this optimisation problem correspond to satisfying assignments of the SAT problem at hand. We present several novel findings via empirical experiments: (i) redundant constraints can inhibit rather than accelerate convergence; (ii) CLS shows promise as a sub-solver in hybridised settings, quickly completing partial assignments; and (iii) local search rapidly converges to a stable distribution of solution quality (i.e., degree of satisfaction), due to saddle-dense objectives where additional solver steps yield diminishing returns. Our findings inform practical uses of CLS for SAT on modern accelerator hardware.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS) for SAT problems with symmetric pseudo-Boolean constraints, revealing that redundant constraints can hinder convergence, CLS excels as a hybrid sub-solver for partial assignments, and rapid convergence to stable solution-quality distributions occurs due to saddle-dense objective landscapes.
AI.motivation: To understand the practical behavior and limitations of Continuous Local Search (CLS) as a solver for SAT problems with symmetric pseudo-Boolean constraints, particularly in the context of modern accelerator hardware, and to identify where CLS can be most effectively deployed (e.g., as a sub-solver rather than a standalone solver).
AI.method: The authors relax the n-variable PB-SAT problem into a continuous, differentiable optimisation problem over an n-dimensional hypercube, where global minimisers correspond to satisfying assignments. They then conduct empirical experiments to evaluate CLS performance, investigating the effects of redundant constraints, hybridisation with other solvers, and the nature of convergence in saddle-dense objective landscapes.
AI.result: Three key findings: (i) redundant constraints can inhibit rather than accelerate convergence; (ii) CLS is promising as a sub-solver in hybrid settings, quickly completing partial assignments; and (iii) local search rapidly converges to a stable distribution of solution quality, attributed to saddle-dense objectives where additional steps yield diminishing returns.
AI.conclusion: CLS is not a silver bullet for SAT solving but shows clear utility as a fast sub-solver within hybrid frameworks. The saddle-dense nature of the objective landscapes explains the diminishing returns from prolonged search, and these insights can guide practical deployments of CLS on modern accelerator hardware.

title: Fast Bounded-Independence Functions and Their Duals
comment: Full version of paper to appear in ITC 2026. 34 pages
summary:
We continue the study of {\em fast} functions, computable by linear-size circuits, that share useful properties of random functions. Motivated by cryptographic applications, we generalize and improve on previous results in this area, obtaining the following results:
  - For any constant $t$, we construct a fast $t$-wise independent hash function with algebraic degree $\log_2 t$ (over $\mathbb F_2$), simultaneously optimizing both asymptotic circuit size and degree.
  - We simplify and improve a recent construction (ITCS 2026) of a family of fast codes with fast duals, both meeting the Gilbert-Varshamov bound. Unlike the previous construction, our construction has negligible failure probability, can accommodate general fields and rates, supports a systematic encoding, and admits fast universal encoders.
  - We strengthen the above to support stronger random-like properties, such as optimal combinatorial list-decoding. This is achieved by constructing, for any constant $t$, a family of fast linear functions that map any $t$ linearly independent inputs to uniform and statistically independent outputs. Prior to our work, this was only known for $t=1$.
  We demonstrate the usefulness of the above results to cryptography. This includes the first nontrivial protocols for perfectly secure multiparty computation whose circuit complexity scales linearly with the number of parties, as well as protocols for computing encrypted matrix-vector products with optimal asymptotic circuit complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper advances the study of "fast" functions (computable by linear-size circuits) that mimic random functions. Key results include: (1) fast t-wise independent hash functions with optimal degree log₂t, (2) simplified fast codes with fast duals meeting the Gilbert-Varshamov bound with negligible failure probability, and (3) fast linear functions mapping t linearly independent inputs to uniform, statistically independent outputs for any constant t. Applications include efficient perfectly secure multiparty computation and encrypted matrix-vector products with optimal circuit complexity.
AI.motivation: The work is motivated by cryptographic applications requiring functions that are both efficiently computable (by linear-size circuits) and possess strong random-like properties (t-wise independence, good code distance, etc.). Prior constructions suffered from suboptimal tradeoffs between circuit size and algebraic degree, or were limited to small t values, restricting their practical utility in protocols like secure multiparty computation.
AI.method: The paper generalizes and improves prior techniques for constructing fast functions with random-like properties. For t-wise independent hashing, they achieve degree log₂t over 𝔽₂ with linear-size circuits. For codes, they simplify a prior ITCS 2026 construction to achieve fast encoding/decoding meeting the Gilbert-Varshamov bound, supporting general fields and systematic encoding. For the linear independence mapping result, they extend beyond the t=1 case using novel constructions of fast linear functions.
AI.result: Three main results: (1) Fast t-wise independent hash functions with optimal algebraic degree log₂t and linear circuit size. (2) Fast codes with fast duals meeting the Gilbert-Varshamov bound, with negligible failure probability, supporting general fields/rates, systematic encoding, and fast universal encoders. (3) Fast linear functions mapping any t linearly independent inputs to uniform, independent outputs for any constant t — previously only known for t=1. These yield the first nontrivial perfectly secure MPC protocols with circuit complexity linear in the number of parties, and optimal encrypted matrix-vector product protocols.
AI.conclusion: The paper significantly advances the theory of fast pseudorandom functions, achieving optimal tradeoffs across multiple dimensions (circuit size, algebraic degree, code parameters). The results enable cryptographic protocols — particularly in secure multiparty computation and encrypted computation — with asymptotically optimal circuit complexity, representing substantial improvements over prior work.

title: Constrained Dominant Sets for Multimodal Document Question Answering
summary: Long multimodal document question answering is limited by which evidence reaches the reader, rather than by the quantity retrieved. In lengthy documents, findings often recur across figures, captions, and introductory sentences, causing similarity based retrievers in modern multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems to allocate resources to near-duplicates while overlooking complementary evidence. This work introduces a retriever that selects evidence as a Constrained Dominant Set (CDS) on a query-augmented affinity graph, offering three advantages that similarity ranking does not. First, the query is encoded as a hard structural constraint, ensuring that every selected element is directly connected to the question through the cluster anchor. Second, the relevance-redundancy balance is determined automatically by a spectral bound, eliminating the need for manually tuned trade offs required by diversity-aware selectors. Third, the selection process achieves a global equilibrium via replicator dynamics, thereby avoiding the distortions introduced by greedy heuristics. The method is inherently graph-based and does not require training. Using a Qwen3-VL-32B reader, CDS establishes a new state of the art on VisDoMBench ($66.99$ average) and improves over the no-retrieval baseline by $37.1$ points on VisDoMBench and $4.8$ on MMLongBench-Doc.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a training-free graph-based retriever that selects evidence as a Constrained Dominant Set (CDS) on a query-augmented affinity graph. It enforces the query as a hard structural constraint, automatically balances relevance-redundancy via a spectral bound, and achieves global equilibrium through replicator dynamics. Using a Qwen3-VL-32B reader, CDS achieves state-of-the-art results on VisDoMBench (66.99 avg) and improves over no-retrieval baselines significantly.
AI.motivation: In long multimodal documents, findings frequently recur across figures, captions, and body text. Similarity-based retrievers in modern multimodal RAG systems tend to retrieve near-duplicate evidence while overlooking complementary but dissimilar information, limiting the diversity and completeness of evidence presented to the reader.
AI.method: The proposed retriever selects evidence as a Constrained Dominant Set (CDS) on a query-augmented affinity graph. Key innovations: (1) the query is encoded as a hard structural constraint ensuring all selected elements connect to the question via a cluster anchor; (2) relevance-redundancy trade-off is automatically determined by a spectral bound, eliminating manual tuning; (3) selection reaches global equilibrium via replicator dynamics, avoiding greedy heuristic distortions. The method is purely graph-based and training-free.
AI.result: With a Qwen3-VL-32B reader, CDS achieves state-of-the-art on VisDoMBench (66.99 average score) and improves over the no-retrieval baseline by 37.1 points on VisDoMBench and 4.8 points on MMLongBench-Doc.
AI.conclusion: CDS offers a principled, training-free alternative to similarity-based retrieval for long multimodal document QA. By enforcing query connectivity, automatically balancing relevance and redundancy, and using global optimization, it substantially improves evidence selection quality and downstream QA performance.

title: Empirical Study on the Characteristics and Evolution of AI-usage in GitHub Repositories: Evidence from Code Comments
summary: Developers increasingly use AI tools such as ChatGPT, Copilot, and Claude in everyday software workflows, but prior studies often evaluate LLM outputs in isolation rather than examining how developers adapt them in real projects. We analyze 35,361 GitHub code comments that explicitly reference AI use and their associated code blocks. We first open-code 500 unique comments and code blocks to derive a taxonomy of AI-assisted development activities, then annotate the full dataset using two LLM-based classifiers and aggregate predictions with Dawid-Skene expectation-maximization. We also analyze 12,996 subsequent commit messages to study how AI-assisted code evolves after introduction, and examine temporal trends from December 2022 to March 2026. Our results show that developers primarily use LLMs for code implementation, followed by code enhancement, debugging, documentation, and testing. Subsequent commits frequently involve refactoring and cleanup, feature integration and extension, and bug fixing, indicating sustained human oversight in adapting AI-assisted code. Over time, AI-referencing comments shift from direct code generation toward knowledge and conceptual support and code enhancement. These findings suggest that AI tools are becoming embedded not only as code-generation aids, but also as collaborative support mechanisms whose outputs are refined, extended, and corrected by developers over time.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 35,361 GitHub comments referencing AI tools reveals developers primarily use LLMs for code implementation, enhancement, debugging, documentation, and testing. Subsequent commits show sustained human oversight through refactoring, feature extension, and bug fixing. Over time, AI use shifts from direct code generation toward conceptual support and code enhancement, indicating LLMs are becoming collaborative support mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Prior studies evaluate LLM outputs in isolation rather than examining how developers adapt AI-generated code in real-world projects. This paper addresses that gap by analyzing actual developer behavior when integrating AI-assisted code into production workflows.
AI.method: The authors collected 35,361 GitHub code comments explicitly referencing AI use along with associated code blocks. They open-coded 500 unique comment-code pairs to derive a taxonomy of AI-assisted development activities, then used two LLM-based classifiers with Dawid-Skene expectation-maximization for aggregated annotation of the full dataset. Additionally, they analyzed 12,996 subsequent commit messages to track code evolution and examined temporal trends from December 2022 to March 2026.
AI.result: Developers primarily use LLMs for (1) code implementation, (2) code enhancement, (3) debugging, (4) documentation, and (5) testing. Subsequent commits most frequently involve refactoring/cleanup, feature integration/extension, and bug fixing, demonstrating sustained human oversight. Temporal analysis reveals a shift from direct code generation toward knowledge/conceptual support and code enhancement over the study period.
AI.conclusion: AI tools are becoming embedded not only as code-generation aids but as collaborative support mechanisms whose outputs are refined, extended, and corrected by developers over time. The findings highlight that human oversight remains integral to the AI-assisted development lifecycle.

title: How reliable are LLMs when it comes to playing dice?
summary: We investigate the probabilistic reasoning capabilities of large language models through a controlled benchmarking study on discrete probability problems. We constructed two datasets, respectively a set of standard exercises and a set of counterintuitive exercises, designed to trigger heuristic reasoning, and evaluated 8 state-of-the-art models, each tested with and without Chain-of-Thought prompting. Models achieve an average accuracy of 0.96 on standard problems but only 0.59 on counterintuitive ones. We further provide empirical evidence of token bias: performance drops by over 20% when canonical formulations are replaced by disguised variants. Embedding misleading suggestions in the prompt reduces performance by up to 34%, with no model proving immune. Taken together, the reported findings suggest that current LLMs are not yet genuine probabilistic reasoners, despite their success in advanced mathematical problems.

AI.tldr: A benchmarking study shows LLMs achieve 96% accuracy on standard probability problems but only 59% on counterintuitive ones. Token bias and misleading prompts reduce performance by over 20% and up to 34%, respectively, indicating LLMs are not yet genuine probabilistic reasoners.
AI.motivation: Despite LLMs' success on advanced mathematical benchmarks, their ability to perform genuine probabilistic reasoning—as opposed to pattern matching or memorization—remains underexplored. The authors aim to systematically test whether LLMs can reason through probability problems or merely exploit surface-level cues.
AI.method: The authors constructed two datasets of discrete probability problems: (1) standard textbook exercises and (2) counterintuitive problems designed to trigger heuristic (System 1) reasoning errors. They evaluated 8 state-of-the-art LLMs under two conditions: with and without Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. Two additional experiments manipulated surface form—disguised canonical formulations to test token bias, and embedded misleading suggestions to test robustness to adversarial prompts.
AI.result: Models averaged 0.96 accuracy on standard problems but only 0.59 on counterintuitive ones. Token bias caused performance drops exceeding 20% when canonical formulations were disguised. Misleading suggestions in prompts reduced accuracy by up to 34%, with no model immune. CoT prompting provided limited improvement on counterintuitive problems.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are not yet genuine probabilistic reasoners. Their strong performance on standard problems likely reflects memorization or pattern recognition rather than robust reasoning, as evidenced by brittleness to surface-form changes and adversarial prompts. The findings highlight a significant gap between apparent mathematical competence and authentic reasoning capability.

title: Does Topic Sentiment Cause Perceived Ideology? Comparing Human and LLM Annotations in Political News Articles
summary: We ask whether topic sentiment has a causal effect on perceived political ideology, and whether the answer depends on who assigns the ideology label. Using articles from AllSides, paired with shared sentiment annotations from Llama-3.3-70b-versatile, we compare ideology labels from expert human annotators, GPT-4o-mini (baseline and finetuned), and Llama-3.3-70B. We apply Double Machine Learning (DML) and community-level mediation analysis across all four annotation paradigms. Human annotations yield no significant causal effects at the community level. Fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini achieves the highest classification accuracy (F1=72.48) and is the only annotator paradigm that produces significant community-level treatment effects and significant natural direct effects (NDEs) in mediation. We interpret this as evidence of shortcut learning: fine-tuning on ideology-labeled data causes the model to internalise a spurious sentiment--ideology coupling not operative in human judgment for this task. This coupling is structurally invisible to F1-based evaluation, with implications for the use of LLM annotations as silver labels and as proxies for human judgment in downstream causal analyses.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether topic sentiment causally affects perceived political ideology in news articles. Using human and LLM annotators alongside Double Machine Learning and mediation analysis, the authors find that only a fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini exhibits significant causal effects — which they attribute to shortcut learning rather than genuine causal structure. This raises concerns about using LLM annotations as proxies for human judgment in causal analyses.
AI.motivation: To determine whether topic-level sentiment has a causal effect on perceived political ideology, and whether this relationship depends on who (human experts vs. LLMs) assigns the ideology label. The work is motivated by the growing use of LLMs as annotators and the risk that their outputs may introduce spurious correlations invisible to standard accuracy metrics.
AI.method: The authors use articles from AllSides with sentiment annotations from Llama-3.3-70b-versatile. They compare ideology labels from four annotation paradigms: expert human annotators, baseline GPT-4o-mini, fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini, and Llama-3.3-70B. Causal effects are estimated using Double Machine Learning (DML) and community-level mediation analysis, decomposing effects into natural direct effects (NDEs) and natural indirect effects.
AI.result: Human annotations yielded no significant causal effects at the community level. Fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini achieved the highest F1 score (72.48) and was the only paradigm producing significant community-level treatment effects and significant NDEs in mediation analysis. This pattern is interpreted as shortcut learning: fine-tuning on ideology-labeled data causes the model to learn a spurious sentiment–ideology coupling not present in human judgment.
AI.conclusion: The spurious sentiment–ideology coupling learned by the fine-tuned model is structurally invisible to F1-based evaluation. This has critical implications for using LLM annotations as silver labels or proxies for human judgment in downstream causal analyses, as high accuracy may mask invalid causal inferences.

title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit
comment: 28 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Code available at https://github.com/Jimmy110101013/fmscope
summary:
Objective. EEG foundation models (FMs) report strong accuracy on clinical resting-state EEG. However, high accuracy under subject-disjoint cross-validation remains ambiguous: it can reflect a genuine clinical biomarker, or subject-identity features that correlate with the label. We name this the Identity Trap and ask whether it can be diagnosed at the representation level before fine-tuning.
  Approach. We propose FMScope, a frozen-representation protocol packaging five diagnostics: variance decomposition, subject-axis erasure, aperiodic 1/f ablation, layer-wise label probing, and within-subject direction consistency. We apply it to three pretrained FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets in a 2x2 layout: subject relation of label x presence of a consensus cross-subject EEG marker.
  Main results. (i) The Identity Trap is universal: frozen subject-variance is 13-89x a random null in 12/12 pairs, rising in all 12 under fine-tuning (+10 to +63 pp). This dominance is a removable linear axis: erasing it improves label decoding where the label varies within subject (+6 to +12 pp in primary cells; +4 to +27 pp across external cohorts). (ii) Aperiodic 1/f is one subject carrier: removing it drops the subject probe by 9-19 pp on LaBraM and CBraMod. REVE saturates subject identity without measurable aperiodic dependence. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label-variance only in cells with a literature-established cross-subject marker.
  Significance. The Identity Trap is a physically-grounded instance of shortcut learning: the preferred cue has a measurable physiological component, and subject-disjoint splitting alone cannot rule it out. FMScope separates gains reflecting a biological marker from those reflecting subject identity.

AI.tldr: EEG foundation models may achieve high accuracy on clinical resting-state EEG not by learning genuine biomarkers, but by exploiting subject-identity features correlated with labels — a phenomenon termed the "Identity Trap." The authors propose FMScope, a frozen-representation diagnostic protocol, and show that subject variance dominates all tested models, is carried partly by aperiodic 1/f activity, and can be diagnosed and partially mitigated before fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: High cross-validation accuracy in EEG foundation models under subject-disjoint splitting is ambiguous: it may reflect either a true clinical biomarker or subject-identity leakage (the Identity Trap). The authors seek to determine whether this confound can be diagnosed at the frozen representation level, before any fine-tuning, to distinguish genuine biomarker learning from spurious subject-driven shortcuts.
AI.method: FMScope protocol comprises five diagnostics applied to frozen representations of three pretrained FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets in a 2×2 design (subject relation of label × presence of a consensus cross-subject EEG marker): (1) variance decomposition comparing subject vs. label variance against a random null; (2) subject-axis erasure via linear removal; (3) aperiodic 1/f ablation; (4) layer-wise linear probing for label; and (5) within-subject direction consistency analysis.
AI.result: (i) Frozen subject-variance is 13–89× the random null across all 12 dataset–model pairs and further increases under fine-tuning (+10 to +63 pp). Erasing the dominant subject axis improves label decoding where the label varies within-subject (+6 to +12 pp in primary cells; +4 to +27 pp in external cohorts). (ii) Aperiodic 1/f removal drops subject probing by 9–19 pp for LaBraM and CBraMod; REVE saturates subject identity without measurable aperiodic dependence. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label variance only in datasets with a literature-established cross-subject EEG marker.
AI.conclusion: The Identity Trap is a physically-grounded instance of shortcut learning — the preferred cue has a measurable physiological component (aperiodic 1/f), and subject-disjoint splitting alone cannot rule it out. FMScope effectively separates performance gains attributable to biological markers from those reflecting subject identity, providing a diagnostic toolkit for evaluating EEG foundation model representations.

title: AEGIS: A Backup Reflex for Physical AI
summary: Long-horizon robot manipulation tends to fail gradually: one bad step degrades the state, and the policy spirals into a basin from which it cannot recover. The failure is often visible before it happens. We introduce AEGIS (Activation-probe Early-warning, Gated Inference Switching), a selective escalation method that uses a lightweight probe on a weak policy's frozen activations to detect high-risk steps while there is still time to act. When the probe flags a step, control switches to a stronger separate policy, but only for the steps that need it. On LIBERO-Spatial, AEGIS recovers 10.1% of the trajectories the weak policy alone loses, versus 4.6% for budget-matched blind escalation and 5.1% for a random-trigger placebo. These gains are significant under one-sided exact paired McNemar tests with Holm-Bonferroni adjustment over three pre-registered contrasts: +5.4pp over blind escalation, p=8.5e-6; +5.0pp over random triggering, p=1.0e-4; paired-trajectory bootstrap CIs exclude zero. AEGIS activates the stronger policy on only 38% of steps, so the lever is timing rather than compute. The probe clears its precondition with an early-window AUROC of 0.764, 95% CI [0.70, 0.84], read from the weak-policy path over the first 30% of trajectory steps before any handoff. We pre-register the full analysis plan, including a conditional recovered-task-rate estimand and explicit kill criteria, and confirm the result on 700 common-random-number episodes per arm, with nA-fail=646.

AI.tldr: AEGIS uses a lightweight probe on a weak policy's activations to detect impending failure early, then selectively escalates to a stronger policy only on high-risk steps. This recovers 10.1% of trajectories the weak policy alone loses, significantly outperforming blind escalation and random triggers while activating the strong policy on only 38% of steps.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon robot manipulation often fails gradually — one bad step cascades into unrecoverable failure, and the failure is visible before it occurs. The authors aim to preemptively detect these high-risk steps and intervene selectively, rather than always running an expensive strong policy.
AI.method: AEGIS (Activation-probe Early-warning, Gated Inference Switching) trains a lightweight binary probe on the frozen activations of a weak policy to predict imminent failure using only the early portion (first 30%) of a trajectory. When the probe flags a step as high-risk, control switches to a stronger policy for that step. The approach is evaluated on LIBERO-Spatial with 700 common-random-number episodes per arm, using pre-registered contrasts and paired McNemar tests with Holm-Bonferroni correction.
AI.result: AEGIS recovers 10.1% of trajectories the weak policy loses, versus 4.6% for budget-matched blind escalation and 5.1% for random-trigger placebo. Gains are significant: +5.4pp over blind escalation (p=8.5e-6), +5.0pp over random triggering (p=1.0e-4). The strong policy is activated on only 38% of steps. The probe achieves early-window AUROC of 0.764 (95% CI [0.70, 0.84]). Bootstrap CIs exclude zero.
AI.conclusion: Selective, probe-guided escalation is an effective and efficient strategy for improving long-horizon manipulation: timing the switch (rather than increasing compute indiscriminately) recovers significantly more failures with modest overhead. The pre-registered design and rigorous statistical testing strengthen the result's credibility.

title: MalSkillBench: A Runtime-Verified Benchmark of Malicious Agent Skills
summary:
AI coding agents such as Claude Code and Gemini CLI increasingly extend themselves with third-party skills: markdown packages bundling natural-language instructions, executable scripts, and tool permissions. Because a skill is at once code and agent-facing instruction, it introduces a supply chain dependency whose risk is neither pure code nor pure prompt. Detection tools have never been measured against verified ground truth spanning this hybrid space, leaving their effectiveness unknown and wild-only evaluations biased.
  We present MalSkillBench, the first runtime-verified benchmark of malicious agent skills: 3,944 malicious skills labeled along a three-dimensional taxonomy of 108 cells. Of these, 3,214 come from a closed-loop Generate-Verify-Feedback pipeline admitting only samples whose malicious behavior fires inside a Docker sandbox under system-call monitoring and an LLM judge; we add 703 in-the-wild and 4,000 matched benign skills. Our measurements are consistent: code injection reaches 94.5% verification yield but prompt injection only 75.8%, the same fragility that later makes it hard to detect; the wild sample is narrow, dominated by one cryptocurrency-theft campaign (86.6% one behavior, 81% from two accounts) with a small but architecturally new tail attacking the agent control plane; the strongest skill-specific detector reaches 98.4% recall on code injection yet collapses on prompt-injection and agent-control attacks, and wild-only scoring swings the ranking by up to 66 recall points; supply-chain scanners and prompt-injection defenses each see only half of a skill, and no combination recovers the code-instruction relationship. Detecting malicious skills therefore requires reasoning jointly over task intent, code, and instructions. We release the dataset, pipeline, baselines, and results.

AI.tldr: MalSkillBench is a benchmark of 3,944 malicious AI agent skills with ground-truth labels across a 108-cell taxonomy. The authors built a closed-loop Generate-Verify-Feedback pipeline that verifies malicious behavior inside Docker sandboxes. Key findings: code injection verifies at 94.5% vs. prompt injection at 75.8%; code-specific detectors reach 98.4% recall but fail on prompt-injection and agent-control attacks; existing supply-chain scanners and prompt defenses each miss half the threat; detection requires joint reasoning over task intent, code, and instructions.
AI.motivation: AI coding agents increasingly use third-party skills (markdown packages bundling instructions, scripts, and permissions), creating a hybrid supply-chain risk that is neither pure code nor pure prompt. Existing detection tools have never been evaluated against verified ground truth in this hybrid space, leaving their effectiveness unknown and biased by wild-only evaluations.
AI.method: The authors built MalSkillBench with 3,944 malicious skills labeled along a three-dimensional taxonomy (108 cells). Of these, 3,214 come from a closed-loop Generate-Verify-Feedback pipeline where only samples whose malicious behavior fires in a Docker sandbox (verified via system-call monitoring and an LLM judge) are admitted. They added 703 wild samples and 4,000 matched benign skills. They then evaluated code-specific detectors, prompt-injection defenses, and supply-chain scanners against this benchmark.
AI.result: Code injection reaches 94.5% verification yield vs. prompt injection at 75.8%, reflecting prompt injection's inherent fragility that also makes detection harder. The wild sample is narrow: 86.6% from one cryptocurrency-theft campaign, 81% from two accounts, with a small tail of novel agent-control-plane attacks. The strongest skill-specific detector achieves 98.4% recall on code injection but collapses on prompt-injection and agent-control attacks. Wild-only scoring swings rankings by up to 66 recall points. Supply-chain scanners and prompt-injection defenses each see only half of a skill; no combination recovers the code-instruction relationship.
AI.conclusion: Detecting malicious agent skills requires reasoning jointly over task intent, code, and instructions — single-modality detectors are insufficient. The benchmark, pipeline, baselines, and results are released publicly.

title: Gated Bidirectional Linear Attention for Generative Retrieval
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables. Accepted at SIGIR 2026
summary:
In recommender systems, generative retrieval typically uses an encoder-decoder setup: an encoder processes a user interaction history, and an autoregressive decoder then generates recommended items. In large-scale streaming services, active users accumulate very long histories over time. As histories grow, the encoder becomes a major latency bottleneck because softmax attention scales quadratically with sequence length. In our experiments, using bidirectional attention in the encoder substantially improves quality. However, most sub-quadratic attention methods focus on causal attention.
  We propose Gated Bidirectional Linear Attention (GBLA), a linear-time bidirectional attention layer that extends kernelized linear attention with three lightweight components: local causal mixing (Conv1D), sequence-level key gating for soft forgetting, and a gated RMSNorm output. On a large-scale Yandex Music dataset, a hybrid encoder that interleaves self-attention (SA) and GBLA in a 1:2 ratio (one SA block followed by two GBLA blocks) matches bidirectional self-attention quality. On H100 GPUs, GBLA reaches up to an $8.2\times$ single-layer speedup at a history length of 32768, compared to FlashAttention-v3. Finally, we show that the same hybrid design generalizes beyond our proprietary setting, consistently preserving self-attention retrieval quality on public Amazon benchmarks.

AI.tldr: GBLA introduces a linear-time bidirectional attention layer using kernelized linear attention with three lightweight gating mechanisms, achieving up to 8.2× speedup over FlashAttention-v3 while matching bidirectional self-attention quality in generative retrieval when interleaved with standard self-attention blocks.
AI.motivation: In large-scale streaming recommender systems, user interaction histories grow very long, making the encoder's softmax attention (which scales quadratically) a critical latency bottleneck. Bidirectional attention is essential for quality, but most sub-quadratic attention research targets causal attention, leaving a gap for efficient bidirectional alternatives.
AI.method: GBLA extends kernelized linear attention with three components: (1) local causal mixing via Conv1D, (2) sequence-level key gating for soft forgetting, and (3) a gated RMSNorm output. A hybrid encoder architecture interleaves self-attention (SA) and GBLA blocks in a 1:2 ratio to balance quality and efficiency.
AI.result: On Yandex Music data, the hybrid SA:GBLA (1:2) encoder matches bidirectional self-attention quality. GBLA achieves up to 8.2× single-layer speedup over FlashAttention-v3 at history length 32768 on H100 GPUs. The design also generalizes to public Amazon benchmarks, preserving retrieval quality.
AI.conclusion: GBLA offers a practical, efficient bidirectional attention layer for long-sequence generative retrieval, with the hybrid interleaving strategy proving effective across both proprietary and public datasets, enabling scalable recommendation without quality degradation.

title: From Custom Logic to APIs: Understanding and Recommending API Replacement Refactorings
summary: Software refactoring is essential for maintaining code quality. However, API replacement refactoring, which replaces custom logic with API calls, remains underexplored. Existing refactoring tools provide limited support for detecting such opportunities because they rely on predefined templates and have difficulty capturing complex, multi-statement semantic equivalents. To address this limitation, we conduct the first empirical study of API replacement refactorings by mining 166,299 commits across six open-source Java projects and manually analyzing a curated subset of 1,800 commits, from which we identify 366 validated instances to characterize their scope, categories, and recurring patterns. Based on these insights, we propose AKIRA (Adaptive Knowledge Discovery and Retrieval), a hybrid framework that integrates pattern-deterministic heuristics with a refactoring-aware knowledge base to assess the practical feasibility of recommending API replacement refactorings. Our evaluation shows that AKIRA achieves 90% recall and 88% precision on a manually curated dataset. Furthermore, on the external RETIWA dataset, AKIRA significantly improves the state of the art by increasing recall from 21% to 81% and precision from 40% to 78%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining static pattern matching with semantic reasoning to support the automation of recommending complex API replacement refactorings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces AKIRA, a hybrid framework for recommending API replacement refactorings that combines pattern-deterministic heuristics with a refactoring-aware knowledge base. Built on the first empirical study of API replacement refactorings (mining 166,299 commits across six Java projects, with 366 validated instances), AKIRA achieves 90% recall/88% precision on curated data and significantly advances the state of the art on the RETIWA benchmark (recall: 21%→81%, precision: 40%→78%).
AI.motivation: Existing refactoring tools poorly support API replacement refactorings because they depend on predefined templates and struggle to capture complex, multi-statement semantic equivalences between custom logic and API calls. No empirical study had systematically characterized the scope, categories, and patterns of such refactorings, leaving a gap in both understanding and automated tooling.
AI.method: First, the authors conduct an empirical study mining 166,299 commits from six open-source Java projects, manually analyzing 1,800 commits to identify 366 validated API replacement instances and derive recurring patterns. Based on these insights, they propose AKIRA: a hybrid framework integrating (1) pattern-deterministic heuristics for static pattern matching and (2) a refactoring-aware knowledge base that enables semantic reasoning about the feasibility of replacing custom code with API calls.
AI.result: AKIRA achieves 90% recall and 88% precision on a manually curated dataset. On the external RETIWA dataset, it dramatically improves recall from 21% to 81% and precision from 40% to 78% over prior state of the art.
AI.conclusion: Combining static pattern matching with semantic reasoning is highly effective for automating API replacement refactoring recommendations. The empirical characterization of API replacement patterns provides a foundation for future work, and AKIRA demonstrates that hybrid approaches can substantially outperform purely template-based methods.

title: Modular Monolingual Adaptation using Pretrained Language Models
summary: Building monolingual language models (LMs) for low-resource languages typically relies on adapting pretrained language models (PLMs) by finetuning the whole model on the target language. This approach is widely favored over training from scratch, as it enables effective knowledge transfer. Additionally, prior work has shown that using a language-specific tokenizer can enhance the adaptability. In this work, we hypothesize that full model tuning is often unnecessary and propose a more modular approach. Specifically, we replace the tokens, freeze the corresponding embeddings, and tune the rest of the model. We use Scottish Gaelic, Irish, and Quechua for our experiments, with Quechua being a very low-resource language (8.5k training instances). Evaluation on natural language understanding (NLU) tasks -- mask filling, NER, and POS -- shows that our proposed approach improves performance when adapting models to low-resource languages. Additionally, we provide a comprehensive analysis of the effectiveness of training strategies, the choice of pretrained embeddings, and models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a modular adaptation approach for low-resource languages where only token embeddings are replaced and frozen, while the rest of the model is fine-tuned, challenging the necessity of full model tuning.
AI.motivation: Full model fine-tuning for adapting pretrained LMs to low-resource languages is computationally expensive and may be unnecessary. The authors hypothesize that a modular approach — replacing tokens, freezing embeddings, and tuning only the remaining parameters — can achieve better performance while being more efficient.
AI.method: The proposed method replaces the tokenizer with a language-specific one, freezes the corresponding input embeddings, and fine-tunes the rest of the pretrained model. Experiments are conducted on Scottish Gaelic, Irish, and Quechua (a very low-resource language with only 8.5k training instances). Evaluation uses NLU tasks: mask filling, named entity recognition (NER), and part-of-speech (POS) tagging.
AI.result: The modular tuning approach improves performance across all three languages on NLU tasks compared to standard full-model fine-tuning, particularly benefiting the extremely low-resource Quechua setting.
AI.conclusion: Full model tuning is often unnecessary for adapting LMs to low-resource languages. A modular strategy of replacing tokens, freezing embeddings, and fine-tuning the rest yields better NLU performance, with additional insights provided on training strategies, pretrained embedding choices, and model selection.

title: Capturing non-Markovian dynamics in non-equilibrium stochastic systems using flow matching
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, Accepted to 2026 Conference on Physics and AI (PAI26)
summary: Hydrodynamic models of stochastic particle systems represented by coarse-grained stochastic partial differential equations (SPDE), such as the regularized Dean-Kawasaki (DK) equation, do not accurately capture the short-time system dynamics that is dominated by non-Markovian effects, and low particle density regimes where the distributions are highly non-Gaussian. We develop a generative flow matching method that directly models the probability distribution of fluxes from particle simulations that explicitly incorporates non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects. As a demonstration, we use this method to simulate the Kramers first passage time problem for a system of non-interacting Brownian particles. We show the model accurately captures the short-time behavior and provides better predictions of the statistical moments of the number density when compared against the solution of the Markovian baseline, regularized DK equation.

AI.tldr: A generative flow matching method is developed to model flux probability distributions from particle simulations, capturing non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects that coarse-grained SPDEs like the regularized Dean-Kawasaki equation miss. The method is demonstrated on the Kramers first passage time problem for non-interacting Brownian particles, showing improved accuracy in short-time dynamics and statistical moment predictions.
AI.motivation: Coarse-grained stochastic PDE models (e.g., regularized Dean-Kawasaki equation) fail to capture short-time dynamics dominated by non-Markovian effects and low-density regimes with highly non-Gaussian distributions. A method that explicitly incorporates these effects is needed.
AI.method: A generative flow matching approach that directly models the probability distribution of fluxes derived from particle simulations, explicitly incorporating non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects.
AI.result: The method accurately captures short-time behavior and yields better predictions of the statistical moments of number density compared to the Markovian baseline (regularized Dean-Kawasaki equation) when applied to the Kramers first passage time problem for non-interacting Brownian particles.
AI.conclusion: Generative flow matching on flux distributions from particle simulations provides a superior alternative to traditional coarse-grained SPDEs for capturing non-Markovian and non-Gaussian dynamics, particularly at short times and low particle densities.

title: A Geometric Account of Activation Steering through Angle-Norm Decomposition
summary: Linear activation steering has gained popularity as a simple and empirically effective way to control language model behavior. More recently, spherical steering paradigms have been proposed to address limitations of additive interventions, often motivated by the assumption that hidden-state norm does not carry concept-relevant information. In this work, we revisit this assumption through a controlled empirical study designed to disentangle the roles of angular and radial components. We show that steering methods differ mainly in how they couple two geometric effects: changing a token's angular alignment with a concept direction and changing its hidden-state norm. Across seven language models, we find that concepts are represented primarily in angular structure, supporting the motivation for spherical methods, but that norm remains important for the stability and downstream effects of steering. Our results explain why interventions with similar concept-level effects can behave differently, and suggest that activation steering should be parameterized by interpretable angular and radial components of the intervention, rather than by a single additive coefficient that entangles these two effects.

AI.tldr: This paper disentangles the angular and radial (norm) components of activation steering in language models, showing that while concepts are primarily encoded in angular structure (supporting spherical methods), hidden-state norm is crucial for steering stability and downstream effects. The authors argue for decoupling these two geometric components rather than using a single additive coefficient.
AI.motivation: Linear activation steering and spherical steering paradigms make different implicit assumptions about whether hidden-state norm carries concept-relevant information. The paper aims to rigorously test this assumption and understand how angular and radial components each contribute to steering behavior, addressing a gap in the mechanistic understanding of why different intervention methods produce different outcomes.
AI.method: A controlled empirical study across seven language models that disentangles angular alignment (with a concept direction) from hidden-state norm changes. The authors systematically vary these two geometric components to isolate their individual and coupled effects on model behavior.
AI.result: Concepts are represented primarily in angular structure, validating spherical methods. However, hidden-state norm is important for steering stability and downstream effects. Interventions with similar concept-level effects can behave differently due to how they couple angular and radial changes.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering should be parameterized by interpretable, decoupled angular and radial components rather than a single additive coefficient that entangles both effects. This would provide more principled control over language model behavior.

title: PaperFlow: Profiling, Recommending, and Adapting Across Daily Paper Streams
summary: Scientific paper recommendation is typically evaluated as static ranking over a fixed candidate set, yet real scientific reading unfolds as a daily, longitudinal process in which interests shift and feedback accumulates. We introduce PaperFlow, a framework that organizes it into three coupled stages: Profiling, which constructs and maintains a structured, inspectable scholarly profile from heterogeneous cold-start evidence; Recommending, which ranks each date-specific paper stream through multi-signal aggregation under a fixed display budget; and Adapting, which updates user state from semantically distinct feedback signals and models interest drift across days. We further define a longitudinal user-day benchmark that fixes users, dates, candidate pools, visible inputs, and hidden simulated relevance labels under a shared temporal information boundary. The benchmark contains 24 simulated research users, 50 daily paper streams, 1,200 user-day episodes, 20,727 unique papers, and 497,448 episode-paper records. We additionally specify a blind human-evaluation protocol to validate alignment between automatic metrics and expert judgments. Experiments against five scientific recommendation baselines show that PaperFlow achieves the strongest oracle-based ranking, the highest behavioral alignment with simulated reading selections, and the best blind human-evaluation score.

AI.tldr: PaperFlow introduces a longitudinal framework for scientific paper recommendation with three stages—Profiling, Recommending, and Adapting—supported by a new benchmark of 24 simulated users over 50 daily streams, demonstrating superior ranking and human-evaluated alignment over static baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing scientific paper recommendation is evaluated as static ranking on fixed candidate sets, ignoring the daily, longitudinal nature of real scientific reading where interests drift and feedback accumulates over time.
AI.method: PaperFlow decomposes longitudinal recommendation into three coupled stages: (1) Profiling—constructing structured, inspectable scholarly profiles from heterogeneous cold-start evidence; (2) Recommending—ranking daily paper streams via multi-signal aggregation under a fixed display budget; (3) Adapting—updating user state from semantically distinct feedback signals to model interest drift across days. A longitudinal user-day benchmark is introduced with 24 simulated users, 50 daily streams, 1,200 episodes, 20,727 papers, and 497,448 episode-paper records under a shared temporal information boundary. A blind human-evaluation protocol validates automatic metrics against expert judgments.
AI.result: PaperFlow achieves the strongest oracle-based ranking, highest behavioral alignment with simulated reading selections, and best blind human-evaluation scores compared to five scientific recommendation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Modeling scientific recommendation as a longitudinal process with explicit profiling, multi-signal ranking, and interest-drift adaptation yields significant improvements over static approaches in both automatic metrics and human judgment alignment.

title: Porting Declarative UI to HarmonyOS: A Heuristic-guided LLM Approach
summary:
As an emerging operating system, HarmonyOS has a significant demand for software migration from platforms such as Android and iOS, where the User Interface (UI) translation accounts for a critical link. However, the latest UI development has shifted to declarative paradigms, e.g., Kotlin Jetpack Compose (KJC) for Android, SwiftUI for iOS, and ArkUI for HarmonyOS, rendering prior translation approaches inapplicable, as they target either backend logic or legacy imperative UIs.
  As such, this paper targets ArkUI and proposes an automatic translation approach, namely ArkTrans, to port UI files from Android and iOS to HarmonyOS.
  ArkTrans overcomes two salient challenges during the translation: (1) Programming Language (PL) unfamiliarity, and (2) severe syntactic chaos. Towards the first challenge, ArkTrans heuristically constructs ArkUI skeletons by extracting metadata from source PL, thereby guiding LLMs' initial translation. As for the second challenge, ArkTrans executes empirically revealed post-fixing rules via pattern matching to repair most of the remaining syntactic errors. To examine the effectiveness of ArkTrans, we construct a 100-sample parallel UI page translation benchmark from KJC/SwiftUI to ArkUI at the file level. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LLMs with direct/one-shot prompting cannot translate a single compilable UI page. In contrast, at most 90.67\% ArkTrans-translated files can be successfully compiled with high visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: ArkTrans is an automatic UI translation tool that ports declarative UI files from Android (Kotlin Jetpack Compose) and iOS (SwiftUI) to HarmonyOS (ArkUI), using heuristic skeleton construction and rule-based post-fixing to overcome PL unfamiliarity and syntactic chaos, achieving up to 90.67% compilable translations where direct LLM prompting yields zero compilable results.
AI.motivation: HarmonyOS, as an emerging OS, faces significant demand for software migration from Android/iOS, particularly UI translation. The shift to declarative UI paradigms (KJC, SwiftUI, ArkUI) renders prior translation approaches—designed for backend logic or imperative UIs—inapplicable, creating a gap that ArkTrans aims to fill.
AI.method: ArkTrans addresses two challenges: (1) Programming Language (PL) unfamiliarity, tackled by heuristically constructing ArkUI skeletons from source PL metadata to guide LLM initial translation; (2) severe syntactic chaos, resolved through empirically revealed post-fixing rules applied via pattern matching to repair remaining syntactic errors.
AI.result: Evaluation on a 100-sample parallel UI translation benchmark (KJC/SwiftUI → ArkUI) shows LLMs with direct/one-shot prompting fail to produce any compilable UI page. ArkTrans achieves up to 90.67% successfully compilable translations with high visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: ArkTrans demonstrates that combining heuristic-driven skeleton construction with rule-based syntactic repair effectively enables cross-platform declarative UI translation to ArkUI, significantly outperforming LLM-only prompting approaches and addressing a critical need in HarmonyOS ecosystem migration.

title: When to Think Deeply: Inhibitory Deliberation for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reasoning Large Language Models can improve problem-solving performance through deliberative inference, but invoking slow reasoning for every input is computationally expensive and often unnecessary. We propose IDPR, a framework for response-conditioned inhibitory deliberation. IDPR first generates a concise intuitive answer and then uses an inhibition controller to decide whether that specific response should be released or suppressed in favor of slow reasoning. Unlike input-only routers, the inhibition controller conditions on the fast answer and fast-side evidence, including confidence, logit margin, parseability, and generation cost. We train the controller from paired fast-slow outcomes and select the inhibition threshold on a held-out validation set under an accuracy-first slow-call budget. On a held-out 5,000-example mathematical reasoning test set, IDPR invokes slow reasoning on only 8.20% of examples and improves accuracy from 47.90% to 48.92%. Under the same slow-call budget, random routing decreases accuracy to 46.76%, while the strongest confidence-based baseline reaches 48.22%. IDPR also achieves the highest corrective precision, showing that response-conditioned inhibition better identifies fast answers that benefit from slow reasoning.

AI.tldr: IDPR (Inhibitory Deliberative Prompting with Response-conditioning) improves LLM reasoning efficiency by generating a fast intuitive answer first, then using a trained inhibition controller—conditioned on the fast response and its confidence/logit metrics—to decide whether to suppress that answer in favor of slow reasoning. On a 5,000-example math test set, it invokes slow reasoning on only 8.20% of examples, achieving 48.92% accuracy vs. 47.90% baseline, outperforming confidence-based and random routing baselines.
AI.motivation: Slow, deliberative reasoning (e.g., chain-of-thought) improves LLM problem-solving but is computationally expensive when applied indiscriminately. Existing input-only routers lack access to response-side signals, limiting their ability to identify when a fast answer is unreliable and would benefit from slow reasoning. The authors aim to selectively invoke slow reasoning only when the fast response is likely incorrect, under a budget constraint.
AI.method: IDPR is a two-stage framework: (1) a fast generation path produces a concise intuitive answer along with response-side evidence (confidence, logit margin, parseability, generation cost); (2) an inhibition controller—trained from paired fast-slow outcomes—processes these features to decide whether to release the fast answer or suppress it and invoke slow reasoning. The inhibition threshold is selected on a held-out validation set under an accuracy-first slow-call budget. The controller conditions on response-level features unavailable to input-only routers.
AI.result: On a 5,000-example mathematical reasoning test set, IDPR invokes slow reasoning on only 8.20% of examples and improves accuracy from 47.90% (fast-only) to 48.92%. Under the same slow-call budget, random routing degrades accuracy to 46.76%, while the best confidence-based baseline reaches 48.22%.
