summary: We study orchestration mechanisms for tool-using AI agents in realistic customer-service workflows over an unstructured knowledge base. We argue that declarative agents -- AI agents equipped with natural-language skill files appended to the system prompt -- are an effective orchestration paradigm. Concretely, we compare (i) a DeclarativeAgent that reads three domain-specific skill files at inference time and decides its own control flow, (ii) an ImperativeAgent based on a programmatic state machine with explicit phases, and (iii) an unscaffolded baseline agent modeled after the $τ$-Knowledge benchmark agent. Our ImperativeAgent is motivated by externalised-control inference as in Recursive Language Models and graph-based orchestration frameworks. We formalise the three agents as policy classes within a decentralised partially-observable Markov decision process and analyse their information-theoretic and structural properties; we then test the predicted differences empirically on five language models and two retrieval regimes. Our results show that retrieval quality is a dominant bottleneck for AI agents: when evidence is incomplete or skewed, all agents degrade substantially, and skill files cannot recover lost performance. Under high-quality retrieval, however, declarative skills consistently improve accuracy on procedural tasks and reduce orchestration errors, while the imperative state machine's brittleness does not reliably improve task success or compliance.

AI.tldr: This paper compares three orchestration paradigms for tool-using AI agents in customer-service workflows: a declarative agent using natural-language skill files, an imperative state-machine agent, and an unscaffolded baseline. Under high-quality retrieval, declarative skills improve procedural accuracy and reduce orchestration errors, while the imperative agent's brittleness offers no reliable advantage. Retrieval quality emerges as the dominant bottleneck across all agents.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agent orchestration frameworks rely on programmatic control flow (imperative) or no scaffolding at all. The authors hypothesize that declarative agents—equipped with natural-language skill descriptions appended to the system prompt—can flexibly decide their own control flow and outperform both imperative and baseline agents, particularly on procedural tasks. They also seek to identify whether retrieval quality or orchestration strategy is the dominant factor in agent performance.
AI.method: The authors formalize three agent policy classes within a decentralized partially-observable Markov decision process (Dec-POMDP), analyzing their information-theoretic and structural properties. They implement: (1) a DeclarativeAgent with domain-specific skill files in the system prompt, (2) an ImperativeAgent using a programmatic state machine with explicit phases (motivated by Recursive Language Models and graph-based orchestration), and (3) an unscaffolded baseline modeled after the τ-Knowledge benchmark agent. Empirical evaluation spans five language models and two retrieval regimes across realistic customer-service workflows over an unstructured knowledge base.
AI.result: Retrieval quality is the dominant bottleneck: under incomplete or skewed evidence, all agents degrade substantially and skill files cannot compensate. Under high-quality retrieval, declarative skills consistently improve accuracy on procedural tasks and reduce orchestration errors. The imperative state machine exhibits brittleness and does not reliably improve task success or compliance compared to the declarative approach.
AI.conclusion: Declarative agents with natural-language skill files represent an effective orchestration paradigm for tool-using AI agents, outperforming imperative state machines in flexibility and reliability. However, retrieval quality remains the primary determinant of overall agent performance, and no orchestration strategy can recover from poor retrieval.

title: Empirical Evaluation of Large Language Models for Migration of Code Fragments to Post-Quantum Cryptography
summary: The transition to post-quantum cryptography (PQC) requires not only replacing vulnerable cryptographic primitives, but also refactoring the surrounding software logic. While existing PQC migration frameworks provide organizational guidance, practical code-level remediation remains largely manual and error-prone. This paper evaluates whether large language models (LLMs) can be trained to assist in the migration of pre-quantum cryptographic code fragments to post-quantum counterparts while preserving functional correctness. To this end, we introduce a reproducible experimental framework built around a synthetic dataset of 800 paired Python code fragments covering six cryptographic families and combined multi-primitive cases. Each pair is validated through category-specific functional tests, enabling both dataset quality control and objective evaluation of model-generated migrations. Four models are assessed: GPT-4.1 in a zero-shot setting, and fine-tuned versions of GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4.1-mini, and CodeLlama-7B-Instruct. The results show that domain-specific fine-tuning is essential for reliable cryptographic migration. The fine-tuned GPT-4.1-mini model achieves the best overall performance, with a mean static similarity of 0.9072 and a dynamic functional correctness rate of 92.5%, substantially outperforming the zero-shot baseline. A complementary validation on six open-source repositories further shows that the approach can produce useful migrations in localized cryptographic modules, while also revealing limitations in larger projects with complex dependencies and cross-module interactions. These findings suggest that fine-tuned LLMs can serve as practical components in future crypto-agile migration pipelines, provided they are coupled with automated verification and dependency-aware validation.

AI.tldr: Evaluates whether fine-tuned LLMs can automate migration of pre-quantum cryptographic code to post-quantum counterparts. Using a synthetic dataset of 800 Python code-fragment pairs across six cryptographic families, fine-tuned GPT-4.1-mini achieved 92.5% functional correctness, demonstrating that domain-specific fine-tuning is essential for reliable cryptographic migration, though real-world validation on open-source repos reveals limitations with complex dependencies.
AI.motivation: Post-quantum cryptography transition requires both replacing vulnerable primitives and refactoring surrounding software logic. Existing PQC migration frameworks provide only organizational guidance, leaving practical code-level remediation as a manual, error-prone process. The authors investigate whether LLMs can be trained to automate this code-level migration while preserving functional correctness.
AI.method: The authors built a reproducible experimental framework around a synthetic dataset of 800 paired Python code fragments spanning six cryptographic families (symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, digital signatures, hashing, key exchange, key derivation) plus multi-primitive combinations. Each pair is validated via category-specific functional tests. Four models were evaluated: GPT-4.1 (zero-shot), and fine-tuned GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4.1-mini, and CodeLlama-7B-Instruct. Evaluation uses static similarity metrics and dynamic functional correctness testing. A complementary validation was performed on six open-source repositories.
AI.result: Domain-specific fine-tuning proved essential. Fine-tuned GPT-4.1-mini performed best: mean static similarity of 0.9072 and dynamic functional correctness of 92.5%, substantially outperforming the zero-shot GPT-4.1 baseline. Validation on open-source repositories showed useful migrations in localized cryptographic modules but revealed limitations in larger projects with complex dependencies and cross-module interactions.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuned LLMs can serve as practical components in crypto-agile migration pipelines, but must be coupled with automated verification and dependency-aware validation. The approach is promising for localized cryptographic modules but faces challenges scaling to complex, interdependent codebases.

title: Socratic-SWE: Self-Evolving Coding Agents via Trace-Derived Agent Skills
summary: LLM-driven software engineering agents have become a central testbed for real-world language-model capability, yet their training remains limited by the availability of high-quality SWE tasks. Existing synthetic data methods typically create tasks through fixed mutation or bug-injection procedures, making the resulting distributions largely independent of the agent's own weaknesses and training progress. We introduce Socratic-SWE, a closed-loop self-evolution framework that reuses the agent's historical solving traces as a source of training signal. Rather than treating traces only as evidence for reward computation, Socratic-SWE distills them into structured agent skills that summarize recurring failures and effective repair patterns. These skills then guide the generation of targeted repair tasks in real repositories. Candidate tasks are checked through execution-based validation and scored with a solver-gradient alignment reward, so that the retained tasks are both verifiable and useful for improving the Solver. The updated Solver produces new traces, enabling the task curriculum to adapt over successive rounds. Across SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Lite, SWE-bench Pro, and Terminal-Bench 2.0, Socratic-SWE consistently improves over self-evolving baselines under the same compute budget, reaching 50.40% on SWE-bench Verified after three iterations. These results suggest that solving traces can serve as a scalable substrate for self-evolving SWE agents.

AI.tldr: Socratic-SWE is a closed-loop self-evolution framework for LLM-driven software engineering agents that mines the agent's own solving traces to distill structured skills (failure patterns and repair strategies), uses these skills to generate targeted, verifiable training tasks, and iteratively improves the agent through solver-gradient-aligned reward scoring. It achieves 50.40% on SWE-bench Verified after three iterations, outperforming self-evolving baselines under the same compute budget.
AI.motivation: Current synthetic data generation for training SWE agents relies on fixed mutation or bug-injection procedures, producing task distributions that are static and agnostic to the agent's specific weaknesses and learning progress. The authors aim to create a curriculum that adapts to the agent's evolving capabilities by exploiting its own solving traces as a rich, scalable source of training signal.
AI.method: Socratic-SWE operates in iterative rounds: (1) the Solver agent generates solving traces on existing tasks; (2) traces are distilled into structured "agent skills" capturing recurring failure modes and effective repair patterns; (3) these skills guide the generation of targeted repair tasks in real repositories; (4) candidate tasks undergo execution-based validation and are scored using a solver-gradient alignment reward, which measures how useful a task is for improving the Solver; (5) retained tasks augment the training set, the Solver is updated, and the cycle repeats with the improved Solver producing richer traces.
AI.result: Socratic-SWE consistently outperforms self-evolving baselines under identical compute budgets across four benchmarks: SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Lite, SWE-bench Pro, and Terminal-Bench 2.0. On SWE-bench Verified, it reaches 50.40% after three iterations.
AI.conclusion: Solving traces represent a scalable and effective substrate for self-evolving SWE agents. By closing the loop between trace generation, skill distillation, targeted task creation, and solver improvement, Socratic-SWE enables a curriculum that automatically adapts to the agent's weaknesses and improves over successive rounds.

title: RECAP: Regression Evaluation for Continual Adaptation of Prompts
summary: Production agentic systems routinely face evolving constraints and must comply from the very next interaction. Scenarios like a tool-call notification changing a compliance threshold or a policy update adding disclosure requirements fit this criteria, having close to no room for errors in production. This proactive adaptation setting is common in deployment, but absent from current benchmarks, which assume either static constraint sets or reactive protocols with evaluation feedback. We introduce RECAP, a benchmark that measures continual-learning phenomena (forgetting, regression, forward transfer) at the constraint level under a strictly proactive adapt-then-test protocol: prompt optimization methods receive only the constraint specification and must generalize before seeing any test data. Evaluating six methods across four LLMs and three schedules with evolving constraints, we find that these methods show no significant improvement in performance, even after incurring a higher latency. These methods, designed for offline or reactive settings, are inadequate for the proactive paradigm. Our work emphasizes the growing need for designing proactive prompt adaptation methods, where the models must remain robust to evolving needs in deployment.

AI.tldr: RECAP is a benchmark for evaluating prompt optimization methods under proactive, evolving constraints—measuring forgetting, regression, and forward transfer. Across six methods, four LLMs, and three schedules, no method significantly improved performance despite higher latency, revealing that existing offline/reactive approaches fail in proactive adapt-then-test deployment scenarios.
AI.motivation: Production agentic systems face evolving constraints (e.g., policy updates, compliance threshold changes) requiring immediate adaptation with zero tolerance for errors. Current benchmarks assume static constraints or reactive protocols with evaluation feedback, ignoring the proactive adaptation setting common in real deployment. This gap motivates a benchmark that explicitly measures continual-learning phenomena under strictly proactive conditions.
AI.method: RECAP is introduced as a benchmark using a proactive adapt-then-test protocol: prompt optimization methods receive only the constraint specification and must generalize without seeing test data. The benchmark measures continual-learning phenomena—forgetting, regression, and forward transfer—at the constraint level. Six prompt optimization methods are evaluated across four LLMs under three schedules with evolving constraints.
AI.result: All six evaluated methods showed no significant performance improvement under the proactive paradigm, even while incurring higher latency. Methods designed for offline or reactive settings proved inadequate when required to adapt before exposure to test data.
AI.conclusion: There is a pressing need to design proactive prompt adaptation methods that enable models to remain robust to evolving deployment needs. Existing approaches fundamentally fall short in this setting, highlighting an important direction for future research.

title: Quantum-Inspired Trace-Augmented Evidence Selection for Reasoning over Structured Hypothesis Spaces
summary: Large language models (LLMs) now solve a wide range of expert-level exams at or above human level, yet remain brittle on specialised, evidence-intensive domains such as law. On these tasks, errors arise not only from gaps in world knowledge but also from subtle distinctions between pieces of evidence and inconsistent use of supporting evidence. The most common aggregator over sampled chain-of-thought (CoT) traces, majority vote, returns the most popular answer regardless of whether its evidence is actually strongest. We propose to treat the selection of CoT reasoning fragments into a set of evidence as an explicit combinatorial optimisation problem, allowing well-supported but minority hypotheses to override noisy majorities, and to evaluate the approach on legal-reasoning benchmarks that are particularly sensitive to evidence quality. We introduce EP-HUBO (Evidence Pool Higher-Order Binary Optimisation), which generates multiple CoT traces with a small local model, parses fragments into per-hypothesis evidence pools, solves a higher-order unconstrained binary optimisation per pool with quality-derived weights (relevance, specificity, distinctiveness), and delegates a single adjudication call per question to a frontier model. We evaluate EP-HUBO on two evidence-intensive legal benchmarks using both simulated annealing on classical hardware and the Dirac-3 photonic entropy-quantum machine from Quantum Computing Inc. HUBO-style optimisation gives a principled way to aggregate reasoning fragments while preserving minority-but-correct hypotheses, and is most valuable in low-contamination domains where frontier models have not already absorbed the benchmark material.

AI.tldr: EP-HUBO reformulates chain-of-thought reasoning aggregation as a combinatorial optimisation problem, using higher-order unconstrained binary optimisation to select evidence fragments by quality-weighted scoring, thereby preserving minority but well-supported hypotheses over noisy majorities in legal reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle on evidence-intensive domains like law, where standard majority-vote aggregation over chain-of-thought traces discards minority hypotheses even when backed by stronger evidence. The authors identify that errors stem from subtle evidentiary distinctions and inconsistent evidence use, motivating a principled evidence-selection framework.
AI.method: EP-HUBO generates multiple CoT traces via a small local model, parses fragments into per-hypothesis evidence pools, constructs quality-derived weights (relevance, specificity, distinctiveness) for each fragment, and solves a higher-order unconstrained binary optimisation (HUBO) per pool to select the best-supported hypothesis. A single frontier model then makes the final adjudication. Optimisation is evaluated using both simulated annealing and the Dirac-3 photonic entropy-quantum machine.
AI.result: EP-HUBO outperforms majority-vote baselines on two evidence-intensive legal benchmarks. It is most impactful in low-contamination domains where frontier models have not memorised benchmark material, demonstrating that principled evidence aggregation benefits reasoning quality when genuine inference is required.
AI.conclusion: Treating CoT evidence aggregation as explicit combinatorial optimisation provides a principled mechanism to recover minority-but-correct hypotheses, offering substantial gains over majority vote in specialised, evidence-sensitive domains like law, particularly where model contamination is low.

title: On the Shoulders of Giants: Empowering Automated Smart Contract Auditing via the GiAnt Corpus
summary: High-quality smart contract auditing datasets are crucial for evaluating security tools and advancing smart contract security research. Two major limitations of existing datasets are the manual-induced scalability bottleneck and the deficiency in data granularity and diversity. To address these limitations, we propose GiANT, an automated framework designed to curate smart contract auditing datasets by distilling vulnerability insights from real-world auditing reports. GiANT employs a divide-and-conquer strategy coupled with the Chain-of-Thought technique to extract structured vulnerability information from Code4rena reports, followed by an LLM-as-a-judge mechanism to perform rigorous quality assurance. To evaluate GiANT's effectiveness, we run it on 388 real-world audit reports and generate the GiAnt Corpus comprising 7,711 vulnerability findings across five severity levels. Manual assessment of the dataset demonstrates exceptional reliability in information extraction, achieving a mean quality score of $4.76\pm0.37$ (out of 5) with inter-rater agreement $κ$ of 0.88. We further validate the practicality of our dataset by benchmarking 4 state-of-the-art LLMs on vulnerability detection, code summarization, mitigation recommendation, and automated gas optimization tasks, to establish performance baselines, thereby providing a valuable data foundation for future research in automated smart contract auditing.

AI.tldr: GiANT is an automated framework that distills structured vulnerability data from real-world Code4rena audit reports using divide-and-conquer with Chain-of-Thought prompting and LLM-as-a-judge quality assurance. Applied to 388 reports, it produces a dataset of 7,711 findings across five severity levels, achieving near-perfect manual quality scores (4.76/5). The corpus is benchmarked on four SOTA LLMs for vulnerability detection, code summarization, mitigation recommendation, and gas optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing smart contract auditing datasets suffer from two critical limitations: (1) a manual-induced scalability bottleneck that prevents large-scale dataset creation, and (2) insufficient data granularity and diversity, which hampers the development and evaluation of automated security tools. There is a pressing need for an automated, scalable approach to generate high-quality, fine-grained auditing datasets from real-world sources.
AI.method: GiANT uses a three-stage automated pipeline: (1) It applies a divide-and-conquer strategy with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting to extract structured vulnerability information (e.g., vulnerability type, severity, location, description, and mitigation) from unstructured Code4rena audit reports. (2) An LLM-as-a-judge mechanism performs rigorous quality assurance on the extracted data. (3) The framework was run on 388 real-world audit reports to produce the GiANT Corpus. Manual evaluation using inter-rater agreement (Cohen's κ) validates extraction reliability.
AI.result: The GiANT Corpus contains 7,711 vulnerability findings across five severity levels. Manual assessment yielded a mean quality score of 4.76 ± 0.37 (out of 5) with an inter-rater agreement κ of 0.88, indicating exceptional extraction reliability. Benchmarking four state-of-the-art LLMs on the dataset established performance baselines across four tasks: vulnerability detection, code summarization, mitigation recommendation, and automated gas optimization.
AI.conclusion: GiANT successfully addresses the scalability and granularity limitations of existing smart contract auditing datasets by automating the curation process from real-world audit reports. The resulting high-quality GiANT Corpus provides a valuable, reliable data foundation for advancing research in automated smart contract auditing, as evidenced by its strong manual validation scores and its utility in benchmarking LLM performance across multiple security-related tasks.

title: Agentic Very Much! Adoption of Coding Agent in New GitHub Projects
summary: In previous work, we investigated the adoption of coding agents in GitHub projects, finding that it was very significant. This study follows this line of work, but analyses new projects, that were created after the previous study. In this new sample, we find that the adoption of coding agents is more than twice as high. We also find that the adoption is significantly more intensive, as the proportion of AI-assisted commits is sensibly higher, despite strong signs that we do not detect all of it.

AI.tldr: A follow-up study reveals that adoption of AI coding agents in new GitHub projects has more than doubled compared to earlier findings, with AI-assisted commits comprising a significantly higher proportion of total commits, despite likely under-detection.
AI.motivation: To track the evolving trajectory of AI coding agent adoption in open-source software development by examining a fresh cohort of GitHub projects created after the authors' prior study, thereby measuring whether adoption is accelerating.
AI.method: Replication of a prior observational study design, but applied to a new sample of GitHub projects created after the original study period, with detection heuristics for AI-assisted commits that the authors acknowledge are incomplete.
AI.result: Adoption of coding agents in the new project sample is more than twice as high as previously reported. The intensity of adoption is also markedly greater, with a substantially higher proportion of commits flagged as AI-assisted, even though the detection methods likely underestimate the true figure.
AI.conclusion: AI coding agent adoption in open-source projects is not only continuing but accelerating rapidly in both breadth (number of projects) and depth (share of AI-generated contributions), signaling a fast-moving shift in software development practices.

title: ShallowBench: Benchmarking Generative Drug Design Models on Shallow-Pocket Targets
summary: While generative AI models have demonstrated remarkable success in structure-based drug design, they predominantly rely on deep binding pockets and struggle to sample effective ligands for challenging low-pocketability targets, such as the historically "undruggable" oncology targets KRAS and MYC. To address this gap, we introduce ShallowBench, a strictly curated benchmark of 5,780 shallow-pocket targets extracted from CrossDocked2020. By computing the difference between an Alpha Shape "lid" volume and the underlying protein atom voxel volume, we successfully isolated targets with low concavity while ensuring sufficient surface area for binding. Evaluating various state-of-the-art generative models reveals weaker predicted binding affinity on these low-concavity interfaces. ShallowBench therefore provides a rigorous benchmark for generative biology models and highlights the necessity of new architectural innovations or loss functions capable of navigating these challenging targets.

AI.tldr: ShallowBench introduces a curated benchmark of 5,780 shallow-pocket targets to evaluate generative AI models for structure-based drug design, revealing that current models struggle with low-concavity binding sites and motivating new approaches for historically undruggable targets like KRAS and MYC.
AI.motivation: Generative AI models for structure-based drug design excel at deep binding pockets but fail on shallow-pocket targets (e.g., KRAS, MYC), which represent historically "undruggable" oncology proteins. No existing benchmark specifically evaluates model performance on these challenging low-pocketability interfaces.
AI.method: The authors constructed ShallowBench by extracting 5,780 shallow-pocket targets from CrossDocked2020. They computed the difference between an Alpha Shape "lid" volume (the convex hull approximation of the pocket opening) and the underlying protein atom voxel volume to quantify concavity, isolating targets with low concavity but sufficient surface area for binding. State-of-the-art generative models were then evaluated against this benchmark.
AI.result: Evaluation of state-of-the-art generative models on ShallowBench revealed significantly weaker predicted binding affinity on low-concavity interfaces compared to deep-pocket targets, confirming that current architectures are ill-suited for shallow-pocket drug design.
AI.conclusion: ShallowBench serves as a rigorous benchmark exposing the limitations of current generative models on shallow-pocket targets, highlighting the need for novel architectural innovations or specialized loss functions to enable effective ligand design for historically undruggable proteins.

title: Accounting for Context: Shaping Moral Credences for Value Alignment
summary: Ensuring that agent behaviours are aligned with human moral values inevitably raises the problem of how to account for the plurality of moral perspectives that societies -- and even individuals -- typically adopt. Work on moral uncertainty proposes mechanisms to fairly and democratically aggregate evaluations of actions across different moral theories. However, this paper argues that one needs to account for contextual factors when aggregating moral evaluations. For example, consequentialist perspectives assume an ability to accurately determine how an agent's actions change the world; an assumption that often does not hold in real world settings. We, therefore, formalise agent decision making under moral uncertainty, while also accounting for these kinds of contextual factors. We thereby show that a seemingly commonsensical property -- the weak Pareto principle -- is violated. We argue that this apparent problem is, in fact, a variation of Simpson's paradox, and hence reveals the limitations of aggregation mechanisms that ignore the impact of contextual factors.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that aggregating moral evaluations across different theories must account for contextual factors (e.g., the reliability of consequentialist predictions in real-world settings). Formalizing moral uncertainty with context reveals that the weak Pareto principle is violated — a manifestation of Simpson's paradox, exposing the limits of context-ignoring aggregation mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Existing work on moral uncertainty aggregates evaluations across moral theories to handle value pluralism, but fails to account for contextual factors that affect how each theory applies in practice. The authors aim to expose this gap and its formal consequences.
AI.method: The authors formalize agent decision-making under moral uncertainty while incorporating contextual factors (e.g., the reliability of predictions under consequentialism). They then analyze whether standard aggregation properties — specifically the weak Pareto principle — hold under this enriched framework.
AI.result: The weak Pareto principle — intuitively requiring that if all moral theories prefer action A over B, the aggregate should also prefer A over B — is violated once contextual factors are included. This violation is identified as an instance of Simpson's paradox.
AI.conclusion: Aggregation mechanisms for moral uncertainty that ignore contextual factors are fundamentally limited. The violation of the weak Pareto principle via Simpson's paradox demonstrates that democratic or fair aggregation of moral theories cannot proceed naively; context must be modeled explicitly.

title: Lost in Migration: Exposing Android Framework Vulnerabilities in Parallel Java-Kotlin Implementations
summary: Android has adopted Kotlin alongside Java across apps and core system components. During this shift, we observe parallel implementations in the Android Open Source Project (AOSP) where the same component is implemented in both Java and Kotlin. In principle, their functional purposes are identical. In practice, subtle semantic divergences can appear. Such divergences are not vulnerabilities by themselves, but they provide useful clues that may reveal flaws in surrounding enforcement logic. To the best of our knowledge, this paper presents the first systematic study of Java-Kotlin parallel implementations in the Android framework and examines their security implications. We design and build ParaDroid, an analysis framework that identifies parallel methods at scale and compares their behaviors. ParaDroid normalizes code into a bytecode-level intermediate representation, reconstructs class-to-source mappings, and uses large language models to reason about method semantics and identify behavioral divergences. Evaluated on AOSP Android 14-16, ParaDroid identified 329 parallel method pairs and 37 vulnerable divergences. We responsibly disclosed the exploitable issues to the Android Security Team. Three vulnerabilities and two bugs have been confirmed, and two CVE IDs have been assigned. Our results demonstrate that parallel Java-Kotlin code paths provide a practical surface for discovering security flaws in modern Android.

AI.tldr: This paper presents ParaDroid, the first systematic study of Java-Kotlin parallel implementations in the Android framework, identifying semantic divergences between functionally identical methods that can reveal security vulnerabilities. The system uses bytecode-level IR normalization, class-to-source mapping, and LLMs to detect behavioral differences at scale, finding 329 parallel pairs and 37 vulnerable divergences across AOSP Android 14-16.
AI.motivation: Android's adoption of Kotlin alongside Java has led to parallel implementations of the same component in both languages within AOSP. While functionally identical in principle, subtle semantic divergences can emerge. These divergences are not vulnerabilities per se, but they serve as clues that may expose flaws in surrounding enforcement logic. No prior systematic study has examined the security implications of such Java-Kotlin parallel code paths.
AI.method: The authors built ParaDroid, an analysis framework operating in three stages: (1) normalizing both Java and Kotlin code into a unified bytecode-level intermediate representation to enable cross-language comparison; (2) reconstructing class-to-source mappings to identify candidate parallel method pairs across the AOSP codebase; (3) leveraging large language models to reason about method semantics and detect behavioral divergences between paired implementations at scale.
AI.result: Evaluated on AOSP Android versions 14 through 16, ParaDroid identified 329 parallel method pairs and uncovered 37 vulnerable divergences. Responsible disclosure to the Android Security Team resulted in confirmation of three vulnerabilities and two bugs, with two CVE IDs assigned.
AI.conclusion: Parallel Java-Kotlin code paths constitute a practical and under-explored attack surface for discovering security flaws in modern Android. The findings demonstrate that cross-language semantic divergence analysis is a fruitful direction for vulnerability research and warrants further attention from both platform maintainers and the security community.

title: Korean Culture into LLM Alignment: Toward Cultural Coherence
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop on Culture X AI
summary: Cultural-aspect work on large language models is dominated by a negative target: which outputs to suppress. We argue that a constructive counterpart is also needed, a working definition of what a culturally coherent response is rather than only what it must avoid, and instantiate it for Korean. We design an alignment-data pipeline around a prompt-based LLM seed generator that expands a Korean harm taxonomy, with a Korean-culturally-adapted safe-response policy at its centre: a per-category guideline grounded in Korean legal frameworks, social norms, and interpretive conventions, against which three frontier models each produce a candidate response. DPO fine-tuning on the resulting triplets improves the Korean cultural safe rate across six open-weight LLMs while causing no large degradation on Korean general-capability benchmarks, and qualitative outputs show fine-tuned models naming Korean statutes and institutional procedures and, where appropriate, supplying constructive Korean-context information alongside refusal.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a constructive framework for culturally coherent LLM responses in Korean, using a DPO fine-tuning pipeline built on a Korean-specific harm taxonomy and culturally-adapted safe-response policy. Fine-tuned models show improved Korean cultural safety without degrading general capabilities, and they appropriately reference Korean laws and institutions.
AI.motivation: Existing cultural alignment work for LLMs focuses predominantly on suppressing harmful outputs (negative target). The authors argue this is insufficient and that a constructive counterpart is needed — a working definition of what constitutes a culturally coherent response, not merely what to avoid. This gap is particularly acute for non-English, culturally specific contexts like Korean.
AI.method: The authors design an alignment-data pipeline: (1) a prompt-based LLM seed generator expands a Korean harm taxonomy; (2) a Korean-culturally-adapted safe-response policy is developed per category, grounded in Korean legal frameworks, social norms, and interpretive conventions; (3) three frontier models each generate candidate responses against this policy; (4) DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) fine-tuning is performed on the resulting triplets across six open-weight LLMs.
AI.result: DPO fine-tuning on the Korean-culturally-aligned triplets improves the Korean cultural safe rate across all six open-weight LLMs. No significant degradation is observed on Korean general-capability benchmarks. Qualitative analysis shows fine-tuned models appropriately name Korean statutes, institutional procedures, and supply constructive Korean-context information alongside refusal responses.
AI.conclusion: A constructive, culturally grounded alignment approach — pairing a positive definition of cultural coherence with DPO fine-tuning — is effective for Korean LLM safety, yielding measurable safety gains without sacrificing general performance. The framework demonstrates the value of moving beyond suppression-only strategies toward culturally informed, constructive response generation.

title: MSAIC-Net: A Multi-Scale Attention and Imbalance-Aware Contrastive Network for ECG-Based Myocardial Substrate Abnormality Detection
summary: Myocardial substrate abnormalities, such as myocardial scar and myocardial infarction (MI), are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes. Electrocardiography (ECG) provides a low-cost and widely available tool for detecting these abnormalities, but ECG-based detection remains challenging due to heterogeneous lead-dependent manifestations, high-dimensional multi-lead signals, class imbalance, and the limited interpretability of deep learning models. We propose a multi-scale attention-enhanced convolutional network (MSAIC-Net) for ECG-based myocardial substrate abnormality detection. MSAIC-Net employs parallel atrous convolutional branches to extract ECG features across multiple temporal receptive fields. %, enabling the model to capture both local and longer-range temporal patterns. Channel attention is then used to adaptively reweight informative lead-wise and feature-channel representations. To address class imbalance and improve feature separability, we introduce a novel imbalance-aware supervised contrastive learning strategy that encourages samples from the same class to form compact representations while increasing separation between abnormal and normal samples. Lead-wise permutation importance is further incorporated to quantify the contribution of each ECG lead and improve model interpretability. The proposed method was evaluated on two complementary datasets: a low-data institutional cohort from the University of Virginia (UVA) Health System for myocardial scar classification and the large-scale public PTB-XL dataset from PhysioNet for MI identification. Experimental results show that MSAIC-Net outperforms baseline models, with particularly pronounced improvements in the low-data UVA cohort. Overall, the proposed framework provides an effective and interpretable approach for ECG-based detection of myocardial substrate abnormalities.

AI.tldr: MSAIC-Net is a multi-scale attention-enhanced convolutional network for ECG-based detection of myocardial scar and infarction. It uses parallel atrous convolutions for multi-scale temporal features, channel attention for adaptive lead/feature reweighting, an imbalance-aware supervised contrastive loss for better class separation, and lead-wise permutation importance for interpretability. The method outperforms baselines on both a small institutional dataset (UVA) and the large public PTB-XL dataset.
AI.motivation: ECG-based detection of myocardial substrate abnormalities (scar, MI) is challenging due to: (1) heterogeneous lead-dependent manifestations, (2) high-dimensional multi-lead signals, (3) class imbalance, and (4) limited interpretability of existing deep learning models. A method is needed that addresses all four issues simultaneously while remaining effective even in low-data regimes.
AI.method: MSAIC-Net comprises: (1) parallel atrous convolutional branches capturing ECG features across multiple temporal receptive fields; (2) channel attention mechanisms that adaptively reweight both lead-wise and feature-channel representations; (3) a novel imbalance-aware supervised contrastive learning strategy that pulls same-class samples together and pushes opposite-class samples apart to improve feature separability; and (4) lead-wise permutation importance to quantify per-lead contributions for interpretability. Evaluated on UVA institutional data (myocardial scar) and PTB-XL (MI).
AI.result: MSAIC-Net outperformed baseline models on both datasets, with particularly pronounced improvements on the low-data UVA cohort, demonstrating strong generalizability and effectiveness in data-scarce settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MSAIC-Net framework provides an effective and interpretable deep learning approach for ECG-based myocardial substrate abnormality detection, successfully addressing key challenges of multi-scale temporal patterns, lead heterogeneity, class imbalance, and model interpretability.

title: Exploring Agentic Tool-Calling Decisions via Uncertainty-Aligned Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents often make suboptimal tool-use decisions, including unsupported tool invocation and hallucinated direct responses, which may accumulate errors throughout multi-step interactions. Existing approaches mainly improve these behaviors through inference-time correction or coarse-grained reward signals based on decision outcomes and structured checklists, leaving the uncertainty characteristics of agent decisions underexplored. We observe that decision-oriented reinforcement learning tends to weaken the uncertainty separation between correct and incorrect actions, resulting in overconfident mistakes and weaker exploration signals. Therefore, we propose TRUST, which incorporates uncertainty quantification into reward design as a repulsive force for maintaining uncertainty separation, and labels lightweight key-turn annotations for unified post-training of multi-turn trajectories. Experimental results across diverse tool-use benchmarks show that TRUST consistently enhances both decision quality and agent performance while maintaining more reliable uncertainty estimates during optimization.

AI.tldr: TRUST incorporates uncertainty quantification into reinforcement learning reward design for LLM-based agents, using uncertainty separation as a repulsive force to prevent overconfident mistakes. Combined with lightweight key-turn annotations for multi-turn post-training, it improves both tool-use decision quality and uncertainty calibration.
AI.motivation: LLM agents suffer from suboptimal tool-use decisions (unsupported tool invocation, hallucinated responses) that compound errors across multi-step interactions. Existing methods rely on inference-time correction or coarse-grained reward signals, neglecting the uncertainty characteristics of agent decisions. The authors observe that decision-oriented RL weakens uncertainty separation between correct and incorrect actions, causing overconfident mistakes and diminished exploration signals.
AI.method: TRUST (Tool-use decision optimization with uncertainty-aware Reward for poST-training) introduces two key components: (1) uncertainty quantification integrated into reward design as a repulsive force that explicitly maintains separation between uncertainty distributions of correct vs. incorrect actions during RL optimization; (2) lightweight key-turn annotations that label critical decision points in multi-turn trajectories, enabling unified post-training across entire interaction sequences rather than isolated decisions.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse tool-use benchmarks demonstrate that TRUST consistently enhances both decision quality and overall agent performance, while maintaining more reliable uncertainty estimates throughout the optimization process compared to standard decision-oriented RL approaches.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware reward shaping is an effective mechanism for preventing overconfidence in LLM agent tool-use decisions. By treating uncertainty separation as a repulsive force in the reward signal and leveraging key-turn annotations for multi-turn post-training, TRUST produces agents that make better decisions with better-calibrated uncertainty.

title: Verifiable and Confidential DNN Inference on Low-End Edge Devices
summary:
Deploying deep neural network (DNN) inference on low-end edge devices raises two key challenges: protecting model confidentiality against a potentially compromised edge system and enabling verifiable inference without incurring prohibitive overhead. Existing approaches either house partial models and inference software within trusted execution environments (TEEs), resulting in high cost and an application-dependent trusted computing base (TCB), or execute in untrusted environments, providing little security.
  In this work, we present VECODI, a framework for verifiable and confidential DNN inference on constrained edge devices. At its core, VECODI introduces SHANGRI-LA, a new execution abstraction on TrustZone-M TEEs that establishes a third runtime environment with privileges strictly between the Secure and Non-Secure Worlds. VECODI leverages SHANGRI-LA to execute untrusted inference code in the Non-Secure World while using minimal application-agnostic Secure-World support to protect model confidentiality and enable verifiability (with respect to proper execution of inference code and model parameters) of inference results. We realize VECODI on a real-world NUCLEO-L552ZE-Q development board and open-source its prototype. Our results show VECODI's small TCB, memory footprint, and runtime overhead, making it a practical option for secure inference in low-end edge devices.

AI.tldr: VECODI is a framework enabling verifiable and confidential DNN inference on low-end edge devices. It introduces SHANGRI-LA, a new execution abstraction on TrustZone-M that creates a third privilege level between Secure and Non-Secure Worlds, allowing untrusted inference to run in the Non-Secure World while minimal Secure-World support protects model confidentiality and verifies inference correctness.
AI.motivation: Deploying DNN inference on low-end edge devices poses two critical challenges: (1) protecting model confidentiality against potentially compromised edge systems, and (2) enabling verifiable inference without prohibitive overhead. Existing solutions are inadequate—TEE-based approaches produce high costs and large application-dependent TCBs, while untrusted execution offers little security.
AI.method: The authors propose VECODI, whose core innovation is SHANGRI-LA—a new execution abstraction on ARM TrustZone-M TEEs. SHANGRI-LA establishes a third runtime environment with privileges strictly between the Secure and Non-Secure Worlds. VECODI runs untrusted inference code in the Non-Secure World and uses minimal, application-agnostic Secure-World support to protect model confidentiality and enable verifiability of inference results (covering both proper code execution and model parameter integrity). The framework is implemented on a NUCLEO-L552ZE-Q development board.
AI.result: VECODI achieves a small Trusted Computing Base (TCB), low memory footprint, and minimal runtime overhead, making it practical for secure inference on resource-constrained edge devices. The prototype is open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: VECODI demonstrates that verifiable and confidential DNN inference is feasible on low-end edge devices by decoupling security-critical operations from untrusted inference execution via a novel intermediate-privilege abstraction (SHANGRI-LA), achieving both strong security guarantees and practical performance.

title: Quantifying Media Representation Dynamics Across 25 Years of News Reporting on Policing-related Deaths
summary: We perform the largest known computational analysis of Canadian news narratives about police-involved deaths, spanning 4,000 articles from the last quarter-century. We develop a novel computational model, PerspectiveGap, grounded in prior sociological work on media representation of policing. We find that reporting on police-involved deaths on average features perspectives from state bureaucrats at a rate nearly three times as much as perspectives from other members of the public, including relatives, community members, eyewitnesses, lawyers representing the family, or civil liberties groups. A considerable fraction of articles contain no points of view from civilian actors, though civilian representation has increased in recent years. Qualitatively, we find that state bureaucrats' accounts of these deaths tend to be clinical and procedural, while civilian discourse carries considerably more emotional valence. The PerspectiveGap framework developed here can be contextualized to other jurisdictions, offering a scalable approach for analyzing how media systems construct narratives around policing and accountability.

AI.tldr: A large-scale computational analysis of 4,000 Canadian news articles over 25 years reveals that state bureaucrat perspectives dominate reporting on police-involved deaths, appearing nearly three times more frequently than civilian voices. The authors introduce PerspectiveGap, a novel computational model for quantifying this disparity.
AI.motivation: Media narratives shape public understanding of police accountability, yet prior sociological work suggests systematic imbalances in whose perspectives are privileged. This study seeks to quantify that gap at scale, moving beyond qualitative case studies to a broad, longitudinal computational analysis of sourcing patterns in Canadian news coverage of police-involved deaths.
AI.method: The authors develop PerspectiveGap, a novel computational model grounded in sociological theory on media representation. They apply it to a corpus of 4,000 Canadian news articles spanning 25 years, using NLP techniques to identify and quantify the prevalence of perspectives from different actor categories: state bureaucrats (police, coroners, officials) versus civilian actors (relatives, community members, eyewitnesses, family lawyers, civil liberties groups). They also conduct qualitative analysis comparing the discourse styles of each group.
AI.result: State bureaucrat perspectives appear at nearly 3× the rate of civilian perspectives on average. A substantial fraction of articles feature no civilian voices at all, though civilian representation shows an upward trend in recent years. Qualitatively, state accounts are clinical and procedural, while civilian discourse carries significantly higher emotional valence.
AI.conclusion: Canadian news media systematically underrepresent civilian perspectives in reporting on police-involved deaths, favoring institutional, bureaucratic accounts. The PerspectiveGap framework offers a scalable, transferable method for auditing narrative imbalance in media systems across different jurisdictions.

title: Flatland: The Adventures of Gradient Descent with Large Step Sizes
comment: Accepted for the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: The training of neural networks often entails objective functions that are not globally $L$-smooth. For these functions, it is both theoretically and practically difficult to reply to the question: what is the largest possible step size that ensures the convergence of gradient descent (GD)? We address this longstanding open question in deep learning by providing a unifying definition of "large" step sizes that requires only local Lipschitz (or even Hölder) continuity of the gradient. We design first-order adaptive methods that provably yield large step sizes and show that they operate at the edge of stability (EoS) right from the start of the training. In particular, the loss decreases nonmonotonically and the product between the step size and sharpness, i.e., the largest eigenvalue of the Hessian, stays above the EoS threshold of 2 throughout training. Using our method, we are also able to minimize the sharpness all the way down to its global minimum. Contrary to expectation, we find that encountering globally-flat regions too early in the training may both slow down convergence and jeopardize the generalization ability of the network. Exploiting a self-stabilization argument, we allow GD to enter slightly sharper valleys and turn unsuccessful training runs into very successful ones.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the open problem of determining the maximum stable step size for gradient descent on non-smooth objectives. The authors introduce a unifying definition of large step sizes requiring only local Lipschitz/Hölder continuity, design adaptive first-order methods that provably achieve large step sizes and operate at the edge of stability (EoS) from training onset, and show that early encounters with flat regions can harm convergence and generalization — while deliberately entering sharper valleys can rescue failed training runs.
AI.motivation: Neural network training often involves objective functions that are not globally L-smooth, making it theoretically and practically challenging to determine the largest step size that still guarantees GD convergence. This is a longstanding open question in deep learning.
AI.method: The authors provide a unifying definition of "large" step sizes based solely on local Lipschitz (or Hölder) continuity of the gradient. They design first-order adaptive methods that provably achieve these large step sizes. The methods leverage a self-stabilization mechanism that keeps the product of step size and sharpness (max Hessian eigenvalue) above the EoS threshold of 2 throughout training, inducing nonmonotonic loss decrease. They also minimize sharpness to its global minimum.
AI.result: The proposed methods operate at the edge of stability from the very start of training with nonmonotonic loss decrease. Surprisingly, encountering globally flat regions too early slows convergence and harms generalization. By allowing GD to enter slightly sharper valleys via self-stabilization, previously unsuccessful training runs become highly successful.
AI.conclusion: Large step sizes at the edge of stability can be provably achieved via adaptive first-order methods without requiring global smoothness. Early flatness is detrimental, and controlled entry into sharper regions can improve both convergence speed and generalization, challenging conventional wisdom favoring flat minima.

title: Teaching the Way, Not the Answer: Privileged Tutoring Distillation for Multimodal Policy Optimization
summary: Recent post-training methods, particularly Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), have significantly enhanced the reasoning ability of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). However, the sparse nature of verifiable rewards provides little token-level supervision for failed rollouts, often leading to inefficient exploration in complex multimodal reasoning tasks. Although policy distillation can offer dense guidance, external teacher based methods introduce substantial computational overhead, while answer conditioned tuning methods may expose answer-level information and induce shortcut-like generation behavior. To address these limitations, we propose PTD-PO, a Privileged Tutoring Distillation Policy Optimization framework for RLVR that provides dense guidance without exposing the answer to the student policy. Specifically, PTD-PO constructs structured privileged hints from spatial attention guidance and intermediate textual reasoning steps, and uses them through in-context learning to produce step-wise token-distribution supervision. The student is still optimized under the original answer-free context, and its failed rollouts are aligned with the hint-augmented reference model at the token-distribution level. To further stabilize distillation under the distribution shift between guided and unguided contexts, we introduce a Top-K Jensen-Shannon divergence objective that focuses alignment on informative token probabilities while reducing memory overhead. Experiments on LVLMs ranging from 2B to 8B parameters show that PTD-PO consistently outperforms RLVR and distillation baselines, mitigates entropy collapse, and improves complex multimodal reasoning performance.

AI.tldr: PTD-PO is a dense token-level distillation framework for RLVR that uses privileged hints (spatial attention + intermediate reasoning) via in-context learning to guide failed rollouts, without exposing answers. A Top-K Jensen-Shannon divergence stabilizes distillation under distribution shift, improving complex multimodal reasoning in 2B–8B LVLMs while mitigating entropy collapse.
AI.motivation: RLVR's sparse verifiable rewards lack token-level supervision for failed rollouts, causing inefficient exploration in complex multimodal reasoning. Existing solutions—external teacher distillation (high compute) or answer-conditioned tuning (exposes answers, induces shortcuts)—are suboptimal. The goal is dense guidance without answer leakage or heavy overhead.
AI.method: PTD-PO (Privileged Tutoring Distillation Policy Optimization): (1) Constructs privileged hints from spatial attention guidance and intermediate textual reasoning steps. (2) Uses these hints via in-context learning in a separate reference model to produce step-wise token-distribution supervision. (3) The student policy trains in an answer-free context; its failed rollouts are aligned at the token-distribution level with the hint-augmented reference model. (4) Introduces a Top-K Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence objective, focusing alignment on high-probability tokens to stabilize distillation under guided/unguided distribution shift while reducing memory.
AI.result: Experiments on LVLMs from 2B to 8B parameters show PTD-PO consistently outperforms RLVR and distillation baselines, mitigates entropy collapse, and improves complex multimodal reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: PTD-PO effectively addresses RLVR's sparse supervision problem by providing dense, answer-free token-level guidance through privileged hints and Top-K JS divergence distillation, yielding stronger multimodal reasoning without the drawbacks of teacher-based or answer-conditioned methods.

title: Hearing the Unspoken: Language Model Priors for Acoustic Adversarial Attacks
summary: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems operating in real-time settings must process acoustic input under strict temporal constraints, where transcription decisions are inherently made on incomplete information. This causal constraint serves as an information bottleneck on attackers, significantly limiting attack performance. Our new Semantic Gambit attack breaks this causal limitation by augmenting the adversary with predictive context derived from a Large Language Model in real-time. Our experiments show that this form of augmentation can elevate the corpus-level Word Error Rate to 35.6% -- a three-fold increase over the current state-of-the-art. Ultimately, this work reveals how common, low-latency LLM tooling can be exploited to systematically subvert real-time ASR pipelines.

AI.tldr: The Semantic Gambit attack uses real-time LLM predictions to overcome the causal information bottleneck in attacking real-time ASR systems, achieving a 3× increase in Word Error Rate (35.6%) over prior state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: Real-time ASR systems operate under causal constraints — transcription decisions must be made with incomplete future context. This same constraint acts as an information bottleneck that limits adversarial attack effectiveness. The authors seek to break this limitation by providing attackers with predictive context about future speech, demonstrating a systematic vulnerability in real-time ASR pipelines.
AI.method: The proposed Semantic Gambit attack augments real-time adversarial perturbations with predictive semantic context generated by a Large Language Model (LLM) operating in real-time. The LLM predicts likely upcoming linguistic content based on partial, already-transcribed speech, and this predictive context is leveraged to craft more effective adversarial examples against the streaming ASR system.
AI.result: The Semantic Gambit attack achieves a corpus-level Word Error Rate (WER) of 35.6%, representing a three-fold increase over the current state-of-the-art attack on real-time ASR systems.
AI.conclusion: Low-latency LLM tooling, now commonplace, can be exploited to systematically subvert real-time ASR pipelines by giving attackers predictive power that circumvents the causal constraints normally limiting adversarial effectiveness.

title: Progress-SQL: Improving Reinforcement Learning for Text-to-SQL via Progressive Rewards
summary: Reinforcement learning has recently shown promise in improving large language models for Text-to-SQL generation, yet existing methods typically optimize one-shot rewards defined over a single SQL state. Such rewards provide limited guidance for iterative SQL correction and are insufficient to capture the improvement of multi-turn SQL refinement. In this paper, we propose Progress-SQL, a multi-turn reinforcement learning framework with progressive rewards for Text-to-SQL. Our approach introduces an Oracle-guided Diagnostic Tree (ODT), which abstracts SQL queries into clause-level structural profiles and produces diagnostic feedback for next-turn refinement. To provide dense and robust reward signals, we combine ODT-based structural alignment with lexical alignment and define a progressive reward that measures the improvement from the initial SQL to the final SQL. We further incorporate a progression latency reward that favors earlier correctness and an execution status reward that encourages recovery from the invalid SQL. Experiments on BIRD, Spider, and Spider robustness variants demonstrate that our method consistently improves Text-to-SQL performance across both primary and robustness evaluations.

AI.tldr: Progress-SQL introduces a multi-turn reinforcement learning framework for Text-to-SQL that uses Oracle-guided Diagnostic Trees to provide clause-level structural feedback, combined with progressive rewards measuring improvement across turns, latency rewards favoring early correctness, and execution rewards encouraging recovery from invalid SQL.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for Text-to-SQL rely on one-shot rewards over a single SQL state, which provide insufficient guidance for iterative SQL correction and fail to capture improvement across multiple refinement turns.
AI.method: Progress-SQL uses: (1) Oracle-guided Diagnostic Tree (ODT) that abstracts SQL into clause-level structural profiles and generates diagnostic feedback per turn; (2) progressive reward combining ODT-based structural alignment with lexical alignment to measure improvement from initial to final SQL; (3) progression latency reward incentivizing earlier correctness; (4) execution status reward encouraging recovery from invalid SQL.
AI.result: Experiments on BIRD, Spider, and Spider robustness variants show consistent improvements in both primary and robustness evaluations for Text-to-SQL performance.
AI.conclusion: Multi-turn RL with progressive, dense reward signals (structural, lexical, latency, execution-based) significantly enhances LLM-based Text-to-SQL generation by enabling effective iterative refinement guided by structured diagnostic feedback.

title: Synthics: Synthetic Physics-like Datasets for Machine Learning
summary: Representative data is fundamental in machine learning, as limited data hinders generalisation. Collecting sufficient real-world samples is often infeasible. Synthetic data generation offers a practical solution, but only if the generated data faithfully reflects the structure of real observations. In this paper, a method for generating synthetic regression datasets that structurally resemble physics equations from a given equation corpus is presented. The approach uses a Bayesian Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar to capture the underlying algebraic structure of the corpus, from which novel equations are sampled. To ensure the generated inputs lie within a physically meaningful domain, the applicability domain is characterised for each equation through non-intrusive probing, also recovering inter-variable constraints. Input sampling further mimics realistic experimental conditions by drawing from random sub-ranges of the valid domain with mixed uniform and truncated normal distributions. The generated data is statistically validated against the Feynman equation corpus using Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests. The generated equations match the corpus on all of the eight studied structural features, compared to only two for an unsmoothed purely probabilistic grammar, demonstrating that the Bayesian prior is essential for structural fidelity given the size of the corpus. In a downstream hyperparameter-tuning task, a gradient-boosted regressor tuned on the synthetic data picks, on average, the 6th-best configuration out of 20 on real data, matching the result of tuning on real data itself and substantially outperforming random expression trees (10th) and noise (19th).

AI.tldr: A Bayesian Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) generates synthetic regression datasets that structurally resemble physics equations. The method characterizes the applicability domain of each equation, samples inputs from valid sub-ranges with mixed distributions, and is validated via Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests against the Feynman equation corpus. Synthetic data matches all eight structural features and performs comparably to real data in hyperparameter tuning, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Collecting sufficient real-world regression data for machine learning is often infeasible. Existing synthetic data generation methods fail to produce datasets that faithfully reflect the algebraic structure of real physical equations. The authors aim to solve this by generating synthetic regression datasets whose structural properties match those of a given corpus of physics equations, enabling effective downstream ML tasks without real data.
AI.method: The approach uses a Bayesian Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) to model the algebraic structure of equation corpora (notably the Feynman equations). A Dirichlet prior governs grammar production probabilities. Novel equations are sampled from this grammar. The applicability domain of each equation is characterized via non-intrusive probing to define physically meaningful input ranges and recover inter-variable constraints. Inputs are sampled from random sub-ranges of the valid domain using a mixture of uniform and truncated normal distributions. Structural fidelity is assessed via Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests across eight structural features. Downstream utility is tested via hyperparameter tuning of a gradient-boosted regressor.
AI.result: The Bayesian-smoothed grammar matches the Feynman corpus on all eight studied structural features, whereas an unsmoothed purely probabilistic grammar matches only two. In hyperparameter tuning on 20 configurations, a gradient-boosted regressor tuned on synthetic data selects the 6th-best configuration on average, matching real-data tuning and substantially outperforming random expression trees (10th) and noise baselines (19th).
AI.conclusion: The Bayesian prior is essential for structural fidelity given limited corpus size. The method produces synthetic regression datasets that are viable substitutes for real data in downstream tasks such as hyperparameter optimization, effectively bridging the gap when real data collection is infeasible.

title: The Sim-to-Real Gap of Foundation Model Agents: A Unified MDP Perspective
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted by KDD 2026 Blue Sky Ideas Track
summary: Foundation model agents are increasingly deployed for real-world decision-making, but suffer from the sim-to-real gap. While robotics and classical control have mature frameworks to address this gap, the foundation model community is treating agent robustness as an entirely novel phenomenon. Our paper proposes formalizing the foundation model agent evaluation and training gap as a classical sim-to-real problem structured entirely around the four elements of a Markov Decision Process, including Observation, Action, Transition, and Reward. In this paper, we set a comprehensive research agenda that translates classical discrepancies into the foundation model domain and advocates for adopting established solutions like domain randomization. We provide concrete examples, such as a multilingual tool calling to demonstrate how severe observation space gaps lead to operationally invalid actions despite correct semantic intent. Ultimately, this agenda aims to drive a paradigm shift, yielding a unified vocabulary and standardized stress test benchmarks to foster a new generation of highly trustworthy agents for reliable real-world applications.

AI.tldr: The paper frames the foundation model agent robustness problem as a classical sim-to-real gap, structuring it around the four MDP elements (Observation, Action, Transition, Reward), and proposes adopting established solutions like domain randomization alongside standardized benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Foundation model agents are increasingly deployed in real-world settings but suffer from robustness failures due to the simulation-to-reality gap. While robotics and control theory have mature frameworks for addressing this, the foundation model community treats agent robustness as a novel phenomenon, leading to fragmented terminology and ad hoc solutions rather than leveraging decades of established methodology.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the sim-to-real gap in foundation model agents by decomposing it along the four canonical elements of a Markov Decision Process: (1) Observation space discrepancies, (2) Action space misalignments, (3) Transition dynamics mismatches, and (4) Reward function gaps. It then maps classical sim-to-real techniques (e.g., domain randomization, system identification) onto each category and proposes a research agenda with concrete instantiations, such as multilingual tool-calling scenarios.
AI.result: The paper is primarily a position/agenda paper, so results are illustrative rather than experimental. A key demonstrative finding is that observation-space gaps (e.g., multilingual inputs in tool-calling) can produce operationally invalid actions even when semantic intent is preserved, highlighting the severity of these discrepancies.
AI.conclusion: The authors call for a paradigm shift: adopting a unified MDP-grounded vocabulary for foundation model agent robustness, developing standardized stress-test benchmarks that systematically probe each sim-to-real gap category, and leveraging classical solutions to produce a new generation of trustworthy agents for reliable real-world deployment.

title: An Expanded Synthetic Conversation Dataset for Multi-Turn Smishing Detection
summary: Our prior work introduced COVA, a synthetically generated multi-turn conversational smishing dataset of 3,201 labeled conversations, establishing baseline detection benchmarks across eight models. While XGBoost with TF-IDF features achieved the best performance, with 72.5\% accuracy and 0.691 macro F1, transformer models underperformed, which was attributed to input truncation and insufficient training data. We present COVA-X, an expanded dataset of 10,985 conversations spanning eight elder-targeted scam categories, produced by an improved generation pipeline addressing contamination, label mismatch, stage-direction bleed, and prompt-design failures from the first iteration. Retraining all classifiers on the expanded dataset yields the central finding of this work: Longformer now surpasses XGBoost on all evaluation metrics, achieving 79.71\% accuracy and 0.7786 macro F1 compared with 78.43\% and 0.7563 for XGBoost. This directly confirms that transformer models require larger conversational corpora to realize their contextual advantages. We additionally document a quality life-cycle including a 12.7$\times$ improvement in label correction rate, from 49.8\% to 3.9\%, an architectural intervention reducing virtual-kidnapping artifact rates from 67.1\% to 46.5\%, and a per-scam-type outcome analysis showing that scam categories modulate results in mechanism-consistent ways. A pre/post-cleanup sensitivity analysis confirms that dataset refinement recovers genuine label-relevant signal across all three classifier architectures.

AI.tldr: Expanding a synthetic smishing (SMS phishing) dataset from 3,201 to 10,985 conversations with improved generation quality enables Longformer to surpass XGBoost, confirming that transformers need larger corpora to leverage contextual understanding for scam detection.
AI.motivation: Previous work (COVA) found that XGBoost outperformed transformer models on a synthetic smishing detection task, counter to expectations. The authors hypothesized this was due to input truncation and insufficient training data volume, motivating the creation of a substantially larger, higher-quality dataset (COVA-X) to test whether transformers would gain an advantage with more data.
AI.method: The authors built COVA-X, an expanded dataset of 10,985 multi-turn conversations spanning eight elder-targeted scam categories, using an improved synthetic generation pipeline that addresses four failure modes: contamination, label mismatch, stage-direction bleed, and prompt-design flaws. They retrained eight classifiers (including XGBoost, Longformer, and other transformers) on the new dataset, conducted per-scam-type outcome analysis, and performed a pre/post-cleanup sensitivity analysis to isolate the effect of data quality improvements.
AI.result: Longformer achieved the highest performance at 79.71% accuracy and 0.7786 macro F1, surpassing XGBoost (78.43%, 0.7563). The label correction rate improved 12.7× (from 49.8% to 3.9% error rate). Virtual-kidnapping artifact rates dropped from 67.1% to 46.5%. Per-scam-type analysis showed scam categories modulate detection outcomes in mechanism-consistent ways. The sensitivity analysis confirmed that dataset cleanup recovers genuine label-relevant signal for all three classifier architectures.
AI.conclusion: Transformer models require larger conversational corpora to realize their contextual advantages over traditional ML approaches like XGBoost. Dataset quality life-cycle management—including contamination control, label verification, and architectural interventions—is critical for synthetic data generation in NLP security tasks.

title: The Dark Regulome: Disentangling Predictability from Regulation in Genomic Foundation Models
summary: High-grade gliomas integrate into neural circuits through functional synapses with neurons, raising the question of which noncoding elements shape synaptogenic gene expression in tumor cells. The regulatory program written across the dark genome, what we call the $\textit{dark regulome}$, is the natural substrate to probe, and sequence foundation models offer a zero-shot route through in-silico mutagenesis (ISM); yet likelihood-based scoring is tautologically coupled to local sequence predictability, leaving the regulatory interpretation underdetermined. Across three architecturally distinct foundation models (Caduceus-Ph, HyenaDNA, Enformer) and 30,448 dark genome elements at 92 glioma-relevant loci, we introduce a residualization-and-permutation diagnostic that separates predictability-driven from regulation-driven RIS variance. A sharp 10kb proximal-regulatory horizon survives every control we apply, but the LM-derived element-class hierarchy does not: a six-feature linear baseline matches Caduceus top-decile membership at AUC $= 0.985$. Cross-architecture decomposition cleanly separates a sequence-predictability layer (the two language models co-rank long well-predicted transposable elements) from a regulatory-output layer (Enformer alone retains residual cCRE-discriminative signal), with literally zero overlap between the two top-100 lists. Conservation, brain cis-eQTL, and STRING-PPI cross-checks then anchor what biology survives: top-100 elements across all three models are $3.3\times$ enriched per model for matching brain eQTLs ($p_\mathrm{emp} < 5\times 10^{-3}$), while a tempting transposable-element regulatory layer and a striking NRXN1+NLGN1 protein-pair convergence both fail proper permutation tests once those tests are constructed. We deliver the diagnostic as a general methodological tool for any ISM-based regulatory study.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a "residualization-and-permutation diagnostic" to disentangle whether in-silico mutagenesis (ISM) scores from sequence foundation models reflect genuine regulatory biology or merely local sequence predictability. Applied across 30,448 dark genome elements at glioma-relevant loci using Caduceus-Ph, HyenaDNA, and Enformer, the method reveals that most ISM variance is predictability-driven, with a sharp 10kb proximal-regulatory horizon. Cross-architecture decomposition separates sequence-predictability signals from true regulatory signals, with biologically validated elements showing enrichment for brain cis-eQTLs.
AI.motivation: High-grade gliomas form functional synapses with neurons, implicating synaptogenic gene regulation. The "dark regulome" (noncoding regulatory elements) is the natural place to search for mechanisms, and sequence foundation models with in-silico mutagenesis (ISM) offer a zero-shot approach. However, likelihood-based ISM scoring is confounded by local sequence predictability — highly predictable regions score high regardless of regulatory function — leaving the regulatory interpretation underdetermined. A method is needed to separate these two sources of variance.
AI.method: The authors develop a residualization-and-permutation diagnostic applied to ISM scores from three architecturally distinct models (Caduceus-Ph, Enformer, HyenaDNA) across 30,448 dark genome elements at 92 glioma-relevant loci. The method regresses out predictability-driven variance and uses permutation testing to isolate regulation-driven signal. They also perform cross-architecture decomposition to separate sequence-predictability vs. regulatory-output layers, and validate surviving signals against conservation, brain cis-eQTLs, and STRING-PPI databases.
AI.result: (1) A sharp 10kb proximal-regulatory horizon persists across all controls. (2) The language-model-derived element-class hierarchy is not robust: a six-feature linear baseline matches Caduceus top-decile membership at AUC=0.985. (3) Cross-architecture decomposition cleanly separates a predictability layer (LM co-ranking of long, well-predicted transposable elements) from a regulatory layer (Enformer retaining cCRE-discriminative signal), with zero overlap between top-100 lists. (4) Top-100 elements across all three models show 3.3× enrichment for matching brain eQTLs (p<5×10⁻³). (5) A proposed transposable-element regulatory layer and NRXN1+NLGN1 protein-pair convergence both fail proper permutation tests.
AI.conclusion: Most ISM variance from foundation models on dark genome elements is driven by sequence predictability rather than regulatory biology. The residualization-and-permutation diagnostic provides a general methodological tool for any ISM-based regulatory study to rigorously separate these confounded signals.

title: TorchKM: A GPU-Oriented Library for Kernel Learning and Model Selection
