Such behavior induces a policy-dependent covariate shift, breaking the standard assumption in existing methods that covariates are exogenous to the policy. Related work addresses this challenge by imposing strong assumptions such as repeated interactions or full knowledge of agents' response behavior, substantially limiting its applicability to OPE. In contrast, we consider a one-shot OPE setting where the decision maker has only partial knowledge of the agents' response behavior. Our key insight is that disclosing local information through post-hoc explanations reveals agents' pre-strategic covariates prior to adaptation, mitigating the information loss induced by strategic behavior. Leveraging this structure, we estimate a statistical model for the agents' responses and construct a doubly robust estimator for policy value. By assuming that the agents' cost sensitivity follows a conditional log-normal distribution, we establish consistency of the proposed estimator and validate our approach empirically. More broadly, our results highlight how interaction design can mitigate information asymmetry by revealing otherwise hidden structure in agents' strategic responses.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses off-policy evaluation when agents strategically modify covariates in response to a policy, causing policy-dependent covariate shift. The authors propose using post-hoc explanations to reveal pre-strategic covariates, enabling estimation of agent response models and a doubly robust policy value estimator, with consistency established under a conditional log-normal cost sensitivity assumption.
AI.motivation: Standard OPE methods assume covariates are exogenous to the policy, but in strategic settings (e.g., loan applications, admissions), agents adapt their features to improve outcomes under a given policy. Existing solutions require strong assumptions like repeated interactions or full knowledge of agent behavior, limiting practical use. The authors aim to enable OPE in one-shot settings with only partial knowledge of agent responses.
AI.method: The authors leverage post-hoc explanations (e.g., SHAP, LIME) to disclose local information about the decision policy. This disclosure reveals agents' pre-strategic covariates, as agents adapt based on this information. Using observed pre- and post-strategic covariates, they estimate a statistical model of agent response behavior. A doubly robust estimator for policy value is then constructed, incorporating both a propensity-style model of strategic adaptation and an outcome regression model. Consistency is proved under the assumption that agents' cost sensitivity follows a conditional log-normal distribution.
AI.result: The proposed doubly robust estimator is shown to be consistent under the conditional log-normal cost sensitivity assumption. Empirical validation demonstrates the approach's effectiveness in recovering policy values under strategic behavior, outperforming naive OPE methods that ignore strategic adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Disclosing local explanations can mitigate information asymmetry in strategic settings by revealing hidden structure in agent responses, enabling valid off-policy evaluation. The work demonstrates that interaction design—specifically, what information is shared with agents—can be a practical tool for handling strategic behavior without requiring strong assumptions like repeated interactions or full behavioral knowledge.

title: Didact: A Cross-Domain Capability Discovery System for Defence
comment: Under Review at CIKM 2026 (System Demonstration Track)
summary: Policymakers in defence and defence-aligned sectors must monitor rapidly evolving research alongside sector priorities relevant to operational and strategic needs. In practice, these sources are fragmented across heterogeneous formats, disjoint repositories, and siloed update streams, making capability discovery slow and difficult to audit. We present Didact, a prototype that integrates publicly available defence reports and policy documents from Australia with a purpose-built knowledge graph derived from Australian research publications. Didact provides natural language conversations for policy-oriented workflows, and leverages a composite retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline. A key feature of Didact is an interactive Evidence Rail that visualises retrieved evidence and source relationships. Our evaluation of the output quality and runtime of Didact highlights its utility. While Didact has been co-developed as an academia-industry project for the Australian context, it is adaptable to other domains where knowledge is similarly fragmented. A demonstration video is available here:

AI.tldr: Didact is a prototype RAG-based conversational system that integrates Australian defence policy documents with a research knowledge graph, featuring an interactive Evidence Rail for source transparency, evaluated for output quality and runtime utility.
AI.motivation: Defence policymakers struggle to monitor evolving research across fragmented, heterogeneous, siloed sources, making capability discovery slow and difficult to audit. Existing repositories and update streams are disjoint, hindering alignment of research with operational priorities.
AI.method: The authors develop Didact, which integrates publicly available Australian defence reports and policy documents with a purpose-built knowledge graph derived from Australian research publications. It employs a composite retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline enabling natural language conversations for policy-oriented workflows. A key component is the interactive Evidence Rail, which visualises retrieved evidence and source relationships for transparency and auditability.
AI.result: Evaluation of Didact's output quality and runtime performance highlights its utility. The Evidence Rail feature enhances transparency by making source relationships visible. The system demonstrates effectiveness in the Australian defence context, with noted adaptability to other fragmented-knowledge domains.
AI.conclusion: Didact demonstrates a viable approach to unifying fragmented defence knowledge through conversational RAG with visual source provenance. While built for the Australian context as an academia-industry collaboration, the architecture generalises to other domains facing similar knowledge fragmentation challenges.

title: Online Pandora's Box for Contextual LLM Cascading
summary:
Motivated by Large Language Model (LLM) cascading, we propose an online contextual Pandora's Box model for adaptively querying and selecting LLM APIs. In each period, a decision-maker observes a request context and faces a two-phase decision problem. In the query phase, the decision-maker sequentially queries APIs, where each query reveals a generated output and the decision-maker incurs an (output-dependent) cost. In the selection phase, the decision-maker selects one of the generated outputs to deploy and observes only the downstream reward of the deployed output. This output-mediated feedback structure differs from classical online contextual Pandora's Box models, in which opening a box directly reveals its reward.
  Rather than estimating the full conditional output and cost distributions of each API, we directly model the reservation index and develop a learning approach for the query phase. Specifically, we impose a parametric structure on the contextual reservation index functions induced by the classical Weitzman's policy. Our policy combines generalized method of moments (GMM) type estimation of these reservation indices with UCB-style confidence bounds for both these indices and the shared output-level reward evaluator. Under regularity conditions, we prove that the resulting policy achieves dimension-dependent $\widetilde O(\sqrt T)$ cumulative regret over a horizon of $T$ periods.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an online contextual Pandora's Box model for adaptively querying and selecting LLM APIs, featuring a two-phase structure (query then selection) with output-mediated feedback. The authors develop a learning policy using GMM estimation of reservation indices with UCB confidence bounds, achieving $\widetilde O(\sqrt T)$ regret.
AI.motivation: The work is motivated by LLM cascading, where a decision-maker must sequentially choose among multiple LLM APIs to handle incoming requests. The key challenge is that classical Pandora's Box models assume opening a box directly reveals its reward, whereas in LLM cascading, querying an API reveals only the generated output (not the reward), and the reward is observed only after selecting one output to deploy. This output-mediated feedback structure necessitates a fundamentally different learning approach.
AI.method: The authors impose a parametric structure on contextual reservation index functions induced by Weitzman's classical policy. They propose a policy combining Generalized Method of Moments (GMM) estimation of reservation indices with UCB-style confidence bounds for both the indices and a shared output-level reward evaluator. This bypasses the need to estimate full conditional output and cost distributions for each API, instead directly modeling and learning the reservation indices that guide optimal querying.
AI.result: Under regularity conditions, the proposed policy achieves dimension-dependent $\widetilde O(\sqrt T)$ cumulative regret over a horizon of $T$ periods.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that by directly modeling reservation indices rather than full distributions, efficient online learning is achievable in the contextual Pandora's Box setting with output-mediated feedback, with near-optimal regret guarantees.

title: Auditing Training Data in Domain-adapted LLMs: LoRA-MINT
comment: IEEE Conf. on Computers, Software, and Applications (COMPSAC), 2026
summary: We present LoRA-MINT, a new methodology for Membership Inference Test (MINT) applied to recent Large Language Models (LLMs) fine-tuned for specific Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks through Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). The primary goal is to assess whether individual samples were part of the training data of these adapted models, providing a useful auditing tool for the management of intellectual property and sensitive data. Our analysis explores the relationship between model perplexity and membership status, providing a systematic framework for estimating data exposure in fine-tuned LLMs. We conducted experiments on four models and three benchmark datasets, obtaining precision values in determining if given data were used for training ranging from 0.77 to 0.92, which outperform state-of-the-art baselines and demonstrate the robustness and generality of the proposed method. In general, our findings underscore the potential of LoRA-MINT as an effective and scalable framework for auditing LLMs, improving transparency, and fostering the ethical and responsible deployment of AI and NLP technologies. For the sake of concreteness and current relevance, our discussion and experiments are centered on LoRAadjusted LLMs, but note that most of the presented methodology is easily applicable for auditing training data given any other technique for adapting LLMs or, more generally, any other domain-adapted AI models.

AI.tldr: LoRA-MINT is a membership inference testing framework for LoRA-fine-tuned LLMs that uses perplexity analysis to determine whether specific samples were part of the training data, achieving precision scores of 0.77–0.92 across multiple models and datasets.
AI.motivation: The growing use of fine-tuned LLMs raises concerns about intellectual property and sensitive data exposure. There is a need for reliable auditing tools that can assess whether individual data samples were used during fine-tuning, particularly for LoRA-adapted models which are increasingly common.
AI.method: LoRA-MINT leverages the relationship between model perplexity and membership status: training samples typically exhibit lower perplexity than non-training samples. The method provides a systematic framework that analyzes perplexity distributions to estimate data exposure, without requiring access to the original training pipeline or multiple shadow models.
AI.result: Experiments on four LLMs and three benchmark datasets yielded membership inference precision values ranging from 0.77 to 0.92, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines and demonstrating robustness and generality across different model–dataset configurations.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-MINT is an effective, scalable framework for auditing fine-tuned LLMs, promoting transparency and responsible AI deployment. Although focused on LoRA-adapted models, the methodology generalizes readily to other fine-tuning techniques and domain-adapted AI models.

title: The Geometry of Last-Layer Model Stealing
summary: This paper uses geometry to explain how a machine learning model can be stolen using an already existing well-known method. The author has shown the exact conditions required to perfectly copy the final layer of a transformer network. When looking deeper into the hidden layers the author has explained clear limits. The author has also demonstrated that a hidden network cannot be fully reverse engineered just by looking at the final results. The research clearly maps out what can and cannot be stolen from a model.

AI.tldr: Geometry-based analysis reveals the theoretical limits of model stealing attacks on transformer networks, delineating exactly which layers can and cannot be reverse-engineered from outputs alone.
AI.motivation: To formally characterize the conditions and boundaries of model stealing attacks using geometric principles, moving beyond empirical demonstrations to establish theoretical foundations for what information leakage is possible from transformer network outputs.
AI.method: The author applies geometric analysis to an established model extraction method, deriving exact conditions under which the final layer of a transformer can be perfectly copied, and analyzing the inherent limitations when attempting to reverse-engineer hidden layers from final output observations.
AI.result: The research establishes that: (1) the final layer of a transformer can be perfectly stolen under specific, precisely defined conditions; (2) hidden layers cannot be fully reverse-engineered solely from final outputs; and (3) there exists a clear boundary between extractable and non-extractable model components.
AI.conclusion: Model stealing has fundamental geometric limits: while the output layer is vulnerable under certain conditions, hidden representations remain partially protected. This provides a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding and mitigating model extraction risks.

title: Act As a Real Researcher: A Suite of Benchmarks Evaluating Frontier LLMs and Agentic Harnesses in Research Lifecycle
summary: As foundation models advance and agent scaffolding becomes increasingly sophisticated, agents have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in complex, long-horizon coding tasks and even autonomous experiment execution. Despite their evolution from research assistants into autonomous research agents, these systems still exhibit significant limitations in field sensitivity, research ethics, and nuanced scientific judgment. Consequently, frontier agents remain unable to fully replace human researchers. To bridge this gap, we conceptualize the AARR (Act As a Real Researcher) benchmark series. Unlike existing benchmarks that primarily assess macro-level execution capabilities, AARR focuses on whether agents can emulate the professionalism, thoroughness, and nuanced reasoning that characterize human researchers in granular research scenarios. In this work, we propose AARRI-Bench (Act As a Real Research Intern), the first benchmark in this series. We conduct extensive experiments across frontier models and agentic systems, revealing that even the best-performing configuration (Mini-SWE-Agent with Claude Opus 4.7) achieves only 68.3\% success rate, frequently overlooking subtle yet critical details that are obvious to real human researchers. Our results indicate that developing researcher-like AI requires further exploration of research behavior, rather than merely complex scaffolding. Our data is released at https://github.com/AARR-bench/AARRI-bench.

AI.tldr: AARRI-Bench is a new benchmark evaluating whether AI agents can emulate the nuanced reasoning, thoroughness, and professionalism of human researchers in granular scientific tasks. The best system (Mini-SWE-Agent + Claude Opus 4.7) achieves only 68.3% success, revealing that frontier agents still miss subtle but critical details, underscoring the need to focus on research behavior rather than just better scaffolding.
AI.motivation: Despite AI agents' growing capability in complex coding and autonomous experimentation, they still lack field sensitivity, research ethics, and nuanced scientific judgment, preventing them from fully replacing human researchers. Existing benchmarks only assess macro-level execution, leaving a gap in evaluating granular, researcher-like behavior.
AI.method: The authors introduce the AARR (Act As a Real Researcher) benchmark series and its first instantiation, AARRI-Bench (Act As a Real Research Intern). They conduct extensive experiments across multiple frontier models and agentic systems, measuring success rates on tasks designed to probe granular research reasoning, thoroughness, and professionalism.
AI.result: The best-performing configuration — Mini-SWE-Agent with Claude Opus 4.7 — achieves only a 68.3% success rate. All tested systems frequently overlook subtle yet critical details that are obvious to human researchers.
AI.conclusion: Building researcher-like AI requires deeper exploration of research behavior and nuanced judgment, not merely more complex agent scaffolding. The benchmark and data are publicly released to facilitate further research in this direction.

title: OpenHalDet: A Unified Benchmark for Hallucination Detection across Diverse Generation Scenarios
comment: Preprint. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Nellie179/Hallucination-Detection
summary: Hallucination detection is essential for the reliable deployment of large language models (LLMs). However, existing evaluations face two core challenges: inconsistent inference configuration and evaluation, and limited coverage of downstream domains and tasks. Consequently, reported detector performance is often difficult to compare, reproduce, and generalize beyond specific experimental settings. We introduce OpenHalDet, a unified benchmark for hallucination detection across diverse generation scenarios. OpenHalDet standardizes the evaluation pipeline, from prompt construction and response generation to truthfulness annotation, detector scoring, and metric computation. It supports heterogeneous detector families under different access settings, including black-box methods that use only generated outputs, gray-box methods that rely on probability-based signals, and white-box methods that exploit internal model signals. By bringing diverse tasks, models, and detectors into a shared framework, OpenHalDet enables controlled comparison and provides a systematic view of how different detection paradigms behave in LLM applications. We release OpenHalDet as an open and extensible codebase to facilitate reproducible evaluation and future development of hallucination detection methods. The code and datasets are available at https://github.com/Nellie179/Hallucination-Detection.

AI.tldr: OpenHalDet is a unified, extensible benchmark for hallucination detection in LLMs that standardizes the entire evaluation pipeline and supports black-box, gray-box, and white-box detection methods across diverse tasks and domains.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination detection evaluations suffer from inconsistent inference configurations/evaluation protocols and limited coverage of downstream domains and tasks, making detector performance difficult to compare, reproduce, and generalize.
AI.method: OpenHalDet standardizes the full evaluation pipeline (prompt construction, response generation, truthfulness annotation, detector scoring, metric computation) and unifies heterogeneous detector families — black-box (output-only), gray-box (probability-based signals), and white-box (internal model signals) — under different access settings within a shared, extensible codebase.
AI.result: The benchmark enables controlled comparison and provides a systematic view of how different detection paradigms behave across diverse LLM applications, though specific numerical results are not detailed in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: OpenHalDet addresses critical fragmentation in hallucination detection research by offering a reproducible, open, and extensible framework that facilitates fair comparison and future development of detection methods.

title: Modeling Nonlinear Feature Interactions with Product-Unit Residual Networks
summary: Understanding nonlinear feature interactions is crucial in science and engineering, yet standard multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) often capture such interactions only implicitly, leading to entangled representations that can impair robustness and interpretability. We investigate product-unit residual networks (PURe) that integrate multiplicative product units with residual connections to explicitly model cross-feature couplings while stabilizing optimization. We conduct a systematic evaluation on an interaction-driven synthetic benchmark and two real-world datasets, assessing predictive accuracy, robustness to Gaussian feature noise, and performance under limited training data, and we compare real- and complex-valued variants under a matched parameter budget. Beyond accuracy, SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP)-based interaction analyses show that PURe learns more concentrated and structurally coherent interaction patterns than MLP baselines. Overall, PURe achieves competitive or improved performance, better robustness and sample efficiency in low-data regimes, and enhanced interaction-level interpretability.

AI.tldr: PURe networks combine multiplicative product units with residual connections to explicitly model feature interactions, outperforming MLPs in robustness, sample efficiency, and interaction interpretability on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard MLPs capture nonlinear feature interactions only implicitly, resulting in entangled representations that degrade robustness and interpretability. There is a need for architectures that explicitly model cross-feature couplings while maintaining stable optimization.
AI.method: The authors propose PURe (product-unit residual) networks, which integrate multiplicative product units into residual blocks to explicitly capture pairwise feature interactions. They systematically evaluate real- and complex-valued PURe variants against MLP baselines under matched parameter budgets on an interaction-driven synthetic benchmark and two real-world datasets. Evaluation covers predictive accuracy, robustness to Gaussian feature noise, performance under limited training data, and SHAP-based interaction analysis for interpretability assessment.
AI.result: PURe achieves competitive or superior predictive accuracy compared to MLPs, exhibits greater robustness to Gaussian feature noise, demonstrates better sample efficiency in low-data regimes, and produces more concentrated, structurally coherent feature interaction patterns as revealed by SHAP interaction analyses.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling feature interactions via product units with residual connections yields networks that are not only more interpretable in terms of learned interactions but also more robust and data-efficient, making PURe a compelling alternative to standard MLPs for interaction-driven tasks.

title: How AI Agents Reshape Knowledge Work: Autonomy, Efficiency, and Scope
summary: Frontier AI systems are bridging the gap between intelligence and utility by shifting from conversational assistants to autonomous agents that execute tasks end to end. Using production data from Perplexity's Search and Computer products, we study this transition by examining how AI agents accelerate and reshape knowledge work. Three key empirical findings emerge. First, using sessions with near-identical initial query pairs as natural experiments for the same underlying task attempted with both products, Computer performs 26 minutes of autonomous work per user session, versus 33 seconds for Search. Computer automates task decomposition and execution that Search users might otherwise manually orchestrate and implement. As a result, Computer shifts follow-up query distribution toward higher-order work such as verification and extension. Autonomy also increases execution quality, with per-query dissatisfaction rates 55% lower on Computer than on Search. Second, due to its autonomy advantage, Computer reduces completion time from 269 to 36 minutes on matched tasks, lowering estimated time and cost by 87% and 94%, respectively, compared to humans equipped with Search alone. Third, Computer changes the scope of work that users attempt: Computer queries more often cross occupational boundaries, require higher-order cognition, draw on broader expertise, take the form of composite tasks that bundle interdependent subtasks into a single query, and unlock work activities that are essentially absent from Search usage among the same users. Together, the evidence indicates that AI agents accelerate workflows, enhance output quality, reduce costs, and expand the breadth and depth of automated work.

AI.tldr: Analysis of Perplexity's production data reveals that AI agents (Computer) perform 26 minutes of autonomous work per session vs. 33 seconds for Search, reducing task completion time by 87% and cost by 94%, while improving quality (55% lower dissatisfaction) and expanding the scope of work users attempt.
AI.motivation: To empirically quantify how the shift from conversational AI assistants to autonomous AI agents transforms knowledge work, using real-world production data to measure differences in task execution, efficiency, quality, and work scope.
AI.method: The authors leverage production data from Perplexity's Search (conversational) and Computer (autonomous agent) products. They use sessions with near-identical initial query pairs as natural experiments to compare the same underlying tasks attempted with both systems. Metrics include autonomous work duration, follow-up query distribution, dissatisfaction rates, task completion time, estimated cost, and task scope characteristics (occupational boundaries, cognitive demands, expertise breadth, composite task structure).
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) Computer performs ~26 min autonomous work/session vs. ~33 sec for Search, with follow-ups shifting toward verification/extension and dissatisfaction rates 55% lower. (2) Computer reduces completion time from 269 to 36 min on matched tasks (87% reduction) and estimated cost by 94%. (3) Computer queries more frequently cross occupational boundaries, require higher-order cognition, draw on broader expertise, bundle interdependent subtasks into composite queries, and enable work activities absent from Search usage.
AI.conclusion: AI agents substantially accelerate workflows, improve output quality, lower costs, and expand both the breadth and depth of automated knowledge work compared to conversational AI interfaces, marking a fundamental shift in how AI systems augment human productivity.

title: Tree-of-Experience: A Structured Experience-Management Solution for Self-Evolving Agents under Low-Repetition and Implicit-Reward Environments
summary: Experience-based self-evolution is crucial for LLM agents, but existing benchmarks often assume explicit goals, stable task patterns, and clear feedback. We study a more challenging setting: low-repetition tasks with implicit rewards, where past experience is difficult to reuse and feedback is delayed, noisy, and outcome-level. We introduce \textsc{FinEvolveBench}, a temporally controlled benchmark for financial sentiment prediction that links daily news-driven predictions to future excess returns. We further propose Tree-of-Experience (ToE), a structured experience-management method that organizes, retrieves, validates, and updates agent experience. Experiments show that general-purpose experience mechanisms do not consistently outperform no-experience baselines, while ToE achieves stronger overall performance. These results highlight the importance of structured experience management for self-evolving agents in implicit-reward environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FinEvolveBench, a benchmark for LLM agent self-evolution in low-repetition, implicit-reward settings (financial sentiment prediction), and proposes Tree-of-Experience (ToE), a structured experience-management method that outperforms general-purpose experience mechanisms and no-experience baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agent benchmarks assume explicit goals, stable task patterns, and clear feedback — conditions that do not hold in many real-world scenarios. The authors aim to study the harder setting where tasks are low-repetition, rewards are implicit (delayed, noisy, outcome-level), and past experience is difficult to reuse, using financial sentiment prediction as a testbed.
AI.method: The authors introduce FinEvolveBench, a temporally controlled benchmark linking daily news-driven sentiment predictions to future excess returns. They also propose Tree-of-Experience (ToE), a structured experience-management framework that organizes, retrieves, validates, and updates agent experience in a tree structure, enabling more effective reuse of past experience under implicit rewards.
AI.result: General-purpose experience mechanisms fail to consistently outperform no-experience baselines in this implicit-reward setting, whereas ToE achieves stronger overall performance, demonstrating the necessity of structured experience management for self-evolving agents.
AI.conclusion: Structured experience management (ToE) is critical for LLM agent self-evolution in environments with implicit rewards, low task repetition, and noisy feedback. The FinEvolveBench benchmark provides a realistic evaluation framework for advancing this research direction.

title: Product units in gated recurrent units improve nuclear-mass prediction
summary: The prediction of masses of atomic nuclei using machine learning can complement theoretical models and advance the exploration of poorly known domains of the nuclear chart. We propose a machine learning technique based on gated recurrent units (GRU), which have demonstrated competitive performance in nuclear-mass prediction by exploiting long-term dependencies. By integrating multiplicative interactions and product-unit transformations within recurrent units, we report significant improvements in nuclear-mass prediction. Computations are performed in the complex domain to jointly capture amplitude and phase dynamics. For interpolation and temporal-extrapolation tasks based on the atomic mass evaluation (AME2016 and AME2020), the complex additive-multiplicative product-unit gated recurrent unit (AM-PU-GRU) model consistently achieves the lowest prediction errors, with an interpolation RMSE of 0.227 $\pm$ 0.004 MeV and an extrapolation RMSE of 0.179 $\pm$ 0.015 MeV. These results surpass other state-of-the-art machine learning models and also outperform the real-valued GRU baseline and product-unit ablation variants, while remaining robust to different theoretical priors, including WS4 and SEMF. Our findings establish complex-valued product-unit recurrent networks as a new benchmark for sequence-based nuclear-mass prediction.

AI.tldr: A complex-valued GRU variant with multiplicative interactions and product-unit activations (AM-PU-GRU) achieves state-of-the-art nuclear mass prediction, with interpolation RMSE of 0.227 MeV and extrapolation RMSE of 0.179 MeV, outperforming both real-valued baselines and other ML models.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of atomic nuclear masses is essential for exploring poorly charted regions of the nuclear landscape. Machine learning can complement physics-based theoretical models, but existing approaches have limitations in capturing long-range dependencies and complex-valued dynamics inherent to nuclear data.
AI.method: The authors propose a complex-valued additive-multiplicative product-unit gated recurrent unit (AM-PU-GRU). This architecture integrates multiplicative interactions and product-unit transformations into GRU recurrent units, with all computations performed in the complex domain to jointly model amplitude and phase. Evaluations cover interpolation and temporal extrapolation tasks using the AME2016 and AME2020 datasets, with robustness tested against different theoretical priors (WS4, SEMF).
AI.result: The AM-PU-GRU achieves an interpolation RMSE of 0.227 ± 0.004 MeV and an extrapolation RMSE of 0.179 ± 0.015 MeV, surpassing state-of-the-art ML models, the real-valued GRU baseline, and product-unit ablation variants. Performance remains robust across different theoretical priors.
AI.conclusion: Complex-valued product-unit recurrent networks establish a new benchmark for sequence-based nuclear-mass prediction, demonstrating that jointly modeling amplitude and phase dynamics with multiplicative and product-unit operations significantly improves predictive accuracy.

title: Zero-Shot Embedding Drift Detection: A Lightweight Defense Against Prompt Injections in LLMs
summary: Prompt injection attacks have become an increasing vulnerability for LLM applications, where adversarial prompts exploit indirect input channels such as emails or user-generated content to circumvent alignment safeguards and induce harmful or unintended outputs. Despite advances in alignment, even state-of-the-art LLMs remain broadly vulnerable to adversarial prompts, underscoring the urgent need for robust, productive, and generalizable detection mechanisms beyond inefficient, model-specific patches. In this work, we propose Zero-Shot Embedding Drift Detection (ZEDD), a lightweight, low-engineering-overhead framework that identifies both direct and indirect prompt injection attempts by quantifying semantic shifts in embedding space between benign and suspect inputs. ZEDD operates without requiring access to model internals, prior knowledge of attack types, or task-specific retraining, enabling efficient zero-shot deployment across diverse LLM architectures. Our method uses adversarial-clean prompt pairs and measures embedding drift via cosine similarity to capture subtle adversarial manipulations inherent to real-world injection attacks. To ensure robust evaluation, we assemble and re-annotate the comprehensive LLMail-Inject dataset spanning five injection categories derived from publicly available sources. Extensive experiments demonstrate that embedding drift is a robust and transferable signal, outperforming traditional methods in detection accuracy and operational efficiency. With greater than 93% accuracy in classifying prompt injections across model architectures like Llama 3, Qwen 2, and Mistral and a false positive rate of <3%, our approach offers a lightweight, scalable defense layer that integrates into existing LLM pipelines, addressing a critical gap in securing LLM-powered systems to withstand adaptive adversarial threats.

AI.tldr: ZEDD is a zero-shot framework that detects prompt injection attacks by measuring semantic embedding drift between benign and suspect inputs via cosine similarity, achieving >93% accuracy with <3% false positive rate across Llama 3, Qwen 2, and Mistral without requiring model internals or retraining.
AI.motivation: LLM applications face growing vulnerability to prompt injection attacks through indirect input channels (emails, user content), and even state-of-the-art aligned models remain susceptible. Current defenses are inefficient, model-specific patches lacking robustness and generalizability, creating an urgent need for a lightweight, architecture-agnostic detection mechanism.
AI.method: ZEDD uses adversarial-clean prompt pairs and quantifies embedding drift via cosine similarity to detect semantic shifts characteristic of injection attacks. It operates zero-shot: no access to model internals, no prior attack-type knowledge, and no task-specific retraining. Evaluation uses the re-annotated LLMail-Inject dataset spanning five injection categories.
AI.result: Embedding drift proves to be a robust and transferable signal. ZEDD achieves >93% classification accuracy and <3% false positive rate across Llama 3, Qwen 2, and Mistral architectures, outperforming traditional detection methods in both accuracy and operational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: ZEDD offers a lightweight, scalable defense layer that integrates into existing LLM pipelines, effectively addressing a critical gap in securing LLM-powered systems against adaptive adversarial threats.

title: Contrastive Training with LLM-generated Near-Misses for Robust Code-Switching Speech Recognition
summary: Code-switching (CS), the alternation between multiple languages within a single utterance, remains challenging for Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR). To address this issue, we propose a Point-of-Interest (POI)-aware contrastive training framework that improves recognition at CS-critical regions. We first identify CS spans by adopting POI detection method from literature, then construct acoustically plausible near-miss hypotheses by perturbing POIs in ASR N-best outputs and expanding candidates with a large language model. Hard but plausible negatives are retained through filtering with acoustic, phonemic, and textual constraints. Finally, we fine-tune Whisper-small with LoRA using a POI-weighted cross-entropy anchor objective together with a multi-negative contrastive ranking loss. Experiments on CS-FLEURS (cmn-eng) and ViMedCSS (vie-eng) show consistent reductions of over 2% in both general and CS-aware error rates compared to standard LoRA fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Proposes a POI-aware contrastive training framework that identifies code-switching spans, generates hard negative examples via perturbation and LLM expansion, and fine-tunes Whisper-small with LoRA using a combined POI-weighted cross-entropy and multi-negative contrastive loss, achieving >2% error reduction on CS-FLEURS and ViMedCSS.
AI.motivation: Code-switching ASR suffers from degraded performance at language transition boundaries. Standard fine-tuning lacks explicit mechanisms to penalize confusions specifically at these critical CS regions, motivating a contrastive learning approach that focuses on these vulnerable spans.
AI.method: (1) Detect CS spans using an existing POI detection method. (2) Generate hard negatives by perturbing POIs in N-best ASR outputs and expanding candidates via LLM, filtered by acoustic, phonemic, and textual constraints. (3) Fine-tune Whisper-small with LoRA using a POI-weighted cross-entropy anchor loss combined with a multi-negative contrastive ranking loss that pushes representations away from these hard negatives at CS-critical regions.
AI.result: Consistent reductions of over 2% in both general and CS-aware error rates on CS-FLEURS (cmn-eng) and ViMedCSS (vie-eng) compared to standard LoRA fine-tuning baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed POI-aware contrastive training effectively improves ASR accuracy at code-switching boundaries by combining hard negative mining with region-specific contrastive objectives, demonstrating cross-lingual generalization across Mandarin-English and Vietnamese-English CS scenarios.

title: Evidence-Grounded Ensemble Diagnosis of 802.11 Packet Captures: A Multi-Stage Pipeline with Deterministic Reliability Scoring
summary:
Diagnosing 802.11 packet captures requires expert protocol knowledge, is slow, inconsistent across engineers, and unscalable. LLM-based approaches sound plausible but fabricate protocol events absent from captures (especially truncated traces), produce uncalibrated confidence scores, and suffer evaluation bias when golden references are co-produced by the model under test. We introduce PROBE (Protocol Reasoning Over evidence-Based Ensembles), a multi-stage pipeline addressing all three failures. It integrates (i) deterministic PCAP-to-text normalization with frame-level verifiability, (ii) multi-run, multi-candidate ensembles with optional cross-model second opinion and progressive obfuscation, (iii) a verdict-aware evidence framework treating absence of failure evidence as contributing evidence, and (iv) a fully deterministic composite reliability score from evidence validity, run-to-run stability, and cross-model agreement without LLM self-assessment.
  On 87 enterprise Wi-Fi captures (104 capture-reviewer pairs), single-pass LLM analysis raises weighted evidence F1 from 0.871 (expert baseline) to 0.912 but misses critical frames in 35% of cases. Naive ensemble voting drops below baseline (0.842) as majority voting amplifies conservative verdicts: 50% of confirmed failures are misclassified as 'no issue' or 'insufficient evidence.' Adding evidence-grounded reconciliation achieves 0.957 F1, a 96% auto-accept rate, and a worst-case floor above 0.70. LLM self-reported confidence clusters at 0.95 regardless of difficulty (71% report exactly 0.95), confirming it is uninformative. We also introduce a model-agnostic evaluation framework using per-field assertion matching, eliminating circular bias from model-co-produced golden references.

AI.tldr: PROBE is a multi-stage pipeline for diagnosing 802.11 packet captures that addresses LLM hallucinations, uncalibrated confidence, and circular evaluation bias. It combines deterministic PCAP-to-text normalization, multi-candidate ensembles with cross-model second opinions, and a deterministic composite reliability score, achieving 0.957 weighted evidence F1 (vs. 0.871 expert baseline) with a 96% auto-accept rate.
AI.motivation: Manual diagnosis of 802.11 packet captures is slow, inconsistent, and requires expert protocol knowledge. While LLMs seem promising, they suffer from three critical failures: (1) fabricating protocol events not present in captures (especially truncated traces), (2) producing uncalibrated confidence scores (e.g., 71% of cases report exactly 0.95), and (3) circular evaluation bias when golden references are co-produced by the model under test.
AI.method: PROBE integrates four components: (i) deterministic PCAP-to-text normalization with frame-level verifiability; (ii) multi-run, multi-candidate ensembles with optional cross-model second opinion and progressive obfuscation to mitigate hallucination; (iii) a verdict-aware evidence framework that treats absence of failure evidence as contributing evidence; and (iv) a fully deterministic composite reliability score derived from evidence validity, run-to-run stability, and cross-model agreement — deliberately avoiding LLM self-assessment. Evaluation uses a model-agnostic per-field assertion matching framework to eliminate circular bias.
AI.result: On 87 enterprise Wi-Fi captures (104 capture-reviewer pairs): single-pass LLM achieves 0.912 weighted evidence F1 (vs. 0.871 expert baseline) but misses critical frames in 35% of cases. Naive ensemble voting drops to 0.842 F1, with majority voting amplifying conservative verdicts — 50% of confirmed failures misclassified as "no issue" or "insufficient evidence." PROBE's evidence-grounded reconciliation achieves 0.957 F1, a 96% auto-accept rate, and a worst-case floor above 0.70. LLM self-reported confidence clusters at 0.95 regardless of difficulty, confirming it is uninformative.
AI.conclusion: PROBE demonstrates that evidence-grounded ensemble reconciliation, rather than naive voting or single-pass LLM analysis, is essential for reliable automated 802.11 diagnosis. The deterministic reliability score eliminates dependence on unreliable LLM self-assessment, and the model-agnostic evaluation framework resolves circular bias in benchmark construction.

title: Trading Engagement for Sustainability: Carbon-Aware Re-ranking for E-commerce Recommendations
summary: E-commerce recommender systems strongly influence which products users consider and purchase, yet sustainability signals such as Product Carbon Footprint (PCF) are almost never available at catalog scale. We study carbon-aware product recommendation in the realistic setting where PCF labels are missing for most items and must be inferred. We first estimate product-level carbon footprints via a retrieval-augmented PCF estimation pipeline that transfers supervision from the Carbon Catalogue, a small set of life-cycle-assessed products, to a large unlabeled e-commerce catalog using semantic similarity search, few-shot LLM prompting, and a nearest-neighbour fallback. We then apply a carbon-aware post-hoc re-ranking strategy on top of relevance scores produced by three established recommendation models: BPR, NeuMF, and LightGCN. The method trades off predicted user-item engagement against estimated carbon footprint through a single tunable parameter, lambda. In this offline study, engagement is operationalized through Amazon review interactions, which serve as implicit feedback and as a proxy for user interest or purchase behavior. We evaluate the framework on the Amazon Reviews dataset across three product categories: Home and Kitchen, Sports and Outdoors, and Electronics. By sweeping lambda, we construct Pareto frontiers that characterize the achievable engagement and carbon trade-off for each model and category. Substantial carbon reductions are achievable at minimal engagement cost across all models and categories. However, the available carbon headroom varies by model and category, underscoring the importance of model choice and domain context.

AI.tldr: This paper studies carbon-aware product recommendation when most items lack Product Carbon Footprint (PCF) labels. The authors propose a retrieval-augmented pipeline to estimate PCF for unlabeled products, then apply a tunable post-hoc re-ranking that trades off engagement against carbon footprint, demonstrating substantial carbon reductions at minimal engagement cost across three Amazon product categories using BPR, NeuMF, and LightGCN models.
AI.motivation: E-commerce recommender systems heavily shape consumer purchasing decisions, yet sustainability signals like Product Carbon Footprint are rarely available at catalog scale. The authors aim to bridge this gap by enabling carbon-aware recommendations in realistic settings where PCF labels are missing for most items, thereby allowing platforms to surface lower-carbon alternatives without sacrificing user engagement.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) PCF estimation via a retrieval-augmented pipeline that transfers supervision from the Carbon Catalogue (a small set of life-cycle-assessed products) to a large unlabeled catalog using semantic similarity search, few-shot LLM prompting, and a nearest-neighbor fallback; (2) carbon-aware post-hoc re-ranking that linearly combines predicted relevance scores (from BPR, NeuMF, LightGCN) with estimated PCF via a single tunable parameter λ. Engagement is operationalized using Amazon review interactions as implicit feedback.
AI.result: Across Home and Kitchen, Sports and Outdoors, and Electronics categories on the Amazon Reviews dataset, sweeping λ yields Pareto frontiers showing substantial carbon reductions are achievable at minimal engagement cost for all model–category combinations. However, the magnitude of feasible carbon reduction (carbon headroom) varies by both model choice and product category.
AI.conclusion: Carbon-aware re-ranking is a practical and effective approach for reducing the environmental footprint of e-commerce recommendations without significant engagement loss. The variability in carbon headroom across models and domains highlights that both algorithmic selection and category context are critical to maximizing sustainability impact.

title: Principles of Concept Representation in Sentence Encoders
summary: What makes a sentence encoder produce good concept representations? We approach this through the lens of representational compositionality: an encoder supports a concept family only when its latent space admits a low-distortion realization of the corresponding semantic operator. This framing predicts both where current encoders succeed and where they are structurally mismatched to their supervision. Through a controlled ablation over encoder conditions trained on 3.3 million synonym and definition pairs from WordNet and Wiktionary, evaluated on three decontaminated splits and a modifier-labeled noun-phrase benchmark, we identify four principles. Fine-tuning recalibrates the latent geometry rather than expanding it (P1). Semantic signal concentrates in the final transformer layer before concept-specific training begins, making cross-layer pooling redundant (P2). Hard negatives improve discrimination and stress-test robustness without improving retrieval ranking, showing that calibration and ranking are independently addressable (P3). Finally, the effectiveness of supervision depends on the composition type of the target concept. Extensional training helps intersective and subsective families while degrading relational and intensional ones, exposing a structural limitation of current training paradigms (P4). We release two new evaluation datasets: a DBpedia semantic-gap benchmark and a modifier-labeled NP paraphrase suite.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates why sentence encoders succeed or fail at learning concept representations through the lens of representational compositionality. Through controlled experiments on 3.3M synonym/definition pairs, the authors identify four principles: fine-tuning recalibrates rather than expands latent geometry (P1), semantic signal concentrates in the final layer making cross-layer pooling unnecessary (P2), hard negatives improve discrimination independently of retrieval ranking (P3), and extensional supervision helps intersective/subsective concepts but degrades relational/intensional ones (P4). Two new evaluation datasets are released.
AI.motivation: Current sentence encoders exhibit inconsistent performance across different concept types, and it is unclear what architectural and training factors make an encoder produce good concept representations. The authors frame this through representational compositionality — the idea that a latent space must admit a low-distortion realization of the semantic operator corresponding to a concept family — to systematically diagnose where and why existing encoders fail.
AI.method: The authors conduct a controlled ablation study over sentence encoder training conditions using 3.3 million synonym and definition pairs from WordNet and Wiktionary. They evaluate on three decontaminated data splits and a modifier-labeled noun-phrase benchmark. The analysis examines latent geometry changes, cross-layer information distribution, the role of hard negatives, and how different supervision types (extensional vs. intensional) interact with different concept composition types (intersective, subsective, relational, intensional).
AI.result: Four principles emerge: (P1) Fine-tuning recalibrates latent geometry rather than expanding it. (P2) Semantic signal concentrates in the final transformer layer before concept-specific training, making cross-layer pooling redundant. (P3) Hard negatives improve discrimination and stress-test robustness but do not improve retrieval ranking, indicating that calibration and ranking are independently addressable. (P4) Extensional training helps intersective and subsective concept families while degrading relational and intensional ones, revealing a structural limitation of current training paradigms.
AI.conclusion: The effectiveness of sentence encoders for concept representation is fundamentally constrained by the match between supervision type and the composition type of the target concept. Current training paradigms are structurally mismatched for relational and intensional concepts. The findings also yield practical guidance: cross-layer pooling is unnecessary, hard negatives should be tuned for the specific downstream task (discrimination vs. retrieval), and fine-tuning primarily recalibrates rather than enriches the latent space. Two new benchmarks (DBpedia semantic-gap and modifier-labeled NP paraphrase suite) are released to support further research.

title: GlucoFM-Bench: Benchmarking Time-Series Foundation Models for Blood Glucose Forecasting
summary: Blood glucose forecasting models are foundational for modern diabetes management systems, as reliable short-term predictions can enable proactive interventions, support automated insulin delivery, and reduce the risk of hypo- and hyperglycemic events. From a modeling perspective, glucose forecasting poses unique challenges due to heterogeneous physiological dynamics across diabetes populations. Traditional machine learning and deep learning models have been extensively evaluated for glucose prediction, yet recent time-series foundation models (TSFMs) remain much less studied in this setting. To bridge this gap, we present GlucoFM-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark evaluating state-of-the-art TSFMs alongside supervised deep learning models for blood glucose forecasting. We assess eight representative architectures, including pre-trained TSFMs, time-series large language models, and task-specific deep learning models, across 15 publicly available diabetes-relevant datasets comprising 1,117 individuals with type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, prediabetes, and no diabetes. Models are evaluated under zero-shot, few-shot, and full-shot protocols, with systematic variation in context length and prediction horizon. Across datasets, pre-trained TSFMs, especially Chronos-2 and TimesFM, show strong zero-shot and few-shot transfer, with the best zero-shot model performing within 5% of the best full-shot supervised model. Yet, when task-specific data are abundant, a lightweight LSTM remains strongest, outperforming TSFMs by 4--21% under full-shot training. Stratified analyses reveal persistent challenges in T1D cohorts and hypo-/hyperglycemic ranges, highlighting the need for evaluation beyond aggregate error metrics. Together, GlucoFM-Bench provides a standardized and reproducible foundation for evaluating, comparing, and improving foundation models for blood glucose forecasting.

AI.tldr: GlucoFM-Bench is a comprehensive benchmark evaluating time-series foundation models (TSFMs) and supervised deep learning models for blood glucose forecasting across 15 datasets. Pre-trained TSFMs (especially Chronos-2, TimesFM) excel in zero-shot/few-shot transfer, approaching fully supervised performance, but a lightweight LSTM remains strongest when abundant task-specific data are available. Hypo-/hyperglycemic ranges and T1D cohorts remain challenging.
AI.motivation: Despite the importance of glucose forecasting in diabetes management and the emergence of time-series foundation models (TSFMs), TSFMs remain largely unstudied for glucose prediction. A standardized benchmark is needed to systematically evaluate their capabilities against traditional supervised models across diverse diabetes populations and prediction protocols.
AI.method: The authors construct GlucoFM-Bench, evaluating 8 architectures (pre-trained TSFMs, time-series LLMs, task-specific deep learning models) on 15 public datasets (1,117 individuals: T1D, T2D, prediabetes, non-diabetic). Models are assessed under zero-shot, few-shot, and full-shot protocols with systematic variation in context length and prediction horizon.
AI.result: Pre-trained TSFMs (Chronos-2, TimesFM) achieve strong zero-shot transfer, within 5% of the best fully supervised model. However, under full-shot training with abundant data, a lightweight LSTM outperforms TSFMs by 4–21%. Stratified analysis reveals persistent difficulties in T1D cohorts and hypo-/hyperglycemic ranges.
AI.conclusion: TSFMs offer compelling zero-shot and few-shot capabilities for glucose forecasting, making them practical when labeled data are scarce. Yet, task-specific models like LSTMs remain superior with sufficient data. Future work should address performance in clinically critical glycemic ranges and T1D populations beyond aggregate metrics.

title: Human Adults and LLMs as Scientists: Who Benefits from Active Exploration?
comment: Accepted at the 48th Annual Conference of the Cognitive Science Society (CogSci 2026)
summary: A long-standing finding in the causal learning literature is that adults struggle to identify conjunctive causal rules, where an effect requires the simultaneous presence of multiple causes, while performing better in disjunctive settings. However, most demonstrations of this ``conjunctive handicap'' rely on passive observation paradigms with limited evidence, where learners have no control over evidence generation. This paper asks whether this bias persists when adults are granted agency through active exploration. Using a modified ``blicket detector'' task, adult participants freely intervened to identify causal objects under conjunctive or disjunctive rule structures. We show that active exploration substantially improves adults' conjunctive causal reasoning, although conjunctive rules still require more tests to infer than disjunctive rules. We further compare human performance to a range of large language models in the same setting. While some state-of-the-art models approach human-level performance on hypothesis inference accuracy, they often exhibit less efficient exploration strategies and similar conjunctive-disjunctive performance gaps.

AI.tldr: Adults struggle with conjunctive causal rules under passive observation, but active exploration significantly improves their conjunctive reasoning, though a performance gap remains compared to disjunctive rules. LLMs approach human-level inference accuracy but show less efficient exploration.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether the well-documented "conjunctive handicap" — adults' difficulty learning conjunctive causal rules relative to disjunctive ones — persists when learners have agency through active intervention, rather than being limited to passive observation.
AI.method: A modified "blicket detector" task where adult participants freely intervened to identify which objects are causal, under either conjunctive (AND) or disjunctive (OR) rule structures. Human performance was also benchmarked against several large language models (LLMs) in the same active exploration setting.
AI.result: Active exploration substantially improved conjunctive causal reasoning, though conjunctive rules still required more tests to infer than disjunctive rules. Some state-of-the-art LLMs approached human-level hypothesis inference accuracy but exhibited less efficient exploration strategies and a similar conjunctive-disjunctive performance gap.
AI.conclusion: Agency through active exploration mitigates but does not eliminate the conjunctive handicap in causal learning. This suggests that the bias is not solely an artifact of passive observation paradigms, and that LLMs, while accurate, do not yet replicate the efficiency of human active learning.

title: MADE: Beyond Scoring via a Multilingual Agentic Diagnosing Engine for Fine-Grained Evaluation Insights
summary: Multilingual and multicultural benchmarks now cover dozens of languages and model families, but the resulting score landscapes remain metric-rich and insight-poor, necessitating fine-grained multilingual post-evaluation diagnosis. However, single LLMs and open-ended agents are easily swamped by the long, noisy diagnostic input, and no reusable taxonomy exists for it. To address this, we propose MADE, a Multilingual Agentic Diagnosing Engine that decomposes post-evaluation analysis into planning, aggregate analysis, instance-level case inspection, multilingual and cultural reflection, and grounded report synthesis. MADE is paired with an expert-led 54-query and 15-language diagnostic set, evaluated on top of a large-scale multilingual evaluation substrate (33 model families, 11 benchmarks, 26 languages, 34 cultures, 8.66M evaluation records). Experiments show that MADE outperforms the strongest shared baseline by 47% in diagnosis report quality and is preferred by human multilingual experts in 87.9% of pairwise comparisons. Applied with multilingual experts, MADE further surfaces four actionable findings on deployment, iteration, and cross-cultural pitfalls, turning benchmark score tables into model-selection and remediation guidance.

AI.tldr: MADE is a multilingual agentic engine that transforms benchmark score tables into actionable diagnostic reports, outperforming baselines by 47% in quality and preferred by human experts in 87.9% of pairwise comparisons.
AI.motivation: Current multilingual benchmarks produce extensive score landscapes across dozens of languages and models, but these remain "metric-rich and insight-poor." There is no reusable taxonomy for fine-grained post-evaluation diagnosis, and single LLMs or open-ended agents are overwhelmed by the long, noisy diagnostic inputs.
AI.method: MADE decomposes post-evaluation analysis into five stages: (1) planning, (2) aggregate analysis, (3) instance-level case inspection, (4) multilingual and cultural reflection, and (5) grounded report synthesis. It is paired with an expert-curated 54-query, 15-language diagnostic set and evaluated on a large-scale substrate spanning 33 model families, 11 benchmarks, 26 languages, 34 cultures, and 8.66M evaluation records.
AI.result: MADE outperforms the strongest shared baseline by 47% in diagnosis report quality and is preferred by human multilingual experts in 87.9% of pairwise comparisons. When applied with multilingual experts, MADE surfaces four actionable findings on deployment, iteration, and cross-cultural pitfalls.
AI.conclusion: MADE successfully bridges the gap between metric-rich benchmarks and actionable insights, providing model-selection and remediation guidance for multilingual LLM deployment. The agentic decomposition with expert-in-the-loop design proves effective for complex multilingual evaluation scenarios.

title: Data-Constrained Language Model Pretraining: Improved Regularization and Scaling Laws
summary: Classical scaling laws for language model pretraining balance model size against training dataset size under a fixed compute budget, assuming abundant data and a single pass over the corpus. As training compute grows faster than the supply of natural language data, pretraining is likely to enter a data-constrained, compute-rich regime where models train for multiple epochs over a finite dataset. We study data-constrained pretraining along two axes, regularization and scaling. For regularization, we study masked-input regularization (MIR), an auxiliary next-token prediction loss on randomly masked inputs. MIR tests whether the random masking central to diffusion language models can benefit autoregressive pretraining without architectural changes or inference overhead. Across 72M to 1.4B parameter models, we find that MIR added on top of strong weight decay improves validation loss over autoregressive strong-weight-decay-only models, with downstream gains at 1.4B. For scaling, we propose SoftQ, a scaling law that couples model size and data size to capture their interaction under repeated data. Classical alternatives such as the Chinchilla law use an additive form that decouples these terms, making them misspecified in the data-constrained regime. We find that SoftQ fits data-constrained experiments substantially better than these alternatives, and estimates MIR's gains as equivalent to roughly 1.3 times as much unique training data. We release our code at https://github.com/yixinw-lab/dc_pretrain.

AI.tldr: This paper studies data-constrained pretraining where models train for multiple epochs over finite data. It proposes (1) Masked-Input Regularization (MIR), an auxiliary loss using random masking that improves autoregressive pretraining, and (2) SoftQ, a scaling law that properly captures the interaction between model size and data size under repeated data, outperforming additive laws like Chinchilla.
AI.motivation: As training compute grows faster than the supply of natural language data, pretraining will increasingly operate in a data-constrained, compute-rich regime where models must train for multiple epochs over finite datasets. Existing scaling laws (e.g., Chinchilla) assume abundant data and a single pass, making them misspecified for this emerging regime.
AI.method: The paper investigates two directions: (1) MIR — an auxiliary next-token prediction loss applied to randomly masked inputs, testing whether diffusion-style random masking benefits autoregressive pretraining without architectural changes; (2) SoftQ — a new scaling law formulation that couples model size and data size multiplicatively (rather than additively) to capture their interaction under repeated data epochs.
AI.result: MIR combined with strong weight decay improves validation loss over weight-decay-only baselines across 72M–1.4B parameter models, with downstream task gains at the 1.4B scale. SoftQ fits data-constrained experiments substantially better than additive alternatives like Chinchilla, and estimates MIR's improvement as equivalent to roughly 1.3× more unique training data.
AI.conclusion: In the data-constrained regime, regularization via MIR provides consistent gains, and SoftQ offers a more accurate scaling law by properly modeling the model-data interaction under repeated data. Both contributions address critical gaps as pretraining moves beyond the single-epoch, data-abundant paradigm.

title: Beyond Rubrics: Exploration-Guided Evaluation Skills for Reward Modeling
summary: Open-ended reward modeling requires judges that can follow subtle, domain-specific preferences when verifiable answers are unavailable. Existing rubric-based methods often address this by generating criteria online for each query, but the extra generation step can add inference overhead and produce rigid or misaligned guidance. We introduce Eval-Skill, an exploration-guided method that synthesizes reusable evaluation skills for reward modeling and reframes reward guidance as context evolution rather than parameter training or per-query rubric generation. Using only 100 cases per domain for skill evolution, Eval-Skill synthesizes reusable domain-level evaluation skills through two progressive stages, workflow generation followed by principle generation, with exploration and selection interleaved across both stages. Once generated, a skill is directly injected into the judge context. Across multiple RM benchmarks, Eval-Skill consistently improves diverse judge backbones; on RewardBench 2, it yields significant gains over vanilla judging for each main backbone (+13.44% for Qwen3-8B, and 18.51% for DeepSeek-V4-Flash). Further analyses of evolution-time scaling, generalizability, and transferability show that compact evaluation skills offer an efficient new paradigm for LLM-based evaluation. Code is available at https://github.com/xing-stellus-yue/Eval-Skill.

AI.tldr: Eval-Skill proposes an exploration-guided method that synthesizes reusable, domain-level evaluation skills for reward modeling, reframing reward guidance as context evolution rather than per-query rubric generation or parameter training. Using only 100 cases per domain, it produces compact skills through two-stage synthesis (workflow then principle generation) with interleaved exploration and selection, achieving substantial gains on RewardBench 2 (e.g., +13.44% for Qwen3-8B, +18.51% for DeepSeek-V4-Flash).
AI.motivation: Open-ended reward modeling requires judges capable of following subtle, domain-specific preferences when verifiable answers are unavailable. Existing rubric-based methods generate criteria online per query, which adds inference overhead and often yields rigid or misaligned guidance. The authors seek a more efficient, reusable alternative that avoids both per-query generation cost and parameter training.
AI.method: Eval-Skill synthesizes reusable evaluation skills through two progressive, exploration-guided stages: (1) workflow generation — producing structured procedural steps for evaluating a domain, and (2) principle generation — distilling core evaluation principles. Both stages interleave exploration (generating candidate skills) and selection (filtering for quality). The entire process uses only 100 cases per domain. Once generated, the skill is directly injected into the judge's context at inference time, requiring no additional per-query rubric generation or model fine-tuning.
AI.result: On RewardBench 2, Eval-Skill yields significant improvements over vanilla judging across multiple judge backbones: +13.44% for Qwen3-8B and +18.51% for DeepSeek-V4-Flash. Additional analyses demonstrate favorable evolution-time scaling properties, strong generalizability across domains, and transferability across different judge models.
AI.conclusion: Compact, reusable evaluation skills represent an efficient new paradigm for LLM-based evaluation, offering a practical middle ground between costly per-query rubric generation and parameter-based training. The exploration-guided synthesis approach is both data-efficient and backbone-agnostic, with strong empirical performance on reward modeling benchmarks.

title: GRASP: Geometry-aware Residual Alignment for Scalable Pretraining Data Attribution
summary: Scalable data attribution methods typically assign isolated utility scores to individual training examples. This prevalent additive assumption fundamentally fails to capture critical subset dynamics, including data redundancy and complementary coverage. In this work, we reframe attribution as subset-level counterfactual utility prediction and introduce GRASP, an interaction-aware surrogate. Grounded in a theoretical smoothness lower bound, GRASP explicitly models subset interactions through a quadratic geometric penalty. To achieve pretraining-scale efficiency without relying on hidden oracle tuning, we couple low-dimensional feature sketches with a strictly finite lower-confidence bound selection protocol. Extensive subset-retraining evaluations demonstrate that GRASP decisively outperforms existing scalable baselines. It more than doubles the task-level rank correlation for counterfactual subset fidelity while reducing upfront artifact construction costs by nearly an order of magnitude. Downstream diagnostics further show that this scoring mechanism transfers to language model curation and cross-domain vision selection, establishing a robust foundation for optimizing massive pretraining corpora.

AI.tldr: GRASP reframes data attribution as subset-level counterfactual utility prediction using a quadratic geometric penalty to model interactions, outperforming additive baselines by >2x in rank correlation while reducing construction costs ~10x.
AI.motivation: Existing scalable data attribution methods rely on an additive assumption, assigning isolated scores to individual examples. This fails to capture critical subset dynamics like data redundancy and complementary coverage, leading to poor counterfactual fidelity when selecting or removing subsets of training data.
AI.method: GRASP is an interaction-aware surrogate that models subset interactions via a quadratic geometric penalty, grounded in a theoretical smoothness lower bound. It uses low-dimensional feature sketches for efficiency and a strictly finite lower-confidence bound (LCB) selection protocol to avoid hidden oracle tuning, enabling pretraining-scale operation.
AI.result: GRASP more than doubles task-level rank correlation for counterfactual subset fidelity compared to scalable baselines. It also reduces upfront artifact construction costs by nearly an order of magnitude. The scoring mechanism transfers successfully to language model curation and cross-domain vision selection.
AI.conclusion: The subset-interaction-aware approach establishes a robust foundation for optimizing massive pretraining corpora. GRASP's efficiency and fidelity gains suggest that moving beyond additive attribution is both feasible and necessary for practical data curation at scale.

title: TALAN: Task-Aligned Latent Adaptation Networks for Targeted Post-Training of Large Language Models
summary:
Targeted post-training aims to improve reasoning, math, and code without degrading strengths. Low-rank adapters are efficient but task-global; activation interventions are input-aware but often require separate probes, vectors, or inference-time steering. We introduce TALAN (Task-Aligned Latent Adaptation Networks), a sequence-conditioned latent side path inserted into a transformer's residual stream and co-trained with a low-rank adapter in one SFT loop. TALAN compresses the active sequence into latent memory, remixes it into token-level perturbations, and writes them back through a controlled residual update. It is configured along six axes: insertion location, memory size, mixer, writeback rule, trainability scope, and gradient scale.
  Across four Qwen3-family backbones and four STEM/code benchmarks, TALAN improves matched LoRA and DoRA baselines. With LoRA, it yields a +1.41 point cross-model mean gain, positive on all four backbones and non-negative on all 16 model-benchmark cells. With DoRA, it yields a +1.85 point mean gain, positive on all backbones and on 13 of 16 cells. Paired seed checks support positive average effects but show nontrivial variance, so we treat them as sensitivity checks. Cost is small: <1% trainable parameters relative to the backbone and 1.01-1.02x inference overhead versus matched LoRA. A Llama-3.2-1B transfer probe is also positive under LoRA and rsLoRA across seven paired seeds, supporting a transfer beyond Qwen.
  Internal-state analyses suggest TALAN is a small complementary activation intervention. The matched adapter update is 80-1,700x larger than the TALAN perturbation, yet their directions have near-zero cosine; per-layer measurements show this small orthogonal perturbation propagates and amplifies through depth. TALAN offers a practical platform for studying steerable activation-level adaptation within standard adapter-based post-training.

AI.tldr: TALAN introduces a sequence-conditioned latent side path co-trained with low-rank adapters, yielding consistent gains (+1.41–1.85 points) on STEM/code benchmarks across Qwen3 backbones with <1% parameter overhead and ~1.01x inference cost.
AI.motivation: Low-rank adapters (LoRA/DoRA) are efficient for post-training but operate globally across all inputs, while activation interventions are input-aware but require separate probes or inference-time steering. The authors seek a unified method that is both input-conditioned and trainable within a single SFT loop without sacrificing the simplicity of adapter-based pipelines.
AI.method: TALAN inserts a task-aligned latent adapter network into the transformer residual stream. It compresses the active sequence into a latent memory, remixes it via a mixer into token-level perturbations, and writes them back through a controlled residual update. Six configurable axes govern its behavior: insertion location, memory size, mixer type, writeback rule, trainability scope, and gradient scale. The entire module is co-trained with a low-rank adapter (LoRA or DoRA) in one SFT loop.
AI.result: Across four Qwen3-family backbones and four STEM/code benchmarks, TALAN+LoRA yields +1.41 mean gain (positive on all 16 model-benchmark cells); TALAN+DoRA yields +1.85 mean gain (positive on 13/16 cells). Paired-seed analyses confirm positive average effects with nontrivial variance. A Llama-3.2-1B transfer probe under LoRA/rsLoRA across seven seeds is also positive. Internal analyses reveal the TALAN perturbation is 80–1,700× smaller than the adapter update, yet near-orthogonal in direction and amplifies through depth.
AI.conclusion: TALAN provides a practical, low-cost platform for studying input-conditioned activation-level adaptation within standard adapter-based post-training. Its small, orthogonal perturbations propagate through layers, suggesting it functions as a complementary steering signal rather than a redundant parameter expansion.

title: Modeling semantic association in self-paced reading with language model embeddings
summary: Semantic association between a word and its context has been identified as an important component of reading comprehension, even when word predictability is accounted for. Recent research has highlighted the potential of language model ( LM) embeddings to quantify semantic association. Yet, embedding-based semantic association have been operationalized in a myriad of ways. In this study, we use embeddings from LMs to estimate semantic association on a corpus of joint electroencephalography (EEG) and self-paced reading of natural, Dutch texts. Semantic association is calculated in ten different implementations that vary the embedding model and context lengths. The effects of semantic association across the different implementations on the N400 and self-paced reading times are examined using Bayesian hierarchical models and Bayes factor. The results show that the choice of embedding model can alter the estimated effect of semantic association on both the N400 and self-paced reading times. Furthermore, the results demonstrate a promising potential of sentence embeddings for capturing semantic association, as only implementations relying on sentence embeddings indicate reliable results of semantic association beyond word predictability on both neural and behavioral measures. Together, these findings highlight the importance of methodological choices in quantifying semantic association.

AI.tldr: This study compares ten different implementations of embedding-based semantic association (varying language model and context length) to predict N400 amplitude and self-paced reading times. Results show that methodological choices matter: only sentence-embedding implementations reliably capture semantic association effects beyond word predictability on both neural and behavioral measures.
AI.motivation: Semantic association (the relationship between a word and its context) is known to influence reading comprehension beyond word predictability, but embedding-based operationalizations of semantic association vary widely across studies. There is no consensus on which embedding model or context length best captures this construct, nor on how these methodological choices affect empirical results in neurolinguistic experiments.
AI.method: The authors used a corpus of joint EEG and self-paced reading data from natural Dutch texts. They computed semantic association using ten different implementations that systematically varied the embedding model (e.g., static embeddings, transformer-based models) and context length (ranging from local to sentence-level). Bayesian hierarchical models and Bayes factors were used to evaluate each implementation's effect on N400 amplitude and self-paced reading times, controlling for word predictability.
AI.result: The choice of embedding model significantly alters the estimated effect of semantic association on both N400 and reading times. Critically, only implementations relying on sentence embeddings yielded reliable effects of semantic association beyond word predictability on both neural (N400) and behavioral (reading times) measures.
