AI.result: Multivariate linear regression yielded R² = 0.33, indicating that 33% of the variance in win rate is explained by the three experimental design factors (#Datasets, #Baselines, #NewMethods). This is considered a medium effect size.
AI.conclusion: The medium R² is promising for a first-of-its-kind study but implies that additional factors—such as domain maturity, dataset/baseline types, and regression model simplicity—also influence win rates. The authors synthesize lessons learned into five evidence-based recommendations for future FFS evaluation practices.

title: SV-Detect: AI-generated Text Detection with Steering Vectors
summary: Detecting machine-generated text is especially difficult under distribution shift, such as transfer across domains, source models, and editing attacks. We propose a fake-text detector based on steering vectors extracted from the hidden representations of a frozen language model. At each layer, we construct a direction that separates human-written from machine-generated text, and represent each input by its layer-wise alignment with these directions. A lightweight classifier trained on these projection features yields the final detection score. Our method achieves strong performance both in-distribution and under distribution shift, including across domains, source models, and machine-editing transformations such as polishing and rewriting. Interpretation analyses show that the learned directions align with recognizable stylistic cues while capturing substantial additional signal beyond surface features. These results position fake-text detection as a representation-space probing problem and show that steering vectors provide a simple and effective solution.

AI.tldr: A fake-text detector that uses steering vectors from a frozen language model's hidden representations, trained as a lightweight classifier on layer-wise projection features, achieves strong in-distribution and distribution-shift robustness across domains, source models, and editing attacks.
AI.motivation: Machine-generated text detection suffers under distribution shift—when domains, source models, or editing attacks (polishing, rewriting) change. Existing detectors often fail to generalize, motivating a method that captures robust, transferable signals from model representations.
AI.method: The method extracts steering vectors from each layer of a frozen language model: for each layer, a direction is constructed that maximally separates human-written from machine-generated text. Each input sample is then represented by its projection (alignment) onto these layer-wise directions. A lightweight classifier is trained on these projection features to produce the final detection score.
AI.result: The detector achieves strong performance both in-distribution and under distribution shift (cross-domain, cross-source-model, and under machine-editing transformations like polishing and rewriting). Interpretation analyses reveal that the learned directions align with recognizable stylistic cues while capturing substantial signal beyond surface-level features.
AI.conclusion: Fake-text detection can be effectively framed as a representation-space probing problem, and steering vectors offer a simple, effective solution with strong generalization properties.

title: SlimSearcher: Training Efficiency-Aware Web Agents via Adaptive Reward Gating
summary: Deep research agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in complex information-seeking tasks, yet this power comes at a steep computational cost. Driven by accuracy-focused training paradigms, current models adopt brute-force strategies characterized by blind tool dependency and performative reasoning-generating long, redundant trajectories that are far from necessary for resolving these tasks, leading to wasteful tool calls and excessive token consumption. To overcome this efficiency trap, we propose SlimSearcher, a principled framework that pushes the Pareto frontier between accuracy and computational cost across both Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL). In the SFT stage, SlimSearcher employs Pareto-efficient filtration to distill trajectories that are both successful and economical, guiding the model toward inherently efficiency-aware search behaviors. During RL, we introduce Adaptive Reward Gating, a dynamic reward-shaping mechanism that evaluates relative tool and token efficiency within a sampled cohort. By cascading these adaptive efficiency metrics with a strict correctness gate, our approach effectively avoids the brevity bias associated with absolute penalties and mitigates reward hacking. Extensive experiments on long-horizon benchmarks, including GAIA, BrowseComp, and XBenchDeepSearch, demonstrate that SlimSearcher reduces average tool-call rounds by 17%-58% while maintaining or improving accuracy.

AI.tldr: SlimSearcher introduces Pareto-efficient filtration (SFT) and Adaptive Reward Gating (RL) to train deep research agents that maintain accuracy while cutting tool calls by 17-58% and reducing token waste from brute-force, performative reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current deep research agents, trained under accuracy-focused paradigms, exhibit brute-force strategies with blind tool dependency and performative reasoning, generating excessively long, redundant trajectories. This leads to wasteful tool calls and token overconsumption, creating an efficiency trap where computational cost is disproportionately high relative to task requirements.
AI.method: SlimSearcher combines two stages: (1) In SFT, Pareto-efficient filtration selects trajectories that are both successful and economical, distilling efficiency-aware behaviors. (2) In RL, Adaptive Reward Gating provides a dynamic reward-shaping mechanism that evaluates relative tool and token efficiency within a sampled cohort, cascaded with a strict correctness gate to avoid brevity bias from absolute penalties and prevent reward hacking.
AI.result: Experiments on GAIA, BrowseComp, and XBenchDeepSearch benchmarks show SlimSearcher reduces average tool-call rounds by 17%-58% while maintaining or improving accuracy compared to baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: SlimSearcher successfully pushes the Pareto frontier between accuracy and computational cost for deep research agents, demonstrating that principled efficiency-aware training (via Pareto SFT filtering and Adaptive Reward Gating in RL) can eliminate wasteful behaviors without compromising task performance.

title: LLM-Guided Evolution for Medical Decision Pipelines
summary:
Adapting large language models (LLMs) to clinical workflows often requires costly fine-tuning or manual prompt and pipeline engineering. We study LLM-guided MAP-Elites evolution as an inference-time alternative for discovering medical decision strategies and provide an implementation repository at https://github.com/univanxx/llm_guided_evo_medical. We formulate urgency triage, interactive consultation, and medical image classification as evolutionary searches over executable artifacts optimized by task-specific fitness functions.
  Across all three settings, evolution improves over manually designed baselines under practical constraints. In triage, evolved programs increase Semigran accuracy from $77.3\%$ to $87.1\%$ and emergency recall from $0.60$ to $0.97$, while improving safety-weighted held-out MIMIC-ESI performance. In interactive consultation, evolved policies improve the accuracy--cost frontier across Llama-3, Qwen-3.5, and Gemma-4 and transfer to held-out iCRAFTMD. In PneumoniaMNIST, prompt-only evolution improves frozen MedGemma VLMs while preserving strict JSON outputs. Qualitative analysis shows that the gains come from interpretable program-level mechanisms, calibrated triage boundaries, targeted evidence acquisition, selective commitment, and finding-oriented visual decision rules, rather than superficial prompt rewording alone.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using LLM-guided MAP-Elites evolutionary search at inference time as an alternative to costly fine-tuning for adapting LLMs to clinical workflows. The method evolves executable artifacts (programs/prompts) optimized by task-specific fitness functions across three medical settings: urgency triage, interactive consultation, and medical image classification, consistently outperforming manually designed baselines.
AI.motivation: Adapting LLMs to clinical workflows typically requires expensive fine-tuning or labor-intensive manual prompt and pipeline engineering. The authors seek an inference-time alternative that can automatically discover effective medical decision strategies without these costs.
AI.method: LLM-guided MAP-Elites evolution: a quality-diversity evolutionary algorithm where an LLM generates and mutates executable artifacts (programs, prompts, policies). These artifacts are evaluated by task-specific fitness functions and organized into a behavior-performance grid. The approach is applied to three clinical tasks: urgency triage (evolving triage programs), interactive consultation (evolving acquisition policies for clinical dialogues), and medical image classification (evolving visual prompts for frozen VLMs).
AI.result: In triage, evolved programs improved Semigran accuracy from 77.3% to 87.1% and emergency recall from 0.60 to 0.97, with improved safety-weighted MIMIC-ESI performance. In consultation, evolved policies improved the accuracy-cost frontier across Llama-3, Qwen-3.5, and Gemma-4 and transferred to held-out iCRAFTMD. In PneumoniaMNIST, prompt-only evolution improved a frozen MedGemma VLM while preserving strict JSON output formatting. Gains stemmed from interpretable mechanisms: calibrated triage boundaries, targeted evidence acquisition, selective commitment, and finding-oriented visual decision rules.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided MAP-Elites evolution is a viable inference-time alternative to fine-tuning for clinical LLM adaptation, producing interpretable, transferable, and effective decision strategies across diverse medical tasks under practical constraints.

title: On the Geometry of On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) is increasingly used to improve large language model reasoning, but its training dynamics remain poorly understood. We characterize the trajectory of OPD updates in parameter space and compare it with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). A suite of parameter-space diagnostics consistently places OPD in a relaxed off-principal regime: compared with SFT, its updates affect fewer weights and avoid principal directions more strongly, while compared with RLVR, they remain less tightly constrained. Beyond this static localization, OPD exhibits subspace locking: its cumulative updates rapidly enter a narrow low-dimensional channel. Constraining training to the update subspace formed early in training preserves OPD performance but substantially degrades SFT, indicating that the locked subspace is functionally sufficient for OPD. Control experiments further show that sparsifying the update tokens and shifting rollout generation off-policy preserve the rank dynamics, whereas mixing the OPD objective with RLVR changes them. Overall, these results suggest that OPD is not merely an intermediate point between SFT and RLVR, but induces its own update geometry in parameter space.

AI.tldr: On-policy distillation (OPD) exhibits a distinct parameter-space geometry—characterized by low-rank updates confined to a narrow subspace that "locks" early in training—which is functionally sufficient for performance and distinguishes it from both SFT and RLVR.
AI.motivation: Despite the growing use of on-policy distillation for improving LLM reasoning, its training dynamics in parameter space remain poorly understood; the authors aim to characterize these dynamics and determine whether OPD is merely an intermediate between SFT and RLVR or has its own distinct update geometry.
AI.method: The authors employ a suite of parameter-space diagnostics—including analyses of weight update sparsity, principal direction avoidance, effective rank, and cumulative update trajectories—to compare OPD, SFT, and RLVR. They introduce the concept of "subspace locking," testing whether constraining training to the early-formed update subspace preserves or degrades performance. Control experiments manipulate update token sparsity, rollout generation regime, and objective mixing to probe causal factors.
AI.result: OPD updates affect fewer weights and avoid principal directions more strongly than SFT, yet are less constrained than RLVR. OPD cumulative updates rapidly enter a narrow low-dimensional channel ("subspace locking"). Constraining training to this early subspace preserves OPD performance but degrades SFT. Sparsifying update tokens and shifting to off-policy rollouts preserve OPD rank dynamics, while mixing with RLVR changes them.
AI.conclusion: OPD is not merely an intermediate between SFT and RLVR but induces its own distinct update geometry in parameter space, with subspace locking being a defining feature that is functionally sufficient for its performance.

title: M$^3$Exam: Benchmarking Multimodal Memory for Realistic User-Agent Interactions
summary: Language agents are increasingly deployed over accumulating multimodal information, yet existing benchmarks assume a human-human form with sparse visuals and straightforward content, evaluating neither reasoning over authentic multimodal file interaction nor the interpretation of concealed user information. We therefore introduce M$^3$Exam, a query-centric multimodal conversational memory benchmark built on realistic user-agent interaction, with multi-dimensional evaluation spanning cross-modal grounding and implicit information inference. Benchmarking MLLMs and memory systems reveals persistent gaps in cross-modal grounding, cross session reasoning, and the efficiency cost of accumulating multimodal context. We further propose M$^3$Proctor, a multimodal memory method that detects query modality bias and consumes raw visual sources only on demand, improving accuracy by 13% while cutting index-construction time and retrieved tokens by over 70%.

AI.tldr: M³Exam is a realistic multimodal conversational memory benchmark that reveals LLM limitations in cross-modal grounding and session reasoning, while M³Proctor improves accuracy by 13% and cuts retrieval costs by >70% via query-modality-aware, on-demand visual processing.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for language agents assume sparse visuals and straightforward human-human interactions, failing to evaluate authentic multimodal file interaction or the interpretation of implicit user information. There is a gap in assessing how agents handle accumulating multimodal context over conversational sessions.
AI.method: The authors introduce M³Exam, a query-centric multimodal conversational memory benchmark with multi-dimensional evaluation for cross-modal grounding and implicit information inference. They also propose M³Proctor, a multimodal memory method that detects query modality bias and retrieves raw visual sources only on demand, rather than pre-encoding all visual content.
AI.result: Benchmarking reveals persistent gaps in cross-modal grounding, cross-session reasoning, and efficiency costs when accumulating multimodal context. M³Proctor improves accuracy by 13% while reducing index-construction time and retrieved tokens by over 70%.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs and memory systems struggle with realistic multimodal conversational memory, particularly in cross-modal grounding and session-spanning reasoning. On-demand visual processing via M³Proctor offers a promising efficiency-accuracy trade-off for deploying language agents over multimodal information streams.

title: Residual-Controlled Multiplier Learning for Stochastic Constrained Decision-Making
summary: Stochastic constrained decision-making requires optimizing performance objectives while enforcing statistical requirements such as safety or fairness. However, standard primal--dual methods struggle to update multipliers robustly under stochastic mini-batch feedback, as the noise of mini-batch gradients and constraint estimates can be directly accumulated into the multiplier memory. To address this issue, we propose Residual-Controlled Multiplier Learning (RCML), which reformulates multiplier updating as projected-pressure feedback. The central idea is to decompose the projected multiplier into an effective pressure signal for primal descent and a pressure-memory residual for finite-gain multiplier tracking. To handle heterogeneous and noisy observations, we further augment this residual-integral backbone with modular stochastic stabilization components. For the convex-affine backbone, we establish finite-gain convergence, derive a stochastic residual bound under mini-batch feedback, and show that the residual feedback law admits a local KKT-residual interpretation near regular KKT points of nonconvex problems. Experiments across optimization, allocation, and fair-ranking tasks show that RCML improves feasibility control and multiplier stability while maintaining competitive objective performance. Code is available here.

AI.tldr: RCML proposes a residual-controlled multiplier learning method for stochastic constrained optimization, decomposing multiplier updates into pressure signals and memory residuals with stabilization components to handle noisy mini-batch feedback, improving feasibility and stability.
AI.motivation: Standard primal-dual methods suffer from noise accumulation in multiplier states when using stochastic mini-batch feedback, as gradient and constraint estimate noise directly corrupts the multiplier memory, leading to instability in enforcing statistical constraints like fairness or safety.
AI.method: RCML reformulates multiplier updating as projected-pressure feedback: the projected multiplier is decomposed into (1) an effective pressure signal driving primal descent, and (2) a pressure-memory residual enabling finite-gain multiplier tracking. Modular stochastic stabilization components augment this residual-integral backbone to handle heterogeneous, noisy observations. The convex-affine backbone yields finite-gain convergence and stochastic residual bounds, with a local KKT-residual interpretation near regular KKT points in nonconvex settings.
AI.result: Finite-gain convergence is established for the convex-affine backbone; a stochastic residual bound under mini-batch feedback is derived; the residual feedback law admits a local KKT-residual interpretation near regular KKT points of nonconvex problems. Experiments across optimization, allocation, and fair-ranking tasks demonstrate improved feasibility control, multiplier stability, and competitive objective performance.
AI.conclusion: RCML effectively addresses noise accumulation in multiplier updates under stochastic mini-batch settings, providing theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements in constrained decision-making tasks requiring statistical feasibility constraints.

title: The Masked Advantage: Uncovering Local-Language Access to Cultural Knowledge in LLMs
summary: Large language models are increasingly used to answer culturally grounded questions across languages, yet it remains unclear whether local cultural knowledge is better accessed through English or the local language. Existing evaluations face two key limitations: many rely on parallel template-based questions that may not reflect how cultural knowledge naturally appears, and raw accuracy conflates general language proficiency with language-conditioned knowledge access. We address these issues with a controlled framework built on real-world cultural questions collected from regional benchmarks and local sources. By crossing question type (culture-agnostic vs. culture-specific) with query language (English vs. local language), and estimating ability with a shared 1PL item response theory model, we separate proficiency from localized knowledge access. Across 13 locales and roughly 80 models, we find a consistent English advantage on culture-agnostic questions, indicating stronger English proficiency. However, after accounting for this proficiency gap, local languages show a positive knowledge-access advantage in nearly all locale-model settings. This advantage is often masked in raw accuracy but becomes more visible for frontier, regionally aligned, or language-adapted models. Our results suggest that weaker local-language performance does not necessarily imply weaker cultural knowledge; rather, local cultural knowledge may be more accessible through the local language but hidden by limited language proficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether local cultural knowledge in LLMs is better accessed through English or local languages, using a controlled IRT-based framework across 13 locales and ~80 models. It finds that while English dominates on culture-agnostic questions due to higher proficiency, local languages provide a positive knowledge-access advantage for culture-specific questions once the proficiency gap is accounted for.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of LLMs on culturally grounded questions suffer from two key issues: (1) reliance on parallel template-based questions that fail to capture how cultural knowledge naturally surfaces, and (2) raw accuracy conflates general language proficiency with language-conditioned knowledge access. This makes it unclear whether weaker local-language performance reflects genuinely weaker cultural knowledge or merely limited language proficiency.
AI.method: The authors construct a controlled evaluation framework using real-world cultural questions from regional benchmarks and local sources. They cross two factors: question type (culture-agnostic vs. culture-specific) and query language (English vs. local language). They then apply a shared 1PL item response theory (IRT) model to jointly estimate general language ability and localized knowledge access, thereby disentangling proficiency from culture-specific knowledge retrieval conditioned on language.
AI.result: Across 13 locales and approximately 80 models, English consistently outperforms local languages on culture-agnostic questions, reflecting superior English proficiency. However, after controlling for this proficiency gap via IRT, local languages exhibit a positive knowledge-access advantage for culture-specific questions in nearly all locale-model settings. This advantage is often masked in raw accuracy comparisons but becomes more salient in frontier models, regionally aligned models, and language-adapted models.
AI.conclusion: Weaker local-language performance does not necessarily indicate weaker cultural knowledge. Instead, local cultural knowledge may be more accessible through the local language but obscured by limited language proficiency. The findings highlight the importance of separating proficiency from knowledge access when evaluating multilingual and multicultural capabilities of LLMs.

title: The discovery of the effects of women employment participation on the fertility of developing countries: A panel data approach
summary: The fertility trend in developing countries has experienced a significant decline in the last few decades; at the same time, the role of women in the workplace has improved. To have a better insight of the causality of the rate of women participation in the labor market on the total fertility rate in developing world, this paper divides the dataset of 115 developing countries in the period of 1991-2018 into four continents group (Africa, North/South America, Asia/Pacific, Europe) and then applies a data-driven panel data econometric procedure to mitigate omitted bias. The results suggest that the fertility behaviors of women in the North/South America continents are influenced by their career choice; meanwhile in society of other regions, other factors might be more important to women when thinking of having children. In conclusion, policymakers can reference to the paper and formulate policies to have more incentives in making reproductive decisions and further research in the field needs to consider family policies and patrilocality of developing countries as important data.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the causal relationship between female labor force participation and fertility rates across 115 developing countries (1991–2018), using a panel data approach across four continental groups. It finds that women's career choices significantly influence fertility only in the Americas, while other factors dominate elsewhere.
AI.motivation: Developing countries have experienced both declining fertility and rising female workforce participation. The authors aim to clarify the causal direction and heterogeneity of this relationship across different developing regions, addressing potential omitted variable bias in prior cross-country studies.
AI.method: A data-driven panel data econometric procedure is applied to a dataset of 115 developing countries spanning 1991–2018. The sample is stratified into four continental subgroups (Africa, North/South America, Asia/Pacific, Europe) to capture regional heterogeneity, while the methodology specifically targets the mitigation of omitted variable bias.
AI.result: The effect of female labor force participation on fertility is regionally heterogeneous. A significant causal influence of career choice on fertility behavior is found only in the Americas. In Africa, Asia/Pacific, and Europe (developing countries), other socio-cultural or structural factors appear more consequential for reproductive decisions.
AI.conclusion: Policymakers should tailor fertility-related incentives to regional contexts, as the participation–fertility link is not uniform across the developing world. The authors recommend that future research incorporate family policy variables and patrilocality measures to better capture determinants of fertility in developing countries.

title: Sycophantic Praise: Evaluating Excessive Praise in Language Models
summary: Sycophancy in language models is typically studied as excessive agreement or validation, while explicit praise and flattery have received comparatively little attention. We argue that sycophantic praise is a distinct alignment problem that cannot be reliably measured using current methods. We introduce a parameterized framework that measures whether praise is excessive relative to contribution quality and expected user ability. We show that our framework substantially outperforms generic LLM judges in agreement with human annotations, and that sycophantic praise occurs far more frequently in social and interpretive domains than in objective reasoning settings. Together, these findings position praise calibration as a distinct alignment challenge.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies sycophantic praise (excessive flattery) as a distinct alignment problem in language models, introduces a parameterized framework to measure when praise is disproportionate to contribution quality and user ability, demonstrates it aligns better with human judgment than generic LLM evaluators, and finds sycophantic praise is more prevalent in social/interpretive domains than objective reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current sycophancy research focuses narrowly on excessive agreement, neglecting explicit praise and flattery. The authors argue that sycophantic praise constitutes a distinct and understudied alignment failure that existing measurement methods cannot reliably capture.
AI.method: The authors propose a parameterized framework that quantifies praise calibration by measuring whether the level of praise is excessive given two parameters: (1) the actual quality of the user's contribution and (2) the expected ability of the user. They evaluate this framework against human annotations and compare its performance to generic LLM judges across different domain types (social/interpretive vs. objective reasoning).
AI.result: The proposed framework substantially outperforms generic LLM judges in terms of agreement with human annotations. Sycophantic praise is found to occur significantly more often in social and interpretive domains than in objective reasoning settings.
AI.conclusion: Praise calibration should be recognized as a distinct alignment challenge, separate from agreement-based sycophancy. The parameterized framework offers a reliable measurement approach, and the domain-dependence of sycophantic praise has implications for targeted mitigation strategies.

title: OffQ: Taming Structured Outliers in LLM Quantization by Offsetting
summary: Low-bit quantization has been widely adopted to accelerate the inference of large language models (LLMs) by significantly reducing computational cost and memory usage. However, activation outliers pose a major challenge to effective quantization, often leading to notable performance degradation. In this paper, we introduce OffQ, a method designed to mitigate activation outliers in low-bit quantization through a novel offsetting mechanism. Specifically, OffQ first identifies a low-dimensional outlier subspace in the activations using a proposed top-1 PCA, and then concentrates high-magnitude activations into 1 channel via rotation. OffQ then absorbs this concentrated outlier channel by converting its magnitude into a shared offset, thereby reducing the standard deviation of the activations. This offsetting strategy enables effective W4A4KV4 quantization of LLMs using deployment-friendly uniform-grid and uniform-precision quantization. Extensive experiments across diverse LLM architectures and benchmarks demonstrate that OffQ outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, consistently improving model accuracy while preserving low-bit efficiency.

AI.tldr: OffQ introduces a top-1 PCA-based method to identify and isolate activation outliers into a single channel, then absorbs that channel as a shared offset to reduce activation variance, enabling effective W4A4KV4 quantization of LLMs with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Activation outliers in LLMs cause significant performance degradation under low-bit quantization. Existing methods struggle to handle these outliers effectively without introducing complex, non-uniform quantization schemes that are deployment-unfriendly.
AI.method: OffQ uses a three-step offsetting mechanism: (1) Top-1 PCA identifies a low-dimensional outlier subspace in activations; (2) a rotation operation concentrates high-magnitude activations into a single channel; (3) the concentrated outlier channel's magnitude is converted into a shared offset and absorbed, reducing overall activation standard deviation. This enables uniform-grid, uniform-precision W4A4KV4 quantization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse LLM architectures and benchmarks show OffQ consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving superior model accuracy while maintaining low-bit inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: OffQ provides an effective, deployment-friendly solution for low-bit LLM quantization by structurally addressing activation outliers through offsetting, without sacrificing the simplicity of uniform quantization schemes.

title: Supervision versus Demonstration-Based In-Context Learning for Multiword Expression Classification
summary: Turkish idiomatic light verb constructions (LVCs) are challenging for multiword expression processing because they often share the same surface form as fully literal verb-object combinations while functioning as a single, partially idiomatic predicate. We frame Turkish LVC detection as a binary classification task (literal meaning vs. idiomatic meaning) and evaluate on a manually created controlled set (N=147) with matched negatives: out-of-domain random sentences and in-domain literal controls (NLVC), alongside LVC positives. We compare a supervised Turkish encoder baseline (BERTurk with a classifier head) to three instruction-tuned LLMs from different families under zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting, and analyze how demonstrations shift error profiles. In zero-shot, LLMs perform well on negatives but show very low LVC recall. One-shot prompting sharply improves LVC detection but can induce strong, model-specific biases, leading models to overpredict or underpredict LVCs. A richer few-shot prompt improves calibration and yields robust overall performance for GPT-OSS-20B and Qwen 2.5-14B. Overall, the results highlight substantial prompt sensitivity in Turkish metalinguistic classification: the supervised baseline remains competitive, while prompted LLMs can match or exceed it on LVCs with carefully constructed demonstrations.

AI.tldr: Turkish light verb constructions (LVCs) are idiomatically ambiguous; this paper frames LVC detection as binary classification and shows that carefully prompted LLMs can match or exceed a fine-tuned BERTurk baseline, though performance is highly prompt-sensitive.
AI.motivation: Turkish LVCs present a surface-form ambiguity problem—identical strings can be literal or idiomatic—making them difficult for MWE processing systems. The authors aim to assess whether instruction-tuned LLMs can perform metalinguistic classification of this phenomenon without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.method: A controlled evaluation set (N=147) was manually created with LVC positives and matched negatives (in-domain literal NLVCs and out-of-domain random sentences). A supervised BERTurk+classifier baseline is compared against three instruction-tuned LLMs (GPT-OSS-20B, Qwen 2.5-14B, and a third unnamed model) under zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting regimes, with error profile analysis across demonstration conditions.
AI.result: Zero-shot LLMs achieve high precision on negatives but very low LVC recall. One-shot prompting sharply improves LVC recall but introduces model-specific biases (overprediction or underprediction). Few-shot prompting yields the best calibration and robust overall performance for GPT-OSS-20B and Qwen 2.5-14B. The supervised BERTurk baseline remains competitive throughout.
AI.conclusion: Prompted LLMs can perform Turkish LVC detection comparably to or better than a supervised baseline, but only with carefully constructed demonstrations—highlighting substantial prompt sensitivity in metalinguistic classification tasks for Turkish.

title: Beyond Linear and Overcomplete Regimes: A Mean-Field Analysis of Bottleneck Autoencoders
summary: Autoencoders (AEs) learn low-dimensional representations by mapping data into a latent space while minimizing reconstruction error. Despite their empirical success, theoretical understanding remains limited and largely restricted to linear models or settings without a bottleneck. In this work, we study nonlinear AEs with a fixed finite-dimensional bottleneck in the mean-field (MF) regime. We derive explicit MF learning dynamics for both encoder and decoder, providing a tractable characterization of training in the nonlinear setting. We show that, over finite time horizons, the empirical risk of finite-width networks trained with stochastic gradient descent closely tracks the MF risk trajectory with high probability. At optimality, we further establish that the finite-width risk converges to the MF optimum, demonstrating that finite networks are sufficiently expressive to approximate the infinite-width solution.

AI.tldr: The paper studies nonlinear autoencoders with finite-dimensional bottlenecks in the mean-field regime, deriving explicit learning dynamics and proving that finite-width networks trained with SGD closely track the mean-field risk trajectory and converge to the MF optimum.
AI.motivation: Autoencoders are widely used empirically for learning low-dimensional representations, but theoretical understanding is largely limited to linear models or non-bottleneck settings. The authors aim to bridge this gap by providing a rigorous theoretical analysis of nonlinear AEs with finite bottlenecks.
AI.method: The authors analyze nonlinear autoencoders in the mean-field (MF) regime with fixed finite-dimensional bottlenecks. They derive explicit MF learning dynamics for both encoder and decoder. They then prove that finite-width networks trained with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) closely track the MF risk trajectory over finite time horizons with high probability, and that the finite-width risk converges to the MF optimum at optimality.
AI.result: Explicit MF learning dynamics are derived for both encoder and decoder. It is proven that over finite time horizons, the empirical risk of finite-width networks trained with SGD closely tracks the MF risk trajectory with high probability. At optimality, the finite-width risk converges to the MF optimum, showing that finite networks are sufficiently expressive to approximate the infinite-width solution.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a tractable theoretical characterization of nonlinear autoencoder training with bottlenecks, establishing that the mean-field framework accurately captures the behavior of practical finite-width networks trained via SGD, thereby closing a gap between theory and practice in representation learning.

title: Learning Explicit Behavioral Models with Adaptive Questions and World-Model Probes
summary: Interactive agents trained only against task return can achieve high scores while failing to represent the mechanisms that make their actions succeed. This makes brittle behavior difficult to diagnose and limits adaptation when environment dynamics change. Existing LLM reflection and policy-code repair can revise behavior from failed trajectories, but questions and world-understanding tests are usually used only after training. We introduce an Explicit Symbolic Behavioral Model (ESBM), a trainable behavioral model that couples task performance with evidence-grounded question answering and executable mechanism prediction. An ESBM represents behavior through typed predicates, weighted rules, bounded options and mechanism memory; the mechanism layer predicts symbolic events, object changes, rewards and terminal consequences under action interventions. After each rollout, adaptive questions and active world-model probes convert score failures, QA errors and transition-prediction errors into constraints for local ESBM edits. Candidate models are selected by a multi-criterion rule that jointly evaluates task score, answerability and active world-model consistency. Under the tested Atari-style protocols, ESBM learns high-scoring policies while producing explicit answers and executable mechanism predictions, indicating that adaptive questions can serve as both training pressure and reusable benchmarks for mechanistic policy learning in this setting.

AI.tldr: ESBM couples task performance with question answering and executable mechanism prediction via a trainable behavioral model that uses adaptive questions and active world-model probes to convert failures into local edits, enabling high-scoring policies that also provide explicit, verifiable explanations of their actions.
AI.motivation: Interactive agents trained solely on task return achieve high scores but fail to represent the causal mechanisms behind their actions, leading to brittle behavior that is hard to diagnose and cannot adapt to changing environment dynamics. Existing LLM reflection methods only address failures post-hoc rather than integrating mechanistic understanding into training.
AI.method: ESBM uses typed predicates, weighted rules, bounded options, and mechanism memory to represent behavior. The mechanism layer predicts symbolic events, object changes, rewards, and terminal consequences under action interventions. After each rollout, adaptive questions and active world-model probes generate constraints (from score failures, QA errors, and transition-prediction errors) used for local ESBM edits. Candidate models are selected via a multi-criterion rule evaluating task score, answerability, and active world-model consistency.
AI.result: Under Atari-style protocols, ESBM learned high-scoring policies while producing explicit, interpretable answers and executable mechanism predictions.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive questions can serve as both training pressure and reusable benchmarks for mechanistic policy learning, integrating world-understanding directly into the training loop rather than as a post-hoc evaluation.

title: Agentopia: Long-Term Life Simulation and Learning in Agent Societies
summary: Humans learn from social life. Simulating this process with LLM-powered agents represents a promising research direction, raising a natural question: whether LLMs can learn from such simulated social experience to better understand and replicate human behavior. However, prior agent society simulations typically operate at the scale of days, limiting the depth of social interactions and long-term growth. In this paper, we study long-term life simulation and LLM learning in agent societies, with two goals: (1) investigating social behaviors that emerge from life-long simulation, and (2) developing anthropomorphic capabilities in LLMs, particularly intelligence in social life, through years of simulated social experience. Specifically, we present Agentopia, a comprehensive framework for long-term life simulation in multi-agent societies, where 100 agents autonomously pursue personal growth, develop social relationships, and fulfill their needs and goals over 10 simulated years. We define life reward to mirror human well-being, and leverage this reward to train LLMs via rejection sampling. Extensive experiments show that agents exhibit rich emergent social behaviors. Furthermore, life reward training effectively enhances the underlying LLM, which leads to improved agent well-being in simulation, and generalizes to downstream role-playing benchmarks with +15.6% improvement.

AI.tldr: Agentopia simulates 100 LLM-powered agents over 10 years of social life, using a "life reward" metric to train LLMs via rejection sampling. This yields emergent social behaviors and improves downstream role-playing benchmarks by +15.6%.
AI.motivation: Prior agent society simulations are limited to short timescales (days), restricting the depth of social interactions and long-term agent growth. The authors aim to investigate whether LLMs can learn from years of simulated social experience to develop anthropomorphic capabilities, particularly social intelligence.
AI.method: The authors propose Agentopia, a long-term life simulation framework with 100 autonomous agents over 10 simulated years. Agents pursue personal growth, build social relationships, and fulfill needs/goals. A "life reward" metric is defined to mirror human well-being (e.g., happiness, health, wealth, social connections). This reward is used to train LLMs via rejection sampling, selecting high-reward trajectories to fine-tune the model.
AI.result: Agents exhibit rich emergent social behaviors over the 10-year simulation. Life reward training effectively enhances the underlying LLM, leading to improved agent well-being during simulation. The trained model also generalizes to downstream role-playing benchmarks, achieving a +15.6% performance improvement.
AI.conclusion: Long-term life simulation with life reward-driven LLM training enables both the emergence of complex social behaviors and measurable improvements in the model's social intelligence. The approach demonstrates that simulated social experience can enhance LLM anthropomorphic capabilities, with benefits transferring to related tasks.

title: A machine-learning-assisted progressive digit-randomness screening framework for detecting non-random patterns in raw numerical research data
summary: Raw numerical datasets remain less systematically examined in integrity screening than images, plagiarism, or summary-statistic inconsistencies. We developed the Fabrication-risk Digit Randomness Screening model (FDRS), a statistical and machine-learning framework for detecting non-random digit-pattern irregularities in numerical research data. FDRS integrates single- and joint-decimal-digit tests, Cramer's V, entropy metrics, Kullback-Leibler divergence, digit-preference indices, progressive subsampling, and semi-supervised risk scoring. It was evaluated using an instrument-derived enzymatic absorbance dataset (RawData, n=253) and a blinded manually simulated irregular dataset (ErrData, n=255). RawData showed no significant deviation in single third-decimal-digit analysis, whereas ErrData showed a significant deviation. In joint third-fourth decimal digit analysis, ErrData showed higher Cramer's V, lower normalized entropy, higher KL divergence, and a more persistent progressive-subsampling deviation signal. In internal validation, Elastic-net Logistic Regression achieved the highest AUC (0.98395) and lowest Brier score (0.048439), while Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy (0.926667) and balanced accuracy (0.935). RawData received a low ensemble risk score of 0.124627 and was classified as Grade 0; ErrData received a score of 0.740760 and was classified as Grade 3. External real-world benchmarks supported graded risk stratification: three datasets without identified public post-publication concerns were classified as Grade 0 or 1, whereas two datasets from publicly questioned or institutionally handled articles were classified as Grade 2 or 3. FDRS can prioritize raw numerical datasets for further review by integrating interpretable statistical and machine-learning features. It is an auxiliary digit-structure screening tool, not standalone evidence of fabrication or misconduct.

AI.tldr: FDRS is a hybrid statistical/ML framework that detects unnatural digit-pattern irregularities in raw numerical research data, combining decimal-digit tests, divergence metrics, progressive subsampling, and ensemble risk scoring. Validated on real and simulated datasets, it achieved high discrimination (AUC up to 0.984) and aligned with real-world cases of questionable data, serving as an auxiliary screening—not definitive—tool for fabrication risk.
AI.motivation: Raw numerical datasets are systematically under-examined for integrity compared to images, plagiarism, or summary statistics. No existing framework comprehensively integrates digit-level randomness tests with machine learning to flag potential fabrication in raw numerical data.
AI.method: FDRS integrates single and joint decimal-digit distribution tests, Cramér's V, normalized entropy, Kullback-Leibler divergence, digit-preference indices, progressive subsampling for persistence of deviation, and semi-supervised ensemble risk scoring (Elastic-net Logistic Regression, Random Forest, etc.). Validated on instrument-derived RawData (n=253) and manually simulated irregular ErrData (n=255), with external benchmarking against five real-world datasets with known integrity status.
AI.result: RawData showed no significant digit deviation; ErrData showed significant deviations across all metrics. Internal validation: Elastic-net LR AUC=0.984, Brier=0.048; Random Forest accuracy=0.927, balanced accuracy=0.935. RawData scored 0.125 (Grade 0); ErrData scored 0.741 (Grade 3). External validation: datasets without public concerns graded 0–1; datasets from questioned/retracted articles graded 2–3.
AI.conclusion: FDRS effectively stratifies raw numerical datasets by digit-pattern irregularity risk, integrating interpretable statistical and ML features. It is positioned strictly as an auxiliary screening tool to prioritize datasets for expert review, not as standalone evidence of fabrication or misconduct.

title: $α$-PFN: Fast Entropy Search via In-Context Learning
summary: Information-theoretic acquisition functions such as Entropy Search (ES) offer a principled exploration-exploitation framework for Bayesian optimization (BO). However, their practical implementation relies on complicated and slow approximations, i.e., a Monte Carlo estimation of the information gain. This complexity can introduce numerical errors and requires specialized, hand-crafted implementations. We propose a two-stage amortization strategy that learns to approximate entropy search-based acquisition functions using Prior-data Fitted Networks (PFNs) in a single forward pass. A first PFN is trained to be conditioned on information about the optima; second, the $α$-PFN is trained to predict the expected information gain by training on information gains measured with the first PFN. The $α$-PFN offers a flexible learned approximation, which replaces the complex heuristic approximations with a single forward pass per candidate, enabling rapid and extensible acquisition evaluation. Empirically, our approach is competitive with state-of-the-art entropy search implementations on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, while accelerating the different entropy search variants across all our experiments, with speed ups over 50x. Source code: https://github.com/automl/AlphaPFN.

AI.tldr: Proposes α-PFN, a two-stage amortization framework using Prior-data Fitted Networks to accelerate entropy search acquisition functions in Bayesian optimization by replacing complex Monte Carlo approximations with a single forward pass, achieving 50x speedups while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Entropy Search (ES) and its variants provide principled exploration-exploitation in BO, but their practical implementation requires slow, complicated Monte Carlo approximations of information gain that introduce numerical errors and demand specialized hand-crafted implementations.
AI.method: A two-stage amortization strategy: (1) Train a first PFN conditioned on optima information to model the posterior over the optimum; (2) Train an α-PFN to predict expected information gain by learning from information gains measured using the first PFN. This replaces heuristic approximations with a single forward pass per candidate.
AI.result: Competitive with state-of-the-art entropy search implementations on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, with speedups exceeding 50x across all experiments.
AI.conclusion: α-PFN offers a flexible, rapid, and extensible learned approximation for entropy search-based acquisition functions, enabling practical deployment of principled information-theoretic BO at scale.

title: Severity-Aware Curriculum Learning with Multi-Model Response Selection for Medical Text Generation
summary: Telehealth systems have become increasingly important for delivering accessible and timely medical information. Existing large language models often struggle to provide consistent and contextually appropriate medical responses across varying levels of case severity. This limitation highlights the need for models that can effectively adapt to the progressive complexity in medical queries. To address this challenge, we introduce a severity-aware multi-model framework that integrates curriculum training strategy with relevance-based response selection. The proposed framework employs a three-stage curriculum learning strategy, where each model is trained sequentially on mild, moderate, and critical cases to progressively acquire domain knowledge. The approach utilizes five large language models, each independently trained under the same curriculum scheme. During inference, all models generate candidate responses, and the most appropriate response is selected as the final output. The framework is trained and evaluated on the MAQA dataset, which provides annotated medical question-answer pairs. Experimental results evaluated using BERTScore demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to both baseline and fine-tuned models, attaining 86.71% in the baseline setting and 90.30% after fine-tuning. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining curriculum learning with multi-model response selection in improving response quality and relevance in medical text generation.

AI.tldr: A severity-aware multi-model framework combining curriculum learning with relevance-based response selection is proposed for medical telehealth QA. Five LLMs are trained sequentially on mild→moderate→critical cases; at inference, the best candidate response is selected. Achieves 86.71% (baseline) and 90.30% (fine-tuned) BERTScore on MAQA, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs struggle to produce consistent, contextually appropriate responses across varying medical case severities. There is a need for models that can adapt to progressive complexity in medical queries, which motivates a severity-aware, curriculum-based approach.
AI.method: A three-stage curriculum learning strategy where five LLMs are each independently trained sequentially on mild, then moderate, then critical medical cases. At inference, all five models generate candidate responses, and a relevance-based selection mechanism picks the most appropriate one as the final output. Evaluated on the MAQA dataset using BERTScore.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieved 86.71% BERTScore in the baseline setting and 90.30% after fine-tuning, surpassing both baseline and fine-tuned comparator models.
AI.conclusion: Combining curriculum learning with multi-model response selection effectively improves response quality and relevance in medical text generation, demonstrating the value of severity-aware progressive training for telehealth applications.

title: Explaining Unsupervised Disease Staging in Huntington's Disease: Insights into Model Representations and Clusters
comment: Accepted for oral presentation and as a full-length paper at the International Conference on AI in Healthcare 2026 (26-28 August 2026, Imperial College London) and will be published by Springer in the Lecture Notes in Computer Science (LNCS) series
summary: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects motor, cognitive, and behavioral functions, where accurate characterization of disease progression remains essential to improve patient outcome and quality of life. Unsupervised machine learning (ML) approaches have demonstrated the ability to uncover disease progression trajectories and meaningful latent stages from longitudinal data; however, their limited interpretability restricts clinical trust and translation. We extend a previously proposed ML-based disease staging framework by applying an explainability analysis to the extracted feature representations and discovered disease stages. Applied to the Enroll-HD dataset, we first project the learned representations into a lower-dimensional space to intuitively assess whether the resulting clusters align with the progression of established clinical measures. We then use saliency maps to identify the clinical features that most strongly contribute to the learned embeddings over time. Finally, we train a surrogate classifier and apply SHAP to quantify feature importance for cluster assignments and to analyze which clinical variables drive transitions between disease stages. The explainability analysis indicates that the learned embeddings capture clinically meaningful disease structure, aligning with established motor and functional severity scores and exhibiting progressive deterioration across clusters. Within this analysis, SHAP reveals a stratification of disease stages, ranging from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency, consistent with known clinical progression patterns, while also highlighting intra-stage variability.

AI.tldr: This paper applies explainability techniques (saliency maps and SHAP) to an unsupervised ML-based disease staging framework for Huntington's disease, validating that learned embeddings and discovered stages align with established clinical progression patterns using the Enroll-HD dataset.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised ML can uncover disease progression trajectories from longitudinal data, but limited interpretability hinders clinical trust and adoption. There is a need to bridge this gap by making the learned representations and discovered disease stages clinically transparent and verifiable.
AI.method: The authors extend a prior ML-based disease staging framework with three explainability analyses: (1) low-dimensional projection of learned representations to visually assess alignment with clinical measures; (2) saliency maps to identify features driving embeddings over time; (3) training a surrogate classifier with SHAP to quantify feature importance for cluster assignment and stage transitions.
AI.result: The explainability analysis confirmed that learned embeddings capture clinically meaningful structure, aligning with motor and functional severity scores and showing progressive deterioration across clusters. SHAP revealed a stratification from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency, consistent with known HD progression, while also highlighting intra-stage variability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed explainability framework successfully validates that unsupervised ML-derived disease stages are clinically coherent and interpretable, strengthening the case for translational deployment of such models in Huntington's disease monitoring and management.

title: REMEDI: A Benchmark for Retention and Unlearning Evaluation in Multi-label Clinical Disease Inference
summary: Language models trained for clinical disease inference are trained on patient data, which may include sensitive and private information, and data owners may request the removal of their data from a trained model due to privacy or copyright concerns. However, exactly unlearning patient-specific data is intractable, and retraining with minor data removal is resource-intensive. While there exists several machine unlearning methods that can be used, their utility is generally restricted to non-medical domains. Moreover, the existing benchmarks for evaluating such unlearning methods primarily utilize synthetically curated datasets, which are not truly representative of real-world systems. Hence, the effectiveness of these unlearning methods in the medical domain is largely unclear. To this end, we introduce REMEDI, an extensive benchmark for machine unlearning tailored to multi-label and multiclass clinical disease inference, where label correlations, longitudinal structure, and safety constraints make unlearning particularly challenging. Unlike the existing benchmarks, REMEDI considers: (1) a relevant application domain (medical), (2) comprehensive unlearning setups involving diverse sets of forget instances, (3) challenging unlearning scenarios including multi-label and multi-class classification tasks, and (4) evaluation metrics involving performance both in terms of utility and extent of unlearning achieved. REMEDI is developed using the MIMIC-III clinical database that contains comprehensive clinical data of patients. Experiments with existing unlearning methods indicate that there exists a trade-off between utility and unlearning performance. They are also largely unsuited to multi-label classification tasks. To facilitate reproducibility, we make our benchmark publicly available.

AI.tldr: REMEDI is a benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning methods in clinical disease inference, using real MIMIC-III data to test multi-label and multi-class scenarios where existing methods show a trade-off between utility and unlearning performance.
AI.motivation: Patient data removal requests due to privacy concerns are intractable via exact unlearning, and retraining is resource-intensive. Existing machine unlearning methods are evaluated only on synthetic non-medical benchmarks, leaving their effectiveness in real clinical settings unclear.
AI.method: The authors introduce REMEDI, a benchmark built on the MIMIC-III clinical database, designed for multi-label and multi-class disease inference. It incorporates diverse forget instance setups, challenging unlearning scenarios (including label correlations and longitudinal structure), and dual evaluation metrics assessing both model utility and unlearning completeness.
AI.result: Experiments reveal that existing unlearning methods exhibit a clear trade-off between retaining model utility and achieving thorough unlearning, and they are largely inadequate for multi-label classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: REMEDI provides a realistic, medically-grounded evaluation framework that exposes the limitations of current machine unlearning approaches, and the benchmark is made publicly available to encourage further research and reproducibility.

title: Decision-Aware Evaluation of Physics-Informed Surrogates
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables. Code and data available at https://github.com/Dyniel/pinn-gym
summary:
Physics-informed machine learning is often assessed by curve error, although engineering use depends on downstream decisions: ranking candidates, avoiding infeasible designs and limiting regret. We introduce pinn-gym, an open benchmark for material-conditioned lattice design that couples a transparent reduced-order crush-and-impact oracle with five printable polymer cards, dimensionless force-response targets and a protocol spanning curve fidelity, physical admissibility, top-k retrieval and mass regret.
  Across per-material, pooled and cross-material settings, low nRMSE is frequently insufficient to identify useful design selections. Physics-informed losses alter trade-offs rather than monotonically improving all metrics, and dimensionless conditioning improves comparability without making transfer symmetric. The benchmark is not a certified material model; within the released oracle, candidate generator and material cards, pinn-gym provides a reproducible testbed for evaluating PIML surrogates as decision systems rather than curve predictors alone.

AI.tldr: Pinn-gym is an open benchmark for evaluating physics-informed machine learning (PIML) surrogates in material-conditioned lattice design, emphasizing decision-oriented metrics (ranking, feasibility, regret) over curve error. It reveals that low nRMSE is insufficient for useful design selection, and physics-informed losses create trade-offs rather than uniform improvements.
AI.motivation: Current PIML evaluation relies heavily on curve error metrics (e.g., nRMSE), but practical engineering applications require downstream decision-making capabilities: ranking candidate designs, avoiding infeasible designs, and minimizing regret. There is a gap between standard accuracy metrics and real-world utility assessment.
AI.method: The authors introduce pinn-gym, an open benchmark combining: (1) a transparent reduced-order crush-and-impact oracle, (2) five printable polymer material cards with dimensionless force-response targets, and (3) an evaluation protocol covering curve fidelity, physical admissibility, top-k retrieval, and mass regret. Experiments span per-material, pooled, and cross-material settings to assess PIML surrogates as decision systems.
AI.result: Low nRMSE is frequently insufficient for identifying useful design selections. Physics-informed losses alter the trade-off landscape rather than monotonically improving all metrics. Dimensionless conditioning improves comparability but does not make transfer across materials symmetric.
AI.conclusion: Pinn-gym serves as a reproducible testbed for evaluating PIML surrogates as decision systems rather than mere curve predictors. It is not a certified material model, but provides a standardized framework for assessing decision-oriented performance in material-conditioned lattice design.

title: Geodesics of Dynamic Graphs for Regime Change Detection
summary: Traditional change point detection in dynamic networks assumes abrupt transitions between stationary states, overlooking scenarios of continuous evolution which arise in most real-world applications, such as social networks or physical systems. We address this gap by formally defining regimes as periods of coherent dynamics in temporal graphs, which we characterize as trajectories along geodesics in a suitably defined graph space. This original perspective allows us to define regime changes as significant drifts in dynamics, either toward new trajectories or with pace changes. We leverage graph regression methods to measure the cumulative distance of sequences of observed graphs from the estimated geodesics between their endpoints, in the relevant graph space, which we can combine with change point detection algorithms. We present experiments on dynamic networks, with changing trajectories and varying speeds, in which we outperform state of the art change point detection models. Then, we analyse mobility data during the Covid-19 pandemic, and show that our assumptions on regular network evolution lead to change points that are more aligned to external events compared to the outcomes of baseline methods. Our work is the first to model and detect changes between evolving regimes in graph space, providing a realistic and powerful tool for analyzing complex temporal graph data.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel framework for change point detection in dynamic/temporal graphs that models network evolution as trajectories along geodesics in graph space, capturing continuous drift rather than just abrupt transitions. The method uses graph regression to measure cumulative distance from estimated geodesics, outperforming state-of-the-art on synthetic data and yielding more externally-aligned change points in COVID-19 mobility data.
AI.motivation: Traditional change point detection in dynamic networks assumes abrupt transitions between stationary states, which fails to capture continuous, gradual evolution common in real-world applications like social networks and physical systems. The authors aim to fill this gap by modeling regimes as periods of coherent, continuous dynamics rather than static blocks.
AI.method: The authors formally define regimes as trajectories along geodesics in a suitably defined graph space. They characterize regime changes as significant drifts—either toward new trajectories or with pace changes. Graph regression methods are leveraged to measure the cumulative distance of observed graph sequences from estimated geodesics between endpoints, which is then combined with change point detection algorithms to identify where regimes shift.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic dynamic networks with changing trajectories and varying speeds show the method outperforms state-of-the-art change point detection models. Analysis of COVID-19 pandemic mobility data demonstrates that the change points identified align more closely with real external events compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work to model and detect changes between continuously evolving regimes in graph space, offering a more realistic and powerful tool for analyzing complex temporal graph data than traditional abrupt-transition models.

title: RETROSPECT: RETROsynthesis via Sequential Prediction, and Chemically Transformed-ranking
comment: Accepted at the AI for Science workshop (ICML 2026)
summary: Single-step retrosynthesis needs both accurate first-ranked suggestions and candidate lists that are rich enough for downstream selection. We study this as a proposal-selection decomposition. Our system, RETROSPECT, combines a single Transformer proposal model, which we call the ChemAlign Transformer, with a LambdaMART reranker over structural, reaction-template, upstream-score, and optional DFT-derived descriptors. The generator is trained with hybrid root-aligned and random SMILES augmentation, Pre-LayerNorm, tied embeddings, exponential moving average weights, and a differentiable atom-balance auxiliary loss. On the full USPTO-50K test set of 5,007 reactions, the generator reaches 55.00% top-1 and 86.18% top-10 exact-match accuracy with 99.86% top-1 validity. On the merged candidate-pool benchmark used for reranking, which contains 5,007 test products and about 111 candidates per product, a LambdaMART model trained on the structural feature set reaches 59.4% top-1 with 0.7171 mean reciprocal rank. Feature ablations show that upstream proposal score and template-frequency statistics provide most of the reranking signal, while DFT and reaction-center DFT features provide smaller and less consistent gains. These results support a modular view of retrosynthesis: stronger single-model proposal and learned candidate selection are complementary, and the proposal model can serve as a drop-in component for ensemble systems such as RetroChimera (Maziarz et al., 2024)

AI.tldr: RETROSPECT decomposes single-step retrosynthesis into a Transformer-based proposal generator (ChemAlign Transformer) and a LambdaMART reranker. The generator achieves 55.00% top-1 and 86.18% top-10 exact-match accuracy on USPTO-50K. The reranker further boosts top-1 to 59.4% using structural, template, and upstream-score features, with DFT features providing only marginal gains.
AI.motivation: Single-step retrosynthesis requires both high-accuracy top-1 predictions and diverse candidate lists for downstream selection. The authors argue that treating this as a proposal-selection decomposition — where a strong single model generates candidates and a learned reranker selects among them — is more effective than relying on either component alone.
AI.method: RETROSPECT consists of two stages: (1) A ChemAlign Transformer generator trained with hybrid root-aligned and random SMILES augmentation, Pre-LayerNorm, tied embeddings, exponential moving average weights, and a differentiable atom-balance auxiliary loss. (2) A LambdaMART reranker that scores candidates using structural features, reaction-template statistics, upstream proposal scores, and optional DFT-derived descriptors. The system is evaluated on USPTO-50K (5,007 test reactions) and a merged candidate-pool benchmark (~111 candidates per product).
AI.result: The ChemAlign Transformer alone reaches 55.00% top-1 and 86.18% top-10 exact-match accuracy with 99.86% top-1 validity on USPTO-50K. After LambdaMART reranking on the merged candidate-pool benchmark, top-1 improves to 59.4% with 0.7171 MRR. Feature ablation reveals that upstream proposal scores and template-frequency statistics contribute most of the reranking signal; DFT and reaction-center DFT features provide smaller, inconsistent gains.
AI.conclusion: Stronger single-model proposals and learned candidate selection are complementary. The proposal model can serve as a drop-in component for ensemble systems like RetroChimera, supporting a modular architecture for retrosynthesis where improvements to either the generator or the reranker independently boost overall performance.

title: Structure-Preserving Correction Learning for Sparse Bayesian Inference in Brain Source Imaging
summary: Classical sparse Type-II Bayesian methods for M/EEG brain imaging support joint estimation of source and noise hyperparameters, but rely on fixed iterative update rules. Although these updates are principled and interpretable, their dynamics cannot be adapted from data. We propose to learn the update mechanism itself while preserving the underlying Bayesian structure by unfolding a classical joint hyperparameter-learning solver into a trainable neural architecture whose layers mirror the original iterations. The resulting framework is initialized to recover the classical solver exactly before training and is enriched through progressively more expressive correction-learning mechanisms, ranging from learnable biases to adaptive MLP and attention-based contextual refinements. In this way, training does not replace Bayesian inference with a black-box predictor, but instead learns structured correction terms while retaining the interpretability and model-based character of the original update dynamics. Structured correction learning therefore aims to improve empirical reconstruction performance without replacing the original model-based inference mechanism. Experimental results show that the learned correction variants improve reconstruction performance and convergence behavior over the baseline unfolded solver while preserving its algorithmic transparency.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method that unrolls classical sparse Bayesian source imaging solvers into trainable neural architectures, learning structured correction terms (biases, MLPs, attention) while preserving the original Bayesian update structure, thereby improving M/EEG reconstruction performance and convergence without sacrificing interpretability.
AI.motivation: Classical Type-II Bayesian methods for M/EEG source imaging use fixed, principled iterative update rules for joint hyperparameter estimation, but their dynamics are rigid and cannot be adapted or improved from data. The motivation is to enhance empirical reconstruction performance while retaining the interpretability and model-based character of Bayesian inference, rather than replacing it with a black-box predictor.
AI.method: The authors unfold a classical joint hyperparameter-learning solver into a trainable neural architecture where each layer mirrors one iteration. The network is initialized to exactly replicate the classical solver. Progressive correction-learning mechanisms are then introduced: learnable biases, adaptive MLP-based refinements, and attention-based contextual corrections. These structured correction terms are learned during training while the underlying Bayesian update dynamics remain intact.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that the learned correction variants improve both reconstruction performance and convergence behavior compared to the baseline (unmodified) unfolded solver, while preserving algorithmic transparency.
AI.conclusion: Structured correction learning successfully enhances M/EEG source reconstruction by learning adaptive refinements within a model-based Bayesian framework. The approach retains interpretability and outperforms the classical solver, validating that one can improve inference without abandoning its principled, transparent structure.

title: DEFINED: A Data-Efficient Computational Framework for Fine-Grained Creativity Assessment in Debate Scenarios
summary: Human creativity has emerged as a critical competency in the era of large language models. Assessing creativity in complex, open-ended environments is a grand challenge in data mining, currently hindered by a reliance on standardized simple tasks and the scarcity of fine-grained expert data. As an ecologically valid assessment context, debate reflects multiple dimensions of creativity, encompassing both divergent thinking and convergent thinking. Moreover, debate is a data-rich domain, with a large volume of publicly accessible materials. Current mainstream automated scoring methods are poorly suited to complex settings such as debate, and therefore still rely on costly human evaluation. To this end, this paper proposes DEFINED, a data-efficient computational framework for fine-grained creativity assessment in debate scenarios. DEFINED operationalizes debate creativity through a hierarchical eight-dimensional metric system, implemented via a pre-trained autoregressive language model with a hierarchical scoring head that supports both fine-grained and coarse-grained evaluation. Statements and their associated expert scores were obtained from authentic debate competitions, and a constrained data augmentation strategy was employed to address the elite bias inherent in the original data. DEFINED adopts a mixed-granularity training strategy enabling robust learning from limited fine-grained supervision annotated by trained graduate experts. To rigorously validate ecological validity beyond synthetic benchmarks, we incorporate an empirical study with debate-naive participants, utilizing these authentic data to serve as a qualitative case study for mid-to-low proficiency populations. Across our evaluation protocol, our scoring model achieves accurate and stable scoring, outperforming prompt-based large language model evaluators and existing debate scoring methods.

AI.tldr: DEFINED is a data-efficient framework for fine-grained creativity assessment in debate, using a hierarchical 8-dimensional metric system with a pre-trained language model. It employs constrained data augmentation and mixed-granularity training on expert-annotated debate data, outperforming LLM evaluators and existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current creativity assessment relies on standardized simple tasks and lacks fine-grained expert data, making it unsuitable for complex, open-ended settings. Debate is an ecologically valid, data-rich domain reflecting both divergent and convergent thinking, but mainstream automated scoring methods cannot handle its complexity, forcing reliance on costly human evaluation.
AI.method: DEFINED operationalizes debate creativity via a hierarchical 8-dimensional metric system, implemented with a pre-trained autoregressive language model featuring a hierarchical scoring head for both fine- and coarse-grained evaluation. Expert-scored statements from authentic debate competitions are augmented with a constrained data augmentation strategy to mitigate elite bias. A mixed-granularity training strategy enables robust learning from limited fine-grained supervision. Ecological validity is further validated through an empirical study with debate-naive participants.
AI.result: The scoring model achieves accurate and stable scoring, outperforming prompt-based large language model evaluators and existing debate scoring methods across the evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: DEFINED provides a viable, data-efficient computational solution for fine-grained creativity assessment in complex, open-ended domains like debate, reducing reliance on costly human evaluation while maintaining ecological validity across proficiency levels.

title: Generative Molecular Morphing for Flexible-Size Design via Unbalanced Optimal Transport
summary: The success of generative molecular design hinges on a model's steerability toward high-reward samples. Because many molecular properties are intrinsically linked to molecular size, accurately capturing the joint distribution of properties and the number of atoms is essential. However, current diffusion and flow-based models fix the number of atoms, which ultimately limits their ability to navigate this complex relationship. To address this, we introduce Morph, a flexible-size generative model for conditional and unconditional 3D molecular design based on geometric graphs. By dynamically adapting size, Morph can seamlessly integrate existing structural priors, like scaffolds, and significantly enhances property steering. We show that Morph matches current fixed-size state-of-the-art models while offering the benefit of unparalleled sampling flexibility. We demonstrate out-of-distribution generation in regimes where previous models fail, paving the way for enhanced generative modeling for molecular design.

AI.tldr: Morph is a flexible-size generative model for 3D molecular design that dynamically adapts the number of atoms, overcoming the fixed-size limitation of current diffusion/flow models and enabling superior property steering and out-of-distribution generation.
AI.motivation: Current generative models for molecular design (diffusion and flow-based) fix the number of atoms, which limits their ability to capture the intrinsic relationship between molecular properties and molecular size. This prevents effective property steering toward high-reward samples.
