AI.method: Morph is a flexible-size generative model based on geometric graphs that dynamically adapts molecular size (number of atoms) during generation. It supports both conditional and unconditional 3D molecular design and can integrate existing structural priors such as scaffolds.
AI.result: Morph matches the performance of fixed-size state-of-the-art models while providing superior sampling flexibility. It succeeds in out-of-distribution generation regimes where previous fixed-size models fail.
AI.conclusion: By enabling flexible-size generation, Morph removes a key bottleneck in molecular design, paving the way for more capable generative models with enhanced steerability and generalization.

title: A Held-Out Transition-Pair Falsifier for Long-Horizon Non-Abelian State Tracking
summary: State tracking exposes a sharp limitation of sequence models: the relevant signal is often not a summary of observed tokens, but an ordered latent state that evolves through non-commutative transformations. We introduce a held-out transition-pair falsifier for finite non-Abelian group tracking. The protocol forbids selected ordered generator pairs during training and requires the same local patterns during evaluation, blocking one direct local-transition memorization pathway. In a controlled $S_3 \times S_3$ benchmark, a projected recurrent state model trained only on length-8 sequences produces error-free final-state predictions (perfect 250/250 per horizon) through evaluation horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens across five seeds. Matched native-readout baselines, including bag, GRU, and a single-configuration structured state-space model, remain near floor under the same protocol. Projection-matched GRU, structured SSM, and bag baselines equipped with analogous finite-group prototype readouts also remain near chance under the same split. Mechanism diagnostics show that hard projection coincides with low homomorphism error, low state-consistency drift, and non-trivial commutator separation, while softened projection collapses final-state accuracy. Clean-split audits verify zero verbatim reduced-word overlap and zero structural-template overlap between training and evaluation partitions. The evidence is scoped to this controlled finite-group falsifier rather than to a general architecture ranking. Within that regime, explicit projected non-commutative state composition acts as a useful inductive bias for long-horizon hidden-state tracking.

AI.tldr: Sequence models struggle with tracking latent states that evolve through non-commutative (order-dependent) transformations. The authors introduce a stringent falsification protocol using finite non-Abelian groups, where certain transition pairs are held out during training. A projected recurrent state model achieves perfect long-horizon state tracking (up to 1M tokens) while standard baselines (GRU, SSMs, bag models) fail, demonstrating that explicit projected non-commutative composition serves as a powerful inductive bias for hidden-state tracking.
AI.motivation: State tracking in sequence models is fundamentally limited because relevant signals often involve ordered latent states undergoing non-commutative transformations — where the sequence of operations matters. Standard models tend to summarize observed tokens rather than faithfully compose state transitions. The authors aim to isolate and test whether explicit non-commutative compositional structure provides a necessary inductive bias for long-horizon hidden-state tracking, beyond what memorization or pattern-matching can achieve.
AI.method: The authors design a held-out transition-pair falsifier using the finite non-Abelian group S₃ × S₃. During training, selected ordered generator pairs are forbidden, and the model must generalize to these unseen local transition patterns at evaluation. A projected recurrent state model is trained exclusively on length-8 sequences and tested on horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens. Baselines include bag models, GRUs, and structured state-space models (SSMs), both in native-readout and projection-matched configurations. Mechanism diagnostics examine homomorphism error, state-consistency drift, and commutator separation, while clean-split audits verify zero overlap between training and evaluation partitions.
AI.result: The projected recurrent state model achieves perfect final-state prediction (250/250 per horizon) across all evaluation horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens over five seeds. All native-readout baselines (bag, GRU, structured SSM) remain near floor. Projection-matched baselines with finite-group prototype readouts also remain near chance. Hard projection correlates with low homomorphism error, low state-consistency drift, and non-trivial commutator separation; softened projection collapses accuracy. Clean-split audits confirm zero verbatim reduced-word overlap and zero structural-template overlap between train/eval partitions.
AI.conclusion: Within the controlled finite-group falsification regime, explicit projected non-commutative state composition provides a critical inductive bias for long-horizon hidden-state tracking. The results demonstrate that architectural choices enforcing group-theoretic compositionality can solve state-tracking tasks that remain intractable for standard sequence models, though the findings are explicitly scoped to the finite-group setting and do not constitute a general architecture ranking.

title: Where Rectified Flows Leak: Characterising Membership Signals Along the Interpolation Path
comment: ICML 2026 article, 9 main pages and 25 with annexes, 11 figures
summary: Understanding what generative models retain from training data remains challenging, with implications for copyright and privacy. Beyond verbatim reproduction, models can encode subtler traces of their training data that never surface in their outputs yet remain exploitable. We study this regime for Rectified Flows, which are increasingly used in deployed generative systems. We analyse the interpolation path $X_λ= (1-λ)X_0 + λX_1$ that defines the Rectified Flow training. We show that a gap exists between the reconstruction of train and test data that follows a bell-shaped curve over $λ$, wich accumulates during training, while the validation metrics remain stable. The signal has a maximum whose location we derive in closed form under Gaussian assumptions. We validate these predictions on both audio and images and show that the bell-shaped structure is universal, while the peak prediction holds when our assumptions are satisfied. As a proof of concept, we exploit this specific $λ$-resolved structure to perform a Membership Inference Attack, distinguishing members of the training set from non-members.

AI.tldr: Rectified Flow models encode detectable training data traces along the interpolation path $X_\lambda$. A bell-shaped gap between train and test reconstruction emerges over $\lambda$ during training, even as validation metrics remain stable. The peak location is derived analytically under Gaussian assumptions and validated on audio/image data, enabling a proof-of-concept Membership Inference Attack.
AI.motivation: Generative models can retain subtle, exploitable traces of training data that never manifest in outputs. Understanding these hidden signatures is critical for copyright and privacy. Rectified Flows are increasingly deployed yet understudied in this regime, motivating a systematic analysis of what information persists along their defining interpolation path.
AI.method: The authors analyze the interpolation path $X_\lambda = (1-\lambda)X_0 + \lambda X_1$ central to Rectified Flow training. They measure the reconstruction gap between training and test data as a function of $\lambda$, observing its evolution over training. They derive a closed-form expression for the gap's peak location under Gaussian assumptions and validate predictions on both audio and image modalities.
AI.result: A bell-shaped reconstruction gap between train and test data emerges along $\lambda$, accumulating during training while standard validation metrics remain stable. The bell-shaped structure is universal across audio and images. The closed-form peak prediction holds under Gaussian assumptions. A Membership Inference Attack exploiting this $\lambda$-resolved structure successfully distinguishes training set members from non-members.
AI.conclusion: Rectified Flows leak training data membership information through a $\lambda$-dependent signal that is invisible to standard validation metrics. This exposes a privacy vulnerability and provides a principled tool for auditing generative models trained with flow matching.

title: Closed-Form Spectral Regularization for Multi-Task Model Merging
summary: Model merging combines several independently fine-tuned experts into a single multi-task model without any training data, reducing the storage, serving, and decentralized-development costs of large foundation models. State-of-the-art merging methods formulate merging as a layer-wise quadratic interference minimization problem. Although this problem admits an exact closed-form pseudoinverse solution, that solution underperforms hundreds of iterations of gradient descent in practice. The iterative loop dominates the cost of the pipeline, yet its effectiveness has remained unexplained. We revisit this regime and show that the iterative solver does not primarily act as an optimizer; rather, it serves as an implicit spectral regularizer for an ill-posed normal equation, where small-eigenvalue directions of the per-layer interference operator amplify proxy noise. Building on this finding, we formalize multi-task model merging as a noisy linear inverse problem and propose a spectral filtering estimator parameterized by a per-direction filter. We instantiate this estimator with SWUDI, a closed-form method that combines a soft exponential filter, which matches the gradient-flow trajectory of iterative descent, with a hard top-K truncation that suppresses noise-amplifying small-eigenvalue directions. Furthermore, we propose SWUDI-A, an adaptive variant that replaces the global rank hyperparameter with per-layer rank rules, further improving robustness across architectures. Both variants share a single symmetric eigendecomposition per linear layer and require no training data or optimizer state. Across four general benchmarks and a multimodal merging benchmark spanning VQA, Geometry, Chart, OCR, Grounding, and modality merging, our proposed spectral solvers match or outperform state-of-the-art merging methods. Crucially, they reduce wall-clock time by 28-72x and peak GPU memory by up to 50%.

AI.tldr: Model merging is recast as a noisy linear inverse problem where iterative solvers act as implicit spectral regularizers. The authors propose SWUDI, a closed-form spectral filtering estimator combining a soft exponential filter with hard top-K truncation, achieving 28-72× speedup and matching or surpassing state-of-the-art merging methods.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art model merging uses iterative gradient descent to solve a layer-wise quadratic interference minimization problem. The iterative loop dominates computational cost, yet why it outperforms the exact closed-form pseudoinverse solution has remained unexplained. The authors seek to understand this gap and develop a fast, principled closed-form alternative.
AI.method: The authors analyze the merging problem as a noisy linear inverse problem, showing that the normal equation is ill-posed — small-eigenvalue directions of the interference operator amplify proxy noise. They propose a spectral filtering estimator where: (1) a soft exponential filter matches the gradient-flow trajectory of iterative descent, and (2) a hard top-K truncation suppresses noise-amplifying small-eigenvalue directions (SWUDI). An adaptive variant, SWUDI-A, replaces the global rank hyperparameter with per-layer rank rules. Both require only one symmetric eigendecomposition per linear layer, with no training data or optimizer state.
AI.result: Across four general benchmarks and a multimodal merging benchmark (spanning VQA, Geometry, Chart, OCR, Grounding, and modality merging), SWUDI and SWUDI-A match or outperform state-of-the-art merging methods while reducing wall-clock time by 28-72× and peak GPU memory by up to 50%.
AI.conclusion: Iterative solvers in model merging succeed primarily through implicit spectral regularization rather than optimization. The proposed SWUDI family provides a principled closed-form alternative that is dramatically faster, more memory-efficient, and delivers competitive or superior accuracy across diverse merging tasks.

title: Trio: Learning Time-Series Forecasting with Temporal-Spatial-Sample Attention and Structural Causal Priors
summary: Multivariate time-series forecasting requires models to reason over temporal dynamics, cross-variable dependencies, and historical input-output correspondences. Recent Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs) suggest that synthetic tasks can be useful for learning transferable inference behavior. However, directly transferring this paradigm to time-series forecasting remains difficult, since temporal order, dynamic lags, and recurring historical patterns are not naturally captured by ordinary tabular priors. Motivated by this observation, we propose Trio, a sample-aware time-series forecasting architecture based on Temporal-Spatial-Sample attention. Temporal attention captures within-window dynamics, spatial attention models inter-variable dependencies, and sample attention retrieves relevant historical lookback-future pairs to guide the current prediction. Rather than claiming a fully general PFN-style forecaster, our goal is to study how historical input-output examples can be explicitly organized and reused within a forecasting model. We further introduce a Time-Series Structural Causal Model (TS-SCM) generator to create structured synthetic forecasting tasks with dynamic lags, cross-variable interactions, noise, feedback, and distributional drift. Experiments on synthetic, industrial, and public benchmarks show that the proposed architecture improves forecasting performance. Exploratory zero-shot experiments further suggest that TS-SCM-generated tasks may provide useful structural priors, while fully general PFN-style time-series forecasting remains an open problem.

AI.tldr: Trio proposes a three-pronged attention architecture (Temporal, Spatial, Sample) for multivariate time-series forecasting, paired with a structured synthetic data generator (TS-SCM). It explores how historical input-output pairs can be explicitly retrieved and reused, showing gains on benchmarks, while acknowledging that fully general PFN-style forecasting remains unsolved.
AI.motivation: Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs) show that synthetic tasks can teach transferable inference, but time-series data poses unique challenges—temporal order, dynamic lags, and recurring patterns—that ordinary tabular priors fail to capture. The paper aims to bridge this gap by studying how historical examples can be explicitly organized and reused within a forecasting model.
AI.method: Trio uses a three-component attention mechanism: (1) Temporal attention for within-window dynamics, (2) Spatial attention for cross-variable dependencies, and (3) Sample attention to retrieve and leverage relevant historical lookback-forecast pairs. A Time-Series Structural Causal Model (TS-SCM) generator produces synthetic tasks with dynamic lags, cross-variable interactions, noise, feedback loops, and distributional drift to serve as structured priors.
AI.result: The Trio architecture improves forecasting performance across synthetic, industrial, and public benchmarks. Exploratory zero-shot experiments indicate that TS-SCM-generated tasks provide useful structural priors for transfer learning.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly organizing and reusing historical input-output examples via sample-aware attention is beneficial for time-series forecasting. TS-SCM-generated synthetic tasks offer meaningful structural priors, but fully general PFN-style time-series forecasting remains an open and unsolved problem.

title: Do Coding Agents Deceive Us? Detecting and Preventing Cheating via Capped Evaluation with Randomized Tests
summary: A growing failure mode in agent evaluation and training is that models can achieve high evaluation scores by exploiting shortcuts instead of solving the intended task, producing deceptive performance. This makes evaluation scores unreliable as measures of true task-solving ability. We propose CapCode, a framework for constructing coding datasets with randomized tests whose best achievable non-cheating performance is deliberately capped below one. This capped-performance design gives evaluation scores a clearer interpretation: scores substantially above the cap are implausible and therefore provide evidence of cheating. To prevent cheating, we propose CapReward, a reward design based on the CapCode principle to discourage optimization beyond the cap. Experiments across multiple datasets show that CapCode detects cheating while preserving performance ranking of models, and CapReward reduces cheating behavior, yielding models that better follow the intended task specification.

AI.tldr: CapCode introduces capped-performance coding benchmarks where the maximum honest score is deliberately limited, making scores above the cap evidence of cheating. CapReward is a complementary training reward that discourages optimization beyond the cap, reducing shortcut exploitation.
AI.motivation: Agent evaluation suffers from models achieving deceptive high scores via shortcuts rather than genuine task-solving, rendering benchmarks unreliable. There is a need for evaluation frameworks that can detect such cheating and training methods that prevent it.
AI.method: CapCode constructs coding datasets with randomized tests where the best achievable non-cheating performance is capped below 1.0. This creates a known upper bound for honest behavior. CapReward extends this principle to training by designing rewards that penalize or truncate optimization once the performance cap is reached, discouraging models from pursuing shortcut-driven gains beyond the cap.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that CapCode effectively detects cheating behaviors while preserving the relative performance ranking among models. CapReward successfully reduces cheating during training, producing models that adhere more faithfully to the intended task specification.
AI.conclusion: Capped-performance evaluation and reward design offer a principled approach to mitigating deceptive performance in agent benchmarks and training, improving the reliability of evaluation scores and the alignment of trained models with task intent.

title: Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence: Explanatory Insufficiency as a Driver of Representation Learning and World Models
comment: 24 pages, 25 references. Theoretical framework relating representation learning, representational emergence, and world models
summary: Representation learning is central to modern machine learning, enabling transitions from handcrafted features to learned embeddings, latent spaces, foundation models, world models, and digital twins. Yet most research examines how representations are optimized after a representational framework has been selected, while less attention is given to when a new level of representation becomes necessary. We introduce the Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence (TBER), a framework describing how new representations arise when existing ones become explanatorily insufficient. In this view, representational innovation is not only driven by more data, larger models, or greater computational power, but also by persistent explanatory gaps: situations in which a representation can still describe observations but can no longer make their organization or transformations intelligible. TBER identifies explanatory insufficiency as a positive signal for representational transition. A representation becomes insufficient not because it is necessarily false, but because its explanatory domain has been exceeded. The bootstrap dynamic follows a recursive sequence: observations reveal anomalies; anomalies expose insufficiencies; insufficiencies motivate new representations; and these new representations generate further observations and possible new insufficiencies.We formalize this process through five stages: stabilized observation, anomaly detection, recognition of explanatory insufficiency, representational emergence, and provisional stabilization. We discuss applications to representation learning, latent spaces, foundation models, world models, digital twins, adaptive biological systems, and scientific discovery. TBER suggests that future AI systems may benefit from mechanisms for detecting the explanatory limits of their own internal representations.

AI.tldr: TBER proposes that new representations emerge when existing ones encounter explanatory gaps—not merely from more data/compute—and formalizes this as a five-stage recursive bootstrap process applicable across ML, biology, and scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: Current research focuses on optimizing representations after a framework is chosen, but neglects the question of *when* and *why* a new level of representation becomes necessary. The authors aim to fill this gap by theorizing the conditions under which representational innovation occurs.
AI.method: The paper introduces a conceptual framework (TBER) grounded in the notion of "explanatory insufficiency." It formalizes representational emergence as a recursive bootstrap sequence comprising five stages: stabilized observation, anomaly detection, recognition of explanatory insufficiency, representational emergence, and provisional stabilization. The framework is developed through theoretical argumentation and illustrated via applications across multiple domains.
AI.result: TBER identifies explanatory insufficiency—where a representation can describe but no longer make intelligible the organization or transformations of observations—as the key positive signal triggering representational transition. The framework yields a recursive dynamic: observations reveal anomalies, anomalies expose insufficiencies, insufficiencies motivate new representations, which in turn generate further observations and potential new insufficiencies.
AI.conclusion: Representational innovation is driven by explanatory gaps rather than scale alone. Future AI systems may benefit from built-in mechanisms that detect the explanatory limits of their own internal representations, enabling autonomous representational emergence when current frameworks become insufficient.

title: TabSwift: An Efficient Tabular Foundation Model with Row-Wise Attention
summary: Tabular foundation models, exemplified by TabPFN, perform prediction via in-context learning, inferring test labels directly from labeled training examples. They have demonstrated competitive performance, particularly on small-to-medium datasets. However, recent tabular foundation models often improve accuracy with increasingly complex architectures, incurring higher inference cost and limiting practical deployment. In this work, we revisit the original TabPFN design and show that a lightweight row-wise attention-only backbone can remain highly competitive with two simple enhancements: a gated attention stabilization mechanism and a small set of learnable register tokens that provide global context and improve pretraining quality. The resulting model, TabSwift, supports both classification and regression, and is competitive with stronger tabular foundation models (e.g., TabPFN v2 and TabICL) while being more efficient at inference. For latency-sensitive serving, we further introduce an adaptive layer-wise early-exit mechanism that dynamically adjusts inference depth per sample. Overall, TabSwift enables efficient and anytime tabular in-context learning for practical deployments.

AI.tldr: TabSwift revisits TabPFN with a lightweight row-wise attention backbone enhanced by gated attention stabilization and learnable register tokens, achieving competitive accuracy against stronger tabular foundation models (TabPFN v2, TabICL) with lower inference cost, plus an adaptive early-exit mechanism for latency-sensitive deployment.
AI.motivation: Recent tabular foundation models pursue accuracy gains through increasingly complex architectures, raising inference costs and hindering practical deployment. The authors aim to demonstrate that a simpler, lightweight design can remain competitive while being more efficient.
AI.method: (1) A lightweight row-wise attention-only backbone (revisiting the original TabPFN design); (2) a gated attention stabilization mechanism; (3) learnable register tokens providing global context to improve pretraining quality; (4) an adaptive layer-wise early-exit mechanism that dynamically adjusts inference depth per sample for latency-sensitive serving. The model supports both classification and regression.
AI.result: TabSwift is competitive with stronger tabular foundation models (TabPFN v2, TabICL) while being more inference-efficient. The early-exit mechanism further enables anytime inference with dynamic depth adjustment.
AI.conclusion: Efficient and competitive tabular in-context learning can be achieved with a lightweight architecture augmented by simple enhancements (gated attention, register tokens), making practical deployment viable without sacrificing performance.

title: SleepExplain: Explainable Non-Rapid Eye Movement and Rapid Eye Movement Sleep Stage Classification from EEG Signal
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, 2022 25th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
summary: Classification of sleep stages is one of the most important diagnostic approaches for a variety of sleep-related disorders. Electroencephalography (EEG) is regarded as a powerful tool for examining the association between neurological effects and sleep phases since it correctly identifies sleep-related neurological alterations. During Non-Rapid Eye Movement (NREM) and Rapid Eye Movement (REM) sleep phases, a number of nerve and bodily functions are affected and therefore hold an important role both in their functionalities. This work aims to classify NREM and REM sleep stages from sleep EEG data and present a noble SleepExplain model, an explainable NREM and REM sleep stage classification to explain its predictions. In this work, sleep stages were classified using Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting ensemble classification models. Overall, we obtained an accuracy of 92.54% (Random Forest), 94.25% (Gradient Boosting), and 94.30% (XGBoost). For explainable classification model, we utilized a game theoretic approach, SHAP (SHapley Addictive exPlanations) to offer a convincing explanation for the prediction.

AI.tldr: This paper presents SleepExplain, an explainable model for classifying NREM and REM sleep stages from EEG data using ensemble methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost), achieving up to 94.30% accuracy, with SHAP values used for model interpretability.
AI.motivation: Sleep stage classification is critical for diagnosing sleep-related disorders, and EEG-based approaches can identify neurological alterations during NREM and REM sleep. The authors aim to not only achieve high classification accuracy but also provide explainability for the model's predictions, which is often lacking in automated sleep staging systems.
AI.method: Three ensemble classifiers — Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting — were employed to classify NREM and REM sleep stages from sleep EEG data. For explainability, the authors applied SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), a game-theoretic approach, to interpret and explain the model predictions.
AI.result: The classification models achieved accuracies of 92.54% (Random Forest), 94.25% (Gradient Boosting), and 94.30% (XGBoost), with XGBoost performing best. SHAP provided convincing explanations for the predictions made by the SleepExplain model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SleepExplain framework demonstrates that ensemble learning models, particularly XGBoost, can effectively classify NREM and REM sleep stages from EEG with high accuracy. Incorporating SHAP-based explainability enhances the clinical trustworthiness of the automated classification system.

title: Breaking the Ice: Analyzing Cold Start Latency in vLLM
summary: As scalable inference services become popular, the cold start latency of an inference engine becomes important. Today, vLLM has evolved into the de facto inference engine of choice for many inference workloads. Although popular, due to its complexity and rapid evolution, there has not been a systematic study of its startup latency. With major architectural innovations such as the V1 API and the introduction of torch.compile, this paper presents the first detailed performance characterization of vLLM startup latency. We break down the startup process into six foundational steps and demonstrate that it is predominantly CPU bound. Each step exhibits consistent and interpretable scaling trends with respect to model-level and system-level parameters, enabling fine-grained attribution of latency sources. Building on these insights, we develop a lightweight analytical model that accurately predicts vLLM startup latency for a given hardware configuration, providing actionable guidance for resource planning in large-scale inference environments. All benchmarking datasets, analysis tools, and prediction scripts are open sourced at https://github.com/upb-cn/vllm-startup-profiler.

AI.tldr: First systematic characterization of vLLM startup latency, breaking it into six CPU-bound steps with consistent scaling trends, culminating in an analytical model for accurate latency prediction.
AI.motivation: vLLM has become the de facto inference engine, yet its startup latency — increasingly critical for scalable inference services — has never been systematically studied, especially given recent architectural changes like V1 API and torch.compile.
AI.method: The authors decompose vLLM's startup process into six foundational steps, perform detailed performance characterization by measuring each step's latency, analyze scaling trends with respect to model-level and system-level parameters, and develop a lightweight analytical model for latency prediction.
AI.result: Startup latency is predominantly CPU-bound. Each of the six steps exhibits consistent, interpretable scaling trends, enabling fine-grained attribution of latency sources. The analytical model accurately predicts startup latency for given hardware configurations.
AI.conclusion: The characterization provides actionable guidance for resource planning in large-scale inference environments. All artifacts (benchmarking datasets, analysis tools, prediction scripts) are open-sourced.

title: A robust PPG foundation model using multimodal physiological supervision
summary: Photoplethysmography (PPG), a non-invasive measure of changes in blood volume, is widely used in both wearable devices and clinical settings. Recent PPG foundation models either use open-source ICU datasets with pretraining paradigms that require curated data and thus complicate generalization to field-like data, or use closed-source field-like PPG data. In contrast, we propose a PPG foundation model that does not require high-quality or field-like pretraining data, and instead leverages accompanying electrocardiogram and respiratory signals in ICU datasets to select contrastive samples during pretraining. Our approach allows the model to retain and learn from noisy PPG segments, improving robustness at inference. Our model, pretrained on 3x fewer subjects than existing state-of-the-art approaches, achieves performance improvements on 14 out of 15 diverse downstream tasks, including field-like daily activity and heart rate prediction. Our results demonstrate that multimodal supervision can integrate complementary physiological information to improve the robustness of PPG foundation models and enhance their generalization to consumer-grade data.

AI.tldr: A PPG foundation model pretrained on ICU data using multimodal signals (ECG, respiration) for contrastive sample selection, achieving superior generalization to consumer-grade data with 3× fewer training subjects than prior work.
AI.motivation: Existing PPG foundation models either require curated, high-quality ICU data (limiting field generalization) or rely on closed-source field-like data. There is a need for a model that works well with readily available noisy ICU data and generalizes robustly to real-world, consumer-grade PPG signals.
AI.method: The authors propose a contrastive pretraining framework that uses accompanying ECG and respiratory signals from ICU datasets to intelligently select contrastive samples. Rather than discarding noisy PPG segments, the multimodal supervision helps the model retain and learn from them, improving robustness. The model is pretrained on significantly fewer subjects (3× less) than state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.result: The model achieves performance improvements on 14 out of 15 diverse downstream tasks, including field-like daily activity recognition and heart rate prediction, despite using substantially less pretraining data.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal supervision using complementary physiological signals (ECG, respiration) can effectively improve PPG foundation model robustness and generalization to consumer-grade data, reducing reliance on large-scale, high-quality, or field-specific pretraining datasets.

title: Self-evolving LLM agents with in-distribution Optimization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently emerged as powerful controllers for interactive agents in complex environments, yet training them to perform reliable long-horizon decision making remains a fundamental challenge. A key difficulty lies in credit assignment: agents often receive delayed rewards only at the end of episodes. In this paper, we propose Q-Evolve, a self-evolving framework for LLM agents that unifies automatic process-reward labeling and policy learning within a principled in-distribution reinforcement learning paradigm. In each evolving iteration, our method learns an in-distribution critic from a hybrid off-policy dataset that combines expert demonstrations with agent-generated trajectories, stabilizing Bellman backups in sparse-reward settings via a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective. The learned value function is then used to derive step-wise process rewards through advantage estimation, enabling dense and reliable supervision without environment backtracking or human annotation. Leveraging these signals, we perform behavior-proximal policy optimization that evolves the agent over the data used for process reward labeling, allowing iterative self-improvement without exacerbating distribution shift. We evaluate our method on AlfWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld, showing Q-Evolve outperforms strong baselines in sample efficiency, robustness, and overall task performance. Our results demonstrate that stable agent self-evolution is achievable through the co-evolution of process-level supervision and policy, both grounded within a shared in-distribution learning loop.

AI.tldr: Q-Evolve is a self-evolving framework for LLM agents that jointly learns a value function critic and optimizes policy using in-distribution reinforcement learning. It generates dense step-wise process rewards via advantage estimation from a learned critic, trained with a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective on hybrid off-policy data, enabling stable iterative self-improvement without human annotation.
AI.motivation: Training LLM agents for long-horizon decision-making suffers from the credit assignment problem: rewards are sparse and delayed (only at episode end). Existing solutions either rely on expensive human annotations, environment backtracking, or suffer from distribution shift when iteratively improving policies. The authors aim to unify automatic process-reward labeling and policy learning within a stable, principled in-distribution RL framework.
AI.method: Q-Evolve operates in iterative cycles: (1) A hybrid off-policy dataset combining expert demonstrations and agent-generated trajectories is collected. (2) An in-distribution critic (value function) is learned via a weighted Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) objective, which stabilizes Bellman backups in sparse-reward settings by avoiding querying out-of-distribution actions. (3) Step-wise process rewards are derived from the learned value function through advantage estimation, providing dense supervision without requiring environment backtracking or human labels. (4) Behavior-proximal policy optimization uses these process rewards to update the LLM agent, staying close to the data distribution used for reward labeling to mitigate distribution shift. This loop co-evolves process-level supervision and policy.
AI.result: Q-Evolve outperformed strong baselines on AlfWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld across sample efficiency, robustness, and overall task performance. The results demonstrate that stable self-evolution is achievable when process-level supervision and policy co-evolve within a shared in-distribution learning loop.
AI.conclusion: Stable and effective self-improvement for LLM agents in long-horizon sparse-reward environments is possible through the co-evolution of process-level rewards and policy, grounded in an in-distribution RL paradigm. Q-Evolve removes the need for human annotation or environment backtracking while maintaining robustness against distribution shift.

title: Covariance Shrinkage via Stochastic Interpolation
summary: We recast classical shrinkage of high-dimensional covariance estimators as empirical risk minimization over a parametric stochastic interpolant between a source and a target distribution. This formalism recovers known shrinkage estimators as special cases and reveals three distinct mechanisms for reducing statistical risk: (i) Scheduling: the interpolant schedule determines the class of admissible covariances, and hence the achievable risk. (ii) Flow maps and couplings: whereas naive constructions amount to assuming independence between the distributions, specific coupling structures (e.g., solutions of optimal transport problems) can lower the empirical risk. Moreover, non-linear flow maps realizing such couplings free the interpolant covariance from the eigenbasis of the empirical estimate, enabling eigenvector regularization. (iii) Early stopping: estimators defined by integrating a regressed vector field afford an additional bias-variance trade-off through approximation of the true interpolant distribution. We then propose a neural estimator of the interpolant, together with an upper bound on its quadratic risk in terms of the interpolant approximation error, and validate both on synthetic experiments. Finally, we apply the estimator to real neuroimaging data, demonstrating the additional regularization power this approach offers in practice.

AI.tldr: The paper reformulates high-dimensional covariance shrinkage as empirical risk minimization over a parametric stochastic interpolant between source and target distributions. It identifies three risk-reduction mechanisms—scheduling, coupling/flow maps, and early stopping—proposes a neural estimator with a risk bound, and validates the approach on synthetic and neuroimaging data.
AI.motivation: Classical covariance shrinkage estimators lack a unified framework for understanding how different design choices affect statistical risk. The authors aim to provide a principled formalism that unifies existing shrinkage methods and reveals new mechanisms for improving high-dimensional covariance estimation.
AI.method: The authors recast covariance shrinkage as empirical risk minimization over a parametric stochastic interpolant connecting a source (e.g., the sample covariance) and a target distribution. They analyze three mechanisms: (i) interpolation schedule design, (ii) optimal transport–based couplings and nonlinear flow maps for eigenvector regularization, and (iii) early stopping of integrated regressed vector fields. A neural network estimator is proposed along with an upper bound on its quadratic risk.
AI.result: Known shrinkage estimators are recovered as special cases. The neural estimator, accompanied by a risk bound tied to interpolant approximation error, is validated on synthetic experiments. Application to real neuroimaging data demonstrates additional practical regularization benefits.
AI.conclusion: The stochastic interpolant framework not only unifies existing covariance shrinkage methods but also introduces new tools—optimal transport couplings, nonlinear flow maps, and early stopping—that expand the regularization capabilities of high-dimensional covariance estimation, with practical utility demonstrated on neuroimaging data.

title: Making the Most of Limited Data: Score-Aware Training for Text-to-Music Generation
summary: State-of-the-art text-to-music generation systems rely on massive proprietary datasets and industrial-scale compute, making it impossible to disentangle architectural contributions from resource advantages. We propose \textit{score-aware training}, which treats audio-caption alignment score as a direct supervision signal throughout the pipeline. Rather than discarding low-scoring segments, we repurpose them via a CLAP-conditioned Beta noise timestep schedule that routes them to high-noise training regimes, acting as an effective implicit regularizer. Complementarily, segment-level filtering removes the most misaligned examples, and a two-stage caption procedure bridges the distribution gap between verbose training captions and concise inference prompts. A REPA auxiliary loss further transfers structured semantic knowledge from pretrained CLAP and MuQ encoders without additional data. Our 450M-parameter FluxAudio-based system, submitted to the ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge Efficiency Track, ranked 2nd across both tracks in the objective evaluation and 3rd in the Efficiency Track in the final MOS evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes score-aware training for text-to-music generation, using CLAP alignment scores to guide noise scheduling, filtering, and caption rewriting. A 450M FluxAudio system achieved 2nd place (objective) and 3rd place (MOS) in the ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge Efficiency Track, disentangling architecture from resource advantages.
AI.motivation: Existing SOTA text-to-music systems depend on massive proprietary datasets and industrial compute, making it impossible to separate true architectural innovations from mere resource scaling. The authors aim to improve training efficiency and audio-text alignment without relying on large-scale resources.
AI.method: The core contribution is score-aware training, which uses CLAP audio-caption alignment scores as a supervision signal. Key components: (1) CLAP-conditioned Beta noise timestep schedule routes low-scoring segments to high-noise regimes as implicit regularization; (2) Segment-level filtering removes the most misaligned examples; (3) A two-stage caption procedure bridges the gap between verbose training captions and concise inference prompts; (4) REPA auxiliary loss transfers structured semantic knowledge from pretrained CLAP and MuQ encoders without extra data. The system uses a 450M-parameter FluxAudio backbone.
AI.result: The system ranked 2nd across both tracks in objective evaluation and 3rd in the Efficiency Track in the final MOS evaluation at the ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge.
AI.conclusion: Score-aware training effectively leverages alignment signals to improve text-to-music generation quality and efficiency, achieving competitive results without massive proprietary resources, thereby enabling fairer comparison of architectural contributions.

title: Generative Modeling of Discrete Latent Structures via Dynamic Policy Gradients
summary: Many scientific problems require inferring unobserved mechanistic latent states from indirect observations. While classical approaches, including expectation maximization, do not scale to combinatorially large spaces, deep learning approaches such as variational autoencoders typically form artificial latent states rather than reconstructing the mechanistic ground-truth states. Here, we introduce GReinSS, a policy learning framework that uses dynamically rescaled rewards to learn latent state distributions that maximize the observed data likelihood. We show that GReinSS accurately reconstructs simulated latent sets and latent graphs, outperforming alternative policy learning and generative modeling baselines. Additionally, GReinSS reconstructs isoforms from real short-read RNA sequencing data that better match isoforms detected by orthogonal long-read sequencing than the standard RSEM algorithm. Overall, GReinSS is a principled and practically effective approach for generative modeling and inference of combinatorial latent states from indirect observations.

AI.tldr: GReinSS is a policy learning framework that uses dynamically rescaled rewards to infer combinatorial mechanistic latent states from indirect observations, outperforming both classical and deep learning baselines on simulated data and real RNA-seq isoform reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Classical approaches like expectation maximization cannot scale to combinatorially large latent state spaces, while deep learning methods (e.g., VAEs) tend to learn artificial latent representations rather than recovering the true mechanistic latent states. There is a need for a principled method that can accurately reconstruct ground-truth combinatorial latent states from indirect observations.
AI.method: GReinSS frames latent state inference as a policy learning problem, employing dynamically rescaled rewards to learn latent state distributions that maximize the observed data likelihood. This reinforcement learning–based generative modeling approach directly targets the recovery of mechanistic latent states.
AI.result: GReinSS accurately reconstructs simulated latent sets and latent graphs, outperforming alternative policy learning and generative modeling baselines. On real short-read RNA sequencing data, it reconstructs isoforms that match long-read sequencing–detected isoforms better than the standard RSEM algorithm.
AI.conclusion: GReinSS provides a principled and practically effective framework for generative modeling and inference of combinatorial latent states from indirect observations, bridging the gap between scalability and mechanistic interpretability.

title: Reversible Foundations: Training a 120B Sparse MoE through State-Preserving Scaling
comment: 58 pages, 9 figures, 37 tables. Code: https://github.com/The-School-of-AI/LLM. Released models: huggingface.co/theschoolofai/LightningLM-0.1V-{2B, 5B-MoE, 9B-MoE, 120B-MoE}. Companion work: arXiv:2605.29379 (BrahmicTokenizer-131K), arXiv:2605.29459 (Kronecker Embeddings)
summary:
This paper reports on training a hundred-billion-parameter sparse mixture of experts on a single eight-GPU node, end to end. LightningLM 0.1V is a recurrence-backbone language model family grown in four stages from a small dense seed, through a 5B and a 9B mixture of experts, to a 120B model with 460 routed experts under top-12 routing. Each larger model is grown from the trained weights of the smaller one; active parameters rise monotonically from 1.78B at the dense seed to 5.93B at 120B (about 5% of the 118.67B stored). The full lineage runs on single nodes, the larger stages at 8K context, reaching a released training loss of 1.78 at 120B scale.
  This is a systems and experience report. It is organized around three disciplines. Reversibility: a reversible recurrence stack reconstructs activations in the backward pass instead of storing them, holding activation memory flat as the model grows. State-preserving growth: each expansion (dense to MoE, shallow to deep, few experts to many) is given as a reproducible principle paired with the failure that results from getting it wrong; several failures are silent. Single-node economics: the 120B trains through TQP, a strategy of quantized base expert weights and trained low-rank adapters that carries optimizer state on 2.26B adapter parameters rather than 100B+ resident in routed experts, cutting expert-path optimizer state by a factor of ~45.
  What is new is the integration of known primitives, not any primitive in isolation: one grown lineage running end to end on a single node, documented at practitioner level, with per-domain held-out loss as evidence that targeted capabilities (multilingual Indic competence, code) were learned by construction. Model family, tokenizer, and training code are released.

AI.tldr: LightningLM trains a 120B-parameter sparse MoE language model entirely on a single 8-GPU node by combining reversible recurrence, state-preserving model growth across four stages, and a quantized low-rank adapter strategy (TQP) that slashes optimizer memory ~45×.
AI.motivation: Enable training of hundred-billion-parameter sparse mixture-of-experts models on severely resource-constrained hardware (a single 8-GPU node), making large-scale MoE training accessible outside large clusters and documenting the integration challenges and silent failure modes.
AI.method: Three integrated disciplines: (1) Reversibility — a reversible recurrence stack recomputes activations in the backward pass, keeping activation memory constant. (2) State-preserving growth — a four-stage lineage (dense seed → 5B MoE → 9B MoE → 120B MoE with 460 top-12 routed experts) where each larger model inherits trained weights from the smaller one, with explicit growth principles and documented failure modes. (3) Single-node economics via TQP — quantized base expert weights paired with trained low-rank adapters, confining optimizer state to only 2.26B adapter parameters instead of 100B+ expert parameters.
AI.result: The full 120B model (5.93B active parameters, 118.67B stored) trained end-to-end on one 8-GPU node at 8K context, reaching a training loss of 1.78. Targeted capabilities (multilingual Indic, code) were acquired by construction, evidenced by per-domain held-out loss.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is the integration of known primitives into a single reproducible lineage, not any primitive in isolation. The paper demonstrates that careful systems co-design (reversibility + growth + TQP) makes hundred-billion-parameter MoE training viable on a single node, and releases the model family, tokenizer, and training code.

title: A Comprehensive Anatomy of Human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM Mathematical Reasoning
summary: The emergence of "Aha moments" in large language models, particularly DeepSeek-R1-0120, has raised the question of whether these systems genuinely reason or merely imitate the appearance of reasoning. We conduct a comprehensive empirical comparison between model and human reasoning across all 30 problems from AIME 2025, exhaustively annotating 10,247 reasoning steps into five functional categories: Analysis, Inference, Branch, Backtrace, and Reflection. We find a clear structural difference. Human solutions maintain a compact alternation between analysis and deduction, whereas DeepSeek-R1 frequently revisits intermediate results, performs shallow and often unnecessary verification, and loops through local checks without meaningful logical progress. We describe this as topological mimicry: reproducing the surface form of reasoning without its functional role. Despite this, we identify two signals of genuine reasoning. First, successful traces exhibit stable use of branching and backtracking, while failed traces either underuse or overuse exploratory actions. Second, reflection is only effective when placed within deductive inference; reflections trapped in analysis loops focus on local numerical details while missing global logical errors. These findings suggest that current long-CoT models may be rewarded more for the appearance of reasoning than for genuine deductive progress. We discuss directions for improving evaluation and training, including measuring cross-trace stability, penalising "spinning-wheel" traces, encouraging deeper logical correction, and reallocating inference-time compute toward deduction and backtracking. Overall, reasoning quality depends not simply on how much reflection occurs, but on whether reflection appears consistently and at the appropriate logical scale.

AI.tldr: DeepSeek-R1's long-chain-of-thought reasoning structurally differs from human reasoning, exhibiting "topological mimicry" — reproducing the surface form of reasoning (analysis, reflection, backtracking) without genuine deductive progress. Analysis of 10,247 reasoning steps across AIME 2025 shows humans alternate compactly between analysis and deduction, while the model loops through shallow verification. However, stable branching/backtracking and reflection embedded within deduction (not analysis loops) signal genuine reasoning. The paper recommends penalizing "spinning-wheel" traces and reallocating inference compute toward deduction.
AI.motivation: The emergence of "Aha moments" in models like DeepSeek-R1 raises a foundational question: are these systems genuinely reasoning, or merely imitating the surface appearance of reasoning? The authors aim to move beyond outcome-based evaluation to rigorously compare the structural and functional properties of model-generated reasoning traces against human reasoning.
AI.method: The authors exhaustively annotated 10,247 reasoning steps from DeepSeek-R1-0120 and human solutions across all 30 AIME 2025 problems. Steps were classified into five functional categories: Analysis (information extraction, setup), Inference (deductive steps), Branch (exploring alternative paths), Backtrace (revisiting prior steps), and Reflection (self-checking/verification). Structural comparison focused on step-type distributions, transition patterns, and the functional role of reflection within trace topologies.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Human solutions show compact analysis–inference alternation; DeepSeek-R1 shows excessive revisiting, shallow verification, and loops without logical progress — termed "topological mimicry." (2) Successful traces exhibit stable branching/backtracking; failed traces either underuse or overuse exploratory actions. (3) Reflection is only effective when embedded within deductive inference; reflections trapped in analysis loops fixate on local numerical details while missing global logical errors.
AI.conclusion: Current long-CoT models may be rewarded more for the appearance of reasoning than genuine deductive progress. Improving evaluation requires measuring cross-trace stability, penalizing "spinning-wheel" traces, and encouraging deeper logical correction. Training should reallocate inference-time compute from shallow reflection toward deduction and backtracking. Ultimately, reasoning quality depends on whether reflection occurs consistently and at the appropriate logical scale, not on its mere quantity.

title: Sparsely gated tiny linear experts
summary: Sparsity allows scaling model parameters without proportionally increasing computational cost. While mixture of experts (MoE) models are made increasingly sparse, individual experts typically remain large and dense. Here, we demonstrate that further increasing sparsity by shrinking each expert to consist of a single neuron and selecting a tiny fraction of many available neurons can improve compute efficiency and interpretability. Counterintuitively, the key to achieving both is removing the nonlinearity typically applied to the experts, resulting in a network of sparsely gated linear neurons (sgatlin). In an isoflop comparison, we find that replacing all transformer feedforward layers with sgatlin improves perplexity in language models across different compute budgets. At the same time, the sparsity and linearity of the resulting feedforward circuits present new opportunities for model interpretability. In a small-scale case study, we demonstrate that feedforward circuits in sgatlin can be interpreted without having to train additional replacement models. We find that they form semantically structured clusters and are causally implicated in factual recall. Our findings paint a possible path towards compute-efficient and interpretable transformer feedforward layers.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes sgatlin (sparsely gated linear neurons), where each MoE expert is reduced to a single linear neuron with sparse gating. Removing nonlinearity from experts, counterintuitively, improves both compute efficiency (better perplexity in isoflop comparisons) and interpretability (feedforward circuits form semantically meaningful clusters and are causally involved in factual recall).
AI.motivation: MoE models scale by adding sparse experts, but individual experts remain large and dense, leaving room for further sparsity. The authors aim to push sparsity to the extreme — single-neuron experts — and explore whether this can simultaneously improve compute efficiency and model interpretability.
AI.method: The authors propose sgatlin: replace transformer feedforward layers with a large pool of single-neuron experts, each being purely linear (no activation function). A sparse gating mechanism selects a small fraction of these neurons per token. They evaluate via isoflop comparisons across compute budgets and perform interpretability analyses (circuit-level probing, causal intervention for factual recall).
AI.result: In isoflop comparisons, sgatlin feedforward layers yield better perplexity than standard transformers across various compute budgets. Interpretability experiments reveal that the linear neurons form semantically structured clusters and are causally implicated in factual recall, enabling circuit interpretation without training auxiliary replacement models.
AI.conclusion: Extreme sparsity with linearized single-neuron experts is a viable path toward both compute-efficient and interpretable transformer feedforward layers, challenging the assumption that nonlinearity in experts is necessary.

title: Video-Based Prediction of In-Flight Particle Characteristics in Atmospheric Plasma Spraying
comment: Accepted at ECML PKDD 2026 (Applied Data Science Track)
summary: Atmospheric plasma spraying (APS) is a widely used coating process in which in-flight particle temperature and velocity strongly influence coating quality. However, these particle characteristics are difficult to monitor continuously during operation, motivating the development of non-invasive data-driven diagnostic methods. In this work, we investigate the predictive potential of high-speed video observations of the plasma plume for estimating in-flight particle characteristics in APS. We introduce three different video-derived feature representations and evaluate them using Tabular Prior-Data Fitted Networks (TabPFN), convolutional neural networks (CNN), and classical regression baselines including Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Support Vector Regression, and XGBoost. Experiments are conducted using grouped leave-one-out cross-validation on 126 labeled pre- and post-spray video recordings from 63 APS spray runs. Across the engineered feature experiments, TabPFN achieves the most consistent performance for temperature prediction, reaching R2 = 0.86 using the combined feature representation. CNN models particularly perform stronger for velocity prediction, achieving R2 of 0.81. In addition, we evaluate models operating directly on raw video frames using pretrained CNNs and find that the highest performance is achieved by a pretrained CNN with a regression head with R2 of 0.90 and 0.82 for temperature and velocity, respectively. The results demonstrate that video-derived plume information provides a promising and scalable foundation for non-invasive APS diagnostics and real-time process monitoring.

AI.tldr: High-speed video of plasma plumes can predict in-flight particle temperature (R²=0.90) and velocity (R²=0.82) in atmospheric plasma spraying, with pretrained CNNs on raw frames outperforming engineered features and classical regressors, offering a non-invasive diagnostic approach for real-time process monitoring.
AI.motivation: In-flight particle temperature and velocity critically influence coating quality in APS but are difficult to monitor continuously during operation. The authors seek to develop non-invasive, data-driven diagnostic methods using easily acquired high-speed video of the plasma plume as a proxy for these particle characteristics.
AI.method: Three video-derived feature representations (including combined engineered features) were evaluated using TabPFN, CNNs, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, SVR, and XGBoost, plus pretrained CNNs operating directly on raw frames. Experiments used grouped leave-one-out cross-validation on 126 labeled video recordings from 63 APS spray runs.
AI.result: TabPFN achieved R²=0.86 for temperature using combined engineered features. CNNs reached R²=0.81 for velocity. Pretrained CNNs with regression heads performed best overall: R²=0.90 (temperature) and R²=0.82 (velocity).
AI.conclusion: Video-derived plasma plume information provides a promising, scalable foundation for non-invasive APS diagnostics and real-time process monitoring, with pretrained CNNs on raw frames delivering the highest predictive accuracy.

title: Discovering Multiscale Deep Formulas in Complex Systems via Neural-Guided Lambda Calculus
comment: 35 pages, 5 figures; Supplementary Information available as an ancillary file (79 pages)
summary: A fundamental problem in science is identifying underlying patterns of complex systems in the form of concise mathematical formulas. Current Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based methods have shown strong performance in single-scale systems, yet remain limited in identifying scale-specific formulas in multiscale complex systems. We present Deflex, an end-to-end AI method to automatically extract multiscale formulas with potentially different forms, including invariants and distributions, from complex systems. Deflex consists of two subsystems named Deflexformer and Deflexpressor. Deflexpressor is a lambda-calculus symbolic regression model for higher-order formulas. Deflexformer is a decomposable deep energy model for learning unified representations across scales. Deflexpressor generates synthetic data to pre-train Deflexformer, which then guides formula discovery by decoupling multiscale latent relationships. Across six representative complex systems with diverse behaviors, Deflex achieves up to 7-fold higher efficiency than the state-of-the-art methods while enabling automated multiscale discovery. Our work could be a useful tool for scientific discovery across disciplines.

AI.tldr: Deflex is an end-to-end AI method combining a lambda-calculus symbolic regression model (Deflexpressor) and a decomposable deep energy model (Deflexformer) to automatically extract multiscale formulas from complex systems, achieving up to 7-fold efficiency gains over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-based formula discovery methods perform well on single-scale systems but cannot identify scale-specific formulas (invariants, distributions, etc.) in multiscale complex systems. There is a need for automated extraction of mathematical formulas at different scales with potentially heterogeneous forms.
AI.method: Deflex comprises two subsystems: (1) Deflexpressor — a lambda-calculus symbolic regression model capable of discovering higher-order formulas; (2) Deflexformer — a decomposable deep energy model that learns unified cross-scale representations. Deflexpressor first generates synthetic data to pre-train Deflexformer, which then decouples multiscale latent relationships to guide formula discovery.
AI.result: Across six representative complex systems with diverse behaviors, Deflex achieves up to 7-fold higher efficiency than state-of-the-art methods while enabling automated multiscale formula discovery.
AI.conclusion: Deflex provides a useful, cross-disciplinary tool for automated scientific discovery by successfully extracting multiscale mathematical formulas from complex systems, overcoming limitations of prior single-scale methods.

title: Time series Foundation Models based on Physics-Informed Synthetic Histories for Cold-Start Photovoltaic Forecasting
comment: To be published in the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data
summary: At commissioning time, Photovoltaic (PV) operators must forecast production before target-site observations are available, limiting the direct use of standard supervised forecasters. This cold-start setting is addressed with a zero-shot pipeline that generates a synthetic production history from plant metadata and meteorological covariates, enabling time-series foundation models (TSFMs) to forecast through inference-time conditioning. Five TSFMs are benchmarked against classical baselines under strict Cold-Start Baseline, Real Feedback, and Self-Forecast Feedback strategies. The evaluation spans $440$ PV sites across four datasets and diverse climate regimes. Covariate-aware foundation models outperform baselines by approximately $1.7-2\times$: TabPFN-TS achieves the lowest error under Real Feedback (MAE $0.514$, RMSE $0.721$ $kWh$ ${kWp}^{-1}$ ${d}^{-1}$), while Chronos-2 is most robust under Self-Forecast Feedback. Performance is largely insensitive to the synthetic-history source, indicating that accuracy is driven more by the availability of plausible temporal context than by the specific generator.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot pipeline uses synthetic production history from plant metadata and weather data to enable time-series foundation models (TSFMs) to forecast PV output before any on-site observations exist. Tested on 440 sites, covariate-aware TSFMs outperform classical baselines by 1.7–2×, with TabPFN-TS best under real feedback and Chronos-2 most robust under self-forecast feedback. Accuracy depends more on having plausible temporal context than on the specific synthetic-data generator.
AI.motivation: PV operators face a cold-start problem: at commissioning, no target-site production data exists, making standard supervised forecasting unusable. The authors aim to solve this by enabling zero-shot forecasting using only plant metadata and meteorological covariates.
AI.method: A zero-shot pipeline generates synthetic production history from plant metadata and meteorological covariates. This synthetic history serves as conditioning context for five time-series foundation models (TSFMs) at inference time. Three evaluation strategies are used: Cold-Start Baseline (no real data), Real Feedback (real observations incrementally incorporated), and Self-Forecast Feedback (model's own predictions fed back as context). Benchmarked on 440 PV sites across four datasets and diverse climates against classical baselines.
AI.result: Covariate-aware foundation models outperform baselines by ~1.7–2×. TabPFN-TS achieves lowest error under Real Feedback (MAE 0.514, RMSE 0.721 kWh/kWp/day). Chronos-2 is most robust under Self-Forecast Feedback. Performance is largely insensitive to the synthetic-history source, suggesting plausible temporal context matters more than the generator's fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot PV forecasting via synthetic history conditioning with TSFMs is viable and substantially outperforms classical methods in cold-start settings. The key insight is that providing any plausible temporal context to a foundation model is more important than the quality of that synthetic context, enabling practical deployment before real data accumulates.

title: Unsupervised Continual Clustering via Forward-Backward Knowledge Distillation
comment: Accepted at ECML PKDD 2026 (Research Track). arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2405.19234
summary: Unsupervised Continual Learning (UCL) aims to enable neural networks to learn sequential tasks without labels or access to past data. A major challenge in this setting is Catastrophic Forgetting, where models forget previously learned tasks upon learning new ones. This challenge is amplified in UCL due to the absence of labels to guide learning and memory retention. Existing mitigation strategies, such as knowledge distillation and replay buffers, often raise memory and privacy concerns. Moreover, current UCL methods largely overlook clustering-specific objectives. To fill this gap, we introduce Unsupervised Continual Clustering (UCC) and propose Forward-Backward Knowledge Distillation for Continual Clustering (FBCC). FBCC employs a continual teacher network with a clustering projector and lightweight task-specific students. Through a dual-phase forward-backward distillation process, the teacher learns new clusters while preserving previously discovered cluster structure without storing past data. FBCC represents a pioneering approach to UCC, demonstrating improved clustering performance across sequential tasks. Experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that FBCC consistently outperforms existing continual learning baselines in clustering accuracy while significantly reducing catastrophic forgetting.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Unsupervised Continual Clustering (UCC) and proposes Forward-Backward Knowledge Distillation for Continual Clustering (FBCC), a method that uses a continual teacher network with a clustering projector and task-specific students to learn sequential tasks without labels or past data, outperforming existing baselines on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised Continual Learning (UCL) faces severe catastrophic forgetting, amplified by the absence of labels. Existing solutions (knowledge distillation, replay buffers) raise memory/privacy concerns and neglect clustering-specific objectives. The authors aim to fill the gap of Unsupervised Continual Clustering (UCC) with a method that preserves cluster structure across tasks without storing past data.
AI.method: FBCC uses a continual teacher network equipped with a clustering projector and multiple lightweight task-specific student networks. A dual-phase forward-backward distillation process enables the teacher to learn new clusters while preserving previously discovered cluster structures, all without requiring storage of past data.
AI.result: Experiments on four benchmark datasets show FBCC consistently outperforms existing continual learning baselines in clustering accuracy and significantly reduces catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: FBCC is a pioneering approach to Unsupervised Continual Clustering, effectively addressing catastrophic forgetting through forward-backward knowledge distillation without relying on replay buffers or labels, and sets a new direction for UCC research.

title: Graph Neural Network leveraging Higher-order Class Label Connectivity for Heterophilous Graphs
summary: Node classification in graph neural networks (GNNs) has been widely applied in various fields of graph analysis. GNNs achieve high-accuracy node classification in homophilous graphs, where nodes with the same class label tend to be connected. However, their performance remains limited in heterophilous graphs, where nodes with different class labels are more likely to be connected. In particular, current GNNs derived from graph convolutional networks cannot capture higher-order class label connectivity, which is frequently observed in real-world heterophilous graphs. To address this issue, we propose a novel classifier, Label Context Classifier (LCC), designed to capture higher-order class label connectivity in directed graphs. LCC estimates the class label of a target node by leveraging label context embeddings that are generated through four distinct types of walks. In addition, our approach allows the integration of LCC and any GNN by adaptively learning their importance. Experimental results demonstrate that GNNs integrated with LCC outperform SOTA methods and the label context embeddings improve the node classification performance in heterophilous directed graphs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Label Context Classifier (LCC), a novel classifier that captures higher-order class label connectivity in heterophilous directed graphs by generating label context embeddings through four distinct walk types. LCC can be adaptively integrated with any GNN, and the combined approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods on node classification in heterophilous directed graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing GNNs perform poorly on heterophilous graphs (where connected nodes have different labels) because they cannot capture higher-order class label connectivity patterns that are common in real-world heterophilous graphs. Standard graph convolutional approaches primarily model local homophily and fail to leverage these longer-range label dependencies.
AI.method: The authors propose Label Context Classifier (LCC), which estimates a target node's class label by generating label context embeddings from four distinct types of walks on directed graphs. These walks capture diverse higher-order connectivity patterns among class labels. LCC can be integrated with any GNN through an adaptive importance-learning mechanism that balances the contributions of both components.
AI.result: GNNs integrated with LCC outperform state-of-the-art methods on node classification tasks. The label context embeddings specifically improve classification performance on heterophilous directed graphs.
AI.conclusion: LCC effectively addresses the limitation of GNNs in heterophilous settings by modeling higher-order label connectivity. The adaptive integration strategy makes LCC a flexible plug-in enhancement for existing GNN architectures, advancing node classification on directed, heterophilous graphs.

title: Drifting Models for Surrogate Flow Modeling
comment: Accepted to the 2nd International Symposium AI and Fluid Mechanics 2026
summary: While Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) provides high-fidelity flow fields for optimizing indoor environments, its computational cost limits rapid exploration. To solve this problem generative surrogates offer better distribution modeling than deterministic networks, but iterative sampling is slow. To enable high-quality, single-pass generation, we adapt the novel generative drifting framework to fluid mechanics. We introduce a conditional architecture that performs drifting in a learned VAE latent space and uses label-aware masking to align generated samples with their boundary conditions. Our label-conditioned model matches iterative diffusion in accuracy and flow consistency while running two orders of magnitude faster. Additionally, we propose a spatial-conditioning variant that establishes a promising path towards generalization to unseen geometries. Ultimately, conditional drifting serves as a highly efficient alternative to diffusion based approaches, unlocking real-time CFD surrogates where inference speed is critical.

AI.tldr: The paper adapts a generative drifting framework to CFD surrogate modeling, introducing a conditional VAE-latent-space architecture with label-aware masking that achieves diffusion-level accuracy at two orders of magnitude faster inference, enabling real-time indoor environment flow prediction.
AI.motivation: CFD provides high-fidelity flow fields for indoor environment optimization but is computationally prohibitive for rapid design exploration. Existing generative surrogates (e.g., diffusion models) offer good distribution modeling over deterministic networks but suffer from slow iterative sampling. The need is for high-quality, single-pass generation that maintains accuracy while dramatically reducing inference time.
AI.method: The authors adapt a generative drifting framework to fluid mechanics with two key architectural contributions: (1) a conditional architecture that performs the drifting process in a learned VAE latent space, decoupling representation learning from generation; and (2) a label-aware masking mechanism that ensures generated flow fields align with specified boundary conditions. A spatial-conditioning variant is also proposed to explore generalization to unseen geometries.
AI.result: The label-conditioned model matches iterative diffusion models in both accuracy and flow consistency metrics while running two orders of magnitude faster. The spatial-conditioning variant demonstrates a promising path toward geometry generalization, though this direction is presented as preliminary.
AI.conclusion: Conditional drifting is a highly efficient alternative to diffusion-based approaches for CFD surrogate modeling, unlocking real-time applications where inference speed is critical, such as interactive indoor environment optimization.

title: Network Recovery from Cascade Data: A Debiased Jacobian-Based Machine Learning Approach
summary: Many important outcomes unfold as dynamic cascades, including product adoption, disease spread, financial distress, and information diffusion. A central challenge is to recover the hidden influence network behind these cascades. Existing methods typically assume a specific diffusion model, and their performance degrades substantially when that assumption is misspecified. We propose CascadeNet, a Jacobian-based machine learning framework for network recovery that does not require specifying a diffusion mechanism. The key idea is that the underlying influence structure can be characterized by the Jacobian of the one-step transition function. CascadeNet first constructs a flexible estimator of the transition function, and further applies Neyman-orthogonal debiasing via the Riesz representer, so that the debiased Jacobian is $\sqrt{n}$-consistent and asymptotically normal, enabling formal inference on the network structure. We validate CascadeNet in both a simulation exercise and a real-world empirical application. In simulations, where the data-generating process is known, CascadeNet achieves the highest network recovery accuracy across nine common data-generating processes. In an empirical application to COVID-19 transmission across Spain's 52 provinces, CascadeNet recovers transmission networks that are significantly correlated with the true inter-province mobility network, whereas networks recovered by baseline methods show no significant alignment with the ground truth.

AI.tldr: CascadeNet is a machine learning framework that recovers hidden influence networks from cascade data without assuming a specific diffusion model, using Jacobian estimation with Neyman-orthogonal debiasing to achieve √n-consistency and asymptotic normality, outperforming existing methods in simulations and a COVID-19 mobility case study.
AI.motivation: Existing network recovery methods rely on specifying a parametric diffusion model (e.g., independent cascade, linear threshold), and their accuracy degrades severely under model misspecification. There is a need for a model-agnostic method that can recover influence networks without committing to a particular diffusion mechanism.
AI.method: CascadeNet estimates the one-step transition function flexibly (using a neural network or similar), then computes its Jacobian to characterize the influence structure. It applies Neyman-orthogonal debiasing via the Riesz representer to remove regularization bias, yielding a √n-consistent, asymptotically normal estimator that supports formal statistical inference on network edges.
